topics.title: Current and Voltage Division Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Current and Voltage Division Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage Division Rule (VDR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Division Rule (VDR):** When a total voltage is applied across a series combination of two or more resistors, the voltage drop across any individual resistor is **directly proportional** to the value of that resistor relative to the total series resistance. It is essentially a direct practical application of **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Ohm's Law**.

In a series circuit, the **same current** flows through every component, but the voltage distributes (divides) across the resistors according to their ohmic values. The larger the resistance, the greater the share of the total voltage it consumes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way — The Water Pipe Analogy:**
> Imagine two narrow pipes connected end-to-end (in series), forcing the same water flow through both. If the second pipe is much narrower (higher resistance), the pressure drop (voltage) across that pipe will be much higher than across the first wider pipe. The total pressure (voltage) is shared between the two pipes in proportion to how much they resist the flow.

## 1.2 Current Division Rule (CDR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current Division Rule (CDR):** When a total current enters a parallel combination of two or more resistors, the current through any individual branch is **inversely proportional** to the resistance of that branch. It is a direct practical application of **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** and **Ohm's Law**.

In a parallel circuit, all branches share the **same voltage** across them, but the current distributes (divides) inversely to the resistance — the path of least resistance carries the most current.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Traffic Junction Analogy:**
> Picture a road that splits into two lanes at a junction. One lane is wide and smooth (low resistance — carries more cars/current), while the other is narrow and bumpy (high resistance — carries fewer cars/current). The total incoming traffic (current) splits between the two lanes in inverse proportion to how restrictive each lane is.

## 1.3 Why These Rules Matter in KTU Examinations

Both rules are **high-weightage foundational tools** used throughout circuit analysis, network theorems, and AC circuits. They appear in nearly every module of the syllabus and form the basis for solving Thevenin/Norton equivalents, superposition problems, and bridge circuits. Mastering these rules is non-negotiable for a strong KTU score.

The standard metric used throughout this topic is the **SI unit of Resistance: the Ohm (Ω)**, where **1 Ω = 1 V/A**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Voltage Division Rule — Operational Logic

Consider two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ connected in **series** across a source voltage $V$:

- The same current $I$ flows through both resistors.
- The total (equivalent) resistance seen by the source is $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2$.
- Applying Ohm's law: $I = \dfrac{V}{R_1 + R_2}$.
- The voltage across $R_1$ is $V_1 = I \cdot R_1$.
- The voltage across $R_2$ is $V_2 = I \cdot R_2$.

**The "Why" Behind VDR:**
- KVL states $V = V_1 + V_2$, so voltage MUST split.
- Since current is common (series), $V \propto R$ (Ohm's law).
- Hence, the resistor with the **larger value** drops the **larger share** of voltage.

**The "How" — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Identify all resistors that are in **series** with each other and the source.
2. Compute the **total resistance** $R_{total} = \sum R_i$.
3. Compute the **common series current** $I = V / R_{total}$.
4. For each resistor, multiply $I$ by that resistor's value to get its voltage drop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extension to N Resistors:** VDR is not limited to two resistors. For $N$ resistors in series, the voltage across resistor $R_k$ is given by $V_k = V \cdot \dfrac{R_k}{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_N}$.

## 2.2 Current Division Rule — Operational Logic

Consider two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ connected in **parallel** with branch currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ respectively, and a total incoming current $I$:

- The same voltage $V$ appears across both resistors.
- By Ohm's law: $I_1 = \dfrac{V}{R_1}$ and $I_2 = \dfrac{V}{R_2}$.
- By KCL: $I = I_1 + I_2 = \dfrac{V}{R_1} + \dfrac{V}{R_2} = V \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$.
- Therefore: $V = I \cdot \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ (which is $I \cdot R_{eq}$ for parallel combination).

**The "Why" Behind CDR:**
- KCL states that incoming current MUST split among branches.
- Since voltage is common (parallel), $I \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$.
- Hence, the branch with the **smaller resistance** carries the **larger share** of current.

**The "How" — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Identify all resistors that are in **parallel** with each other.
2. Determine the total incoming current $I$.
3. For each branch, apply the inverse-resistance weighting.
4. The branch with the smallest resistance hogs the most current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extension to N Resistors:** For $N$ parallel resistors, the current through resistor $R_k$ is $I_k = I \cdot \dfrac{G_k}{G_1 + G_2 + \cdots + G_N}$ where $G_k = \dfrac{1}{R_k}$ is the conductance in **siemens (S)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula a KTU 2024 student MUST memorize for this topic.**

| # | Rule | Circuit Type | Formula (Two Resistors) | General Form (N Resistors) | Key Relationship |
|---|------|--------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Voltage Division | Series | $V_1 = V \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $V_k = V \cdot \dfrac{R_k}{\sum R_i}$ | $V \propto R$ |
| 2 | Voltage Division | Series | $V_2 = V \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | (same as above) | $V \propto R$ |
| 3 | Current Division | Parallel | $I_1 = I \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $I_k = I \cdot \dfrac{G_k}{\sum G_i}$ | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$ |
| 4 | Current Division | Parallel | $I_2 = I \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | (same as above) | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$ |
| 5 | Series Resistance | — | $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2$ | $R_{eq} = \sum R_i$ | Additive |
| 6 | Parallel Resistance | — | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{R_i}$ | Harmonic |
| 7 | Conductance | — | $G = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | — | Unit: **siemens (S)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary / Sanity Conditions to Remember:**
> - If $R_1 = R_2$ in series, each drops **half** the voltage.
> - If $R_1 = R_2$ in parallel, each carries **half** the current.
> - If $R_1 \to 0$ (short) in parallel with $R_2$, **all** current goes through $R_1$ (short circuit path).
> - If $R_1 \to \infty$ (open) in parallel with $R_2$, **all** current goes through $R_2$.
> - VDR is invalid for parallel branches; CDR is invalid for series branches.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of VDR | Application of CDR |
|-------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Electronics Design** | Biasing of transistor amplifier stages using resistor divider networks | Shunt resistors for ammeters to extend range |
| **Power Systems** | Voltage drop calculations across transmission lines | Current sharing between parallel generator units |
| **Sensor Circuits** | Potentiometer-based position/voltage sensors | Signal distribution in audio mixer networks |
| **Battery Technology** | Cell balancing in series battery packs | Load sharing in parallel battery configurations |
| **PCB Design** | Pull-up / pull-down resistor networks for digital logic | Current mirrors in analog integrated circuits |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Voltage Division Rule

**Starting Circuit:** A DC source $V$ connected across two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**
The sum of voltage rises equals the sum of voltage drops around a closed loop.

$$V = V_{R_1} + V_{R_2}$$

where $V_{R_1}$ is the voltage drop across $R_1$ and $V_{R_2}$ is the voltage drop across $R_2$.

**Step 2 — Identify the Equivalent Resistance:**
For resistors in series, resistances add directly.

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to the Whole Loop:**
The single current $I$ flowing through the series loop is the source voltage divided by total resistance.

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{V}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ohm's Law to Each Individual Resistor:**
Since the same current $I$ flows through each series resistor, the voltage across each is $I$ times its own resistance.

$$V_{R_1} = I \cdot R_1$$

$$V_{R_2} = I \cdot R_2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Expression for I into Each Voltage Equation:**

$$V_{R_1} = \left( \frac{V}{R_1 + R_2} \right) \cdot R_1$$

$$V_{R_1} = V \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{R_2} = \left( \frac{V}{R_1 + R_2} \right) \cdot R_2$$

$$V_{R_2} = V \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 6 — Final Consolidated Form (Voltage Division Rule):**

$$\boxed{V_{R_k} = V \cdot \frac{R_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i}}$$

**Verification using KVL:** Adding the two voltage drops should recover the source voltage.

$$V_{R_1} + V_{R_2} = V \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} + V \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{R_1} + V_{R_2} = V \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{R_1} + V_{R_2} = V \cdot 1 = V \quad \checkmark$$

The derivation is mathematically consistent with KVL.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Current Division Rule

**Starting Circuit:** A DC source delivering total current $I$ into a node that splits into two parallel branches containing $R_1$ and $R_2$, with branch currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ respectively.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):**
The total current entering a node equals the sum of currents leaving it.

$$I = I_1 + I_2$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that Both Branches See the Same Voltage:**
In a parallel connection, both resistors are connected directly across the source, so the voltage across each is identical. Call this common voltage $V$.

$$V = V_{R_1} = V_{R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Express Each Branch Current Using Ohm's Law:**

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the KCL Equation:**

$$I = \frac{V}{R_1} + \frac{V}{R_2}$$

**Step 5 — Factor Out the Common Voltage $V$:**

$$I = V \cdot \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

$$I = V \cdot \left( \frac{R_2 + R_1}{R_1 R_2} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the Common Voltage $V$:**

$$V = I \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

This is simply $V = I \cdot R_{eq,parallel}$, which validates the standard parallel resistance formula.

**Step 7 — Substitute V Back Into the Branch Current Equations:**

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1} = \frac{1}{R_1} \cdot \left( I \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$$

$$I_1 = I \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 (R_1 + R_2)}$$

$$I_1 = I \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 8 — Repeat the Process for $I_2$:**

$$I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2} = \frac{1}{R_2} \cdot \left( I \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$$

$$I_2 = I \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_2 (R_1 + R_2)}$$

$$I_2 = I \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 9 — Final Consolidated Form (Current Division Rule):**

$$\boxed{I_k = I \cdot \frac{G_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i} = I \cdot \frac{1/R_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (1/R_i)}}$$

For two resistors, the compact forms are:

$$\boxed{I_1 = I \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{and} \quad I_2 = I \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

**Verification using KCL:** Adding the two branch currents should recover the total current.

$$I_1 + I_2 = I \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + I \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$I_1 + I_2 = I \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$I_1 + I_2 = I \quad \checkmark$$

The derivation is mathematically consistent with KCL.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Demonstrating Both Rules)

**Problem Statement:** A **24 V** DC source is connected across a series combination of $R_1 = 4\ \Omega$ and $R_2 = 8\ \Omega$. A resistor $R_3 = 6\ \Omega$ is connected in parallel with $R_2$. Find:
- (a) The current through $R_1$.
- (b) The voltage across $R_3$.
- (c) The current through $R_3$ using the Current Division Rule.

**Step 1 — Simplify the Network:**
$R_2$ and $R_3$ are in parallel. Their equivalent is:

$$R_{23} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{8 \cdot 6}{8 + 6} = \frac{48}{14} = \frac{24}{7}\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Total Series Resistance:**
$R_1$ is in series with $R_{23}$:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_{23} = 4 + \frac{24}{7} = \frac{28 + 24}{7} = \frac{52}{7}\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Total Source Current (= Current through $R_1$):**

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_{total}} = \frac{24}{52/7} = \frac{24 \cdot 7}{52} = \frac{168}{52} = \frac{42}{13} \approx 3.23\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage across $R_1$ (using VDR):**

$$V_1 = V \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{23}} = 24 \cdot \frac{4}{52/7} = 24 \cdot \frac{28}{52} = \frac{672}{52} \approx 12.92\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Voltage across the Parallel Section (= Voltage across $R_3$):**

$$V_3 = V - V_1 = 24 - 12.92 = 11.08\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Voltage across $R_3$ using VDR inside the parallel block (verification):**

$$V_3 = I_1 \cdot R_{23} = \frac{42}{13} \cdot \frac{24}{7} = \frac{42 \cdot 24}{13 \cdot 7} = \frac{1008}{91} \approx 11.08\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Current through $R_3$ using CDR:**
The total current $I_1 = \dfrac{42}{13}\ \text{A}$ enters the parallel combination of $R_2$ and $R_3$.

$$I_3 = I_1 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{42}{13} \cdot \frac{8}{8 + 6} = \frac{42}{13} \cdot \frac{8}{14} = \frac{42 \cdot 8}{13 \cdot 14} = \frac{336}{182} = \frac{24}{13} \approx 1.85\ \text{A}$$

**Step 8 — Cross-check using Ohm's Law on $R_3$:**

$$I_3 = \frac{V_3}{R_3} = \frac{11.08}{6} \approx 1.85\ \text{A} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice how VDR and CDR are used **together** in the same problem — VDR splits voltage in the series portion, and CDR splits current in the parallel portion. This is a recurring pattern in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Voltage Division Rule — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical flow of how VDR distributes voltage across a series resistor chain. (Adapted as a functional flow since Mermaid cannot render physical circuit schematics.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Voltage Source V] --> B[Series Resistor R1]
    B --> C[Series Resistor R2]
    C --> D[Series Resistor Rn]
    D --> E[Return to Source Negative]

    F[Total Resistance Rtotal] --> G[Compute Rtotal = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn]
    G --> H[Compute Series Current I = V / Rtotal]

    H --> I[Apply VDR Formula]
    I --> J[V1 = V * R1 / Rtotal]
    I --> K[V2 = V * R2 / Rtotal]
    I --> L[Vn = V * Rn / Rtotal]

    J --> M[KVL Check: V1 + V2 + ... + Vn = V]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Current Division Rule — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical flow of how CDR distributes current across a parallel resistor network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Current I enters node] --> B[Node Splits into N Parallel Branches]
    B --> C1[Branch 1: R1]
    B --> C2[Branch 2: R2]
    B --> C3[Branch N: Rn]

    D[Total Conductance Gtotal] --> E[Compute Gtotal = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... + 1/Rn]
    E --> F[Apply CDR Formula]
    F --> G1[I1 = I * R2 / R1+R2 for 2-resistor case]
    F --> G2[I2 = I * R1 / R1+R2 for 2-resistor case]
    F --> G3[In = I * Gn / Gtotal for N-resistor case]

    G1 --> H[KCL Check: I1 + I2 + ... + In = I]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Which Rule to Apply?

The following decision tree helps a student identify whether to apply VDR or CDR when solving a KTU problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Circuit Topology] --> B{Are the resistors in SERIES?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply VOLTAGE Division Rule]
    B -- No --> D{Are the resistors in PARALLEL?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply CURRENT Division Rule]
    D -- No --> F[Reduce Network Step-by-Step]
    F --> G{After reduction, are remaining resistors in series?}
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> E

    C --> H[Use formula: Vk = V * Rk / Rtotal]
    E --> I[Use formula: Ik = I * Rother / R1 + R2]

    H --> J[Solve and verify with KVL]
    I --> K[Solve and verify with KCL]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node represents a logical step in the analysis. The arrows indicate the direction of computation. The final verification nodes (KVL/KCL checks) are critical — KTU examiners often award partial credit for these cross-checks even if the primary answer has a minor computational error.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the Voltage Division Rule for two resistors connected in series.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Division Rule:** When a DC voltage $V$ is applied across a series combination of two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$, the voltage drop across each resistor is proportional to its resistance value.

**Derivation (1 mark):**

The series current is $I = \dfrac{V}{R_1 + R_2}$.

By Ohm's law, the voltage across each resistor is:

$$V_1 = I R_1 = V \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \quad (1\ \text{mark})$$

$$V_2 = I R_2 = V \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad (1\ \text{mark})$$

**Conclusion:** The larger resistor drops the larger share of the total voltage.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Current Division Rule for two resistors connected in parallel.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current Division Rule:** When a total current $I$ enters a parallel combination of two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$, the current through each branch is inversely proportional to the resistance of that branch.

**Derivation (1 mark):**

The common voltage across the parallel combination is $V = I \cdot \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.

By Ohm's law, the branch currents are:

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1} = I \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad (1\ \text{mark})$$

$$I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2} = I \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \quad (1\ \text{mark})$$

**Conclusion:** The branch with the smaller resistance carries the larger share of the current.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Two resistors $R_1 = 6\ \Omega$ and $R_2 = 12\ \Omega$ are connected in **series** across a **36 V** DC source.
- (i) Calculate the current flowing through the circuit using Ohm's Law. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Using the Voltage Division Rule, determine the voltage drop across each resistor. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** A **10 A** total current splits into two parallel branches with $R_1 = 4\ \Omega$ and $R_2 = 6\ \Omega$.
- (i) Using the Current Division Rule, find the current through each branch. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) Verify your answer by computing the total power dissipated and the equivalent resistance. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Choice A:**

**Part (a)(i) — Series Current (3 marks):**

Total series resistance:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 = 6 + 12 = 18\ \Omega$$

By Ohm's Law:

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{total}} = \frac{36}{18} = 2\ \text{A}$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Voltage Division (4 marks):**

Applying VDR:

$$V_1 = V \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 36 \cdot \frac{6}{18} = 36 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 12\ \text{V}$$

$$V_2 = V \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 36 \cdot \frac{12}{18} = 36 \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 24\ \text{V}$$

**KVL Verification:** $V_1 + V_2 = 12 + 24 = 36\ \text{V} = V \quad \checkmark$

**Part (b)(i) — Current Division (4 marks):**

Applying CDR:

$$I_1 = I \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 10 \cdot \frac{6}{4 + 6} = 10 \cdot \frac{6}{10} = 6\ \text{A}$$

$$I_2 = I \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 10 \cdot \frac{4}{4 + 6} = 10 \cdot \frac{4}{10} = 4\ \text{A}$$

**KCL Verification:** $I_1 + I_2 = 6 + 4 = 10\ \text{A} = I \quad \checkmark$

**Part (b)(ii) — Power and Equivalent Resistance Verification (3 marks):**

Equivalent parallel resistance:

$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{4 \cdot 6}{4 + 6} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4\ \Omega$$

Voltage across the parallel combination:

$$V = I \cdot R_{eq} = 10 \cdot 2.4 = 24\ \text{V}$$

Total power dissipated:

$$P_{total} = V \cdot I = 24 \cdot 10 = 240\ \text{W}$$

Cross-check using branch powers:

$$P_1 = I_1^2 \cdot R_1 = 6^2 \cdot 4 = 36 \cdot 4 = 144\ \text{W}$$

$$P_2 = I_2^2 \cdot R_2 = 4^2 \cdot 6 = 16 \cdot 6 = 96\ \text{W}$$

$$P_1 + P_2 = 144 + 96 = 240\ \text{W} = P_{total} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### **Question Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A **48 V** battery is connected across a series-parallel network consisting of $R_1 = 10\ \Omega$ (in series with the source) followed by a parallel combination of $R_2 = 20\ \Omega$ and $R_3 = 30\ \Omega$.
- (i) Compute the equivalent resistance seen by the source. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) Find the current supplied by the battery and the voltage across the parallel section using VDR. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** For the same network:
- (i) Using the Current Division Rule, determine the current through $R_2$ and $R_3$. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the total power supplied by the source and the power dissipated in each resistor. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Choice B:**

**Part (a)(i) — Equivalent Resistance (4 marks):**

$R_2$ and $R_3$ are in parallel:

$$R_{23} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{20 \cdot 30}{20 + 30} = \frac{600}{50} = 12\ \Omega$$

$R_1$ is in series with $R_{23}$:

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_{23} = 10 + 12 = 22\ \Omega$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Source Current and VDR (3 marks):**

Source current (= current through $R_1$):

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{48}{22} = \frac{24}{11} \approx 2.18\ \text{A}$$

Voltage across $R_1$ using VDR:

$$V_1 = V \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_{eq}} = 48 \cdot \frac{10}{22} = \frac{480}{22} = \frac{240}{11} \approx 21.82\ \text{V}$$

Voltage across the parallel section:

$$V_{23} = V - V_1 = 48 - 21.82 = 26.18\ \text{V}$$

**Part (b)(i) — Current Division (4 marks):**

Total current entering the parallel section is $I_1 = \dfrac{24}{11}\ \text{A}$.

Applying CDR:

$$I_2 = I_1 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{30}{20 + 30} = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{30}{50} = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{3}{5} = \frac{72}{55} \approx 1.31\ \text{A}$$

$$I_3 = I_1 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{20}{50} = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{2}{5} = \frac{48}{55} \approx 0.87\ \text{A}$$

**KCL Verification:** $I_2 + I_3 = \dfrac{72}{55} + \dfrac{48}{55} = \dfrac{120}{55} = \dfrac{24}{11} = I_1 \quad \checkmark$

**Part (b)(ii) — Power Calculations (3 marks):**

Total power supplied by the source:

$$P_{total} = V \cdot I_1 = 48 \cdot \frac{24}{11} = \frac{1152}{11} \approx 104.73\ \text{W}$$

Power in each resistor:

$$P_1 = I_1^2 \cdot R_1 = \left( \frac{24}{11} \right)^2 \cdot 10 = \frac{576}{121} \cdot 10 = \frac{5760}{121} \approx 47.60\ \text{W}$$

$$P_2 = I_2^2 \cdot R_2 = \left( \frac{72}{55} \right)^2 \cdot 20 = \frac{5184}{3025} \cdot 20 = \frac{103680}{3025} \approx 34.27\ \text{W}$$

$$P_3 = I_3^2 \cdot R_3 = \left( \frac{48}{55} \right)^2 \cdot 30 = \frac{2304}{3025} \cdot 30 = \frac{69120}{3025} \approx 22.85\ \text{W}$$

**Verification:** $P_1 + P_2 + P_3 \approx 47.60 + 34.27 + 22.85 = 104.72\ \text{W} \approx P_{total} \quad \checkmark$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mixing up CDR formulas:** Students often write $I_1 = I \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ instead of the correct $I_1 = I \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$. **Remember:** In CDR, the current through a branch uses the **OTHER** resistor's value in the numerator. **Loss: Up to 3 marks per instance.**
> - **Applying VDR to parallel branches:** VDR is ONLY valid for series combinations. Applying $V = V_{source} \cdot R/(R_1+R_2)$ to a parallel branch will yield wrong answers. **Loss: Up to 4 marks.**
> - **Forgetting to reduce the network first:** For series-parallel networks, students sometimes apply VDR or CDR directly to the mixed circuit. Always reduce the network to a single equivalent resistance first. **Loss: Up to 2 marks for methodology.**
> - **Unit inconsistency:** Always carry units ($\Omega$, V, A, W) throughout the calculation. Examiners deduct marks for unitless final answers. **Loss: 0.5 to 1 mark.**
> - **Skipping verification:** KTU examiners award 0.5 to 1 mark extra for KCL/KVL cross-checks. Do not skip them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Current & Voltage Division Rule**

- [x] **VDR applies ONLY to series circuits; CDR applies ONLY to parallel circuits.**
- [x] **VDR formula (2 resistors):** $V_k = V \cdot \dfrac{R_k}{R_1 + R_2}$. The voltage is **directly proportional** to the resistance.
- [x] **CDR formula (2 resistors):** $I_k = I \cdot \dfrac{R_{\text{other}}}{R_1 + R_2}$. The current uses the **opposite** resistor in the numerator.
- [x] **CDR — current is inversely proportional to resistance.** Smaller resistance $\Rightarrow$ larger current.
- [x] **VDR — voltage is directly proportional to resistance.** Larger resistance $\Rightarrow$ larger voltage drop.
- [x] **Always reduce the network to its Thevenin/equivalent form before applying either rule.**
- [x] **Verify with KCL (sum of currents in = sum of currents out) and KVL (sum of voltages around a loop = 0).**
- [x] **Sanity check:** If $R_1 = R_2$ in series, each drops exactly half the source voltage.
- [x] **Sanity check:** If $R_1 = R_2$ in parallel, each carries exactly half the total current.
- [x] **Sanity check:** A short circuit ($R \to 0$) in parallel carries ALL the current; an open circuit ($R \to \infty$) carries NONE.
- [x] **Generalized N-resistor VDR:** $V_k = V \cdot \dfrac{R_k}{\sum R_i}$.
- [x] **Generalized N-resistor CDR:** $I_k = I \cdot \dfrac{G_k}{\sum G_i}$ where $G_k = 1/R_k$ is conductance in **siemens (S)**.
- [x] **Units to remember:** Resistance in **Ω**, Voltage in **V**, Current in **A**, Power in **W**, Conductance in **S**.
- [x] **Both rules are derived from Ohm's Law + KVL/KCL** — they are not independent laws.
- [x] **Common exam trap:** The total source voltage/current is given; you must find branch quantities. Never confuse source with branch.
- [x] **Power dissipation check:** $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ must always hold — use it as a cross-validation tool.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Potential, Capacitors and Inductors: V-I Relations and Energy Stored, Ohm's Law and Kirchhoff's Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relative Potential, Capacitors and Inductors: V-I Relations and Energy Stored, Ohm's Law and Kirchhoff's Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔋 Module 1: Foundations of Electrical Circuit Elements

## 1.1 Relative Potential — The Concept of Voltage Difference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Relative Potential):**
> *Relative Potential* between two points **A** and **B** in a circuit is the work done per unit charge in moving a positive test charge from point **A** to point **B**. It is denoted as **V<sub>AB</sub>** and represents the *potential difference* — not the absolute potential w.r.t. earth.

$$\boxed{V_{AB} = \frac{W_{AB}}{q}}$$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English):**

Imagine a hill. The "height" of the hill is like *absolute potential* (measured from sea level). The "steepness between two points on the hill" is like *relative potential*. In a circuit, what matters is **not the absolute potential of a point, but the *difference* between two points**, because that difference is what pushes current through a load — just as the slope of a hill determines how fast water flows down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A 1.5 V AA battery has terminal voltages of, say, +0.75 V and −0.75 V relative to its midpoint. The *relative potential* (1.5 V) is what lights your torch, not the absolute numbers.

---

## 1.2 Capacitor — The Electric-Field Energy Reservoir

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Capacitor):**
> A **capacitor** is a *passive two-terminal electrical component* that stores energy in an *electric field*. It physically consists of two conducting plates separated by an insulating dielectric. Its defining property is **capacitance (C)**, measured in **Farads (F)**.

$$\boxed{C = \frac{Q}{V}}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**

Think of a capacitor as a **thin, stretchy water tank with a rubber diaphragm in the middle**. When you push water in one side, the diaphragm stretches and stores mechanical energy. Release the pressure, and the energy comes back out. A capacitor works identically — it "stretches" an electric field between its plates to store energy that can be released later.

---

## 1.3 Inductor — The Magnetic-Field Energy Reservoir

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Inductor):**
> An **inductor** is a *passive two-terminal electrical component* that stores energy in a *magnetic field*. It is typically constructed as a coil of wire. Its defining property is **inductance (L)**, measured in **Henries (H)**.

$$\boxed{L = \frac{N \Phi}{i}}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**

Picture a **heavy flywheel connected to a shaft**. Once you spin the flywheel, it has a lot of *kinetic energy* stored. The flywheel "resists" sudden changes in speed — it keeps spinning even if you momentarily stop pushing. An inductor behaves the same way: it stores energy in its magnetic field and **opposes any sudden change in current**, exactly like a flywheel opposes sudden changes in rotational speed.

---

## 1.4 Ohm's Law — The Linear V–I Relation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Ohm's Law):**
> *At constant physical conditions (temperature, pressure, etc.), the voltage (V) across a conductor is directly proportional to the current (I) flowing through it.* The constant of proportionality is the **resistance R** in **Ohms (Ω)**.

$$\boxed{V = I R}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**

Water flowing through a narrow pipe is *resisted* by friction. The more you push (pressure = V), the more flow (current = I) you get, in direct proportion. A wider pipe (lower R) allows more flow for the same pressure. That's Ohm's Law in hydraulic disguise.

---

## 1.5 Kirchhoff's Laws — The Two Pillars of Circuit Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Kirchhoff's Two Laws:**

**Law 1 — Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):** *The algebraic sum of currents entering any node (junction) in an electrical network is zero.*

$$\boxed{\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k = 0}$$

**Law 2 — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):** *The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop in a network is zero.*

$$\boxed{\sum_{k=1}^{m} V_k = 0}$$

**Conceptual Analogy (KCL):** A node is like a 4-way junction of water pipes. Whatever amount of water flows *in* per second must equal the amount that flows *out* per second — water doesn't pile up at a junction.

**Conceptual Analogy (KVL):** Imagine hiking a closed mountain loop. You climb up some hills (voltage rises) and down others (voltage drops). When you return to your starting point, the **net elevation change is zero**. KVL says the same about voltages around a closed loop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capacitor — V–I Relation & Energy Storage

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

- **Step 1:** When a voltage *V* is applied across a capacitor, charge *Q = CV* accumulates on the plates (one plate +, the other −).
- **Step 2:** The accumulated charge creates an *electric field E* between the plates, which is where the energy is stored.
- **Step 3:** The **rate at which charge accumulates** is the current: *i = dQ/dt*. Since *Q = Cv*, we get the V–I relation.
- **Step 4:** Because voltage across a capacitor *cannot change instantaneously* (would require infinite current), the capacitor **opposes sudden voltage changes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind energy storage:** Work has to be done against the electric field of the already-deposited charge to deposit *more* charge. That work is what gets stored as energy.

---

## 2.2 Inductor — V–I Relation & Energy Storage

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

- **Step 1:** When current *i* flows through a coil, a magnetic flux *Φ* is set up in the core.
- **Step 2:** By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, a *changing* current induces a back-EMF that *opposes* the change — this is the V–I relation.
- **Step 3:** The magnetic field *B* is where the energy is stored.
- **Step 4:** Because current through an inductor *cannot change instantaneously* (would require infinite voltage), the inductor **opposes sudden current changes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind energy storage:** To build up a magnetic field, work must be done against the back-EMF generated by the changing flux itself. This work is stored as magnetic energy.

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|--------------------|---------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | Capacitance definition | $C = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | Farad (F) | $C \geq 0$ for passive elements |
| 2 | Capacitor V–I relation | $i = C \,\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | A | Voltage continuous: $v(0^+) = v(0^-)$ |
| 3 | Capacitor V–I (integral) | $v(t) = v(0) + \dfrac{1}{C}\displaystyle\int_0^t i(\tau)\,d\tau$ | V | Initial voltage $v(0)$ needed |
| 4 | Energy stored in capacitor | $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = \tfrac{Q^2}{2C} = \tfrac{1}{2} QV$ | Joule (J) | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 5 | Inductance definition | $L = \dfrac{N \Phi}{i}$ | Henry (H) | $L \geq 0$ for passive elements |
| 6 | Inductor V–I relation | $v = L \,\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V | Current continuous: $i(0^+) = i(0^-)$ |
| 7 | Inductor V–I (integral) | $i(t) = i(0) + \dfrac{1}{L}\displaystyle\int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau$ | A | Initial current $i(0)$ needed |
| 8 | Energy stored in inductor | $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Joule (J) | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 9 | Ohm's Law | $V = I R$ | V (with I in A, R in $\Omega$) | Linear; R is constant |
| 10 | KCL at any node | $\sum I_k = 0$ | — | Algebraic (with sign convention) |
| 11 | KVL around any loop | $\sum V_k = 0$ | — | Algebraic (with sign convention) |
| 12 | Power dissipated (R) | $P = V I = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Watt (W) | Only true for purely resistive R |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠️ CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Tables:** In KTU valuation, the symbols $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used to denote "absolute value" or "magnitude". The vertical pipe character in a markdown table is reserved by the table parser — therefore, the cheat sheet above uses standard mathematical notation, not pipes, to avoid breaking table syntax.

---

## 2.4 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capacitors** are used in *power-factor correction*, *signal coupling/decoupling*, *filter circuits*, *flash photography*, and *defibrillators*.
- **Inductors** are used in *transformers*, *switched-mode power supplies (SMPS)*, *induction motors*, *choke coils*, and *RFID tags*.
- **Ohm's Law** underpins *every* resistor-based sensor readout (e.g., thermistors, strain gauges, photoresistors).
- **KCL & KVL** form the algorithmic backbone of *SPICE*, *PSpice*, *LTspice*, and every modern **PCB design tool** (Altium, KiCad).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Derivations of capacitor and inductor energy storage equations are *guaranteed* 7-mark questions in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE. The steps below are exhaustive — every algebraic transition is shown.

---

## 3.1 ⚡ Derivation — Energy Stored in a Capacitor

**Statement to prove:** *The energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance **C** charged to a voltage **V** is $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$.*

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of work done.**

When an incremental charge $dq$ is transferred from one plate to the other against the instantaneous voltage $v$ across the capacitor, the infinitesimal work done is:

$$dW = v \cdot dq$$

**Step 2 — Express charge in terms of voltage using capacitance.**

For a linear capacitor, $q = C v$. Differentiating both sides w.r.t. *v*:

$$dq = C \, dv$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $dq$ into the work expression.**

$$dW = v \cdot (C \, dv) = C v \, dv$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from the uncharged state ($v = 0$, $q = 0$, $W = 0$) to the final state ($v = V$, $q = Q$, $W = W_C$).**

$$W_C = \int_{0}^{V} C v \, dv$$

**Step 5 — Perform the integration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C &= C \int_{0}^{V} v \, dv \\
    &= C \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{V} \\
    &= C \left( \frac{V^2}{2} - \frac{0^2}{2} \right) \\
    &= \frac{1}{2} C V^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent alternative forms.**

Using $Q = CV$, the energy can also be written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C &= \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} C V \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} Q V \\
    &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{Q}{V} \cdot V^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_C = \frac{Q^2}{2C}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\therefore W_C = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} Q V = \frac{Q^2}{2C}}$$

---

## 3.2 🧲 Derivation — Energy Stored in an Inductor

**Statement to prove:** *The energy stored in an inductor of inductance **L** carrying a current **I** is $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$.*

**Step 1 — Start with the V–I relation of the inductor.**

The voltage across an inductor is:

$$v_L = L \frac{di}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous power delivered to the inductor.**

Electrical power is the product of voltage and current:

$$p(t) = v_L(t) \cdot i(t) = \left( L \frac{di}{dt} \right) \cdot i = L i \, \frac{di}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Infinitesimal energy supplied in time $dt$.**

$$dW = p(t) \, dt = L i \, \frac{di}{dt} \, dt = L i \, di$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from the de-energized state ($i = 0$, $W = 0$) to the final state ($i = I$, $W = W_L$).**

$$W_L = \int_{0}^{I} L i \, di$$

**Step 5 — Perform the integration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_L &= L \int_{0}^{I} i \, di \\
    &= L \left[ \frac{i^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{I} \\
    &= L \left( \frac{I^2}{2} - 0 \right) \\
    &= \frac{1}{2} L I^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\therefore W_L = \frac{1}{2} L I^2}$$

---

## 3.3 📊 Analytical Matrix — Energy Density Comparison

| Storage Element | Field Used | Energy Formula | Energy Density (in field) | Continuity Constraint |
|-----------------|------------|----------------|---------------------------|-----------------------|
| Capacitor       | Electric ($E$-field) | $\tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | $u_E = \tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon E^2$ (J/m³) | $v(t)$ is continuous |
| Inductor        | Magnetic ($B$-field) | $\tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | $u_B = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu}$ (J/m³) | $i(t)$ is continuous |
| Resistor        | None (dissipative) | $W_R = \int i^2 R \, dt$ (lost as heat) | $u_R = i^2 \rho$ (W/m³) | $v$ and $i$ can step freely |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix matters:** In KTU problems, students often confuse *continuous* and *discontinuous* quantities at switching instants. **Voltage across C is continuous; current through L is continuous.** Resistor voltages and currents can change abruptly.

---

## 3.4 🔢 Numerical Worked Example — Capacitor Energy

> **Problem:** A 100 μF capacitor is charged to 50 V. Find the charge stored and the energy stored. If the voltage is doubled to 100 V (same capacitance), what is the new energy?

**Solution:**

Given: $C = 100 \,\mu F = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ F, $V = 50$ V.

**Charge stored:**

$$Q = C V = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 50 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ C} = 5 \text{ mC}$$

**Energy stored:**

$$W_C = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times (50)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 2500$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.25 \\
    &= 0.125 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**When voltage is doubled to 100 V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C' &= \frac{1}{2} C (2V)^2 = \frac{1}{2} C \cdot 4 V^2 = 4 W_C \\
     &= 4 \times 0.125 = 0.5 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{Q = 5 \text{ mC}, \quad W_C = 0.125 \text{ J at 50 V}, \quad W_C' = 0.5 \text{ J at 100 V}}$$

**Physical insight:** *Doubling the voltage quadruples the energy stored.* This non-linear scaling is critical when designing capacitor banks for pulsed-power applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relative Potential Concept

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Point A: +12V absolute"] -->|Relative potential VAB = VA - VB| A2["Point B: +5V absolute"]
    A2 -->|Relative potential VBA = VB - VA| A1
    R1["Load Resistor R"] --> A1
    R1 --> A2
    A1 --> I1["Current I flows from A to B"]
```

**Interpretation:** The voltage *across the load* is the *difference* of the two absolute potentials, not their individual values.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — KCL at a Node

```mermaid
graph TD
    I1["I1 = 3A entering"] --> nodeK((Node K))
    I2["I2 = 5A entering"] --> nodeK
    nodeK --> I3["I3 leaving"]
    nodeK --> I4["I4 = 4A leaving"]
    nodeK --> I5["I5 leaving"]
    SUM["KCL: I1 + I2 = I3 + I4 + I5"]
    nodeK -.-> SUM
    I3 -.-> SUM
    I4 -.-> SUM
    I5 -.-> SUM
```

**Interpretation:** Whatever current enters Node K must equal the current leaving Node K. The KCL equation becomes: $3 + 5 = 4 + I3 + I5 \Rightarrow I3 + I5 = 4$ A.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — KVL Around a Series Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1["V1 = 12V battery +"] --> R1["R1 = 4 ohm drop IR"]
    R1 --> R2["R2 = 6 ohm drop IR"]
    R2 --> V2["V2 = 2V battery -"]
    V2 -->|Return to start| V1
    EQN["KVL: +12 - I*R1 - I*R2 - 2 = 0"]
    V1 -.-> EQN
    R1 -.-> EQN
    R2 -.-> EQN
    V2 -.-> EQN
```

**Interpretation:** The IR-drops across resistors are *rises* (with sign), and the EMF of the second battery (opposing direction) is a *drop*. The algebraic sum must vanish.

**Solving the example:** $12 - 4I - 6I - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow 10 = 10I \Rightarrow I = 1$ A.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Functional Block — Energy Flow in Passive Elements

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC["Electrical Source"] -->|Provides P=VI| RBLOCK["Resistor Block"]
    SRC -->|Provides P=VI| CBLOCK["Capacitor Block"]
    SRC -->|Provides P=VI| LBLOCK["Inductor Block"]
    RBLOCK -->|Dissipates| HEAT["Heat Loss I2R"]
    CBLOCK -->|Stores| EFIELD["Electric Field Energy 1/2 CV2"]
    LBLOCK -->|Stores| MFIELD["Magnetic Field Energy 1/2 LI2"]
    HEAT --> ENDS["Irreversible"]
    EFIELD --> BACK1["Recoverable"]
    MFIELD --> BACK2["Recoverable"]
```

**Interpretation:** Resistors *consume* electrical energy irreversibly (heat); capacitors and inductors *store* energy reversibly in fields.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Capacitor vs Inductor Behavioural Contrast

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Voltage applied to C"] -->|Integrates| B1["Charge Q builds up"]
    B1 --> C1["Stores 1/2 CV2 in E-field"]
    D1["Current applied to L"] -->|Integrates| E1["Flux Phi builds up"]
    E1 --> F1["Stores 1/2 LI2 in B-field"]
    C1 --> G1["Opposes sudden change in V"]
    F1 --> H1["Opposes sudden change in I"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`

**Define *relative potential*. How is it different from *absolute potential*? Give one practical example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Relative potential* between two points A and B in a circuit is the work done per unit positive charge in moving it from A to B. It is given by $V_{AB} = V_A - V_B$.

**Difference (1 mark):** *Absolute potential* is the potential of a point measured w.r.t. the earth (defined as 0 V). *Relative potential* is the difference between the potentials of any two points in the circuit — independent of the earth reference.

**Example (1 mark):** In a 9 V battery, the two terminals are at +4.5 V and −4.5 V w.r.t. the midpoint (absolute potentials). The *relative potential* across the terminals is 9 V, which is what drives current through a load. Hence, only the *relative potential* matters for circuit operation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`

**State Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) and Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL). Write the mathematical form of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**KCL (1.5 marks):** *The algebraic sum of currents at any node (junction) in an electrical network is zero.* Mathematically: $\sum I_k = 0$, where currents *entering* the node are taken positive and those *leaving* are negative (or vice versa, consistently).

**KVL (1.5 marks):** *The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop in a network is zero.* Mathematically: $\sum V_k = 0$, where voltage rises (e.g., from − to + of a battery) are taken positive and voltage drops (across resistors in the direction of current) are taken negative.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern Reminder]:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7** between sub-parts (a) and (b). Students must answer **only one of the two choices**.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C when charged to a voltage V. Show that the energy can also be expressed as* $W = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Two capacitors of capacitance 6 μF and 3 μF are connected in (i) series and (ii) parallel across a 100 V DC supply. Determine the equivalent capacitance, the charge on, and the energy stored in each capacitor in both cases.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Solution to (a):** *Derivation of energy in a capacitor*

**Step 1:** Consider a capacitor being charged from 0 to final voltage $V$. Let at any instant the voltage be $v$ and the corresponding charge be $q$. We have $q = C v$.

**Step 2:** To transfer an additional small charge $dq$ from one plate to the other, the work done is $dW = v \, dq$.

**Step 3:** Substitute $dq = C \, dv$ (differentiating $q = C v$):

$$dW = v \cdot C \, dv = C v \, dv$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $v = 0$ (uncharged) to $v = V$ (fully charged):

$$W_C = \int_{0}^{V} C v \, dv = C \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{V} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

**Step 5:** Show alternative forms using $Q = CV$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C &= \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} (C V) \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} Q V \\
W_C &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{Q}{V} \cdot V^2 = \frac{Q^2}{2C}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{W_C = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} Q V = \frac{Q^2}{2C}}$$

---

**Solution to (b):** *Series and parallel capacitor network*

**Given:** $C_1 = 6\,\mu F$, $C_2 = 3\,\mu F$, $V = 100$ V.

**Case (i) — Series Connection:**

Equivalent capacitance:

$$
C_{eq,\,s} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{6 \times 3}{6 + 3} = \frac{18}{9} = 2 \;\mu F
$$

In series, **charge is the same** on each capacitor: $Q = C_{eq,\,s} \cdot V = 2 \,\mu F \times 100 = 200 \,\mu C$.

Voltage across $C_1$: $V_1 = Q/C_1 = 200/6 = 33.33$ V.
Voltage across $C_2$: $V_2 = Q/C_2 = 200/3 = 66.67$ V. (Check: $V_1 + V_2 = 100$ V ✓)

Energy in $C_1$: $W_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} C_1 V_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times (33.33)^2 \approx 3.33 \times 10^{-3}$ J.
Energy in $C_2$: $W_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} C_2 V_2^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times (66.67)^2 \approx 6.67 \times 10^{-3}$ J.
Total energy: $W_{total} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{eq,\,s} V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 100^2 = 0.01$ J ✓

**Case (ii) — Parallel Connection:**

Equivalent capacitance:

$$C_{eq,\,p} = C_1 + C_2 = 6 + 3 = 9 \;\mu F$$

In parallel, **voltage is the same** across each: $V_1 = V_2 = 100$ V.

Charge on $C_1$: $Q_1 = C_1 V = 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 600 \,\mu C$.
Charge on $C_2$: $Q_2 = C_2 V = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 300 \,\mu C$.
Total charge: $Q_{total} = 900 \,\mu C$ (Check: $C_{eq,\,p} \cdot V = 9 \times 100 = 900 \,\mu C$ ✓)

Energy in $C_1$: $W_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 100^2 = 0.03$ J.
Energy in $C_2$: $W_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 100^2 = 0.015$ J.
Total energy: $W_{total} = 0.045$ J (Check: $\tfrac{1}{2} C_{eq,\,p} V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-6} \times 10000 = 0.045$ J ✓)

$$\boxed{C_{eq,\,s} = 2\,\mu F, \; C_{eq,\,p} = 9\,\mu F, \; W_{total,\,s} = 0.01 \text{ J}, \; W_{total,\,p} = 0.045 \text{ J}}$$

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the energy stored in an inductor of inductance L carrying a steady current I.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For the circuit shown (a 24 V battery connected in series with $R_1 = 4\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 6\,\Omega$, and a 4 V battery opposing the 24 V source), apply Kirchhoff's laws and determine the current flowing in the loop and the voltage drop across each resistor.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Solution to (a):** *Derivation of energy in an inductor*

**Step 1:** The voltage across an inductor carrying a time-varying current is $v_L = L \, \dfrac{di}{dt}$.

**Step 2:** The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the inductor:

$$p(t) = v_L \cdot i = \left( L \frac{di}{dt} \right) i = L i \frac{di}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Energy supplied in time $dt$:

$$dW = p(t) \, dt = L i \, di$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $i = 0$ (no current) to $i = I$ (final steady current):

$$W_L = \int_{0}^{I} L i \, di = L \left[ \frac{i^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{I} = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$

$$\boxed{W_L = \frac{1}{2} L I^2}$$

---

**Solution to (b):** *Applying Kirchhoff's laws to a two-source series loop*

**Given:** $V_1 = 24$ V (driving), $V_2 = 4$ V (opposing), $R_1 = 4\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 6\,\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Choose a loop current direction** (assume clockwise, $I$ A).

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the loop** (going clockwise, treating the 24 V source as a rise and the 4 V source as a drop):

$$+24 - I R_1 - I R_2 - 4 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve:**

$$24 - 4 = I(4 + 6) \;\Rightarrow\; 20 = 10 I \;\Rightarrow\; I = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage drops across each resistor:**

$$V_{R_1} = I R_1 = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{R_2} = I R_2 = 2 \times 6 = 12 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — KVL Verification:** $+24 - 8 - 12 - 4 = 0$ ✓

$$\boxed{I = 2 \text{ A}, \quad V_{R_1} = 8 \text{ V}, \quad V_{R_2} = 12 \text{ V}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Sign convention in KVL (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often *forget* the negative sign of the opposing battery or assign the wrong sign to IR-drops. The golden rule: *pick a loop direction, stick to it, and write down every term with its sign.*
>
> 2. **Units in energy formulas (–1 mark):** Capacitance must be in **Farads** (not μF or nF) when computing energy in Joules. Always convert: $1\,\mu F = 10^{-6}$ F.
>
> 3. **Series vs Parallel confusion (–2 marks):** In **series**, *charge* is the same on each capacitor. In **parallel**, *voltage* is the same across each. Memorize the cheat-sheet row for this.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to write the V–I relation explicitly (–1 mark):** Examiners expect to see $i = C \tfrac{dv}{dt}$ (for C) or $v = L \tfrac{di}{dt}$ (for L) at the start of a derivation, *not* just the final energy formula.
>
> 5. **"Instantaneous" vs "Steady-state" (–2 marks):** Energy in a capacitor is *stored* in the electric field, not dissipated. Make it clear in the answer that capacitor/inductor energy is **recoverable** — only resistors dissipate energy.
>
> 6. **Missing the relative-vs-absolute distinction (–1 mark):** A question on "potential at a node" requires specifying the **reference**. If you don't mention the reference, full marks will not be awarded.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this for last-minute review):**

- ⭐ **Relative Potential** is the *difference* between the potentials of two points; it is what drives current. Absolute potential requires an earth reference.
- ⭐ **Capacitor** stores energy in an *electric field*; the key V–I relation is $i = C \dfrac{dv}{dt}$; voltage across it is *continuous*.
- ⭐ **Inductor** stores energy in a *magnetic field*; the key V–I relation is $v = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$; current through it is *continuous*.
- ⭐ **Energy in Capacitor:** $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} Q V = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C}$ (Joules).
- ⭐ **Energy in Inductor:** $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ (Joules).
- ⭐ **Ohm's Law:** $V = IR$ — applies *only* to linear, passive, bilateral resistors at constant temperature.
- ⭐ **KCL (Current Law):** $\sum I_k = 0$ at any node — based on *conservation of charge*.
- ⭐ **KVL (Voltage Law):** $\sum V_k = 0$ around any closed loop — based on *conservation of energy*.
- ⭐ In **series** capacitors: same charge, voltages add as reciprocals of C.
- ⭐ In **parallel** capacitors: same voltage, capacitances add directly.
- ⭐ **Capacitor and inductor are dual elements**: swap $V \leftrightarrow I$ and $C \leftrightarrow L$ to get the dual relationship.
- ⭐ Resistor energy is *dissipated* (heat); capacitor and inductor energy is *stored* (recoverable).
- ⭐ Power absorbed by a passive element: $P = V \cdot I$ (always positive for passive R, L, C).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Star-Delta Conversion (Resistive Networks Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Star-Delta Conversion (Resistive Networks Only) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Transformation** is a network reduction technique used in linear passive resistive networks to convert between two equivalent three-terminal configurations: the **Star (Y)** connection and the **Delta (Δ)** connection. The transformation preserves the equivalent resistance measured between any pair of external terminals, thereby allowing complex bridge or non-series-parallel networks to be reduced into simpler series-parallel combinations for analysis. In a **Star (Y) connection**, three resistors $R_A$, $R_B$, and $R_C$ share a single common node called the **neutral point (N)**, and the free ends connect to the external terminals A, B, and C. In a **Delta (Δ) connection**, three resistors $R_{AB}$, $R_{BC}$, and $R_{CA}$ are connected head-to-tail in a closed triangular loop between the same external terminals A, B, and C. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The current scope is restricted strictly to **resistive networks** — capacitors, inductors, and AC impedance transformations are NOT part of this module. Only DC equivalent resistance reduction is examined. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Pipeline Model Imagine three water pipelines meeting at a **single junction (Star)** versus three pipelines forming a **closed triangle loop (Delta)**, where water can flow either across the direct pipe or around the long way through two pipes. - In the **Star (Y)** model: water entering from terminal A must first pass through $R_A$ to reach the central node, then out through $R_B$ or $R_C$. - In the **Delta (Δ)** model: water entering from A can go **directly** to B via $R_{AB}$, **OR** detour through C using $R_{AC}$ and $R_{CB}$ — two parallel paths. For a layman to see the *exact same flow* (current) and *exact same pressure drop* (voltage) at the external terminals, the resistors in the two shapes must be **mathematically related**. This is precisely the essence of the Y-Δ transformation. ### 1.3 The Three Standard Boundary Conditions > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Parameters to Remember for the KTU Board Exam:** > - **Number of terminals:** 3 (labeled A, B, C) > - **Number of resistors in Star:** 3 > - **Number of resistors in Delta:** 3 > - **Equivalence metric:** Equivalent resistance between ANY two terminals (with the third open) MUST be identical > - **Universal constant:** No physical constant is involved — it is a pure geometric/topological equivalence > - **Standard unit:** All resistances measured in **Ohms (Ω)** ### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Real Engineering - **Power Distribution Networks:** Three-phase power systems use Y and Δ transformer windings, where understanding equivalence aids fault-current calculations. - **Bridge Circuit Analysis:** Wheatstone bridge and its unbalanced variant (e.g., for sensor measurement) require Y-Δ transformation to solve. - **Filter Network Design:** Passive filter prototypes often require converting between T and π sections (mathematical equivalents of Y and Δ). - **PCB and IC Design:** Internal resistive networks in integrated circuits frequently use these configurations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Transformation (Bulleted Breakdown) The transformation is governed by **one single principle**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equivalence Principle:** The resistance measured between any pair of terminals (A-B, B-C, C-A) — with the third terminal kept open — must be **exactly equal** in both the Star and Delta configurations. This produces **three independent equations** (one per terminal pair), which are then solved simultaneously to express each set of resistors in terms of the other. **Why does this work?** - A resistive network is a **linear, bilateral, and passive** system. - For such systems, the **port behavior** at the external terminals completely characterizes the network's electrical nature. - If the port behavior is identical, the networks are interchangeable for any analysis purpose. ### 2.2 The Three Equations from the Equivalence Principle **Step 1 — Resistance between terminals A and B (terminal C open):** In Star: $$R_{AB}^{(Y)} = R_A + R_B$$ In Delta (C is open, so $R_{CA}$ and $R_{BC}$ are in series, and this series combo is in parallel with $R_{AB}$): $$R_{AB}^{(\Delta)} = R_{AB} \;\vert\vert\; (R_{BC} + R_{CA}) = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$$ Equating: $$R_A + R_B = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$ **Step 2 — Resistance between terminals B and C (terminal A open):** $$R_B + R_C = \frac{R_{BC}(R_{AB} + R_{CA})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$ **Step 3 — Resistance between terminals C and A (terminal B open):** $$R_C + R_A = \frac{R_{CA}(R_{AB} + R_{BC})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)}$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Master these six formulas — they cover 95% of KTU board exam questions on this topic.** | Transformation Direction | Formula | Memory Trick | |---|---|---| | **Delta → Star ($R_A$)** | $R_A = \dfrac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$ | Product of the two Delta resistors connected to terminal A, divided by the **sum of all three Delta resistors** | | **Delta → Star ($R_B$)** | $R_B = \dfrac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{BC}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$ | Product of the two Delta resistors connected to terminal B, divided by the **same sum** | | **Delta → Star ($R_C$)** | $R_C = \dfrac{R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$ | Product of the two Delta resistors connected to terminal C, divided by the **same sum** | | **Star → Delta ($R_{AB}$)** | $R_{AB} = R_A + R_B + \dfrac{R_A \cdot R_B}{R_C}$ | Sum of the two Star resistors + (their product divided by the **opposite** Star resistor) | | **Star → Delta ($R_{BC}$)** | $R_{BC} = R_B + R_C + \dfrac{R_B \cdot R_C}{R_A}$ | Sum of the two Star resistors + (their product divided by the **opposite** Star resistor) | | **Star → Delta ($R_{CA}$)** | $R_{CA} = R_C + R_A + \dfrac{R_C \cdot R_A}{R_B}$ | Sum of the two Star resistors + (their product divided by the **opposite** Star resistor) | | **Balanced Network Shortcut** | $R_{\Delta} = 3 \cdot R_Y$ and $R_Y = R_{\Delta}/3$ | Only valid when all three resistors are **equal** | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Three-phase motor windings:** Engineers convert between Y and Δ to optimize starting current and running torque. - **Wheatstone bridge balance detection:** Sensor calibration uses Y-Δ equivalence to derive null conditions. - **Passive attenuator design:** T-networks and π-networks (Star and Delta equivalents) are designed using these transformations. - **Power system analysis:** Per-unit impedance calculations on transmission lines often require Y-Δ conversion of load networks. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Delta → Star Transformation We begin from Equations 1, 2, and 3 stated in Section 2.2. Let us denote the common denominator as: $$\Sigma = R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}$$ Now, the three equations become: $$R_A + R_B = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{CA} + R_{AB} \cdot R_{BC}}{\Sigma} = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA})}{\Sigma} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ $$R_B + R_C = \frac{R_{BC}(R_{AB} + R_{CA})}{\Sigma} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ $$R_C + R_A = \frac{R_{CA}(R_{AB} + R_{BC})}{\Sigma} \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Adding all three equations:** $$2(R_A + R_B + R_C) = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA}) + R_{BC}(R_{AB} + R_{CA}) + R_{CA}(R_{AB} + R_{BC})}{\Sigma}$$ Expanding the numerator on the right: $$R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{AB}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{BC}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA} + R_{BC}R_{CA}$$ Grouping: $$2R_{AB}R_{BC} + 2R_{BC}R_{CA} + 2R_{AB}R_{CA} = 2(R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{BC}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA})$$ Therefore: $$2(R_A + R_B + R_C) = \frac{2(R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{BC}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA})}{\Sigma}$$ $$R_A + R_B + R_C = \frac{R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{BC}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(4)}$$ **Isolating $R_A$:** We compute (1) + (3) − (2): $$(R_A + R_B) + (R_C + R_A) - (R_B + R_C) = 2R_A$$ Right-hand side: $$\frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA}) + R_{CA}(R_{AB} + R_{BC}) - R_{BC}(R_{AB} + R_{CA})}{\Sigma}$$ Expanding the numerator: $$R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{AB}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA} + R_{BC}R_{CA} - R_{AB}R_{BC} - R_{BC}R_{CA}$$ Cancelling terms: $R_{AB}R_{BC}$ cancels, $R_{BC}R_{CA}$ cancels. We are left with: $$R_{AB}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA} = 2R_{AB}R_{CA}$$ Therefore: $$2R_A = \frac{2R_{AB}R_{CA}}{\Sigma}$$ $$\boxed{R_A = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}}$$ By symmetry of the terminal labels, we get: $$\boxed{R_B = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{BC}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}}$$ $$\boxed{R_C = \frac{R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}}$$ ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Star → Delta Transformation We start from Equation (4) solved earlier, plus the isolated forms for $R_A$, $R_B$, $R_C$. **Step 1 — Compute $R_A \cdot R_B$:** $$R_A \cdot R_B = \frac{R_{AB}R_{CA}}{R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{BC}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA}} \cdot \frac{R_{AB}R_{BC}}{R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{BC}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA}} \cdot \Sigma^2$$ Wait — let me re-state cleanly. Let us denote $N = R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{BC}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA}$. From Eq. (4): $R_A + R_B + R_C = N/\Sigma$ And the product: $$R_A \cdot R_B = \frac{R_{AB}^2 \cdot R_{CA} \cdot R_{BC}}{N^2/\Sigma^2} \cdot \Sigma^2 / N^2$$ A cleaner approach uses the identity: $$\frac{R_A \cdot R_B}{R_C} = \frac{R_{AB}R_{CA} \cdot R_{AB}R_{BC}}{N^2} \cdot \frac{N}{R_{BC}R_{CA}}$$ Let me re-derive more carefully. $$R_A \cdot R_B = \frac{R_{AB} R_{CA}}{\Sigma} \cdot \frac{R_{AB} R_{BC}}{\Sigma} = \frac{R_{AB}^2 \cdot R_{CA} \cdot R_{BC}}{\Sigma^2}$$ $$\frac{R_A \cdot R_B}{R_C} = \frac{R_{AB}^2 \cdot R_{CA} \cdot R_{BC}}{\Sigma^2} \cdot \frac{\Sigma}{R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}} = \frac{R_{AB}^2}{\Sigma}$$ **Step 2 — Use Eq. (1) to add the missing term:** $$R_A + R_B = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA})}{\Sigma} = \frac{R_{AB}(\Sigma - R_{AB})}{\Sigma} = R_{AB} - \frac{R_{AB}^2}{\Sigma}$$ **Step 3 — Combine:** $$R_A + R_B + \frac{R_A R_B}{R_C} = R_{AB} - \frac{R_{AB}^2}{\Sigma} + \frac{R_{AB}^2}{\Sigma} = R_{AB}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{R_{AB} = R_A + R_B + \frac{R_A R_B}{R_C}}$$ By cyclic symmetry of the terminal labels A → B → C → A: $$\boxed{R_{BC} = R_B + R_C + \frac{R_B R_C}{R_A}}$$ $$\boxed{R_{CA} = R_C + R_A + \frac{R_C R_A}{R_B}}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A delta network has $R_{AB} = 10\,\Omega$, $R_{BC} = 20\,\Omega$, $R_{CA} = 30\,\Omega$. Find the equivalent star network. **Step 1 — Compute the sum $\Sigma$:** $$\Sigma = 10 + 20 + 30 = 60\,\Omega$$ **Step 2 — Compute $R_A$ (resistor connected to terminal A, sharing Delta resistors $R_{AB}$ and $R_{CA}$):** $$R_A = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{CA}}{\Sigma} = \frac{10 \times 30}{60} = \frac{300}{60} = 5\,\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Compute $R_B$ (resistor connected to terminal B, sharing Delta resistors $R_{AB}$ and $R_{BC}$):** $$R_B = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{BC}}{\Sigma} = \frac{10 \times 20}{60} = \frac{200}{60} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33\,\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Compute $R_C$ (resistor connected to terminal C, sharing Delta resistors $R_{BC}$ and $R_{CA}$):** $$R_C = \frac{R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}}{\Sigma} = \frac{20 \times 30}{60} = \frac{600}{60} = 10\,\Omega$$ **Step 5 — Verification (Equivalence Check):** For Star: $R_A + R_B = 5 + 3.33 = 8.33\,\Omega$ For Delta (with C open): $R_{AB} \;\vert\vert\; (R_{BC} + R_{CA}) = 10 \;\vert\vert\; (20 + 30) = 10 \;\vert\vert\; 50 = \frac{10 \times 50}{60} = 8.33\,\Omega$ ✓ The transformation is verified correct. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Guide for KTU Problems | Given Network Type | Goal | Action | Formula to Use | |---|---|---|---| | Delta with **all three values given** | Find Star | Apply Delta → Star | Three product-over-sum equations | | Star with **all three values given** | Find Delta | Apply Star → Delta | Three sum-plus-product-over-opposite equations | | **Balanced** Delta (all equal) | Find Star | Use shortcut | $R_Y = R_\Delta/3$ | | **Balanced** Star (all equal) | Find Delta | Use shortcut | $R_\Delta = 3 R_Y$ | | **Bridge circuit** (Wheatstone-type) | Find equivalent resistance | Convert one Delta to Star (or vice versa) | After conversion, reduce via series-parallel | | Two values equal, one different | Find equivalent | Apply full formulas, not shortcut | Use general case formulas | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Star (Y) and Delta (Δ) Network Topologies ```mermaid graph TD subgraph DELTA_CONFIG ["DELTA CONFIGURATION (Closed Triangle)"] termA1["TERMINAL A"] termB1["TERMINAL B"] termC1["TERMINAL C"] Rab1["R_AB"]:::deltaRes Rbc1["R_BC"]:::deltaRes Rca1["R_CA"]:::deltaRes termA1 --- Rab1 Rab1 --- termB1 termB1 --- Rbc1 Rbc1 --- termC1 termC1 --- Rca1 Rca1 --- termA1 end subgraph STAR_CONFIG ["STAR CONFIGURATION (Y with Neutral Point)"] termA2["TERMINAL A"]:::term termB2["TERMINAL B"]:::term termC2["TERMINAL C"]:::term nodeN["NEUTRAL POINT N"]:::neutral Ra2["R_A"]:::starRes Rb2["R_B"]:::starRes Rc2["R_C"]:::starRes termA2 --- Ra2 Ra2 --- nodeN termB2 --- Rb2 Rb2 --- nodeN termC2 --- Rc2 Rc2 --- nodeN end subgraph EQUIV_BLOCK ["EQUIVALENCE CRITERION"] cond1["R_AB between terminals A and B in DELTA"]:::crit cond2["R_A + R_B between terminals A and B in STAR"]:::crit cond1 --- EQUAL_REL["MUST BE EQUAL"]:::equalMark EQUAL_REL --- cond2 cond3["R_BC between terminals B and C in DELTA"]:::crit cond4["R_B + R_C between terminals B and C in STAR"]:::crit cond3 --- EQUAL_REL2["MUST BE EQUAL"]:::equalMark EQUAL_REL2 --- cond4 cond5["R_CA between terminals C and A in DELTA"]:::crit cond6["R_C + R_A between terminals C and A in STAR"]:::crit cond5 --- EQUAL_REL3["MUST BE EQUAL"]:::equalMark EQUAL_REL3 --- cond6 end classDef deltaRes fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef starRes fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef term fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef neutral fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef crit fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef equalMark fill:#FF1493,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Algorithm Flowchart for Problem Solving ```mermaid graph TD startNode["START: Analyze given resistive network"]:::start identifyStep["Identify the network topology: Is it Star, Delta, or Bridge?"]:::step isBridge{"Does the network contain a bridge
(non-series-parallel) element?"}:::decision convertStep["Apply Y-Delta or Delta-Y conversion to simplify"]:::convert balancedCheck{"Are all three resistors equal
(balanced case)?"}:::decision balancedShortcut["Use balanced formulas:
R_Delta = 3 R_Star OR
R_Star = R_Delta / 3"]:::shortcut generalFormula["Apply general six formulas
(product-over-sum or sum-plus-product)"]:::general reduceStep["Reduce resulting series-parallel network"]:::reduce finalResult["Compute final equivalent resistance R_eq"]:::final endNode["END: Present answer with units Ohms"]:::end startNode --> identifyStep identifyStep --> isBridge isBridge -->|YES| convertStep isBridge -->|NO| reduceStep convertStep --> balancedCheck balancedCheck -->|YES| balancedShortcut balancedCheck -->|NO| generalFormula balancedShortcut --> reduceStep generalFormula --> reduceStep reduceStep --> finalResult finalResult --> endNode classDef start fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef step fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef convert fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef shortcut fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef general fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef reduce fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef final fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef end fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Physical Context — Application in Wheatstone Bridge ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BRIDGE ["WHEATSTONE BRIDGE WITH UNBALANCED CONDITION"] sourceV["V (Source)"]:::src nodeTop["Top Node A"]:::junction nodeBot["Bottom Node B"]:::junction nodeMid["Mid Node C (Bridge Output)"]:::mid nodeMid2["Mid Node D (Bridge Output)"]:::mid R1["R1"]:::bridgeR R2["R2"]:::bridgeR R3["R3"]:::bridgeR R4["R4"]:::bridgeR R5["R5 (Galvanometer / Detector)"]:::detector sourceV --- nodeTop sourceV --- nodeBot nodeTop --- R1 nodeTop --- R2 R1 --- nodeMid R2 --- nodeMid nodeMid --- R5 R5 --- nodeMid2 nodeMid2 --- R3 nodeMid2 --- R4 R3 --- nodeBot R4 --- nodeBot end subgraph TRANSFORM ["Y-DELTA TRANSFORMATION APPLIED HERE"] deltaBlock["Delta formed by R1, R5, R3
(or any 3-arm triangle)"]:::deltaMark starBlock["Convert to equivalent Star"]:::starMark finalEq["Then solve as simple series-parallel circuit"]:::final deltaBlock --> starBlock starBlock --> finalEq end BRIDGE -.-> TRANSFORM classDef src fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef junction fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bridgeR fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef detector fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef deltaMark fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef starMark fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef final fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF ``` ### 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Direct Comparison of Port Resistances ``` DELTA (Δ) STAR (Y) Terminal A o---[ R_AB ]---o Terminal B Terminal A o---[ R_A ]---o N \ / \ \ \ / \ \ \ / \ o Terminal B [ R_CA ] [ R_BC ] [ R_B ]---o N \ / / \ / / \/ o Terminal C [ R_C ] ``` **Reading the schematic:** The Delta has 3 resistors forming a closed triangle between A, B, C. The Star has 3 resistors meeting at a common neutral point N and connecting outward to A, B, C. For these two networks to be **electrically equivalent at the external terminals**, the formulas derived in Section 3 must hold. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the Star-Delta transformation. State the formulas to convert a Delta-connected resistive network into an equivalent Star-connected network.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Star-Delta (Y-Δ) transformation is a network reduction technique used to convert a three-terminal resistive network from Star (Y) configuration to an equivalent Delta (Δ) configuration, or vice versa, such that the equivalent resistance measured between any pair of external terminals remains the same in both configurations. **Formulas (2 marks):** For a Delta network with resistors $R_{AB}$, $R_{BC}$, $R_{CA}$ between terminals A-B, B-C, C-A respectively, the equivalent Star resistors are: $$R_A = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$$ $$R_B = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{BC}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$$ $$R_C = \frac{R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **For a balanced resistive Star network with each branch resistance of $6\,\Omega$, find the equivalent Delta resistance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Given (1 mark):** Balanced Star network with $R_A = R_B = R_C = R_Y = 6\,\Omega$. **Formula application (1 mark):** For a balanced network, the relationship between Star and Delta resistances is: $$R_\Delta = 3 \cdot R_Y$$ **Calculation (1 mark):** Substituting the value: $$R_\Delta = 3 \times 6 = 18\,\Omega$$ **Answer:** The equivalent Delta resistance is **$18\,\Omega$**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning — Read Carefully!** > - Students **commonly lose 2 marks** by mixing up which terminals the Delta resistors connect to when applying the product-over-sum formula. Always confirm: the Star resistor $R_A$ corresponds to the **product of the two Delta resistors connected TO terminal A**, divided by the **sum of all three Delta resistors**. > - Another 2-mark deduction occurs when students **forget the unit (Ω)** in the final answer. > - In numerical problems, **3 marks are reserved for the final answer and verification step** — always substitute back to check. > - **Do NOT use the balanced formula $R_\Delta = 3 R_Y$** unless the problem explicitly states all three resistors are equal. This is a common trap for 3-mark deductions. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the expressions for the equivalent Star-connected resistances $R_A$, $R_B$, $R_C$ in terms of the Delta-connected resistances $R_{AB}$, $R_{BC}$, $R_{CA}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bridge network consists of four arms: $R_{AB} = 10\,\Omega$, $R_{BC} = 20\,\Omega$, $R_{CD} = 30\,\Omega$, and $R_{DA} = 40\,\Omega$, with a cross-resistor $R_{AC} = 50\,\Omega$ connecting opposite nodes. Find the equivalent resistance between the input terminals A and C by first converting the Delta $R_{AB}, R_{BC}, R_{AC}$ to a Star. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** The equivalence condition requires that the resistance between any two terminals (with the third open) must be identical in both the Star and Delta configurations. **Resistance between A and B (C open) (1 mark):** In Star: $$R_{AB}^{(Y)} = R_A + R_B$$ In Delta, with C open, $R_{BC}$ and $R_{CA}$ are in series, and this combination is in parallel with $R_{AB}$: $$R_{AB}^{(\Delta)} = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$$ Equating: $$R_A + R_B = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Similarly for other pairs (1 mark):** $$R_B + R_C = \frac{R_{BC}(R_{AB} + R_{CA})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ $$R_C + R_A = \frac{R_{CA}(R_{AB} + R_{BC})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Let $\Sigma = R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}$. Adding (1), (2), and (3) (1 mark):** $$2(R_A + R_B + R_C) = \frac{2(R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{BC}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA})}{\Sigma}$$ **Computing (1) + (3) − (2) to isolate $R_A$ (2 marks):** $$2R_A = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA}) + R_{CA}(R_{AB} + R_{BC}) - R_{BC}(R_{AB} + R_{CA})}{\Sigma}$$ Expanding the numerator: $$R_{AB}R_{BC} + R_{AB}R_{CA} + R_{AB}R_{CA} + R_{BC}R_{CA} - R_{AB}R_{BC} - R_{BC}R_{CA} = 2R_{AB}R_{CA}$$ Therefore: $$R_A = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{CA}}{\Sigma} = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$$ **By symmetry (2 marks):** $$R_B = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{BC}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}, \quad R_C = \frac{R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}}$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** Delta with $R_{AB} = 10\,\Omega$, $R_{BC} = 20\,\Omega$, $R_{AC} = 50\,\Omega$ (note: $R_{CA} = R_{AC} = 50\,\Omega$). **Step 1 — Convert Delta to Star (3 marks):** Sum: $$\Sigma = 10 + 20 + 50 = 80\,\Omega$$ Star resistors: $$R_A = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{CA}}{\Sigma} = \frac{10 \times 50}{80} = \frac{500}{80} = 6.25\,\Omega$$ $$R_B = \frac{R_{AB} \cdot R_{BC}}{\Sigma} = \frac{10 \times 20}{80} = \frac{200}{80} = 2.5\,\Omega$$ $$R_C = \frac{R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}}{\Sigma} = \frac{20 \times 50}{80} = \frac{1000}{80} = 12.5\,\Omega$$ **Step 2 — Identify the new network topology (1 mark):** After conversion, we have: - A series path from A: $R_A$ to neutral N - A series path from C: $R_C$ to neutral N - The branch $R_B$ from N to B, and the branch from B continues through $R_{CD} = 30\,\Omega$ to D, then $R_{DA} = 40\,\Omega$ to A - So the path from N to A through B-D is: $R_B + R_{CD} + R_{DA} = 2.5 + 30 + 40 = 72.5\,\Omega$ **Step 3 — Compute parallel combination between A and C (2 marks):** Two parallel paths from A to C: - Direct path: $R_A + R_C = 6.25 + 12.5 = 18.75\,\Omega$ - Long path via B and D: $R_A + (R_B + R_{CD} + R_{DA}) + R_C = 6.25 + 72.5 + 12.5 = 91.25\,\Omega$ **Equivalent resistance (1 mark):** $$R_{AC} = \frac{18.75 \times 91.25}{18.75 + 91.25} = \frac{1710.9375}{110} \approx 15.55\,\Omega$$ **Answer:** $R_{AC} \approx 15.55\,\Omega$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative)]** **(a)** Derive the formulas to convert a Star-connected network with resistances $R_A$, $R_B$, $R_C$ to an equivalent Delta-connected network. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A resistive network has a Star section with $R_A = 4\,\Omega$, $R_B = 6\,\Omega$, $R_C = 12\,\Omega$ connected to a load resistor of $R_L = 10\,\Omega$ between terminals B and C. Calculate the equivalent resistance between terminals A and C, and find the current drawn from a $24\,V$ DC source connected between A and C. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Starting from the three equivalence equations: $$R_A + R_B = \frac{R_{AB}(R_{BC} + R_{CA})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ $$R_B + R_C = \frac{R_{BC}(R_{AB} + R_{CA})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ $$R_C + R_A = \frac{R_{CA}(R_{AB} + R_{BC})}{R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}} \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Multiplying Eq.
