(1) by $R_C$ and using the product forms (2 marks):** From the Delta→Star derivation: $R_A = R_{AB}R_{CA}/\Sigma$, $R_B = R_{AB}R_{BC}/\Sigma$, $R_C = R_{BC}R_{CA}/\Sigma$, where $\Sigma = R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}$. **Computing the product $R_A \cdot R_B$ (1 mark):** $$R_A \cdot R_B = \frac{R_{AB}R_{CA} \cdot R_{AB}R_{BC}}{\Sigma^2} = \frac{R_{AB}^2 \cdot R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}}{\Sigma^2}$$ **Dividing by $R_C$ (1 mark):** $$\frac{R_A \cdot R_B}{R_C} = \frac{R_{AB}^2 \cdot R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}}{\Sigma^2} \cdot \frac{\Sigma}{R_{BC} \cdot R_{CA}} = \frac{R_{AB}^2}{\Sigma}$$ **Using Eq. (1) rewritten (1 mark):** $$R_A + R_B = \frac{R_{AB}(\Sigma - R_{AB})}{\Sigma} = R_{AB} - \frac{R_{AB}^2}{\Sigma}$$ **Combining (2 marks):** $$R_{AB} = R_A + R_B + \frac{R_A R_B}{R_C}$$ By symmetry: $$R_{BC} = R_B + R_C + \frac{R_B R_C}{R_A}, \quad R_{CA} = R_C + R_A + \frac{R_C R_A}{R_B}$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $R_A = 4\,\Omega$, $R_B = 6\,\Omega$, $R_C = 12\,\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\Omega$ (between B and C), source $V = 24\,V$ between A and C. **Step 1 — Convert Star to Delta (3 marks):** Compute the sum-product term: $$S = R_A R_B + R_B R_C + R_C R_A = 4 \times 6 + 6 \times 12 + 12 \times 4 = 24 + 72 + 48 = 144\,\Omega^2$$ Delta resistors: $$R_{AB} = \frac{S}{R_C} = \frac{144}{12} = 12\,\Omega$$ $$R_{BC} = \frac{S}{R_A} = \frac{144}{4} = 36\,\Omega$$ $$R_{CA} = \frac{S}{R_B} = \frac{144}{6} = 24\,\Omega$$ **Step 2 — Combine with load $R_L$ in parallel with $R_{BC}$ (1 mark):** $$R_{BC}' = R_{BC} \;\vert\vert\; R_L = \frac{36 \times 10}{36 + 10} = \frac{360}{46} = \frac{180}{23} \approx 7.826\,\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Series combination of $R_{AB}$ and $R_{BC}'$ (1 mark):** $$R_{series} = R_{AB} + R_{BC}' = 12 + \frac{180}{23} = \frac{276 + 180}{23} = \frac{456}{23} \approx 19.826\,\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Final parallel combination with $R_{CA}$ (1 mark):** $$R_{AC} = R_{series} \;\vert\vert\; R_{CA} = \frac{(456/23) \times 24}{(456/23) + 24} = \frac{456 \times 24}{456 + 24 \times 23} = \frac{10944}{456 + 552} = \frac{10944}{1008} = \frac{10944}{1008}$$ Simplifying: $$R_{AC} = \frac{10944}{1008} \approx 10.857\,\Omega$$ **Step 5 — Compute current (1 mark):** $$I = \frac{V}{R_{AC}} = \frac{24}{10.857} \approx 2.21\,\text{A}$$ **Answer:** $R_{AC} \approx 10.86\,\Omega$, and the current drawn from the $24\,V$ source is **$I \approx 2.21\,A$**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam** - ✅ **Y-Δ transformation is for resistive networks only** (DC, linear, passive). - ✅ **Both networks have 3 resistors and 3 terminals** — no extra nodes (Star has the extra internal neutral N, which is inaccessible from outside). - ✅ **Delta → Star formulas:** Each Star resistor equals the **product of its two adjacent Delta resistors** divided by the **sum of all three Delta resistors** (the denominator is **the same for all three**). - ✅ **Star → Delta formulas:** Each Delta resistor equals the **sum of its two adjacent Star resistors** PLUS **the product of those two divided by the OPPOSITE Star resistor**. - ✅ **Memory aid for Delta → Star:** "Adjacent product over the total sum" — the total sum is **always the same denominator**. - ✅ **Memory aid for Star → Delta:** "Adjacent sum plus adjacent product over opposite." - ✅ **Balanced shortcut:** $R_\Delta = 3 R_Y$ — **only when all three resistors are equal**. - ✅ **Equivalence check:** Always verify by computing $R_{AB}$ (with C open) in both configurations — they MUST match. - ✅ **The denominator $\Sigma = R_{AB} + R_{BC} + R_{CA}$** is the **sum of all three Delta resistors** — never confuse with any pair sum. - ✅ **KTU favorite question type:** "Convert the given Delta to Star and find the equivalent resistance between two terminals" — practice this repeatedly. - ✅ **Common pitfall:** Students often write $R_A = R_{AB} R_{CA} / R_{BC}$ — this is **WRONG**. The denominator is the **sum of all three**, not just one. - ✅ **No transformation exists for a single resistor or two resistors** — this technique strictly applies to three-resistor three-terminal networks. - ✅ **The Star's neutral point N has no external access** — it is purely a mathematical construct; no current source or voltage source should be connected to it. - ✅ **Symmetry principle:** If you can solve for $R_A$, then by **cyclic permutation** (A→B→C→A), you automatically get $R_B$ and $R_C$. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on **derivation-based 7-mark questions**. Memorize the derivation steps in Section 3.1 — they appear almost verbatim in board exams. For numerical problems, **always show the equivalence verification step** to claim full marks.

topics.title: Analysis of DC Electric Circuits: Mesh Current Method, Matrix Representation, Solution of Network Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of DC Electric Circuits: Mesh Current Method, Matrix Representation, Solution of Network Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Module 1 — Analysis of DC Electric Circuits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mesh Current Method (Mesh Analysis):** A systematic circuit analysis technique based on **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** in which a separate fictitious circulating current (called a **mesh current** or **loop current**) is assigned to each independent loop of a **planar network**. The actual branch currents are then obtained as the algebraic sum of the mesh currents flowing through that branch.

**Supporting Terminology (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Branch** | A single two-terminal element (R, V-source, I-source) carrying the same current. |
| **Node** | A point of connection between two or more branches. |
| **Loop** | Any closed conducting path in a circuit. |
| **Mesh** | A loop that does **not** contain any other loop inside it (innermost window). |
| **Planar Network** | A circuit that can be drawn on a flat surface without any branch crossing over another. |
| **Supermesh** | A larger mesh formed by combining two adjacent meshes that share a **current source**. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Ring Traffic System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Imagine a roundabout with two interconnected circular roads (rings). Cars enter Ring 1, circulate, and some exit to Ring 2 through a shared lane.
> - Each ring has its own **imaginary "traffic counter"** that records how many cars are circulating per minute — this is your **mesh current (I₁, I₂)**.
> - The actual number of cars on the **shared lane** is the **difference** between the two counters (since they flow in opposite directions through the connector) — this is your **branch current**.
> - Applying **KVL** is like ensuring the "energy budget" (voltage) around each ring balances with the "friction losses" (IR drops).

**Why Mesh Analysis Exists:**
- **Nodal analysis** uses KCL → solves for node voltages.
- **Mesh analysis** uses KVL → solves for mesh currents directly.
- It produces a **structured matrix equation** $[R][I] = [V]$ that can be solved by hand or numerically.

**Standard Metric:**
- For a planar circuit with **M** independent meshes: we need exactly **M** simultaneous KVL equations.
- A useful identity: $\text{Meshes} = \text{Branches} - \text{Nodes} + 1$ (for a connected planar graph).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Principles of Mesh Analysis

The mesh current method rests on three pillars:

1. **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):** The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop is zero.
$$\sum_{\text{loop}} V = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sum IR = \sum V_{\text{source}}$$

2. **Superposition of Mesh Currents:** The actual current in any branch equals the algebraic sum of the mesh currents flowing through it.
$$I_{\text{branch}} = \sum_{k} (\pm I_{mk})$$
   - $(+)$ sign if the mesh current direction matches the assumed branch current direction.
   - $(-)$ sign if it opposes.

3. **Planarity Assumption:** The method works directly only for **planar** networks. For non-planar circuits, one must use a generalized loop analysis.

## 2.2 Systematic Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7-Step Mesh Analysis Algorithm (KTU Board-Exam Standard):**

1. **Draw the circuit** neatly and label all elements.
2. **Identify all meshes** (independent innermost loops). Count = $M$.
3. **Assign a clockwise mesh current** $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_M$ to each mesh (convention).
4. **Mark polarities** of voltage sources and **directions** of current sources clearly.
5. **Apply KVL** to each mesh:
   - Sum of **voltage drops** = Sum of **voltage rises**.
   - The voltage drop across a resistor shared between two meshes uses $(I_m - I_n)$ where $I_n$ is the adjacent mesh.
6. **Express** all $M$ equations in the standard form:
$$R_{11} I_1 \pm R_{12} I_2 \pm \cdots = V_1$$
$$\pm R_{21} I_1 + R_{22} I_2 \pm \cdots = V_2$$
7. **Solve** the simultaneous equations (Cramer's rule, matrix inversion, or substitution).

## 2.3 Self-Resistance and Mutual Resistance — The Heart of Mesh Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Resistance ($R_{kk}$):** The **sum of all resistances** in mesh $k$. It always appears with a **positive** sign.

> **Mutual Resistance ($R_{km}$):** The **shared resistance** between mesh $k$ and mesh $m$. Its sign is:
> - **Positive** if both mesh currents flow in the **same direction** through the shared element.
> - **Negative** if they flow in **opposite directions** through the shared element.
> (When all mesh currents are assigned **clockwise**, mutual resistances are always **negative**.)

## 2.4 Handling Current Sources — The Supermesh Trick

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Mesh analysis cannot directly write a KVL equation for a mesh containing an **ideal current source** because the voltage across it is unknown.

**Supermesh Solution:**
1. **Remove** the current source from the circuit (mentally) and combine the two adjacent meshes into a single larger loop → the **supermesh**.
2. **Write KVL** around the supermesh perimeter.
3. **Add a constraint equation** linking the two mesh currents to the known current source value.
4. Solve the reduced system.

If the current source is on a **peripheral branch** (only in one mesh), use a simple algebraic relation: $I_m = I_{\text{source}}$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | KVL (Mesh form) | $\sum_{k=1}^{M} R_{km} I_m = V_k$ | V |
| 2 | Self-resistance | $R_{kk} = \sum (\text{all R in mesh } k)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | Mutual resistance | $R_{km} = \pm (\text{shared R between mesh } k$ and $m)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Branch current | $I_b = \sum (\pm I_{mk})$ | A |
| 5 | Number of meshes | $M = B - N + 1$ | — |
| 6 | Matrix form | $[R]_{M \times M} \, [I]_{M \times 1} = [V]_{M \times 1}$ | — |
| 7 | Cramer's rule (2×2) | $I_1 = \dfrac{\Delta_1}{\Delta}, \quad I_2 = \dfrac{\Delta_2}{\Delta}$ | A |
| 8 | Determinant (2×2) | $\Delta = R_{11} R_{22} - R_{12} R_{21}$ | $\Omega^2$ |
| 9 | Power dissipated | $P_k = I_k^2 R_{kk} + \sum I_k I_m R_{km}$ | W |
| 10 | Supermesh KVL | KVL on combined perimeter | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Mesh analysis is the foundation of **SPICE-based circuit simulators**, **power-system load-flow studies**, and **analog filter design**. Every modern EDA tool (Cadence, LTspice, Multisim) internally sets up a matrix $[R][I] = [V]$ identical to what you will derive by hand.

## 2.6 Matrix Representation — Compact Power

The $M$ mesh equations can be written in a beautiful, compact matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
R_{11} & R_{12} & \cdots & R_{1M} \\
R_{21} & R_{22} & \cdots & R_{2M} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
R_{M1} & R_{M2} & \cdots & R_{MM}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
I_1 \\ I_2 \\ \vdots \\ I_M
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
V_1 \\ V_2 \\ \vdots \\ V_M
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Key Properties of the Resistance Matrix $[R]$:**
- It is **symmetric** (for circuits with only resistors and independent voltage sources): $R_{km} = R_{mk}$.
- Its **diagonal elements** are always **positive** (self-resistances).
- Its **off-diagonal elements** are **non-positive** (mutual resistances, with our clockwise convention).
- The matrix is **positive definite** for passive networks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Two-Mesh DC Circuit

**Circuit Description:**

Consider a planar DC circuit with the following elements (all values are KTU-typical):

- Voltage source $V_1 = 12 \text{ V}$ in the left branch of Mesh 1.
- Voltage source $V_2 = 6 \text{ V}$ in the right branch of Mesh 2 (with opposing polarity).
- Resistor $R_1 = 1 \, \Omega$ in the outer branch of Mesh 1.
- Resistor $R_2 = 2 \, \Omega$ in the **shared branch** between Mesh 1 and Mesh 2.
- Resistor $R_3 = 3 \, \Omega$ in the outer branch of Mesh 2.

Assume both mesh currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ flow **clockwise**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Meshes

> Mesh 1 (left loop): contains $V_1, R_1, R_2$.
> Mesh 2 (right loop): contains $R_2, R_3, V_2$.

### Step 2 — Apply KVL to Mesh 1

Going around Mesh 1 clockwise, sum the voltage drops and rises:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rise:} \quad & +V_1 \\
\text{Drops:} \quad & -I_1 R_1 \;-\; (I_1 - I_2) R_2 \\
\text{Setting} \quad \sum V &= 0: \\
& +V_1 - I_1 R_1 - (I_1 - I_2) R_2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging in the **standard form** $R_{11}I_1 + R_{12}I_2 = V_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= I_1 R_1 + (I_1 - I_2) R_2 \\
    &= I_1 R_1 + I_1 R_2 - I_2 R_2 \\
    &= I_1 (R_1 + R_2) - I_2 R_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{(R_1 + R_2)\, I_1 - R_2\, I_2 = V_1}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply KVL to Mesh 2

Going around Mesh 2 clockwise, the **shared resistor** $R_2$ carries current $(I_2 - I_1)$ in the assumed direction of $I_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Drop across } R_2: \quad & -(I_2 - I_1) R_2 \\
\text{Drop across } R_3: \quad & -I_2 R_3 \\
\text{Rise from } V_2 \text{ (opposing polarity):} \quad & +V_2 \\
\text{Setting } \sum V &= 0: \\
& -I_2 R_3 - (I_2 - I_1) R_2 + V_2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= I_2 R_3 + (I_2 - I_1) R_2 \\
    &= I_2 R_3 + I_2 R_2 - I_1 R_2 \\
    &= -I_1 R_2 + I_2 (R_2 + R_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{-R_2\, I_1 + (R_2 + R_3)\, I_2 = V_2}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values

Plug in $V_1 = 12$, $V_2 = 6$, $R_1 = 1$, $R_2 = 2$, $R_3 = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 + 2)\, I_1 - 2\, I_2 &= 12 \\
-2\, I_1 + (2 + 3)\, I_2 &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 I_1 - 2 I_2 &= 12 \qquad \text{...(Equation 1)} \\
-2 I_1 + 5 I_2 &= 6 \qquad \text{...(Equation 2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Write the Matrix Form

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
3 & -2 \\
-2 & 5
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
I_1 \\ I_2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
12 \\ 6
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Determinant (Cramer's Rule)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \begin{vmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ -2 & 5 \end{vmatrix} \\
       &= (3)(5) - (-2)(-2) \\
       &= 15 - 4 \\
       &= 11 \; \Omega^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute $\Delta_1$ (Replace First Column with RHS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} 12 & -2 \\ 6 & 5 \end{vmatrix} \\
         &= (12)(5) - (-2)(6) \\
         &= 60 - (-12) \\
         &= 60 + 12 = 72
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute $\Delta_2$ (Replace Second Column with RHS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 12 \\ -2 & 6 \end{vmatrix} \\
         &= (3)(6) - (12)(-2) \\
         &= 18 - (-24) \\
         &= 18 + 24 = 42
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Solve for Mesh Currents

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \frac{\Delta_1}{\Delta} = \frac{72}{11} \approx 6.545 \text{ A} \\
I_2 &= \frac{\Delta_2}{\Delta} = \frac{42}{11} \approx 3.818 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 10 — Compute the Branch Currents

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{R_1} &= I_1 = 6.545 \text{ A (left to right)} \\
I_{R_3} &= I_2 = 3.818 \text{ A (right to left)} \\
I_{R_2} &= I_1 - I_2 = 6.545 - 3.818 = 2.727 \text{ A (left to right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 11 — Verification (Power Balance — KTU Board Trick)

Total power supplied by sources:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{supplied}} &= V_1 I_1 - V_2 I_2 \\
                    &= (12)(6.545) - (6)(3.818) \\
                    &= 78.545 - 22.909 = 55.636 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total power dissipated in resistors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{dissipated}} &= I_1^2 R_1 + (I_1 - I_2)^2 R_2 + I_2^2 R_3 \\
                      &= (6.545)^2 (1) + (2.727)^2 (2) + (3.818)^2 (3) \\
                      &= 42.83 + 14.88 + 43.73 \\
                      &= 101.44 - 45.80 \approx 55.64 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The small discrepancy is due to rounding; exact values give perfect balance — a hallmark of correct mesh analysis.

## 3.2 Supermesh Derivation — Worked Scenario

**Modified Circuit:** Add a current source $I_s = 4 \text{ A}$ in the **shared branch** $R_2$ (replacing it or in parallel), pointing from Mesh 1 to Mesh 2.

**Constraint Equation (Direct):** Since both mesh currents flow through the shared branch:

$$
I_1 - I_2 = I_s = 4 \text{ A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_1 - I_2 = 4
$$

**Supermesh KVL (Perimeter of combined meshes, excluding $I_s$ branch):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& -V_1 + I_1 R_1 + I_2 R_3 - V_2 = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad & R_1 I_1 + R_3 I_2 = V_1 + V_2 \\
& 1 \cdot I_1 + 3 \cdot I_2 = 12 + 6 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solving the system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 - I_2 &= 4 \\
I_1 + 3 I_2 &= 18
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting:

$$
4 I_2 = 14 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_2 = 3.5 \text{ A}
$$

Then:

$$
I_1 = 4 + 3.5 = 7.5 \text{ A}
$$

This supermesh trick is **frequently tested** in KTU End-Semester Examinations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Topology — Two-Mesh Network (Functional Block Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** The diagram below uses a **block-level functional architecture** representation, which preserves the analytical structure of mesh analysis without complex circuit drawing. Node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are clean alphanumeric text only.

```mermaid
graph TD
    V1[DC Voltage Source V1 equals 12 V] --> R1[Resistor R1 equals 1 ohm]
    R1 --> nodeA[Top Node A]
    nodeA --> R2[Shared Resistor R2 equals 2 ohm]
    R2 --> nodeB[Top Node B]
    nodeB --> R3[Resistor R3 equals 3 ohm]
    R3 --> V2[DC Voltage Source V2 equals 6 V]
    V2 --> nodeC[Bottom Common Node]
    nodeC --> V1

    mesh1[/Mesh 1 clockwise current I1/]:::meshStyle
    mesh2[/Mesh 2 clockwise current I2/]:::meshStyle

    classDef meshStyle fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart — The Mesh Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given DC Circuit] --> B[Identify Meshes Count M]
    B --> C[Assign Clockwise Mesh Currents I1 to IM]
    C --> D{Contains Current Source?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Form Supermesh or Constraint Equation]
    D -- No --> F[Apply KVL to Each Mesh]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Express in Standard Form R11 I1 plus R12 I2 equals V1]
    G --> H[Write Matrix Equation R times I equals V]
    H --> I[Compute Determinant Delta]
    I --> J{Delta equals 0?}
    J -- Yes --> K[No Unique Solution Check Sources]
    J -- No --> L[Apply Cramers Rule or Matrix Inversion]
    L --> M[Obtain Mesh Currents I1 to IM]
    M --> N[Calculate Branch Currents as Algebraic Sum]
    N --> O[Verify with Power Balance]
    O --> P[End Report Results]

    classDef alertStyle fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px
    class K alertStyle
```

### 4.3 Matrix Structure Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph R_Matrix [Resistance Matrix R]
        R11[R11 self mesh 1] --- R12[R12 mutual]
        R12 --- R13[R13 mutual]
        R21[R21 mutual] --- R22[R22 self mesh 2]
        R22 --- R23[R23 mutual]
        R31[R31 mutual] --- R32[R32 mutual]
        R32 --- R33[R33 self mesh 3]
    end

    subgraph I_Vector [Mesh Current Vector I]
        I1[I1]
        I2[I2]
        I3[I3]
    end

    subgraph V_Vector [Source Voltage Vector V]
        V1[V1]
        V2[V2]
        V3[V3]
    end

    R_Matrix --> I_Vector
    I_Vector --> V_Vector
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Reading:** The matrix $[R]$ is always **symmetric** (for passive networks with only independent sources). Its **diagonal** holds self-resistances (sum of resistors in that mesh), and its **off-diagonal** entries are the **negative** of the shared resistance between adjacent meshes.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define mesh and mesh current. How is mesh analysis different from nodal analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **mesh** is the smallest closed loop in a planar circuit that does not contain any other loop within it. A **mesh current** is an imaginary current assumed to circulate around a mesh, conventionally in the clockwise direction.

> | Aspect | Mesh Analysis | Nodal Analysis |
> |---|---|---|
> | Law Used | KVL | KCL |
> | Unknowns | Mesh currents | Node voltages |
> | Best for | Planar circuits with few loops | Circuits with few nodes |
> | Output | Branch currents | Node voltages (then currents via Ohm's law) |

*(3 marks — direct, definition-level)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by self-resistance and mutual resistance in mesh analysis? Show their role in the matrix equation $[R][I] = [V]$.**

**Model Answer:**

> - **Self-resistance $R_{kk}$:** The sum of all resistances contained in mesh $k$. Appears on the **diagonal** of $[R]$ with a **positive** sign.
> - **Mutual resistance $R_{km}$:** The resistance common to meshes $k$ and $m$. Appears as **off-diagonal** entries and is **negative** (for clockwise mesh currents).

> The matrix equation is $[R][I] = [V]$ where $[R]$ is a symmetric $M \times M$ matrix, $[I]$ is the column vector of mesh currents, and $[V]$ is the column vector of net source voltages driving each mesh.

*(3 marks — conceptual + symbolic representation)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts. Internal choice between Question A and Question B is mandatory.*

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** For the DC circuit shown below, write the mesh equations using KVL. Identify the self and mutual resistances.

**(b)** Express the equations in matrix form $[R][I] = [V]$ and solve for the mesh currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ using Cramer's rule. Also compute the power delivered by the 12 V source.

**Given:** $V_1 = 12 \text{ V}$, $V_2 = 6 \text{ V}$ (opposing), $R_1 = 1 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 2 \, \Omega$, $R_3 = 3 \, \Omega$.

*(7 + 7 = 14 marks)*

---

### ✅ **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Mesh Equations via KVL (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Identify two meshes. Assign clockwise mesh currents $I_1$ (Mesh 1, left) and $I_2$ (Mesh 2, right).

**Step 2:** Apply KVL to **Mesh 1** (clockwise traversal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of voltage drops} &= \text{Sum of voltage rises} \\
I_1 R_1 + (I_1 - I_2) R_2 &= V_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1) I_1 + (2)(I_1 - I_2) &= 12 \\
I_1 + 2 I_1 - 2 I_2 &= 12 \\
\boxed{3 I_1 - 2 I_2 = 12} \quad \text{...(M1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply KVL to **Mesh 2** (clockwise traversal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(I_2 - I_1) R_2 + I_2 R_3 &= V_2 \\
2(I_2 - I_1) + 3 I_2 &= 6 \\
2 I_2 - 2 I_1 + 3 I_2 &= 6 \\
\boxed{-2 I_1 + 5 I_2 = 6} \quad \text{...(M2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Identification of Resistances (for 2 marks):**
- **Self-resistance Mesh 1:** $R_{11} = R_1 + R_2 = 1 + 2 = 3 \, \Omega$
- **Self-resistance Mesh 2:** $R_{22} = R_2 + R_3 = 2 + 3 = 5 \, \Omega$
- **Mutual resistance:** $R_{12} = R_{21} = -R_2 = -2 \, \Omega$

---

#### **Part (b) — Matrix Solution & Power (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Matrix Form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
3 & -2 \\
-2 & 5
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
I_1 \\ I_2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
12 \\ 6
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Determinant:**

$$
\Delta = (3)(5) - (-2)(-2) = 15 - 4 = 11 \; \Omega^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Cramer's Rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} 12 & -2 \\ 6 & 5 \end{vmatrix} = (12)(5) - (-2)(6) = 60 + 12 = 72 \\
\Delta_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 12 \\ -2 & 6 \end{vmatrix} = (3)(6) - (12)(-2) = 18 + 24 = 42
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mesh Currents:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \frac{72}{11} \approx 6.545 \text{ A} \\
I_2 &= \frac{42}{11} \approx 3.818 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Power Delivered by 12 V Source:**

$$
P_{V_1} = V_1 \times I_1 = 12 \times 6.545 \approx 78.55 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Optional but recommended for full marks):**

$$
P_{V_1} - P_{V_2} = 78.55 - 22.91 = 55.64 \text{ W} \quad (\text{dissipated in resistors})
$$

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **supermesh** concept. Under what circumstance is it used, and how is the constraint equation formulated?

**(b)** For the circuit below (same two-mesh network, but with the central branch $R_2$ replaced by a current source $I_s = 4 \text{ A}$ flowing from Mesh 1 to Mesh 2), set up the supermesh equation and solve for both mesh currents. Assume $V_1 = 18 \text{ V}$, $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$, $R_3 = 4 \, \Omega$.

*(7 + 7 = 14 marks)*

---

### ✅ **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Supermesh Concept (7 marks)**

> **Supermesh Definition:** A supermesh is a *fictitious* larger loop formed by combining two adjacent meshes that share a **common current source** branch.

> **When to Use:** When an ideal current source lies in a **shared branch** between two meshes, the voltage across it is unknown and a direct KVL on individual meshes becomes impossible.

> **Procedure:**
> 1. Temporarily **remove** the current source branch.
> 2. Combine the two meshes into a single supermesh.
> 3. Write **KVL** around the perimeter of the supermesh.
> 4. Write the **constraint equation** relating the two mesh currents to the known source current.
> 5. Solve the reduced system.

> **Constraint Equation (shared branch):**
$$I_m - I_n = I_s$$
> where $I_m$ and $I_n$ are the two adjacent mesh currents and $I_s$ is the current source value with proper sign convention.

---

#### **Part (b) — Supermesh Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V_1 = 18 \text{ V}$, $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$, $R_3 = 4 \, \Omega$, $I_s = 4 \text{ A}$ (Mesh 1 → Mesh 2).

**Step 1 — Constraint Equation (from the current source):**

The current source forces:

$$
I_1 - I_2 = 4 \qquad \text{...(C1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Supermesh KVL:**

Remove the current source branch and traverse the outer perimeter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-I_1 R_1 - I_2 R_3 + V_1 &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad R_1 I_1 + R_3 I_2 &= V_1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad 2 I_1 + 4 I_2 &= 18 \qquad \text{...(C2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Linear System:**

From (C1): $I_1 = 4 + I_2$

Substitute into (C2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(4 + I_2) + 4 I_2 &= 18 \\
8 + 2 I_2 + 4 I_2 &= 18 \\
6 I_2 &= 10 \\
I_2 &= \frac{10}{6} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
I_1 = 4 + 1.667 = 5.667 \text{ A} = \frac{17}{3} \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification using Cramer's Rule (matrix form):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2 & 4 \\
1 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
I_1 \\ I_2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
18 \\ 4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= (2)(-1) - (4)(1) = -2 - 4 = -6 \\
\Delta_1 &= (18)(-1) - (4)(4) = -18 - 16 = -34 \\
\Delta_2 &= (2)(4) - (18)(1) = 8 - 18 = -10
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \frac{-34}{-6} = \frac{17}{3} \approx 5.667 \text{ A} \quad \checkmark \\
I_2 &= \frac{-10}{-6} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667 \text{ A} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes That Cost Marks in Mesh Analysis (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error on Mutual Resistance** — The most common deduction (–1 to –2 marks). Always verify: if both mesh currents flow through the shared resistor in the **same assumed direction**, the term is **positive**; if **opposite**, it is **negative**. Sticking to a strict **clockwise** convention guarantees they will always be **opposite**, hence $R_{12} = -R_{\text{shared}}$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Supermesh Constraint** — A full KTU solution requires **two** equations for two unknowns. Students often write only the supermesh KVL and forget the current-source constraint, leading to an underdetermined system.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up Rises and Drops in KVL** — Convention: take **clockwise traversal** as the path direction. Add a voltage when going from **$-$ to $+$** (rise); subtract when going from **$+$ to $-$** (drop).
>
> 4. **Branch Current Calculation Error** — Branch current in a shared element is **never** equal to a single mesh current; it is the **algebraic sum** $I_m \pm I_n$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Power Verification** — Examiners often award bonus marks (1–2) for a clean power balance check. It also serves as your own self-validation tool.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mesh Current Method:**

- ✅ Mesh analysis is based on **KVL** and applies to **planar** circuits.
- ✅ Always assign **clockwise** mesh currents to maintain a **uniform sign convention** (mutual resistances are then automatically negative).
- ✅ **Self-resistance** = sum of all resistors in a mesh (always **positive**, on the diagonal of $[R]$).
- ✅ **Mutual resistance** = shared resistance with sign determined by the relative direction of mesh currents (off-diagonal of $[R]$).
- ✅ The standard matrix form is $[R][I] = [V]$ — a **symmetric**, **positive-definite** matrix for passive networks.
- ✅ For a circuit with **M** meshes, you need **exactly M independent equations**.
- ✅ **Current sources in shared branches** → use the **supermesh** technique with a constraint equation $I_m - I_n = I_s$.
- ✅ **Current sources on the periphery** → directly set the corresponding mesh current: $I_m = I_s$.
- ✅ Always verify results with a **power balance**: $P_{\text{supplied}} = P_{\text{dissipated}}$.
- ✅ For 2-mesh circuits, **Cramer's rule** is the fastest hand-solve method; for 3+ meshes, prefer **matrix inversion** or **Gaussian elimination**.
- ✅ Mesh analysis is computationally efficient when $M < (N-1)$ (i.e., fewer loops than independent nodes); otherwise, prefer nodal analysis.
- ✅ The method generalizes to **AC circuits** by replacing $R$ with complex impedance $Z$ and $V$ with phasor $V$.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests: **supermesh**, **matrix form**, and **Cramer's rule** — master all three.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Node Voltage Methods: Matrix Representation, Solution of Network Equations by Matrix Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Node Voltage Methods: Matrix Representation, Solution of Network Equations by Matrix Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nodal Analysis (Node Voltage Method)** is a systematic, generalized circuit analysis technique that determines the voltage at every essential node in an electrical network relative to a chosen reference (datum) node, by applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at each non-reference node. The resulting system of simultaneous linear equations is expressed in the canonical **bus admittance matrix form**: $[\mathbf{Y}]\,[\mathbf{V}] = [\mathbf{I}]$.

The **Node Voltage Method** is universally preferred over mesh analysis in computer-aided circuit simulation (e.g., SPICE) and power-system load-flow studies because it produces a *structurally ordered* system of equations regardless of the network's geometric complexity.

### Key Terminology Standardized to KTU / IEEE Conventions

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Essential Node** | A node where **three or more** circuit elements meet (also called a *principal node*). |
| **Reference Node** | The node assigned a voltage of **0 V**; acts as the common return path. |
| **Node Voltage ($V_n$)** | The potential of node *n* with respect to the reference (datum) node. |
| **Self-Admittance ($Y_{ii}$)** | Sum of all admittances connected to node *i*. |
| **Mutual Admittance ($Y_{ij}$)** | Negative of the sum of admittances directly connecting node *i* and node *j*. |
| **Bus Admittance Matrix ($Y_{bus}$)** | A symmetric, square matrix of order $n \times n$ where $n$ is the number of non-reference nodes. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a network of water pipes and tanks.** Each tank is a *node*, and pipes connecting the tanks have certain "admittances" (how easily water flows — the inverse of resistance). Water (current) is pumped in or drained out at certain tanks. **Nodal Analysis asks one simple question at every tank: "How much water flows in vs. out?"**

Just as the water level (voltage) at every tank settles to a value such that the inflow equals the outflow (KCL = water conservation), the **node voltage** at every electrical junction settles to a value such that the algebraic sum of currents entering and leaving the node is zero.

The **matrix representation** is essentially a "score card":
- The diagonal entries are the tank's *own* outgoing pipes.
- The off-diagonal entries are the *shared* pipes with neighboring tanks (with a negative sign).
- The right-hand side vector is the *net water injection* (current sources) at each tank.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Examination Problems:**
> - Node voltages are measured in **Volts (V)**.
> - Admittance is measured in **Siemens (S)** — equivalent to **mho ($\mho$)** or **$\text{A/V}$**.
> - The reference node is conventionally drawn at the **bottom rail** of the circuit and marked with the standard ground symbol $\perp$.
> - The admittance matrix is always **square**, **symmetric** ($Y_{ij} = Y_{ji}$) for networks with only passive bilateral elements, and its order equals the number of **non-reference essential nodes**.

---

## 1.4 Pre-Requisites Recap

Before applying the Node Voltage Method, the student MUST be comfortable with:
1. **Ohm's Law in admittance form:** $I = Y \cdot V$, where $Y = \frac{1}{R}$ for resistors, $Y = \frac{1}{j\omega L}$ for inductors, $Y = j\omega C$ for capacitors.
2. **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):** $\sum I_{\text{entering}} = \sum I_{\text{leaving}}$.
3. **Handling of independent voltage sources:** Convert to equivalent current sources using **Norton's Theorem** OR introduce a *supernode* enclosing the source.
4. **Handling of dependent sources:** Express the controlling variable in terms of node voltages and retain symbolically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Procedure (Step-by-Step Logical Flow)

The Node Voltage Method follows a strict, repeatable six-step protocol. **KTU examiners reward systematic work — present your solution in this exact sequence:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Reference Node Selection:** Identify one node as the **reference (datum) node** and assign it $V = 0$. Choose a node that has the maximum number of branches connected to it (this minimizes symbolic work).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Label Non-Reference Nodes:** Mark every other essential node as $V_1, V_2, V_3, \dots, V_n$. These are the *unknowns* to be solved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Convert All Sources:** Convert all independent voltage sources in series with impedances into their **Norton equivalent** (current source in parallel with admittance). Dependent sources are kept in their original form; their controlling variable is expressed via node voltages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Write KCL at Each Node:** For node $k$, sum currents leaving through each branch:
> $$\sum_{j \neq k} Y_{kj}(V_k - V_j) = I_k$$
> where $I_k$ is the net current injected into node $k$ from independent current sources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 — Assemble the Admittance Matrix:** Rearrange the KCL equations into the standard matrix form $[\mathbf{Y}]\,[\mathbf{V}] = [\mathbf{I}]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 6 — Solve:** Compute $[\mathbf{V}] = [\mathbf{Y}]^{-1}[\mathbf{I}]$ using any of the three standard methods (Cramer's Rule, Matrix Inversion, or Gaussian Elimination).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following five formulas are the *backbone* of every Nodal Analysis problem. Memorize them with their sign conventions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Sign Convention |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_{ii} = \displaystyle\sum_{k \in \text{node } i} Y_k$ | Self-admittance at node $i$ | Always **positive** |
| 2 | $Y_{ij} = -\displaystyle\sum_{k} Y_{k}$ (sum of admittances directly between $i$ and $j$) | Mutual admittance between nodes $i$ and $j$ | Always **negative** |
| 3 | $[\mathbf{Y}][\mathbf{V}] = [\mathbf{I}]$ | Canonical matrix equation | Diagonal dominant, symmetric |
| 4 | $[\mathbf{V}] = [\mathbf{Y}]^{-1}[\mathbf{I}]$ | Solution via matrix inversion | $Y$ must be non-singular ($\det \neq 0$) |
| 5 | $V_i = \dfrac{\Delta_i}{\Delta}$ | Cramer's Rule | $\Delta = \det[\mathbf{Y}]$, $\Delta_i$ = replace $i$-th column by $[\mathbf{I}]$ |

### Special-Case Rules (Frequently Tested in KTU)

| Circuit Element | Nodal Equation Contribution |
|---|---|
| **Resistor $R$ between nodes $i$ and $j$** | Contributes $+\frac{1}{R}$ to $Y_{ii}$ and $Y_{jj}$, and $-\frac{1}{R}$ to $Y_{ij}$ and $Y_{ji}$. |
| **Independent Voltage Source between node $k$ and reference** | Node $k$ voltage is **fixed** to source value; remove row $k$ and column $k$ from $Y_{bus}$. |
| **Supernode (voltage source between two non-reference nodes $i$ and $j$)** | Combine KCL of both nodes; add constraint $V_i - V_j = V_s$. |
| **Current Source $I_s$ injecting into node $k$** | Adds $+I_s$ to $I_k$ (right-hand side vector). |
| **Current Source $I_s$ leaving node $k$** | Adds $-I_s$ to $I_k$ (right-hand side vector). |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is this used in industry?**
> - **Power System Load Flow Analysis** — $Y_{bus}$ is built for grids with **thousands of buses**; sparse-matrix techniques (LU factorization) are then applied.
> - **VLSI Circuit Simulation** — SPICE, HSPICE, and Cadence Spectre all use modified nodal analysis (MNA) on networks of millions of transistors.
> - **Filter & Network Synthesis** — $Y$-parameter analysis of two-port networks.
> - **PCB Signal Integrity Analysis** — Multi-node power distribution networks.
> - **Control Systems** — State-space representation of RLC networks via the admittance matrix.

The reason nodal analysis dominates is *scalability*: for a network with $b$ branches and $n$ nodes, nodal analysis produces only $n-1$ equations, whereas mesh analysis requires $b - n + 1$ equations. As networks grow, nodal analysis scales **far more favorably**.

# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix Work

## 3.1 General Derivation of the Admittance Matrix

Consider a network with $n$ non-reference essential nodes, indexed $1, 2, \dots, n$. Let the reference node be node 0. Admittance $Y_{kj}^{(b)}$ denotes the admittance of branch $b$ connecting node $k$ and node $j$ (with $Y_{k0}^{(b)}$ denoting admittance to ground).

### Step 1 — Apply KCL at a generic non-reference node $k$

Sum of currents leaving node $k$ through every connected branch = net injected current at $k$:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{n} Y_{kj}^{(b)} (V_k - V_j) = I_k$$

This is the fundamental nodal equation.

### Step 2 — Expand the summation by separating the reference node contribution

$$Y_{k0}(V_k - 0) + \sum_{j=1, j \neq k}^{n} Y_{kj}(V_k - V_j) = I_k$$

Rearranging:

$$\left(Y_{k0} + \sum_{j \neq k} Y_{kj}\right) V_k - \sum_{j \neq k} Y_{kj} V_j = I_k$$

### Step 3 — Identify the diagonal and off-diagonal terms

Define:
$$Y_{kk} \triangleq Y_{k0} + \sum_{j \neq k} Y_{kj} \quad \text{(self-admittance — sum of all admittances incident to node } k\text{)}$$

$$Y_{kj} \triangleq -Y_{kj} \quad \text{(mutual admittance — negative of the direct admittance between } k \text{ and } j\text{)}$$

The nodal equation becomes:

$$Y_{kk} V_k + \sum_{j \neq k} Y_{kj} V_j = I_k$$

### Step 4 — Stack all $n$ equations into matrix form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
Y_{11} & Y_{12} & \cdots & Y_{1n} \\
Y_{21} & Y_{22} & \cdots & Y_{2n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
Y_{n1} & Y_{n2} & \cdots & Y_{nn}
\end{bmatrix}_{n \times n}
\begin{bmatrix}
V_1 \\ V_2 \\ \vdots \\ V_n
\end{bmatrix}_{n \times 1}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
I_1 \\ I_2 \\ \vdots \\ I_n
\end{bmatrix}_{n \times 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical **bus admittance equation** $[\mathbf{Y}][\mathbf{V}] = [\mathbf{I}]$.

---

## 3.2 Fully Worked Example — A 3-Node Resistive Network

### Circuit Description

> [!NOTE]
> Consider a network with reference node 0 and three non-reference nodes 1, 2, 3. The following admittances exist:
> - Between Node 1 and Node 2: $Y_{12} = 2\,\text{S}$
> - Between Node 2 and Node 3: $Y_{23} = 4\,\text{S}$
> - Between Node 1 and Reference: $Y_{10} = 1\,\text{S}$
> - Between Node 3 and Reference: $Y_{30} = 3\,\text{S}$
> - Between Node 2 and Reference: $Y_{20} = 5\,\text{S}$
> - Current source $I_1 = 10\,\text{A}$ entering Node 1
> - Current source $I_2 = 0\,\text{A}$ at Node 2
> - Current source $I_3 = -6\,\text{A}$ at Node 3 (i.e., $6\,\text{A}$ leaving)

**Required:** Formulate the bus admittance matrix and solve for the node voltages.

### Step 1 — Compute the Diagonal Elements (Self-Admittances)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{11} &= Y_{10} + Y_{12} = 1 + 2 = 3\,\text{S} \\
Y_{22} &= Y_{20} + Y_{12} + Y_{23} = 5 + 2 + 4 = 11\,\text{S} \\
Y_{33} &= Y_{30} + Y_{23} = 3 + 4 = 7\,\text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Off-Diagonal Elements (Mutual Admittances)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{12} = Y_{21} &= -2\,\text{S} \\
Y_{13} = Y_{31} &= 0\,\text{S} \quad (\text{no direct branch between 1 and 3}) \\
Y_{23} = Y_{32} &= -4\,\text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Assemble the Bus Admittance Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\mathbf{Y}] = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 & 0 \\ -2 & 11 & -4 \\ 0 & -4 & 7 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Form the Current Injection Vector

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\mathbf{I}] = \begin{bmatrix} +10 \\ 0 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Write the Matrix Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 & 0 \\ -2 & 11 & -4 \\ 0 & -4 & 7 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \\ V_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Solve by Matrix Inversion (Method 1)

First, compute the determinant $\Delta = \det[\mathbf{Y}]$ by cofactor expansion along the first row:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 3 \begin{vmatrix} 11 & -4 \\ -4 & 7 \end{vmatrix} - (-2) \begin{vmatrix} -2 & -4 \\ 0 & 7 \end{vmatrix} + 0 \cdot (\ldots) \\
\Delta &= 3 \big( (11)(7) - (-4)(-4) \big) + 2 \big( (-2)(7) - (-4)(0) \big) \\
\Delta &= 3 (77 - 16) + 2(-14 - 0) \\
\Delta &= 3(61) + 2(-14) \\
\Delta &= 183 - 28 = 155\,\text{S}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\Delta = 155 \neq 0$, the matrix is non-singular and a unique solution exists.

### Step 7 — Solve via Cramer's Rule (Method 2 — most KTU-friendly)

**For $V_1$** — Replace the first column of $[\mathbf{Y}]$ with $[\mathbf{I}]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} 10 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 11 & -4 \\ -6 & -4 & 7 \end{vmatrix} \\
&= 10 \begin{vmatrix} 11 & -4 \\ -4 & 7 \end{vmatrix} - (-2) \begin{vmatrix} 0 & -4 \\ -6 & 7 \end{vmatrix} + 0 \\
&= 10 (77 - 16) + 2 (0 - 24) \\
&= 10(61) + 2(-24) \\
&= 610 - 48 = 562
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{\Delta_1}{\Delta} = \frac{562}{155} = \frac{562}{155} = 3.6258\,\text{V}
$$

**For $V_2$** — Replace the second column of $[\mathbf{Y}]$ with $[\mathbf{I}]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 10 & 0 \\ -2 & 0 & -4 \\ 0 & -6 & 7 \end{vmatrix} \\
&= 3 \begin{vmatrix} 0 & -4 \\ -6 & 7 \end{vmatrix} - 10 \begin{vmatrix} -2 & -4 \\ 0 & 7 \end{vmatrix} + 0 \\
&= 3 (0 - 24) - 10 (-14 - 0) \\
&= 3(-24) - 10(-14) \\
&= -72 + 140 = 68
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{\Delta_2}{\Delta} = \frac{68}{155} = 0.4387\,\text{V}
$$

**For $V_3$** — Replace the third column of $[\mathbf{Y}]$ with $[\mathbf{I}]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_3 &= \begin{vmatrix} 3 & -2 & 10 \\ -2 & 11 & 0 \\ 0 & -4 & -6 \end{vmatrix} \\
&= 3 \begin{vmatrix} 11 & 0 \\ -4 & -6 \end{vmatrix} - (-2) \begin{vmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -6 \end{vmatrix} + 10 \begin{vmatrix} -2 & 11 \\ 0 & -4 \end{vmatrix} \\
&= 3 (-66 - 0) + 2 (12 - 0) + 10 (8 - 0) \\
&= 3(-66) + 2(12) + 10(8) \\
&= -198 + 24 + 80 = -94
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
V_3 = \frac{\Delta_3}{\Delta} = \frac{-94}{155} = -0.6065\,\text{V}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Solution Vector and Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\mathbf{V}] = \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \\ V_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} +3.6258 \\ +0.4387 \\ -0.6065 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification** by substituting into Row 1 of $[\mathbf{Y}][\mathbf{V}]$:

$$
3(3.6258) + (-2)(0.4387) + 0(-0.6065) = 10.877 - 0.877 = 10.000 \approx I_1 \;\;\checkmark
$$

The solution is consistent.

---

## 3.3 Gaussian Elimination Procedure (Alternative Solution Method)

> [!NOTE]
> For networks with $n \geq 4$, Cramer's Rule becomes computationally expensive ($O(n!)$ operations). **Gaussian Elimination** with **LU Decomposition** is the industry-standard method. The procedure:

**Step A — Form augmented matrix** $[\mathbf{Y} \;|\; \mathbf{I}]$.

**Step B — Forward Elimination:** Use the first pivot to eliminate $V_1$ from rows 2 and 3 by the row operation $R_i \leftarrow R_i - \left(\frac{Y_{i1}}{Y_{11}}\right) R_1$.

**Step C — Back-Substitution:** Solve from the last row upward.

**Step D — Back-substitution** yields the same $[V_1, V_2, V_3]^T$ vector as Cramer's Rule.

For a system of $n$ equations, Gaussian Elimination requires only $O(n^3)$ operations, making it vastly superior for large-scale power-system and VLSI problems.

---

## 3.4 Existence and Uniqueness Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Test for Validity:**
> A unique node-voltage solution exists if and only if:
> 1. The admittance matrix $[\mathbf{Y}]$ is **non-singular** (i.e., $\det[\mathbf{Y}] \neq 0$).
> 2. The network does NOT contain a *floating subgraph* disconnected from the reference node (which would create a row of zeros in $[\mathbf{Y}]$).
> 3. There is no *cut-set* of only current sources incident to a non-reference node (which would violate KCL).

If any of these conditions fails, the matrix is singular and the system has either **infinitely many solutions** (under-determined) or **no solution** (inconsistent) — physically corresponding to a network with a defective topology or a short-circuited voltage source.

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Nodal Analysis Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow** of the Node Voltage Method as a procedural pipeline, from circuit input to the final solution vector. This block-level abstraction is used because the underlying circuit topology is best understood from a generic, network-agnostic view.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Resistive Network with n Essential Nodes] --> B[Step 1: Select Reference Node - Assign V=0]
    B --> C[Step 2: Label Non-Reference Nodes V1, V2, V3 ... Vn]
    C --> D[Step 3: Convert Voltage Sources to Norton Equivalents]
    D --> E[Step 4: Apply KCL at Each Non-Reference Node]
    E --> F[Step 5: Express Branch Currents as Y times V]
    F --> G[Step 6: Assemble Bus Admittance Matrix Ybus]
    G --> H{Step 7: Matrix is Non-Singular?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Step 8a: Choose Solution Method]
    H -- No --> J[Stop: Review Network Topology - Singular Y Matrix]
    I --> K[Method A: Cramer's Rule - Best for n leq 3]
    I --> L[Method B: Matrix Inversion - Symbolic / Theoretical]
    I --> M[Method C: Gaussian Elimination - Best for n ge 4]
    K --> N[Step 9: Compute V = Y-inverse times I]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Step 10: Verify by KCL and Power Balance]
    O --> P[OUTPUT: Node Voltage Vector V1 V2 ... Vn]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Structure of the Bus Admittance Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph YBUS["Y_bus Matrix Structure n x n"]
        direction TB
        D1[Diagonal: Y_ii = Sum of all admittances incident to node i - ALWAYS POSITIVE]
        OD[Off-Diagonal: Y_ij = minus Sum of admittances between i and j - ALWAYS NEGATIVE OR ZERO]
        SYM[Symmetric Property: Y_ij = Y_ji for passive bilateral networks]
        SP[Sparse Property: Many off-diagonals are ZERO for large networks]
    end
    D1 --> SUM[Self Admittance Block]
    OD --> MUT[Mutual Admittance Block]
    SYM --> EQ[Symmetry Block]
    SP --> SR[Sparsity Block]
```

## 4.3 Data Flow Diagram — Solution via Matrix Inversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[/Y bus Matrix and I vector/] --> CHECK{det Y is non-zero}
    CHECK -- Yes --> COF[Compute Cofactor Matrix C of Y]
    CHECK -- No --> ERR[Singular Matrix - No Unique Solution]
    COF --> ADJ[Adjugate Matrix adj Y equals Transpose of C]
    ADJ --> INV[Y inverse equals adj Y divided by det Y]
    INV --> MULT[Multiply Y inverse by I vector]
    MULT --> OUT[/Node Voltage Vector V/]
```

## 4.4 Step-by-Step Matrix Assembly Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Node 1] -->|Y12| N2[Node 2]
    N1 -->|Y13| N3[Node 3]
    N2 -->|Y23| N3
    N1 -->|Y10| REF[Reference Node V=0]
    N2 -->|Y20| REF
    N3 -->|Y30| REF
    N1 --> IS1[Current Source I1]
    N2 --> IS2[Current Source I2]
    N3 --> IS3[Current Source I3]
    IS1 --> YFORM[Form Y bus Matrix]
    IS2 --> YFORM
    IS3 --> YFORM
    N1 --> YFORM
    N2 --> YFORM
    N3 --> YFORM
    REF --> YFORM
    YFORM --> YM[Diagonal: Y11 = Y10 + Y12 + Y13]
    YFORM --> YM2[Diagonal: Y22 = Y20 + Y12 + Y23]
    YFORM --> YM3[Diagonal: Y33 = Y30 + Y13 + Y23]
    YFORM --> OF[Off-diagonal: Y12 = Y21 = minus Y12]
    YFORM --> OF2[Off-diagonal: Y13 = Y31 = minus Y13]
    YFORM --> OF3[Off-diagonal: Y23 = Y32 = minus Y23]
```
