# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> **Define Faraday's Laws of Electromagnetic Induction. State the significance of the negative sign in Faraday's equation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's First Law:** Whenever the magnetic flux linked with a closed coil/circuit changes, an EMF is induced in the coil. The induced EMF exists as long as the change in flux continues.

**Faraday's Second Law:** The magnitude of the induced EMF in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkages.

$$e \propto N \frac{d\phi}{dt} \implies e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

**Significance of the Negative Sign:** The negative sign represents **Lenz's Law**. It indicates that the induced EMF (and hence the induced current) always opposes the cause (change in flux) that produced it. This ensures **conservation of energy** — without it, perpetual motion machines would be possible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to explain **why** the negative sign is there. Merely stating "it represents Lenz's Law" is incomplete. Always elaborate on the **conservation of energy** aspect for full marks.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series 2]**

> **Differentiate between Statically Induced EMF and Dynamically Induced EMF with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Statically Induced EMF | Dynamically Induced EMF |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Conductor | Stationary | Moving |
| Magnetic Field | Time-varying | Stationary |
| Cause | Time-varying current | Motion of conductor |
| Formula | $e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$ | $e = Blv\sin\theta$ |
| Example | **Transformer** | **DC Generator** |

**Statically Induced EMF Example:** In a transformer, the primary coil is stationary, but the AC current produces a time-varying magnetic flux in the core, which induces an EMF in the secondary coil.

**Dynamically Induced EMF Example:** In a DC generator, the armature conductors rotate through a stationary magnetic field produced by the field poles, inducing an EMF given by $e = Blv$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write "static EMF" in transformers without specifying that the **field** is time-varying. Always clarify which entity is static and which is varying.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **mutual inductance M between two magnetically coupled coils** in terms of the coefficient of coupling k and their self-inductances $L_1$ and $L_2$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two coils, A and B, have self-inductances of $L_A = 8$ mH and $L_B = 2$ mH. The coefficient of coupling between them is $k = 0.5$. Calculate:
> (i) The mutual inductance M
> (ii) The maximum possible mutual inductance
> (iii) The induced EMF in coil B if the current in coil A changes at a rate of $\frac{di_A}{dt} = 1000$ A/s. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Derivation**

Let Coil 1 (primary) have $N_1$ turns carrying current $i_1$, and Coil 2 (secondary) have $N_2$ turns.

**Step 1:** Current $i_1$ produces flux $\phi_1$ in Coil 1:
$$\phi_1 = \frac{N_1 i_1}{S_1}$$
where $S_1$ is the permeance of the magnetic path of Coil 1.

**Step 2:** Self-inductance of Coil 1:
$$L_1 = \frac{N_1 \phi_1}{i_1} = \frac{N_1^2}{S_1}$$

**Step 3:** A fraction of $\phi_1$, say $k\phi_1$, links with Coil 2. So flux linking Coil 2:
$$\phi_{12} = k\phi_1$$

**Step 4:** Mutual inductance:
$$M = \frac{N_2 \phi_{12}}{i_1} = \frac{N_2 \cdot k \phi_1}{i_1} = \frac{k N_2 N_1}{S_1}$$

**Step 5:** Similarly, $L_2 = \frac{N_2^2}{S_2}$ (assuming $S_1 = S_2 = S$ for simplicity):
$$L_1 L_2 = \frac{N_1^2 N_2^2}{S^2} \implies \sqrt{L_1 L_2} = \frac{N_1 N_2}{S}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:
$$\boxed{M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$$

---

**Part (b): Numerical Problem**

**Given:** $L_A = 8$ mH $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ H, $L_B = 2$ mH $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ H, $k = 0.5$, $\frac{di_A}{dt} = 1000$ A/s.

**(i) Mutual Inductance M:**
$$M = k\sqrt{L_A \cdot L_B}$$
$$M = 0.5 \times \sqrt{(8 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^{-3})}$$
$$M = 0.5 \times \sqrt{16 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$M = 0.5 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\boxed{M = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H} = 2 \text{ mH}}$$

**(ii) Maximum possible M (when k = 1):**
$$M_{max} = 1 \times \sqrt{L_A \cdot L_B} = \sqrt{(8 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^{-3})} = 4 \text{ mH}$$

**(iii) Induced EMF in Coil B:**
$$e_B = -M \frac{di_A}{dt}$$
$$e_B = -(2 \times 10^{-3})(1000)$$
$$\boxed{e_B = -2 \text{ V}}$$

The negative sign indicates that the induced EMF opposes the change in current (Lenz's Law). The **magnitude** is **2 V**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Don't forget to convert **mH to H** (×10⁻³) before calculations. Many students lose 1-2 marks by mixing units. Also, the negative sign must be interpreted — it doesn't mean "wrong direction" but rather "opposition."

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]**

> **(a)** Define **Self-Inductance** and **Mutual Inductance**. Derive the expression for the **self-inductance of a coil with N turns wound on a non-magnetic core of permeability $\mu_0$, cross-sectional area A, and mean length l**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A coil of 200 turns is wound uniformly over a non-magnetic ring of mean circumference 50 cm and cross-sectional area 10 cm². Calculate:
> (i) The self-inductance of the coil
> (ii) The energy stored when a current of 4 A flows through the coil
> (iii) The average EMF induced if the current is reduced from 4 A to 0 in 50 ms. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Definitions and Derivation**

**Self-Inductance (L):** Self-inductance is the property of a coil that opposes any change in the current flowing through it. It is defined as the flux linkages per unit current.
$$L = \frac{N\phi}{i}$$

**Mutual Inductance (M):** Mutual inductance is the property of two coils whereby an EMF is induced in one coil due to the rate of change of current in the other coil.
$$M = \frac{N_2 \phi_{12}}{i_1}$$

**Derivation of Self-Inductance:**

**Step 1:** Consider a toroidal coil with N turns, mean length l, cross-sectional area A, wound on a non-magnetic core ($\mu_r = 1$).

**Step 2:** Magnetic field intensity inside the coil:
$$H = \frac{Ni}{l}$$

**Step 3:** Flux density:
$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = \mu_0 \frac{Ni}{l}$$

**Step 4:** Flux per turn:
$$\phi = BA = \frac{\mu_0 N i A}{l}$$

**Step 5:** Total flux linkages:
$$\lambda = N\phi = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 i A}{l}$$

**Step 6:** Self-inductance:
$$L = \frac{\lambda}{i} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$$

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l} \text{ Henries}}$$

---

**Part (b): Numerical Problem**

**Given:** $N = 200$ turns, $l = 50$ cm $= 0.5$ m, $A = 10$ cm² $= 10 \times 10^{-4}$ m² $= 10^{-3}$ m², $I = 4$ A, $\Delta t = 50$ ms $= 50 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

**(i) Self-Inductance L:**
$$L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$$
$$L = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(200)^2 (10^{-3})}{0.5}$$
$$L = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(40000)(10^{-3})}{0.5}$$
$$L = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(40)}{0.5}$$
$$L = \frac{160\pi \times 10^{-7}}{0.5}$$
$$L = 320\pi \times 10^{-7}$$
$$\boxed{L = 1.005 \times 10^{-4} \text{ H} = 100.5 \text{ }\mu\text{H}}$$

**(ii) Energy stored at I = 4 A:**
$$W = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$$
$$W = \frac{1}{2} \times (1.005 \times 10^{-4}) \times (4)^2$$
$$W = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.005 \times 10^{-4} \times 16$$
$$W = 8.04 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\boxed{W = 0.804 \text{ mJ}}$$

**(iii) Average Induced EMF:**
$$e_{avg} = -L \frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t} = -L \frac{(0 - 4)}{50 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$e_{avg} = -(1.005 \times 10^{-4}) \times \frac{-4}{0.05}$$
$$e_{avg} = \frac{4.02 \times 10^{-4}}{0.05}$$
$$\boxed{e_{avg} = 8.04 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 8.04 \text{ mV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** 
> 1. **Always convert all units to SI** (cm → m, cm² → m²) before substituting. 
> 2. **Don't forget the negative sign** in Faraday's Law — interpret it as opposition, not error.
> 3. **Show intermediate steps** — partial marks are awarded for each correct step.
> 4. For toroidal coils, $\mu_r = 1$ (non-magnetic), so use only $\mu_0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### 🔑 **Core Concepts to Memorize:**

- **Faraday's First Law:** Changing flux → Induced EMF (qualitative).
- **Faraday's Second Law:** $e = -N\frac{d\phi}{dt}$ (quantitative, includes Lenz's sign).
- **Lenz's Law:** Induced current opposes the cause of change (conservation of energy).
- **Statically Induced EMF:** Conductor stationary, field time-varying. Example: Transformer.
- **Dynamically Induced EMF:** Conductor moving, field static. Example: Generator.
- **Motional EMF Formula:** $e = Blv\sin\theta$.

### 🔑 **Inductance Formulas:**

- **Self-Inductance:** $L = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 \mu_r A}{l}$ (for toroidal/cylindrical coil).
- **Mutual Inductance:** $M = \frac{N_2 \phi_{12}}{i_1} = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$.
- **Coefficient of Coupling:** $k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ (range: 0 to 1).
- **Energy Stored:** $W = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$.

### 🔑 **Self-Inductance vs. Mutual Inductance:**

| Aspect | Self-Inductance (L) | Mutual Inductance (M) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Number of Coils | One | Two (coupled) |
| Cause | Own current change | Other coil's current change |
| EMF Formula | $e = -L\frac{di}{dt}$ | $e_2 = -M\frac{di_1}{dt}$ |
| Unit | Henry (H) | Henry (H) |
| Depends on | N, μ, A, l | $k, L_1, L_2$ |

### 🔑 **Common KTU Exam Mistakes to Avoid:**

1. ❌ Writing $e = N\frac{d\phi}{dt}$ **without the negative sign** (forgets Lenz's Law) → Loses 1 mark.
2. ❌ Confusing **statically and dynamically induced EMF** definitions.
3. ❌ Using **cm and cm²** without converting to **m and m²** in numerical problems.
4. ❌ Forgetting **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** value for non-magnetic cores.
5. ❌ Stating $k > 1$ or $k < 0$ — **physically impossible**.
6. ❌ Confusing **flux linkages (Nφ)** with **flux (φ)** in inductance formulas.
7. ❌ Using **Fleming's Right Hand Rule** for statically induced EMF (use it only for dynamically induced EMF).

### 🔑 **Quick Numerical Solving Strategy:**

1. **Read given data** — extract N, A, l, μᵣ, currents, time.
2. **Convert to SI units** — cm → m, cm² → m², mH → H.
3. **Identify the type** — Self or Mutual or Coupling.
4. **Apply the right formula** — refer to the cheat sheet.
5. **Show all intermediate steps** — for partial marks.
6. **Include units in the final answer** — V, H, J, Wb.

### 🔑 **Real-World Connection for Viva:**

> [!TIP]
> "Why are transformers laminated?" — To reduce **eddy current losses** (related to dynamically induced EMF in the core).
> "Why is mutual inductance important in wireless charging?" — Because **k** between the transmitter and receiver coils determines the **power transfer efficiency**.
> "Why do ignition coils in cars produce high voltage?" — Because of the **step-up action** via mutual inductance (statically induced EMF).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternating Current Fundamentals: Generation of Alternating Voltages, Representation of Sinusoidal Waveforms (Frequency, Period, Average Value, RMS Value, Form Factor)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Alternating Current Fundamentals: Generation of Alternating Voltages, Representation of Sinusoidal Waveforms (Frequency, Period, Average Value, RMS Value, Form Factor) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Alternating Current (AC)? An **Alternating Current (AC)** is an electrical current that periodically reverses its direction and continuously changes its magnitude with respect to time, in contrast to Direct Current (DC) which flows in one direction with a constant magnitude. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *An alternating current/voltage is one whose magnitude and direction vary continuously and periodically with time, conforming mathematically to a sinusoidal (or other periodic) function. The most common and analytically privileged form is the **sinusoidal waveform**.* ## 1.2 Sinusoidal Waveform — The Heart of AC Analysis A **sinusoidal waveform** is a mathematical curve that describes a smooth periodic oscillation. It is the natural output of rotating machines and the preferred form for power transmission worldwide. Its general mathematical representation is: $$ v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi) $$ Where: - $V_m$ → Maximum (Peak) value in Volts - $\omega$ → Angular frequency in rad/s - $\phi$ → Phase angle in radians - $t$ → Time in seconds ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Lighthouse and the Ferris Wheel" Imagine standing on a giant **Ferris wheel** (this is your rotating coil inside a magnetic field) and watching a **lighthouse beam** sweep across a wall. - As the Ferris wheel rotates, the lighthouse beam traces a **shadow on the wall** that moves up and down in a perfectly smooth, repeating pattern. - The shadow oscillates from +maximum down to -maximum and back again — this oscillation traces out exactly a **sine wave**. - The speed of rotation of the wheel controls how **fast** the shadow oscillates (this is *frequency*). - The size of the wheel controls how **tall** the shadow swings (this is the *amplitude* / *peak value*). This is *literally* how an AC generator works: a coil of wire rotates inside a magnetic field, and the voltage measured at the coil's terminals naturally traces a sine wave. ## 1.4 Why Sinusoidal AC? The Engineering Preference > [!NOTE] > **Why do engineers love sine waves?** > 1. **Natural Generation:** Rotating machines inherently produce sine waves via Faraday's Law. > 2. **Linear Circuits Preserve Sinusoids:** When a sine wave is applied to a circuit containing only L, C, and R, the output remains sinusoidal (only amplitude and phase change — not the shape!). > 3. **Mathematical Simplicity:** Calculus (derivatives and integrals) of a sine is another sine. > 4. **Power Efficiency:** Sinusoidal AC allows efficient use of transformers for stepping voltage up/down. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever asked "Why sine wave is preferred?", always mention at least 3 of the above 4 reasons to score full marks on this frequent 3-mark question. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Generation of Alternating EMF — Faraday's Law in Action An **AC Generator (Alternator)** is built on the principle of **Electromagnetic Induction** discovered by **Michael Faraday** in 1831. ### Working Principle (Step-by-Step): 1. **The Setup:** A rectangular coil of $N$ turns, each of area $A$ square meters, is placed inside a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$ Tesla. 2. **Rotation:** The coil is rotated about its own axis with a constant angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s by a prime mover (turbine, engine, etc.). 3. **Flux Linkage Change:** As the coil rotates, the **angle $\theta$** between the magnetic field vector and the **normal to the coil plane** continuously changes. 4. **Flux Cutting:** The rotating coil *cuts* the magnetic field lines, inducing an EMF. 5. **Sinusoidal Output:** The induced EMF varies sinusoidally with the angle of rotation. ### The Induced EMF Equation: The instantaneous magnetic flux linked with the coil at any time $t$ is: $$ \phi = B \cdot A \cdot N \cdot \cos(\omega t) $$ By Faraday's Law, the induced EMF is the negative rate of change of flux linkage: $$ e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt} $$ Differentiating gives the well-known sinusoidal EMF equation: $$ e = E_m \sin(\omega t) $$ Where the maximum value is: $$ E_m = N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \omega $$ ## 2.2 Key Parameters of a Sinusoidal Waveform ### (A) Waveform Definition - A **Waveform** is a graph showing the variation of voltage or current with respect to time. - A **Sinusoidal Waveform** is one that obeys the sine function mathematically. ### (B) Instantaneous Value - The value of voltage or current at **any specific instant** of time. - Denoted by lowercase letters: $v(t)$ or $i(t)$. ### (C) Peak Value (Maximum Value / Amplitude / Crest Value) - The **maximum magnitude** attained by the waveform during one complete cycle. - Denoted by uppercase subscript $m$: $V_m$, $I_m$, or $E_m$. ### (D) Peak-to-Peak Value - The value measured from the **positive peak to the negative peak**. - Mathematically: $V_{pp} = 2 V_m$ ### (E) Cycle - One complete set of positive and negative values of an alternating quantity. - One cycle spans **360°** or **$2\pi$ radians**. ### (F) Time Period ($T$) - The **time taken in seconds** to complete one full cycle. - Unit: **seconds (s)** - Symbol: $T$ ### (G) Frequency ($f$) - The **number of complete cycles** that occur per second. - Unit: **Hertz (Hz)** = cycles per second - The relationship between $f$ and $T$ is the foundation of AC analysis: $$ f = \frac{1}{T} \quad \text{and} \quad T = \frac{1}{f} $$ ### (H) Angular Frequency ($\omega$) - The rate of change of angle in **radians per second**. - Unit: **rad/s** $$ \omega = 2\pi f = \frac{2\pi}{T} $$ ## 2.3 Average Value of a Sinusoidal AC Waveform > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Point:** The *average value* of a **complete** sinusoidal AC cycle is mathematically **ZERO** because the positive half-cycle exactly cancels the negative half-cycle. Therefore, by convention, the average value is calculated over the **positive half-cycle only**. ### Definition: The **average value** of an alternating current is defined as the **steady (DC) current that would transfer the same amount of charge** as the AC current does in the **same time interval (half-cycle)**. ### Mathematical Derivation Setup: For $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ over the half-cycle $0$ to $\pi$: $$ I_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta $$ Solving this integral gives the famous result: $$ \boxed{I_{avg} = \frac{2 I_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \, I_m} $$ Similarly for voltage: $$ V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \, V_m $$ ## 2.4 RMS (Root Mean Square) Value — The Effective Value > [!NOTE] > **Why "RMS" exists:** Engineers needed a single number to represent the "effectiveness" of an AC waveform in producing heat (in a resistor) or doing work. This is called the **Effective Value** or **Virtual Value** of AC. ### Definition: The **RMS value** of an AC current is defined as the **steady (DC) current which, when flowing through a given resistance for a given time, produces the same amount of heat** as the AC current does in the **same resistance** for the **same time**. ### The Three Words in "RMS": 1. **R — Root:** Take the square root at the end. 2. **M — Mean:** Take the mean (average) of the squared values. 3. **S — Square:** Square each instantaneous value first. ### Mathematical Result: $$ \boxed{I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, I_m} $$ $$ \boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, V_m} $$ ## 2.5 Form Factor (FF) The **Form Factor** is a dimensionless ratio that indicates how "peaky" or "flat" a waveform is compared to an ideal DC value. $$ \boxed{K_f = \text{Form Factor} = \frac{\text{RMS Value}}{\text{Average Value}}} $$ For a pure sinusoidal wave: $$ K_f = \frac{I_m / \sqrt{2}}{2 I_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11 $$ ## 2.6 Peak Factor (Crest Factor) $$ \boxed{K_p = \text{Peak Factor} = \frac{\text{Peak (Maximum) Value}}{\text{RMS Value}}} $$ For a pure sinusoidal wave: $$ K_p = \frac{I_m}{I_m / \sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 $$ ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Numerical Constant | Unit | |---|-----------|---------|-------------------|------| | 1 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi / T$ | — | rad/s | | 2 | Frequency | $f = 1/T$ | — | Hz | | 3 | Period | $T = 1/f$ | — | s | | 4 | Induced EMF (max) | $E_m = N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \omega$ | — | V | | 5 | Instantaneous EMF | $e = E_m \sin(\omega t)$ | — | V | | 6 | Average Value (half-cycle) | $V_{avg} = 2V_m / \pi$ | $\approx 0.637 \, V_m$ | V | | 7 | RMS Value | $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ | $\approx 0.707 \, V_m$ | V | | 8 | Form Factor | $K_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg}$ | $\pi / (2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.11$ | — | | 9 | Peak Factor | $K_p = V_m / V_{rms}$ | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ | — | | 10 | Peak-to-Peak Value | $V_{pp} = 2 V_m$ | — | V | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams:** Students often confuse *Form Factor* with *Peak Factor*. A simple mnemonic: **"FF = RMS / AVG"** (RMS first) and **"PF = PEAK / RMS"** (PEAK first). Both have the RMS term in the denominator — that's your anchor. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Domestic Power:** The "230 V" written on every Indian appliance is the **RMS value**, not the peak. The actual peak voltage on the socket is $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 \approx 325$ V! - **Fuse & Wire Sizing:** Conductors are rated by RMS current because heating is proportional to $I^2 R$ (i.e., to RMS). - **Multimeter Readings:** Both analog and digital multimeters display the RMS value of any AC measurement. - **Transformer Rating:** All transformer nameplate values (kVA, voltage, current) are given in RMS. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Instantaneous EMF Equation ### Given: - A rectangular coil of $N$ turns, each having an area $A$ m² - Rotated in a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$ Tesla - Constant angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s ### Step 1: Define the angle of rotation At time $t = 0$, let the plane of the coil be parallel to the magnetic field. At any later time $t$, the coil has rotated through an angle: $$ \theta = \omega t \quad \text{(in radians)} $$ ### Step 2: Express the magnetic flux through the coil The magnetic flux $\phi$ linking the coil is given by the dot product of $\vec{B}$ and the area vector $\vec{A}$. As the coil rotates, the angle between $\vec{B}$ and the normal to the coil plane changes: $$ \phi = B \cdot A \cdot \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{(per turn)} $$ For $N$ turns, the total flux linkage is: $$ \Phi = N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \cos(\omega t) $$ ### Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction Faraday's Law states that the induced EMF is the negative rate of change of flux linkage: $$ e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt} $$ > Here, $\phi$ represents the total flux linkage $N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \cos(\omega t)$ in the more general form, but we use $\phi$ to represent the flux linkage for simplicity. ### Step 4: Perform the differentiation Differentiate the flux linkage expression with respect to time: $$ e = -N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[\cos(\omega t)\right] $$ Applying the standard derivative identity $\frac{d}{dt}\cos(\omega t) = -\omega \sin(\omega t)$: $$ e = -N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \left[-\omega \sin(\omega t)\right] $$ ### Step 5: Simplify the result The two negative signs cancel, yielding the final sinusoidal EMF equation: $$ \boxed{e = N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(\omega t) = E_m \sin(\omega t)} $$ Where the peak (maximum) EMF is: $$ E_m = N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \omega $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** $E_m$ depends on three things: the magnetic field strength $B$, the coil size $A$ and number of turns $N$, and the rotational speed $\omega$. Double any of these, and the peak voltage doubles. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Average Value (Half-Cycle) ### Given: A sinusoidal alternating current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\theta)$ over the half-cycle from $\theta = 0$ to $\theta = \pi$. ### Step 1: Set up the averaging integral The average value over an interval is the integral divided by the length of the interval. For a half-cycle, the interval length in radians is $(\pi - 0) = \pi$: $$ I_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi - 0} \int_{0}^{\pi} i \, d\theta = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta $$ ### Step 2: Pull the constant out of the integral Since $I_m$ does not depend on $\theta$: $$ I_{avg} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin(\theta) \, d\theta $$ ### Step 3: Evaluate the integral Using the standard result $\int \sin(\theta) \, d\theta = -\cos(\theta)$: $$ I_{avg} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\theta) \right]_{0}^{\pi} $$ ### Step 4: Apply the limits of integration Substitute $\theta = \pi$ and $\theta = 0$: $$ I_{avg} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \left[ (-\cos(\pi)) - (-\cos(0)) \right] $$ ### Step 5: Substitute trigonometric values Recall that $\cos(\pi) = -1$ and $\cos(0) = 1$: $$ I_{avg} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \left[ -(-1) - (-1) \right] = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \left[ 1 + 1 \right] = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \times 2 $$ ### Step 6: Final result $$ \boxed{I_{avg} = \frac{2 I_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \, I_m} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $I_{avg} = 0$ for a full cycle. While mathematically correct for the full cycle, KTU questions ALWAYS ask for the average value **of the half-cycle**, which gives $0.637 I_m$. The full-cycle average is zero only when symmetry is perfectly balanced. ## 3.3 Derivation of the RMS Value ### Step 1: Define RMS as the square root of the mean of the square By definition, for the function $i(t) = I_m \sin(\theta)$ over a full cycle ($\theta = 0$ to $2\pi$): $$ I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} i^2 \, d\theta} $$ ### Step 2: Square the instantaneous current $i^2 = I_m^2 \sin^2(\theta)$: $$ I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} I_m^2 \sin^2(\theta) \, d\theta} $$ ### Step 3: Pull constants out Since $I_m^2$ is a constant with respect to $\theta$: $$ I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{I_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \sin^2(\theta) \, d\theta} $$ ### Step 4: Apply the half-angle identity Using $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$: $$ I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{I_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2} \, d\theta} $$ ### Step 5: Split the integral $$ I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{I_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{2\pi} 1 \, d\theta - \int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos(2\theta) \, d\theta \right]} $$ ### Step 6: Evaluate each integral separately **First integral:** $$ \int_{0}^{2\pi} 1 \, d\theta = \left[ \theta \right]_{0}^{2\pi} = 2\pi $$ **Second integral:** $$ \int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos(2\theta) \, d\theta = \left[ \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{2} \right]_{0}^{2\pi} = \frac{\sin(4\pi) - \sin(0)}{2} = 0 $$ ### Step 7: Substitute back $$ I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{I_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ 2\pi - 0 \right]} = \sqrt{\frac{I_m^2 \cdot 2\pi}{4\pi}} $$ ### Step 8: Simplify $$ I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{I_m^2}{2}} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} $$ ### Step 9: Final result $$ \boxed{I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, I_m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The factor $1/\sqrt{2}$ is the *sine wave's signature*. It says: for the same heating effect, a 1 A DC current is equivalent to a 1.414 A peak AC current. The 230 V mains delivers peaks of 325 V but is rated at 230 V RMS. ## 3.4 Derivation of Form Factor ### Step 1: Use the definitions Form Factor is defined as the ratio of RMS value to Average value: $$ K_f = \frac{I_{rms}}{I_{avg}} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute the derived values From Sections 3.2 and 3.3: $$ K_f = \frac{I_m / \sqrt{2}}{2 I_m / \pi} $$ ### Step 3: Cancel $I_m$ and simplify $$ K_f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} $$ ### Step 4: Rationalize and compute numerically Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{2}$: $$ K_f = \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{4} = \frac{3.1416 \times 1.4142}{4} = \frac{4.4429}{4} \approx 1.11 $$ ### Step 5: Final result $$ \boxed{K_f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{4} \approx 1.11} $$ ## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Parameter | Expression | Numeric Factor | Sinusoidal Result | |-----------|-----------|----------------|-------------------| | Peak Value | $V_m$ | 1.000 | $V_m$ | | Average Value (half cycle) | $2V_m / \pi$ | 0.637 | $0.637 V_m$ | | RMS Value | $V_m / \sqrt{2}$ | 0.707 | $0.707 V_m$ | | Peak-to-Peak | $2 V_m$ | 2.000 | $2 V_m$ | | Form Factor | $V_{rms} / V_{avg}$ | 1.11 | $1.11$ | | Crest Factor | $V_m / V_{rms}$ | 1.414 | $1.414$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AC Generator — Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an AC generator, showing the interactions between its physical and electrical subsystems: ```mermaid graph TD A[Prime Mover
Turbine or Engine] -->|Mechanical Rotation omega| B[Rotor Coil
N turns, Area A] C[Field Winding
Excitation DC] -->|Produces Magnetic Field B| D[Stator Magnetic Circuit] D -->|Provides Flux Density B Tesla| B B -->|Cuts Magnetic Field Lines| E[Flux Linkage Change
dPhi by dt] E -->|Faradays Law| F[Induced Sinusoidal EMF

Em sin omega t] H[Slip Rings and Brushes] -->|Connects to External Load| G G -->|Measured Values| I[Vm Peak Value] G -->|Integrated Squared| J[Vrms equals Vm by root 2] G -->|Half Cycle Mean| K[Vavg equals 2Vm by pi] J -->|Ratio with Vavg| L[Form Factor equals 1.11] J -->|Ratio with Vm| M[Peak Factor equals 1.414] ``` ## 4.2 Sinusoidal Waveform Parameter Map This diagram visualizes the relationship between all the derived parameters on a single sine wave: ```mermaid graph LR A[Sinusoidal Wave v of t equals Vm sine omega t] --> B[Peak Value Vm] A --> C[Peak to Peak Vpp equals 2 Vm] A --> D[Period T seconds] A --> E[Frequency f equals 1 by T in Hz] A --> F[Angular Frequency omega equals 2 pi f] A --> G[Average Value Vavg equals 0.637 Vm] A --> H[RMS Value Vrms equals 0.707 Vm] G -->|Ratio Vrms to Vavg| I[Form Factor equals 1.11] B -->|Ratio Vm to Vrms| J[Peak Factor equals 1.414] H -->|Used for Power Calculations| K[Equivalent DC Heating Effect] G -->|Used for Rectifier Design| L[DC Output Estimation] ``` ## 4.3 AC vs DC — Comparative Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[Electrical Sources] --> B[Direct Current DC] A --> C[Alternating Current AC] B -->|Constant Magnitude
Fixed Direction| D[Batteries, Solar Cells,
DC Generators] C -->|Sinusoidal Magnitude

Power Grid Mains] D -->|No Frequency Parameter| F[Steady State Analysis] E -->|Has Frequency f, Period T, omega| G[Phasor Analysis Required] G -->|Uses RMS Value for Power| H[P equals Vrms x Irms x cos phi] F -->|Uses Direct Values| I[P equals V x I] ``` ## 4.4 Generation Principle — Coil Rotation Stages ```mermaid graph TD A[Coil Position 0 degrees
Plane parallel to B] -->|Flux linked equals 0| B[e equals 0] A -->|Rotation begins, omega rad per sec| C[Coil Position 90 degrees
Plane perpendicular to B] C -->|Maximum flux cutting rate| D[e equals Em maximum] D -->|Rotation continues| E[Coil Position 180 degrees
Plane parallel to B reverse] E -->|Flux cutting reverses| F[e equals 0] F -->|Rotation continues| G[Coil Position 270 degrees
Plane perpendicular to B reverse] G -->|Maximum flux cutting reverse| H[e equals negative Em] H -->|One Full Cycle 360 degrees| A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In a single 360° rotation of the coil, the EMF completes **one full sine cycle** — going from 0 → +Peak → 0 → -Peak → 0. This is precisely why AC has a **frequency** measured in cycles per second (Hz). ## 4.5 Text-Based Schematic — A Real Sine Wave ``` +Vm • • • • • | | | | | | | | | | ----------+--------+---------+---------+---------+----→ t (time) | | | | | -Vm | | | | | | | | | | 0° 90° 180° 270° 360° 450° (ωt) ← T/2 →← ←————— T —————→ (half cycle) (full cycle period) ``` The above sketch represents a single sinusoidal cycle. Mark on it: - **Peak Value** $V_m$ (vertical line from axis to top peak) - **Peak-to-Peak** $V_{pp} = 2V_m$ (full vertical line from top to bottom) - **Period $T$** (horizontal distance between two positive-going zero crossings) - **RMS = 0.707 $V_m$** (a horizontal line drawn at this level) - **Average = 0.637 $V_m$** (a horizontal line drawn at this level, above RMS) # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define RMS value and average value of an alternating quantity. Why is the RMS value also called the "effective value"?** **Model Answer:** The **Average Value** of a sinusoidal AC waveform (taken over a half-cycle) is defined as the steady DC current that transfers the same charge as the AC current in the same half-cycle. For a sine wave: $$ I_{avg} = \frac{2 I_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637 I_m $$ The **RMS (Root Mean Square) Value** is defined as the steady DC current which, when flowing through a given resistance, produces the same heating effect (over a complete cycle) as the AC current. **Why called "Effective Value":** The RMS value is called the *effective value* because it is the DC equivalent that produces the same useful effect (heat/work) as the AC quantity. For example, a 5 A RMS AC current will heat a resistor by exactly the same amount as a 5 A DC current. Hence, it represents the "effective" magnitude of the AC. For a pure sine wave: $$ I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 I_m $$ --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: What is meant by "form factor" and "peak factor" of an AC waveform? State their values for a sinusoidal waveform.** **Model Answer:** **Form Factor (FF):** It is defined as the ratio of the RMS value to the Average value of the AC waveform: $$ K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} $$ **Peak Factor (PF) / Crest Factor:** It is defined as the ratio of the Peak (Maximum) value to the RMS value of the AC waveform: $$ K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} $$ **For a Pure Sinusoidal Waveform:** $$ K_f = \frac{V_m / \sqrt{2}}{2 V_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11 $$ $$ K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_m / \sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 $$ These values are constant for a pure sine wave and are used as a benchmark to compare the "peakedness" or "flatness" of other waveforms like square, triangular, or sawtooth waves. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question in the University Exam carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B (Module Internal Choice). --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]* **Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the generation of a single-phase sinusoidal AC EMF using a rotating coil in a uniform magnetic field. Derive the expression for the instantaneous EMF in terms of flux density, area, number of turns, angular velocity, and time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a sinusoidal AC voltage $v(t) = 141.4 \sin(314 t)$ Volts, calculate: (i) Peak Voltage (ii) RMS Voltage (iii) Average Voltage (iv) Frequency (v) Time Period (vi) Form Factor (vii) Peak Factor **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: ### Part (a) — Generation of Sinusoidal EMF **Setup:** A single-turn rectangular coil ABCD of area $A$ m² is placed in a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$ Tesla. The coil is rotated about a fixed axis with constant angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s. The axis of rotation is perpendicular to the magnetic field. **Working:** - At time $t = 0$, the plane of the coil lies in the plane of the magnetic field (i.e., normal to the coil is perpendicular to $B$). The flux linkage is therefore zero, and so the rate of change of flux is maximum. - As the coil rotates, the angle $\theta = \omega t$ between the normal to the coil and the magnetic field changes continuously. - The flux linked with the coil at any instant $t$ is: $$ \phi = B \cdot A \cdot \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{(for 1 turn)} $$ - For $N$ turns, total flux linkage $= N B A \cos(\omega t)$ - By Faraday's Law, the induced EMF is: $$ e = -\frac{d}{dt}[N B A \cos(\omega t)] = N B A \omega \sin(\omega t) $$ - Letting $E_m = N B A \omega$, we obtain the standard sinusoidal EMF equation: $$ \boxed{e = E_m \sin(\omega t)} $$ - The waveform completes one full cycle for every $360°$ (or $2\pi$ rad) rotation of the coil. The peak EMF $E_m$ is achieved when the coil plane is parallel to $B$ (i.e., at $90°$ rotation, where $\sin(90°) = 1$). ### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations **Given:** $v(t) = 141.4 \sin(314 t)$ V Comparing with the standard form $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: - $V_m = 141.4$ V - $\omega = 314$ rad/s **(i) Peak Voltage:** $$ V_m = 141.4 \text{ V} $$ **(ii) RMS Voltage:** $$ V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{141.4}{1.414} = 100 \text{ V} $$ **(iii) Average Voltage:** $$ V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 141.4}{3.1416} = \frac{282.8}{3.1416} \approx 90 \text{ V} $$ **(iv) Frequency:** $$ f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{314}{2 \times 3.1416} = \frac{314}{6.2832} = 50 \text{ Hz} $$ **(v) Time Period:** $$ T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms} $$ **(vi) Form Factor:** $$ K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{100}{90} \approx 1.11 $$ **(vii) Peak Factor:** $$ K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{141.4}{100} \approx 1.414 $$ --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]* **Q: (a)** Define the following terms for a sinusoidal AC waveform with a labelled sketch: (i) Instantaneous value (ii) Peak value (iii) Peak-to-peak value (iv) Time period (v) Frequency (vi) Angular frequency **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sinusoidal AC current has a maximum value of 20 A and a frequency of 50 Hz. It passes through a circuit for a time equal to its half-period. (i) Write the instantaneous current equation. (ii) Calculate the RMS value. (iii) Calculate the average value. (iv) If this AC current flows through a 10 Ω resistor for the half-period, find the total heat produced. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: ### Part (a) — Definitions with Labelled Sketch | Term | Definition | |------|-----------| | **(i) Instantaneous Value** | The value of the AC quantity at any particular instant of time. Denoted by lowercase letters: $v(t)$ or $i(t)$. | | **(ii) Peak Value** | The maximum value reached by the waveform in one cycle. Denoted by $V_m$ or $I_m$. Also called *Amplitude* or *Crest Value*. | | **(iii) Peak-to-Peak Value** | The total vertical distance from the positive peak to the negative peak. $V_{pp} = 2V_m$. | | **(iv) Time Period (T)** | The time in seconds required to complete one full cycle. Unit: seconds. | | **(v) Frequency (f)** | The number of complete cycles per second. Unit: Hertz (Hz). $f = 1/T$. | | **(vi) Angular Frequency (ω)** | The angle swept per second. Unit: rad/s. $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi/T$. | **Labelled Sketch (ASCII representation):** ``` +Vm • | ← Peak Value (V_m) |\ | | \ | ← Peak-to-Peak (V_pp = 2V_m) ----------+--\-----+--------→ t | \ | | \ | | \ | | \ | -Vm | • ← Negative Peak ← T/2 →←T/2→ ←———— T ————→ ``` ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** $I_m = 20$ A, $f = 50$ Hz, $R = 10$ Ω, time = $T/2$. **(i) Instantaneous current equation:** First, $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi(50) = 100\pi = 314$ rad/s. $$ \boxed{i(t) = 20 \sin(314 t) \text{ A}} $$ **(ii) RMS value:** $$ I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{2}} = 14.14 \text{ A} $$ **(iii) Average value:** $$ I_{avg} = \frac{2 I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 20}{3.1416} = 12.73 \text{ A} $$ **(iv) Heat produced in $R$ for half-period:** First, find the half-period: $$ \frac{T}{2} = \frac{1}{2f} = \frac{1}{2 \times 50} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms} $$ Heat produced is given by: $$ H = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R \cdot t $$ Substituting: $$ H = (14.14)^2 \times 10 \times 0.01 = 199.9 \times 0.1 = 19.99 \approx 20 \text{ J} $$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing instantaneous and peak values:** When asked for "voltage", always check if they mean $V(t)$, $V_m$, or $V_{rms}$. Many students write $V = 141.4$ V for the given $141.4 \sin(314t)$ — this is $V_m$, not the operating voltage. > 2. **Using full-cycle average:** The average of a *complete* sine cycle is **zero**. The "average value" in AC context **always** refers to the half-cycle average $= 0.637 V_m$. > 3. **Units of $\omega$:** Many students write $\omega = 50$ Hz (wrong). $\omega$ is in **rad/s**, while $f$ is in **Hz**. The relationship is $\omega = 2\pi f$. > 4. **Forgetting to square the current** in heat calculations: $H = I_{rms}^2 R t$, not $H = I_{rms} R t$. > 5. **Form Factor inversion:** Students sometimes write $K_f = V_{avg}/V_{rms}$. The correct form has **RMS in the numerator** and **Average in the denominator**. Loss of 1 mark if inverted. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):** - ⭐ **AC =** Current/voltage that alternates in magnitude **and** direction periodically. - ⭐ **Generation Principle:** Faraday's Law — a coil rotating in a magnetic field generates $e = E_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $E_m = N B A \omega$. - ⭐ **Time Period $T$:** Time for one complete cycle. **Unit: seconds.** - ⭐ **Frequency $f$:** Number of cycles per second. **Unit: Hz.** Relationship: $f = 1/T$. - ⭐ **Angular Frequency $\omega$:** $2\pi f$ rad/s. - ⭐ **Average Value (half-cycle):** $V_{avg} = 2V_m / \pi = 0.637 V_m$. **Never zero for half-cycle!** - ⭐ **RMS Value:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2} = 0.707 V_m$. Used for **all power and heating calculations**. - ⭐ **Form Factor:** $K_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg} = 1.11$ for a pure sine wave. - ⭐ **Peak Factor:** $K_p = V_m / V_{rms} = 1.414$ for a pure sine wave. - ⭐ **Mnemonic:** "**R**MS = **R**esults in **S**ame heat" (Root of Mean of Squares). - ⭐ **Domestic 230 V = RMS** value. Peak = $\sqrt{2} \times 230 \approx 325$ V. - ⭐ **Power formula uses RMS:** $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos(\phi)$. - ⭐ **All meter readings are RMS** by default (unless specified otherwise). - ⭐ **Half-cycle average** is asked 90% of the time in KTU exams — *not* the full-cycle average. - ⭐ **Default Indian mains:** $f = 50$ Hz, $V_{rms} = 230$ V (single-phase). Memorize this. > [!NOTE] > **Final Note:** This topic forms the foundation of every subsequent AC analysis module (Module 3, 4, 5). Mastering these five core formulas — **$E_m = N B A \omega$**, **$V_{avg} = 2V_m/\pi$**, **$V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$**, **$K_f = 1.11$**, **$K_p = 1.414$** — will give you the confidence to tackle any 3-mark, 7-mark, or 14-mark question in this segment of the KTU syllabus.

topics.title: AC Circuits: Phasor Representation of Sinusoidal Quantities, Trigonometric, Rectangular, Polar and Complex Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** AC Circuits: Phasor Representation of Sinusoidal Quantities, Trigonometric, Rectangular, Polar and Complex Forms # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Sinusoidal Quantity — Foundation of AC In Alternating Current (AC) circuits, the voltage and current vary **periodically** with time, most commonly in a **sinusoidal (sine or cosine) waveform**. A generalized sinusoidal quantity is mathematically expressed as: $$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$ $$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \theta)$$ where the symbols carry precise meanings: - $V_m$ and $I_m$ → **Maximum (Peak) amplitude** in Volts (V) and Amperes (A) - $\omega$ → **Angular frequency** in radians/second (rad/s), where $\omega = 2\pi f$ - $f$ → **Frequency** in Hertz (Hz) — cycles per second - $T$ → **Time period** in seconds, $T = 1/f$ - $\phi$ or $\theta$ → **Phase angle** in degrees or radians (defines waveform shift) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The **phase angle $\phi$** is measured with respect to a reference sinusoid (usually $\sin \omega t$). A **leading** quantity has $\phi > 0$ and a **lagging** quantity has $\phi < 0$. ## 1.2 What is a Phasor? > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Phasor** is a **complex number (or a rotating vector in the complex plane)** that represents a sinusoidal function's **magnitude** and **phase angle**, completely stripping away its **time-varying nature** to simplify AC circuit analysis. The instantaneous sinusoidal value $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ can be geometrically visualized as the **vertical (imaginary-axis) projection** of a vector of length $V_m$ rotating counter-clockwise at angular velocity $\omega$ in the complex plane. **Mathematically, the phasor is denoted as:** $$\mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi}$$ The complete instantaneous quantity is recovered as: $$v(t) = \text{Im}\{\mathbf{V} \cdot e^{j\omega t}\} = \text{Im}\{V_m e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}\}$$ ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Clock Hand 🕰️ > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a **clock hand** of fixed length attached to the center of a clock face. > - The **length of the hand** = the **amplitude $V_m$** of the sinusoid. > - The **angle the hand makes with 12 o'clock (horizontal axis)** at $t = 0$ = the **phase angle $\phi$**. > - The **speed of rotation** (once per cycle) = the **angular frequency $\omega$**. > > Now, if you **stand at the tip of the hand** and project a **shadow** onto the **vertical wall behind the clock**, that shadow's length **traces out a perfect sinusoid** as the hand rotates! > > The **Phasor is simply a "photograph" of the clock hand frozen in time** — it captures only the **length** and the **initial angle**, dropping the rotation. When we want the actual sinusoid back, we just "unfreeze" the phasor and start spinning it again. ## 1.4 Why Do We Need Phasors? — The Engineering Motivation | Without Phasors (Time Domain) | With Phasors (Frequency Domain) | |------------------------------|--------------------------------| | Solving **differential equations** (inductors, capacitors) | Simple **algebraic operations** (add, subtract, multiply, divide) | | Painful integration for every circuit | One-shot **Ohm's Law**: $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{Z}$ | | Tedious waveform sketching | Quick **phasor diagram** with vector arithmetic | | Hard to compare magnitudes & phases | **Direct magnitude and phase comparison** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Pearl:** A phasor is **NOT a physical vector** in space — it is a **mathematical construct** that lives in the **complex plane** and **encodes the steady-state sinusoidal response** of a circuit at a single frequency $\omega$. # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Standard Sinusoidal Parameters — The Building Blocks For $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$: ### 2.1.1 Angular Frequency and Period $$\omega = 2\pi f = \frac{2\pi}{T} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$ ### 2.1.2 Peak-to-Peak Value $$V_{pp} = 2 V_m$$ ### 2.1.3 RMS (Root Mean Square) Value — The Effective DC Equivalent $$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \cdot V_m$$ $$I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \cdot I_m$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** RMS value of a sinusoid is the **DC equivalent that delivers the same average power** to a resistor. This is the value shown on **AC voltmeters and ammeters**. ### 2.1.4 Average Value (over a complete cycle) For a pure sine wave over a **full cycle**: $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi) \, dt = 0$$ For **half-cycle average** (used in rectifier analysis): $$V_{avg,\text{half}} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \cdot V_m$$ ### 2.1.5 Form Factor and Peak (Crest) Factor $$\text{Form Factor} = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg,\text{half}}} = \frac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$$ $$\text{Peak (Crest) Factor} = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{V_m/\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$ ## 2.2 The Four Mathematical Forms of a Phasor A single sinusoidal quantity can be written in **four equivalent ways**. Each form is best suited for a specific operation. ### **Form 1: Trigonometric (Time-Domain) Form** The most basic — explicit time dependence: $$v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{or} \quad V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$ ### **Form 2: Rectangular (Cartesian / Algebraic) Form** Uses **real** and **imaginary** components on the complex plane: $$\mathbf{V} = a + jb$$ where $a = V_m \cos\phi$ and $b = V_m \sin\phi$, and $j = \sqrt{-1}$. ### **Form 3: Polar (Modulus–Argument) Form** Uses **magnitude** and **angle** — the **most commonly used in phasor analysis**: $$\mathbf{V} = V \angle \phi = V e^{j\phi}$$ where $V = \vert \mathbf{V} \vert$ and $\phi = \arg(\mathbf{V})$. ### **Form 4: Complex Exponential Form** The **bridge** between trigonometric and polar — uses **Euler's formula**: $$\mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi} = V_m \left(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi\right)$$ ## 2.3 The Master Conversion Table > [!TIP] > **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU exam question on phasors!** | From → To | Rectangular $(a + jb)$ | Polar $(V \angle \phi)$ | Exponential $(Ve^{j\phi})$ | Trigonometric $V_m(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$ | |-----------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------------------| | **Rectangular** $a + jb$ | — | $V = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$
$\phi = \tan^{-1}(b/a)$ | $V e^{j\phi}$ | $V_m\cos\phi + jV_m\sin\phi$ | | **Polar** $V\angle\phi$ | $a = V\cos\phi$
$b = V\sin\phi$ | — | $Ve^{j\phi}$ | $V(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$ | | **Exponential** $Ve^{j\phi}$ | $V\cos\phi + jV\sin\phi$ | $V \angle \phi$ | — | $V(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$ | | **Trigonometric** $V_m(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$ | $\cos\phi + j\sin\phi$ component-wise | $1\angle\phi$ | $e^{j\phi}$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quadrant Awareness Rule (KTU Examiner's Trap!):** When computing $\phi = \tan^{-1}(b/a)$, the angle lies in the **quadrant of $(a, b)$**, not blindly in the first quadrant. > - $a>0,\ b>0$ → Quadrant I → $\phi$ positive > - $a<0,\ b>0$ → Quadrant II → $\phi$ between $90°$ and $180°$ > - $a<0,\ b<0$ → Quadrant III → $\phi$ between $-180°$ and $-90°$ > - $a>0,\ b<0$ → Quadrant IV → $\phi$ negative ## 2.4 Operations on Phasors ### 2.4.1 Addition & Subtraction → Use **Rectangular** Form $$(a_1 + jb_1) + (a_2 + jb_2) = (a_1+a_2) + j(b_1+b_2)$$ ### 2.4.2 Multiplication → Use **Polar** Form $$V_1\angle\phi_1 \times V_2\angle\phi_2 = V_1 V_2 \angle (\phi_1 + \phi_2)$$ ### 2.4.3 Division → Use **Polar** Form $$\frac{V_1\angle\phi_1}{V_2\angle\phi_2} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} \angle (\phi_1 - \phi_2)$$ ## 2.5 Euler's Identity — The Engine of Phasor Algebra > [!NOTE] > **Euler's Formula (the most important identity in AC analysis):** > $$e^{j\phi} = \cos\phi + j\sin\phi$$ > > Special values: > - $e^{j0°} = 1$ > - $e^{j90°} = j$ (the **$j$-operator** rotates any vector by $+90°$) > - $e^{j180°} = -1$ > - $e^{-j90°} = -j$ (rotation by $-90°$) > - $e^{j\phi_1} \cdot e^{j\phi_2} = e^{j(\phi_1 + \phi_2)}$ ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Phasor Application | |-------|--------------------| | **Power Systems** | Load flow analysis, fault analysis, transmission line modeling | | **Electronics** | Frequency response of amplifiers (Bode plots) | | **Communication** | Modulation, demodulation, signal processing in frequency domain | | **Control Systems** | Transfer functions, stability analysis via Nyquist/Bode | | **Electric Machines** | Synchronous machine analysis, induction motor equivalent circuits | ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet 📋 | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes | |---|----------|---------|--------------| | 1 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | | 2 | Period | $T = 1/f = 2\pi/\omega$ | seconds | | 3 | Peak-to-Peak | $V_{pp} = 2V_m$ | Volts | | 4 | RMS value (sine) | $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ | Volts — **most used** | | 5 | Half-cycle average | $V_{avg} = 2V_m/\pi$ | Volts | | 6 | Form Factor | $FF = \pi/(2\sqrt{2}) = 1.11$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Crest Factor | $CF = \sqrt{2} = 1.414$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Rect → Polar (mag) | $V = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ | magnitude | | 9 | Rect → Polar (arg) | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(b/a)$ | watch **quadrant**! | | 10 | Polar → Rect (real) | $a = V\cos\phi$ | real part | | 11 | Polar → Rect (imag) | $b = V\sin\phi$ | imaginary part | | 12 | Euler | $e^{j\phi} = \cos\phi + j\sin\phi$ | core identity | | 13 | Phasor multiplication | $V_1\angle\phi_1 \times V_2\angle\phi_2 = V_1V_2\angle(\phi_1+\phi_2)$ | polar form | | 14 | Phasor division | $V_1\angle\phi_1 / V_2\angle\phi_2 = (V_1/V_2)\angle(\phi_1-\phi_2)$ | polar form | | 15 | Phasor rotation by $90°$ | multiply by $j$ | counter-clockwise | | 16 | Instantaneous recovery | $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | multiply phasor by $e^{j\omega t}$, take real/imag part | # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Sinusoid to Phasor (Euler's Foundation) We start with a real sinusoidal function $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. The key insight is to use **Euler's formula** to express this as the **real part of a complex exponential**: **Step 1:** Apply the angle-addition identity to $\cos(\omega t + \phi)$: $$\cos(\omega t + \phi) = \cos\omega t \cos\phi - \sin\omega t \sin\phi$$ **Step 2:** Recall Euler's formula for the exponential $e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}$: $$e^{j(\omega t + \phi)} = e^{j\omega t} \cdot e^{j\phi} = e^{j\omega t}(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$$ **Step 3:** Expand the product: $$e^{j(\omega t + \phi)} = (\cos\omega t + j\sin\omega t)(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$$ $$\begin{aligned} e^{j(\omega t + \phi)} &= \cos\omega t \cos\phi + j\cos\omega t \sin\phi \\ &\quad + j\sin\omega t \cos\phi + j^2 \sin\omega t \sin\phi \\ &= \cos\omega t \cos\phi - \sin\omega t \sin\phi \\ &\quad + j(\cos\omega t \sin\phi + \sin\omega t \cos\phi) \\ &= \cos(\omega t + \phi) + j\sin(\omega t + \phi) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Extract the real part: $$\text{Re}\{e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}\} = \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$ **Step 5:** Therefore: $$v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi) = \text{Re}\{V_m e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}\} = \text{Re}\{(V_m e^{j\phi}) \cdot e^{j\omega t}\}$$ **Step 6:** Define the **PHASOR** $\mathbf{V}$ as the **time-independent** complex amplitude: $$\boxed{\mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi} = V_m \angle \phi}$$ **Step 7:** The complete reconstruction is: $$v(t) = \text{Re}\{\mathbf{V} \cdot e^{j\omega t}\}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Conceptual Conclusion:** A phasor **is** the complex amplitude $V_m e^{j\phi}$. The factor $e^{j\omega t}$ is the **rotational operator** that spins the phasor at angular frequency $\omega$. The instantaneous sinusoid is the **real-axis projection** of this spinning phasor. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example #1: Rectangular to Polar Conversion **Problem:** Convert the phasor $\mathbf{V} = 40 + j30 \text{ V}$ to **polar form**. **Step 1 — Calculate the magnitude (modulus):** $$\begin{aligned} V &= \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} \\ &= \sqrt{40^2 + 30^2} \\ &= \sqrt{1600 + 900} \\ &= \sqrt{2500} \\ &= 50 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the phase angle (argument):** $$\begin{aligned} \phi &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \\ &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{30}{40}\right) \\ &= \tan^{-1}(0.75) \\ &= 36.87° \text{ (Quadrant I, since } a>0, b>0\text{)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Express in polar form:** $$\boxed{\mathbf{V} = 50 \angle 36.87° \text{ V}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example #2: Polar to Rectangular Conversion **Problem:** Convert the phasor $\mathbf{I} = 10 \angle -60°$ A to **rectangular form**. **Step 1 — Identify magnitude and angle:** $$I = 10 \text{ A}, \quad \phi = -60°$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the real part:** $$\begin{aligned} a &= I \cos\phi \\ &= 10 \cdot \cos(-60°) \\ &= 10 \cdot 0.5 \\ &= 5 \text{ A} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate the imaginary part:** $$\begin{aligned} b &= I \sin\phi \\ &= 10 \cdot \sin(-60°) \\ &= 10 \cdot (-0.8660) \\ &= -8.660 \text{ A} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Express in rectangular form:** $$\boxed{\mathbf{I} = 5 - j8.660 \text{ A}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Example #3: Phasor Multiplication (Using Polar Form) **Problem:** Multiply $\mathbf{V}_1 = 50 \angle 30°$ and $\mathbf{V}_2 = 10 \angle -45°$. **Step 1 — Use polar multiplication rule** (multiply magnitudes, add angles): $$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{V}_1 \cdot \mathbf{V}_2 &= (50 \times 10) \angle (30° + (-45°)) \\ &= 500 \angle -15° \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Verification by converting to rectangular first:** $$\mathbf{V}_1 = 50\cos30° + j50\sin30° = 43.30 + j25.00$$ $$\mathbf{V}_2 = 10\cos(-45°) + j10\sin(-45°) = 7.071 - j7.071$$ $$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{V}_1 \cdot \mathbf{V}_2 &= (43.30 + j25.00)(7.071 - j7.071) \\ &= 43.30 \cdot 7.071 - 43.30 \cdot j7.071 + j25.00 \cdot 7.071 - j^2 \cdot 25.00 \cdot 7.071 \\ &= 306.20 - j306.20 + j176.78 + 176.78 \\ &= (306.20 + 176.78) + j(-306.20 + 176.78) \\ &= 482.98 - j129.42 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Convert result back to polar:** $$V = \sqrt{482.98^2 + 129.42^2} = \sqrt{233,270 + 16,750} = \sqrt{250,020} \approx 500$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}(-129.42 / 482.98) = \tan^{-1}(-0.268) \approx -15°$$ **Confirmed:** $\mathbf{V}_1 \cdot \mathbf{V}_2 = 500 \angle -15°$ ✓ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example #4: Phasor Division (Using Polar Form) **Problem:** Divide $\mathbf{V}_1 = 100 \angle 60°$ by $\mathbf{V}_2 = 20 \angle 20°$. **Step 1 — Apply polar division rule** (divide magnitudes, subtract angles): $$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathbf{V}_1}{\mathbf{V}_2} &= \frac{100}{20} \angle (60° - 20°) \\ &= 5 \angle 40° \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Verification by converting to rectangular and using conjugate method:** Numerator: $100\cos60° + j100\sin60° = 50 + j86.60$ Denominator: $20\cos20° + j20\sin20° = 18.794 + j6.840$ Multiply numerator and denominator by conjugate of denominator: $$\frac{(50 + j86.60)(18.794 - j6.840)}{(18.794)^2 + (6.840)^2}$$ Denominator magnitude squared: $353.21 + 46.79 = 400.00$ Numerator expansion: $$\begin{aligned} &= 50 \cdot 18.794 - 50 \cdot j6.840 + j86.60 \cdot 18.794 - j^2 \cdot 86.60 \cdot 6.840 \\ &= 939.70 - j342.00 + j1627.78 + 592.34 \\ &= (939.70 + 592.34) + j(-342.00 + 1627.78) \\ &= 1532.04 + j1285.78 \end{aligned}$$ Final result: $\frac{1532.04 + j1285.78}{400} = 3.83 + j3.215$ Converting to polar: $\sqrt{3.83^2 + 3.215^2} = \sqrt{14.67 + 10.34} = \sqrt{25.01} \approx 5$ Phase: $\tan^{-1}(3.215/3.83) = \tan^{-1}(0.839) \approx 40°$ **Confirmed:** $\mathbf{V}_1 / \mathbf{V}_2 = 5 \angle 40°$ ✓ --- ## 3.6 Worked Example #5: Complete Conversion Chain + Time-Domain Recovery **Problem:** Given $v(t) = 141.4 \sin(314t + 60°)$ V, express this in **(a)** exponential form, **(b)** rectangular form, and **(c)** polar form, and reconstruct $v(t)$ from the phasor. ### (a) Exponential Form $$\mathbf{V} = 141.4 \, e^{j60°} \text{ V}$$ ### (b) Rectangular Form $$\begin{aligned} a &= 141.4 \cos 60° = 141.4 \times 0.5 = 70.7 \\ b &= 141.4 \sin 60° = 141.4 \times 0.866 = 122.47 \end{aligned}$$ $$\mathbf{V} = 70.7 + j122.47 \text{ V}$$ ### (c) Polar Form $$V = \sqrt{70.7^2 + 122.47^2} = \sqrt{4998.49 + 14998.9} = \sqrt{19997.39} \approx 141.4$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}(122.47/70.7) = \tan^{-1}(1.732) = 60°$$ $$\mathbf{V} = 141.4 \angle 60° \text{ V}$$ ### (d) Reconstruction $$v(t) = \text{Re}\{\mathbf{V} \cdot e^{j\omega t}\} = \text{Re}\{141.4 \, e^{j60°} \cdot e^{j314t}\} = 141.4 \cos(314t + 60°)$$ **Note:** The original was in **sine**, the reconstruction in **cosine** — they differ by $90°$ in phase. The phasor form is $\sin$, so it would be $\mathbf{V} = 141.4 \angle 60°$ but the time recovery is $141.4 \sin(314t + 60°)$. --- ## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix: "Which Form Should I Use?" | Operation | Recommended Form | Reason | |-----------|------------------|--------| | Adding/Subtracting phasors | **Rectangular** | Real and imaginary parts add independently | | Multiplying/Dividing phasors | **Polar** | Multiplication = scale + rotate; division = scale + counter-rotate | | Computing power ($P = VI\cos\theta$) | **Polar** (with phase difference) | Need magnitude of $V$, $I$ and $\cos(\phi_V - \phi_I)$ | | Drawing a phasor diagram | **Polar** (as vectors) | Geometric clarity in the complex plane | | Resolving a vector into components | **Polar → Rectangular** | Easy projection onto axes | | Expressing a single sinusoid | **Exponential** | Compact, mathematically elegant | # 📐 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Phasor as a Rotating Vector in the Complex Plane The following Mermaid diagram shows how a phasor is geometrically constructed from a sinusoid: ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Sinusoidal Quantity
v of t = Vm sin omega t plus phi] --> B[Plot in Time Domain
Amplitude vs Time] B --> C[Freeze the Wave at t = 0
Capture only Magnitude and Phase] C --> D[Create Rotating Vector
Length = Vm, Angle = phi] D --> E[Vector lives in Complex Plane
Real Axis = Horizontal
Imaginary Axis = Vertical] E --> F[Project onto Imaginary Axis
at time t] F --> G[Sinusoid Reconstructed
v of t = Im V times e power j omega t] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#E0F6FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#008000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** The flowchart illustrates the conceptual journey: a **time-domain sinusoid** → **snapshot at $t=0$** → **rotating vector (phasor)** in the complex plane → **projection** back to sinusoid. --- ## 4.2 The Four Forms of Phasor Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR T[Trigonometric Form
Vm cos omega t plus phi] -->|Apply Euler| E[Exponential Form
Vm e power j phi] E -->|Re and Im parts| R[Rectangular Form
a plus jb] E -->|Mod and Arg| P[Polar Form
V angle phi] R -->|atan2 by a| P P -->|V cos phi V sin phi| R R -.->|V at angle phi| T style T fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#E0F6FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** All four phasor forms are mathematically equivalent. **Arrows** show the standard conversion paths — trigonometric ↔ exponential (via Euler), exponential ↔ rectangular (via components), exponential ↔ polar (via modulus/argument). --- ## 4.3 Phasor Diagram — Geometric Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Complex_Plane[COMPLEX PLANE] direction TB O[Origin] ---|Real Axis| RealPoint[Positive Real Axis] O ---|Imaginary Axis| ImagPoint[Positive Imaginary Axis] end O -->|Vector of magnitude V| P1[Phasor V at angle phi] P1 -->|Component a on real axis| RealPoint P1 -->|Component b on imag axis| ImagPoint Note1[Phasor in 1st Quadrant
phi is positive between 0 and 90] Note2[Real part a = V cos phi
Imag part b = V sin phi] Note3[Magnitude V = sqrt a sq plus b sq
Angle phi = atan b by a] style O fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style RealPoint fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style ImagPoint fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style Note1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style Note2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style Note3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** Shows how a phasor in the first quadrant projects onto the real and imaginary axes. The real component is $V\cos\phi$ and the imaginary component is $V\sin\phi$. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Sinusoid ↔ Phasor Transformation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([Sinusoidal Source
v of t = Vm sin omega t plus phi]) --> Step1[Step 1: Identify Parameters
Vm, omega, phi] Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Construct Complex Number
V = Vm e power j phi] Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Choose Representation Form] Step3 --> Form1[Rectangular
a plus jb] Step3 --> Form2[Polar
V angle phi] Step3 --> Form3[Exponential
V e power j phi] Form1 --> Op1[Add or Subtract
with other phasors] Form2 --> Op2[Multiply or Divide
with other phasors] Form3 --> Op3[Use in Euler formula
for time recovery] Op1 --> Recon[Reconstruct v of t
multiply by e power j omega t
take real part] Op2 --> Recon Op3 --> Recon Recon --> Out([Instantaneous Output
v of t in volts]) style Start fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Step1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Step2 fill:#E0F6FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Step3 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Form1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Form2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Form3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Recon fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** A **block-level functional architecture flow** showing the complete pipeline: from a sinusoidal input, through parameter identification, phasor construction, form selection, phasor arithmetic operations, to final reconstruction of the time-domain signal. --- ## 4.5 Quadrant Rule Diagram for Angle Calculation ```mermaid flowchart TB Start{Given a and b
What is phi?} --> Q1[a greater than 0 and b greater than 0] Start --> Q2[a less than 0 and b greater than 0] Start --> Q3[a less than 0 and b less than 0] Start --> Q4[a greater than 0 and b less than 0] Start --> SA[a equals 0 and b greater than 0] Start --> SB[a equals 0 and b less than 0] Start --> SC[b equals 0 and a greater than 0] Start --> SD[b equals 0 and a less than 0] Q1 --> A1[Quadrant I
phi = atan b by a] Q2 --> A2[Quadrant II
phi = 180 minus atan abs b by abs a] Q3 --> A3[Quadrant III
phi = minus 180 plus atan abs b by abs a] Q4 --> A4[Quadrant IV
phi = minus atan abs b by a] SA --> A5[phi equals 90 degrees] SB --> A6[phi equals minus 90 degrees] SC --> A7[phi equals 0 degrees] SD --> A8[phi equals 180 or minus 180 degrees] style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style A1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style A2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style A3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style A4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style A5 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style A6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style A7 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style A8 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** A decision tree for the quadrant-aware angle calculation — a critical concept for KTU exams where students often lose marks by blindly applying $\tan^{-1}(b/a)$ without considering the sign of $a$. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] > **Define RMS value and average value of an AC sinusoidal waveform. Find the RMS value of $v(t) = 100 \sin(100\pi t + 30°)$ V.** **Model Answer:** **RMS Value Definition:** The RMS (Root Mean Square) value of an AC waveform is the **DC equivalent that produces the same heating effect (or power dissipation)** in a resistor. **Average Value Definition:** The average value of a sinusoid over a **complete cycle** is **zero** (positive and negative halves cancel). Over a **half-cycle**, it is the mean of the absolute values. **Calculation:** $$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{2}} = 70.71 \text{ V}$$ The angular frequency and phase angle **do not affect** the RMS value — only $V_m$ matters. **Final Answer:** $\boxed{V_{rms} = 70.71 \text{ V}}$ --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024] > **Convert the phasor $\mathbf{V} = 10 + j17.32$ into polar form and write its time-domain sinusoidal representation if $\omega = 100\pi$ rad/s.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Magnitude:** $$V = \sqrt{10^2 + 17.32^2} = \sqrt{100 + 299.98} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Phase angle** (Quadrant I, since $a>0$, $b>0$): $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{17.32}{10}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.732) = 60°$$ **Step 3 — Polar form:** $$\mathbf{V} = 20 \angle 60° \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Time-domain representation:** $$v(t) = 20 \cos(100\pi t + 60°) \text{ V}$$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{\mathbf{V} = 20 \angle 60° \text{ V}, \quad v(t) = 20\cos(100\pi t + 60°) \text{ V}}$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions in ESE carry 14 marks with **module-level internal choice** (you must answer EITHER OR from the same module). Each part (a) and (b) typically carries 7 marks. --- ### **Question B1 — Option A** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] > **(a)** Derive the relationship between the trigonometric form and the complex exponential (Euler) form of a phasor, starting from Euler's formula. **\[7 Marks\]** > > **(b)** Two phasors are given as $\mathbf{V}_1 = 30 + j40$ V and $\mathbf{V}_2 = 50 - j20$ V. Compute (i) $\mathbf{V}_1 + \mathbf{V}_2$ and (ii) $\mathbf{V}_1 \cdot \mathbf{V}_2$ in polar form. **\[7 Marks\]** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — State Euler's formula:** $$e^{j\phi} = \cos\phi + j\sin\phi$$ **Step 2 — Express a general sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ using Euler:** $$v(t) = \text{Re}\{V_m e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}\}$$ **Step 3 — Separate the time-dependent and time-independent parts:** $$v(t) = \text{Re}\{(V_m e^{j\phi}) \cdot e^{j\omega t}\}$$ **Step 4 — Define the phasor $\mathbf{V}$:** $$\mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi} \quad \text{(complex exponential / phasor form)}$$ **Step 5 — Equivalently, expand using Euler:** $$\mathbf{V} = V_m(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi) = V_m\cos\phi + jV_m\sin\phi$$ This is the **rectangular form**, equivalent to the **trigonometric representation** $V_m(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$. **Step 6 — The complete identity:** $$\boxed{V_m e^{j\phi} \equiv V_m(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi) \equiv V_m\angle\phi}$$ #### **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Sum in rectangular form:** $$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{V}_1 + \mathbf{V}_2 &= (30 + j40) + (50 - j20) \\ &= (30+50) + j(40-20) \\ &= 80 + j20 \end{aligned}$$ Convert to polar: $$V = \sqrt{80^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{6400 + 400} = \sqrt{6800} = 82.46 \text{ V}$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}(20/80) = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04°$$ $$\boxed{\mathbf{V}_1 + \mathbf{V}_2 = 82.46 \angle 14.04° \text{ V}}$$ **(ii) Product — first convert to polar:** $\mathbf{V}_1 = 30 + j40$: $$V_1 = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50$$ $$\phi_1 = \tan^{-1}(40/30) = \tan^{-1}(1.333) = 53.13°$$ $$\mathbf{V}_1 = 50 \angle 53.13°$$ $\mathbf{V}_2 = 50 - j20$: $$V_2 = \sqrt{50^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 400} = \sqrt{2900} = 53.85$$ $$\phi_2 = \tan^{-1}(-20/50) = \tan^{-1}(-0.4) = -21.80°$$ $$\mathbf{V}_2 = 53.85 \angle -21.80°$$ Now multiply (magnitudes multiply, angles add): $$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{V}_1 \cdot \mathbf{V}_2 &= (50 \times 53.85) \angle (53.13° + (-21.80°)) \\ &= 2692.5 \angle 31.33° \text{ V}^2 \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\mathbf{V}_1 \cdot \mathbf{V}_2 \approx 2692.58 \angle 31.33° \text{ V}^2}$$ --- ### **Question B1 — Option B** [KTU University Exam - June 2024] > **(a)** Explain the concept of phasor representation of a sinusoidal quantity with a neat diagram. Why is phasor analysis preferred over direct time-domain analysis for AC circuits? **\[7 Marks\]** > > **(b)** A sinusoidal voltage is given by $v(t) = 200 \sin(1000t + 45°)$ V. Express it in **(i)** exponential form, **(ii)** rectangular form, **(iii)** polar form, and **(iv)** find the RMS value. **\[7 Marks\]** #### **Model Solution for (a):** A sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ can be represented as the **imaginary part of a rotating complex vector** of length $V_m$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ in the complex plane. The "snapshot" of this vector at $t=0$ is the **phasor** $\mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi$. **Why phasor analysis is preferred:** 1. **Differential equations become algebraic** — For an inductor $v = L \, di/dt$, in phasor form it becomes $\mathbf{V} = j\omega L \cdot \mathbf{I}$ — a simple algebraic relation. 2. **Sinusoidal sources at the same frequency can be added/subtracted as vectors** — No need to do trigonometric addition. 3. **Impedance concept unifies R, L, C** — All three elements obey $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{Z} \cdot \mathbf{I}$, where $\mathbf{Z}_R = R$, $\mathbf{Z}_L = j\omega L$, $\mathbf{Z}_C = 1/(j\omega C)$. 4. **Kirchhoff's laws hold for phasors** — Just like in DC, but with complex numbers. 5. **Power calculations become simple** — $P = VI\cos\theta$ uses only magnitudes and phase difference. **Conclusion:** Phasor analysis reduces AC circuit analysis to **DC-like algebra in the complex domain**, which is computationally far more efficient and conceptually cleaner. #### **Model Solution for (b):** Given: $v(t) = 200 \sin(1000t + 45°)$ V, so $V_m = 200$ V, $\omega = 1000$ rad/s, $\phi = 45°$. **(i) Exponential form:** $$\mathbf{V} = 200 \, e^{j45°} \text{ V}$$ **(ii) Rectangular form:** $$a = 200 \cos 45° = 200 \times 0.7071 = 141.42 \text{ V}$$ $$b = 200 \sin 45° = 200 \times 0.7071 = 141.42 \text{ V}$$ $$\mathbf{V} = 141.42 + j141.42 \text{ V}$$ **(iii) Polar form:** $$V = 200, \quad \phi = 45°$$ $$\mathbf{V} = 200 \angle 45° \text{ V}$$ **(iv) RMS value:** $$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{200}{1.414} = 141.42 \text{ V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interesting observation:** The **real part of the rectangular form** $(141.42 \text{ V})$ is **exactly equal to the RMS value** — this is **not a coincidence** for a $45°$ phase! In general, $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ regardless of phase. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 Most Common Mark-Loss Scenarios in Phasor Questions (KTU 2024):** > > 1. **Quadrant Blindness (–2 marks):** Computing $\phi = \tan^{-1}(b/a)$ without checking the quadrant. Example: For $-3 + j4$, the angle is $180° - 53.13° = 126.87°$, **NOT** $-53.13°$. > > 2. **Mixing sine and cosine (–2 marks):** The phasor form is conventionally derived from the **cosine** reference. A $\sin$ function must be converted: $\sin(\omega t) = \cos(\omega t - 90°)$. Forgetting this introduces a $90°$ error. > > 3. **Forgetting the $j$ in rectangular form (–1 mark):** The imaginary part must always carry the $j$ operator — writing "$3 + 4$" instead of "$3 + j4$" loses a mark. > > 4. **Adding phasors in polar form (–2 marks):** You **cannot** add magnitudes and angles directly. Always convert to **rectangular**, add, then convert back to polar if needed. > > 5. **Forgetting to include $\omega t$ in time-domain recovery (–1 mark):** The phasor is only a **snapshot**. The full sinusoid needs $e^{j\omega t}$ multiplied back in. > > 6. **Using peak value where RMS is needed (–1 mark):** AC voltmeter readings, power calculations, and "rated" values are RMS. State peak values explicitly as $V_m$ or $V_{max}$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!NOTE] > **🚀 High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE** - ✅ A sinusoid $v(t) = V_m\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ has three parameters: **amplitude $V_m$**, **angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$**, and **phase angle $\phi$**. - ✅ A **phasor** captures only $V_m$ and $\phi$ — it is a **time-independent complex number** representing the sinusoid. - ✅ The **four equivalent forms** of a phasor are: **Trigonometric**, **Exponential (Euler)**, **Rectangular (Cartesian)**, and **Polar (modulus-argument)**. - ✅ The **master conversion formulas**: - Rect → Polar: $V = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(b/a)$ (watch **quadrant**!) - Polar → Rect: $a = V\cos\phi$, $b = V\sin\phi$ - ✅ **Euler's identity** $e^{j\phi} = \cos\phi + j\sin\phi$ is the **bridge** between all forms. - ✅ The $j$-operator rotates a vector by **$+90°$** counter-clockwise: $j \cdot e^{j\phi} = e^{j(\phi+90°)}$. - ✅ **Addition/Subtraction** of phasors → use **rectangular** form. - ✅ **Multiplication/Division** of phasors → use **polar** form. - ✅ **RMS value** of a sinusoid: $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071 \, V_m$. - ✅ **Half-cycle average**: $V_{avg} = 2V_m/\pi \approx 0.637 \, V_m$. - ✅ **Form factor** (sine): $\pi/(2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.11$. **Crest factor**: $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. - ✅ The instantaneous value is recovered as: $v(t) = \text{Re}\{\mathbf{V} e^{j\omega t}\}$ for cosine reference, or $\text{Im}\{\mathbf{V} e^{j\omega t}\}$ for sine reference. - ✅ Phasor analysis **only works for steady-state, single-frequency** sinusoids of the **same frequency**. - ✅ **Leading phasor**: drawn counter-clockwise (positive angle). **Lagging phasor**: drawn clockwise (negative angle). - ✅ For converting $\sin$ to phasor: $\sin(\omega t + \phi) \rightarrow \phi - 90°$ in cosine reference, OR use sine phasor convention. - ✅ A phasor is **NOT a physical vector** — it is a **mathematical tool** for AC analysis. - ✅ The phasor **magnitude** can be either **peak ($V_m$)** or **RMS ($V_{rms}$)** depending on convention — be consistent and always state which!

topics.title: Analysis of Simple AC Circuits: Purely Resistive, Inductive and Capacitive Circuits, Inductive and Capacitive Reactance, Concept of Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Simple AC Circuits: Purely Resistive, Inductive and Capacitive Circuits, Inductive and Capacitive Reactance, Concept of Impedance # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an AC Circuit? An **Alternating Current (AC) circuit** is one in which the source voltage (and consequently the current) reverses polarity periodically with time, typically following a sinusoidal waveform. The standard mathematical representation of a sinusoidal AC quantity is: $$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi_v)$$ $$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi_i)$$ where **V_m** and **I_m** are the maximum (peak) values, **ω = 2πf** is the angular frequency in rad/s, **f** is the supply frequency in Hertz (Hz), and **φ_v** and **φ_i** are the phase angles of voltage and current respectively. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Indian power grid operates at a standard frequency of **f = 50 Hz** with an angular frequency **ω = 314.159 rad/s**. This is the default value to be used in all numerical problems unless explicitly stated otherwise. ## 1.2 The Three Pure Circuit Elements In AC analysis, the three fundamental passive elements exhibit distinctly different behaviors. Unlike DC, where Ohm's law alone governs the relationship between V and I, in AC circuits the **phase relationship** between voltage and current becomes equally important as their magnitudes. ### A. Purely Resistive (R) Circuit A circuit containing only a resistor **R** (in Ohms, Ω) connected to an AC source. The voltage and current are **in phase** (zero phase difference). ### B. Purely Inductive (L) Circuit A circuit containing only an inductor **L** (in Henry, H). The voltage **leads** the current by **90°** (π/2 radians). Energy is stored and released in the magnetic field surrounding the inductor. ### C. Purely Capacitive (C) Circuit A circuit containing only a capacitor **C** (in Farad, F). The current **leads** the voltage by **90°** (π/2 radians). Energy is stored and released in the electric field between the capacitor plates. ## 1.3 Reactance — The AC Opposition **Reactance** is the opposition offered by an inductor or capacitor to the flow of alternating current. Unlike resistance, reactance does **not dissipate energy**; it only stores and returns it to the source.
