Reactance is measured in **Ohms (Ω)**. - **Inductive Reactance:** $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ - **Capacitive Reactance:** $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ ## 1.4 Concept of Impedance **Impedance (Z)** is the **total opposition** that a circuit presents to alternating current. It is a complex quantity that combines both **resistance (R)** and **reactance (X)**: $$Z = R + jX$$ The magnitude of impedance is $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ and its phase angle is $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Impedance vs. Resistance — The Core Distinction:** > - **Resistance (R)** is a *real* quantity, dissipates energy as heat, and is independent of frequency. > - **Impedance (Z)** is a *complex* quantity, encompasses both energy dissipation (R) and energy storage (X), and is frequency-dependent. ## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **The Water-Pipe Analogy:** Imagine pushing water through a pipe system. > - A **narrow pipe** (Resistance) restricts flow regardless of how hard you push. > - A **heavy flywheel** (Inductor) resists changes in flow speed — it takes effort to start and stop, but once moving, it conserves momentum. > - A **spring-loaded valve** (Capacitor) must first be "charged" (filled with pressure) before flow can pass, and it releases that stored pressure back. > > In an AC circuit, voltage acts like **pressure** and current like **flow rate**. The flywheel effect is **inductance**, the spring effect is **capacitance**, and the pipe friction is **resistance**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Analysis of a Purely Resistive AC Circuit **Circuit Setup:** A resistor R is connected across an AC voltage source. **Step-by-Step Behavior:** - The applied voltage: $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ - Applying Ohm's Law (which holds instantaneously for a resistor): $i(t) = v(t)/R$ - Therefore: $i(t) = \dfrac{V_m}{R}\sin(\omega t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ **Key Observations:** - Voltage and current are **in phase** ($\phi = 0°$). - Peak current: $I_m = V_m / R$ - RMS current: $I_{rms} = V_{rms} / R$ - **Power dissipated:** $P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R$ (only the resistor consumes real power) - **Power factor:** $\cos\phi = \cos 0° = 1$ (Unity) ## 2.2 Analysis of a Purely Inductive AC Circuit **Circuit Setup:** An inductor L is connected across an AC voltage source. **Step-by-Step Behavior:** - Applied voltage: $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ - The fundamental inductor relation: $v(t) = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ - Integrating to get current: $i(t) = \dfrac{1}{L}\int v(t)\, dt$ The result yields a current that **lags** the voltage by exactly **90°** (π/2 rad). **Key Observations:** - Phase angle: $\phi = 90°$ (voltage leads current) - Peak current: $I_m = V_m / X_L$ - **Inductive Reactance:** $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ - **Power consumed:** $P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos(90°) = 0$ (purely reactive — no real power dissipation) - **Power factor:** $\cos 90° = 0$ (Zero) - The inductor alternately **absorbs** energy (during quarter cycle) and **returns** it to the source (during next quarter cycle). ## 2.3 Analysis of a Purely Capacitive AC Circuit **Circuit Setup:** A capacitor C is connected across an AC voltage source. **Step-by-Step Behavior:** - Applied voltage: $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ - The fundamental capacitor relation: $i(t) = C \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ - Differentiating to get current yields a current that **leads** the voltage by exactly **90°**. **Key Observations:** - Phase angle: $\phi = -90°$ (current leads voltage) - Peak current: $I_m = V_m / X_C$ - **Capacitive Reactance:** $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ - **Power consumed:** $P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos(-90°) = 0$ (purely reactive) - **Power factor:** $\cos(-90°) = 0$ (Zero) - The capacitor alternately **stores** energy in its electric field and **returns** it to the source. ## 2.4 Frequency Dependence — The Key Differentiator A critical insight that KTU frequently tests: - **$X_L$ increases linearly with frequency** → Inductors block high frequencies. - **$X_C$ decreases inversely with frequency** → Capacitors block low frequencies and pass high frequencies. - **R is independent of frequency.** This property is the foundation of **filters, tuned circuits, oscillators, and frequency-selective networks** used in every modern communication system. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Purely Resistive (R) | Purely Inductive (L) | Purely Capacitive (C) | |:---------|:---------------------|:---------------------|:----------------------| | **Opposition** | $R$ (Ω) | $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ (Ω) | $X_C = 1/(\omega C) = 1/(2\pi f C)$ (Ω) | | **Voltage–Current Relation** | $V = IR$ | $V = I X_L$ | $V = I X_C$ | | **Phase of V w.r.t. I** | In phase (0°) | Leads by 90° | Lags by 90° | | **Phase of I w.r.t. V** | In phase (0°) | Lags by 90° | Leads by 90° | | **Phasor Impedance Z** | $R$ (real) | $j X_L$ (positive imaginary) | $-j X_C$ (negative imaginary) | | **Frequency Behavior** | Independent of f | Increases with f | Decreases with f | | **DC Behavior (f = 0)** | Behaves as resistor | Short circuit (X_L = 0) | Open circuit (X_C → ∞) | | **Average Power P** | $V_{rms} I_{rms} = I_{rms}^2 R$ | 0 (energy stored & returned) | 0 (energy stored & returned) | | **Reactive Power Q** | 0 VAR | $V_{rms} I_{rms}$ (VAR) | $V_{rms} I_{rms}$ (VAR) | | **Power Factor** | 1 (Unity) | 0 (Zero, lagging) | 0 (Zero, leading) | | **Energy Stored Form** | Heat (dissipated) | Magnetic field | Electric field | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where This Knowledge is Applied:** > - **Power Transmission Grids:** Understanding reactance is essential for managing voltage drop and power factor in long transmission lines. > - **Transformer Design:** Inductance is the operating principle; leakage reactance affects voltage regulation. > - **Filter Circuits (High-pass, Low-pass):** R, L, C combinations form the building blocks of every audio and RF system. > - **Power Factor Correction (PFC):** Industrial consumers add capacitors to cancel inductive reactance from motors, reducing line losses. > - **Resonant Circuits:** Tuning radio receivers, wireless charging, and medical imaging (MRI) all rely on L-C resonance. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Voltage–Current Relationship for a Pure Inductor **Starting Point:** The defining equation for an ideal inductor relates the voltage across it to the time-rate of change of current: $$v(t) = L \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 1:** Assume the current through the inductor is sinusoidal: $$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the time derivative of the current: $$\frac{di(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[I_m \sin(\omega t)\right] = I_m \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute back into the inductor equation: $$v(t) = L \cdot I_m \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4:** Rewrite cosine as a shifted sine using the identity $\cos(\theta) = \sin(\theta + \pi/2)$: $$v(t) = \omega L \cdot I_m \cdot \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Recognize that the peak voltage is $V_m = \omega L \cdot I_m$, leading to: $$V_m = \omega L \cdot I_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_L = \frac{V_m}{I_m} = \omega L = 2\pi f L$$ **Conclusion:** The voltage across an ideal inductor **leads** the current by exactly **90° (π/2 rad)**, and the inductive reactance is **$X_L = \omega L$**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage–Current Relationship for a Pure Capacitor **Starting Point:** The defining equation for an ideal capacitor relates the current to the time-rate of change of voltage: $$i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dv(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 1:** Assume the voltage across the capacitor is sinusoidal: $$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the time derivative of the voltage: $$\frac{dv(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[V_m \sin(\omega t)\right] = V_m \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the capacitor equation: $$i(t) = C \cdot V_m \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t) = \omega C \cdot V_m \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4:** Rewrite as a phase-shifted sine: $i(t) = \omega C \cdot V_m \cdot \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ **Step 5:** Identify the peak current $I_m = \omega C \cdot V_m$, and solve for the ratio: $$X_C = \frac{V_m}{I_m} = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$$ **Conclusion:** The current through an ideal capacitor **leads** the voltage by exactly **90° (π/2 rad)**, and the capacitive reactance is **$X_C = 1/(\omega C)$**. ## 3.3 Derivation: Average Power in a Purely Reactive Element **Starting Point:** Instantaneous power delivered to any element is $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$. **Step 1:** For a purely inductive circuit, $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \pi/2) = -I_m \cos(\omega t)$. **Step 2:** Multiply to get instantaneous power: $$p(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \left[-I_m \cos(\omega t)\right] = -V_m I_m \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity: $\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta)$ $$p(t) = -\frac{V_m I_m}{2}\sin(2\omega t)$$ **Step 4:** The average value of a pure sinusoid over a complete cycle is **zero**: $$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T p(t)\, dt = -\frac{V_m I_m}{2T}\int_0^T \sin(2\omega t)\, dt = 0$$ **Conclusion:** A pure inductor (or capacitor) consumes **zero average power**. Energy is exchanged back and forth with the source, but no net energy is dissipated. ## 3.4 Derivation: Magnitude and Phase of Impedance **Starting Point:** For a series R–L circuit, by Kirchhoff's Voltage Law, the applied voltage phasor is the sum of the individual voltage phasors across R and L: $$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{V_R} + \mathbf{V_L}$$ **Step 1:** Express each voltage phasor in terms of the current phasor I: $$\mathbf{V_R} = I \cdot R \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{V_L} = I \cdot (j\omega L) = I \cdot (j X_L)$$ **Step 2:** Substituting back: $$\mathbf{V} = I \cdot R + I \cdot (j X_L) = I \cdot \left[R + j X_L\right]$$ **Step 3:** Define the complex impedance: $$\mathbf{Z} = \frac{\mathbf{V}}{\mathbf{I}} = R + j X_L$$ **Step 4:** Convert from rectangular to polar form: $$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right)$$ **Step 5:** The complete impedance in polar form is: $$\mathbf{Z} = \vert Z \vert \angle \phi = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} \;\angle\; \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right)$$ **Conclusion:** Impedance is a **complex (phasor) quantity** whose magnitude gives the voltage-to-current ratio and whose angle gives the phase shift between V and I. ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A series circuit consists of $R = 30\,\Omega$ and $L = 0.159\,\text{H}$ connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply. Find (a) the inductive reactance, (b) the impedance, (c) the RMS current, and (d) the phase angle. **Solution:** **(a) Inductive Reactance:** $$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 0.159 \approx 49.95 \;\Omega \approx 50\;\Omega$$ **(b) Impedance (magnitude):** $$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 50^2} = \sqrt{900 + 2500} = \sqrt{3400} \approx 58.31\;\Omega$$ **(c) RMS Current:** $$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{230}{58.31} \approx 3.94\;\text{A}$$ **(d) Phase Angle:** $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{50}{30}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.667) \approx 59.04°$$ The current **lags** the voltage by approximately **59°**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Phasor Diagram: Pure R Circuit The phasor diagram visually represents the magnitude and phase relationship of voltage and current as rotating vectors. For a pure resistor, V and I are aligned along the same direction. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph phasor_R ["PHASOR DIAGRAM: PURE RESISTIVE CIRCUIT"] dirR((Reference Axis 0 deg)) vecVR["V_R and I are IN PHASE
along the 0 degree axis"] dirR --- vecVR end style phasor_R fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Phasor Diagram: Pure L Circuit For a pure inductor, the voltage phasor leads the current phasor by 90°. The voltage is drawn on the positive y-axis and the current on the positive x-axis (reference). ```mermaid graph LR subgraph phasor_L ["PHASOR DIAGRAM: PURE INDUCTIVE CIRCUIT"] axisL((Reference 0 deg axis)) iL["Current I along 0 degree"] vL["Voltage V_L along positive 90 degree axis"] gap90["Phase difference equals 90 degrees"] axisL --- iL axisL --- vL iL --- gap90 vL --- gap90 end style phasor_L fill:#E0F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Phasor Diagram: Pure C Circuit For a pure capacitor, the current phasor leads the voltage phasor by 90°. The voltage is drawn on the reference axis and the current on the positive y-axis. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph phasor_C ["PHASOR DIAGRAM: PURE CAPACITIVE CIRCUIT"] axisC((Reference 0 deg axis)) vC["Voltage V_C along 0 degree"] iC["Current I along positive 90 degree axis"] gapC["Phase difference equals 90 degrees
I leads V"] axisC --- vC axisC --- iC vC --- gapC iC --- gapC end style phasor_C fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Impedance Triangle — The Core Visualization The **impedance triangle** is the single most important diagram in AC circuit analysis. It decomposes the complex impedance Z into its real (R) and imaginary (X) components. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph imp_tri ["IMPEDANCE TRIANGLE Z = R + jX"] hyp["Hypotenuse = Z
magnitude equals sqrt of R squared plus X squared"] adj["Adjacent side = R
Real Resistance"] opp["Opposite side = X
Reactance XL minus XC"] angle["Angle phi = tan inverse of X divided by R"] hyp --- adj hyp --- opp adj --- angle opp --- angle end style imp_tri fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the Triangle:** - **Horizontal (base) = R** (resistive component) - **Vertical = X** (net reactive component = $X_L - X_C$) - **Hypotenuse = |Z|** (magnitude of total opposition) - **Angle φ = tan⁻¹(X / R)** (phase by which V leads I) ## 4.5 Block Architecture: AC Circuit Analysis Methodology ```mermaid graph TD startA([Start: AC Circuit with R, L, C]) --> identifyA{Identify the Elements} identifyA --> pureR[Purely Resistive] identifyA --> pureL[Purely Inductive] identifyA --> pureC[Purely Capacitive] identifyA --> seriesRL[Series R-L or R-C] pureR --> calcR[Compute R, V and I in phase] pureL --> calcL[Compute XL equals 2 pi f L
V leads I by 90 deg] pureC --> calcC[Compute XC equals 1 over 2 pi f C
I leads V by 90 deg] seriesRL --> zcalc[Compute Z equals R plus jX] zcalc --> phasor[Draw Phasor Diagram] calcR --> phasor calcL --> phasor calcC --> phasor phasor --> power[Calculate P, Q, S and Power Factor] power --> result([Final Answer: Magnitude and Phase]) style startA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1, 3 Marks]** > **Q1.** Define the following terms with respect to AC circuits: > (i) Inductive reactance > (ii) Capacitive reactance > (iii) Impedance **Model Answer:** **(i) Inductive Reactance ($X_L$):** It is the opposition offered by a pure inductor to the flow of alternating current. It is directly proportional to the supply frequency $f$ and the inductance $L$. $$X_L = 2\pi f L \quad \text{(measured in Ohms)}$$ **(ii) Capacitive Reactance ($X_C$):** It is the opposition offered by a pure capacitor to the flow of alternating current. It is inversely proportional to the supply frequency $f$ and the capacitance $C$. $$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} \quad \text{(measured in Ohms)}$$ **(iii) Impedance (Z):** It is the total opposition offered by an AC circuit to the flow of current, comprising both resistance and reactance. It is a complex quantity expressed as $Z = R + jX$ with magnitude $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ in Ohms. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2, 3 Marks]** > **Q2.** Compare the behavior of an inductor and a capacitor in a DC circuit once the steady state is reached. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Inductor (L) | Capacitor (C) | |:-------|:-------------|:--------------| | At DC steady state (f = 0) | $X_L = 0$ → acts as a **short circuit** | $X_C \to \infty$ → acts as an **open circuit** | | Initial switching | Opposes sudden change in current | Opposes sudden change in voltage | | Energy form | Stored in magnetic field | Stored in electric field | **Key Statement:** In DC steady state, an ideal inductor behaves like a plain wire (short circuit) and an ideal capacitor behaves like a broken wire (open circuit). This contrasting behavior is widely exploited in filter and switching circuits. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern 2024 Scheme]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the instantaneous current through a pure inductor of **L = 100 mH** when connected to a **230 V, 50 Hz** AC supply. Also draw the phasor diagram showing the voltage and current relationship. **(b)** [7 Marks] A series circuit consists of a resistance of **40 Ω** and a capacitance of **63.66 μF** connected to a **230 V, 50 Hz** supply. Calculate: (i) Capacitive reactance, (ii) Impedance, (iii) Current, and (iv) Phase angle. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Derivation for Pure Inductor **Given:** $L = 100 \text{ mH} = 0.1 \text{ H}$, $V_{rms} = 230 \text{ V}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ **Step 1:** Write the supply voltage equation: $$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) = 230\sqrt{2}\,\sin(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t) = 325.27\,\sin(314.16\,t) \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Recall the defining equation for an ideal inductor: $v(t) = L\,\dfrac{di}{dt}$ **Step 3:** Compute inductive reactance: $$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 0.1 = 31.416 \;\Omega$$ **Step 4:** Compute peak and RMS current: $$I_m = \frac{V_m}{X_L} = \frac{325.27}{31.416} \approx 10.35 \text{ A}$$ $$I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10.35}{1.414} \approx 7.32 \text{ A}$$ **Step 5:** Write the instantaneous current. Since current lags voltage by 90° in a pure inductor: $$i(t) = 10.35\,\sin(314.16\,t - \pi/2) \text{ A} = 10.35\,\sin(314.16\,t - 1.571) \text{ A}$$ **Phasor Diagram (described):** Draw the current phasor I along the positive x-axis (reference) and the voltage phasor V rotated 90° counter-clockwise (pointing up the positive y-axis), clearly showing V leads I by 90°. --- #### Part (b) — Series R–C Circuit Calculation **Given:** $R = 40 \;\Omega$, $C = 63.66 \;\mu F = 63.66 \times 10^{-6}\;F$, $V = 230\text{ V}$, $f = 50\text{ Hz}$ **(i) Capacitive Reactance:** $$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 63.66 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50\;\Omega$$ **(ii) Impedance Magnitude:** $$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2} = \sqrt{40^2 + 50^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 2500} = \sqrt{4100} \approx 64.03\;\Omega$$ **(iii) RMS Current:** $$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{230}{64.03} \approx 3.59 \text{ A}$$ **(iv) Phase Angle:** $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-X_C}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-50}{40}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-1.25) \approx -51.34°$$ The **negative sign** indicates that the current **leads** the voltage — characteristic of a capacitive circuit. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a phasor diagram and waveform, explain the voltage-current relationship in a purely resistive AC circuit. Show that the average power dissipated is $P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$. **(b)** [7 Marks] An AC circuit has an impedance of $(30 + j40)\;\Omega$. Determine: (i) the resistance, (ii) the reactance, (iii) the magnitude of impedance, (iv) the phase angle, and (v) the power factor. State whether the circuit is inductive or capacitive. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — Pure R Circuit Phasor and Power **Voltage and Current Waveform Analysis:** The applied voltage is $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. Since the resistor obeys Ohm's law instantaneously, $i(t) = (V_m / R) \sin(\omega t)$. Both waveforms cross zero, reach peak, and return to zero **simultaneously**, confirming they are in phase. **Phasor Diagram:** Both V and I phasors are drawn along the same horizontal reference line (0° axis), aligned in direction and proportional in length to their RMS values. **Derivation of Average Power:** $$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) = V_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t)$$ Using the identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$: $$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t)$$ Averaging over a complete cycle, the second term averages to zero: $$P_{avg} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Impedance Analysis from Complex Form **Given:** $Z = (30 + j40)\;\Omega$ **(i) Resistance:** $R = 30\;\Omega$ **(ii) Reactance:** $X = +40\;\Omega$ (positive imaginary part → inductive) **(iii) Magnitude of Impedance:** $$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\;\Omega$$ **(iv) Phase Angle:** $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{40}{30}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.333) \approx 53.13°$$ **(v) Power Factor:** $$\cos\phi = \cos(53.13°) \approx 0.6 \text{ (lagging)}$$ **Conclusion:** Since the reactance is **positive** ($+j40$), the circuit behaves **inductively** (voltage leads current). The power factor is **0.6 lagging**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Sign Convention for Capacitors:** Many students forget that in a capacitive circuit, the phase angle is **negative** (current leads voltage). The phasor diagram for R-C must show V lagging I — examiners deduct 1 full mark for sign errors. > > 2. **Using Peak vs. RMS Values:** Ohm's law in AC uses RMS values: $I_{rms} = V_{rms} / \vert Z \vert$. Using peak values ($V_m, I_m$) gives the same ratio, but mixing peak voltage with RMS current (or vice versa) is a **fatal error** — 2 marks penalty. > > 3. **Units of Angular Frequency:** ω must be in **rad/s**, not Hz. The conversion is $\omega = 2\pi f$. A common error is to substitute $f = 50$ directly into $\omega L$, yielding an answer that is 6.28× too small. > > 4. **Power Factor Direction:** Always state whether the power factor is **lagging** (inductive) or **leading** (capacitive). Writing just "0.6" without direction loses a mark. > > 5. **Confusing Reactance with Impedance:** Reactance is a **scalar** quantity (just $\vert X \vert$), but impedance is a **complex** quantity ($R + jX$). Examiners will reject answers that treat $X_L$ as having a "real" and "imaginary" part — it does not. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2** - **AC Supply Standard:** India uses **50 Hz**, **230 V (single-phase residential)**. The angular frequency is **ω = 314.16 rad/s**. - **Pure R Circuit:** $V$ and $I$ in phase. Power dissipated = $V_{rms} I_{rms}$. Power factor = **1**. - **Pure L Circuit:** $V$ **leads** $I$ by 90°. Inductive reactance $X_L = 2\pi f L$. **Zero** real power consumption. Power factor = **0 lagging**. - **Pure C Circuit:** $I$ **leads** $V$ by 90°. Capacitive reactance $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$. **Zero** real power consumption. Power factor = **0 leading**. - **Frequency Behavior:** $X_L$ ↑ as $f$ ↑. $X_C$ ↓ as $f$ ↑. $R$ is frequency-independent. - **DC Behavior (f = 0):** Inductor → short circuit. Capacitor → open circuit. Resistor → unchanged. - **Impedance:** $Z = R + jX$ (complex). Magnitude: $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$. Phase: $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$. - **Ohm's Law in AC:** $I_{rms} = V_{rms} / \vert Z \vert$ (use RMS values for power calculations, peak values are equal in this ratio). - **Impedance Triangle:** Base = R, Perpendicular = X, Hypotenuse = |Z|, Angle = φ. - **Power Formulas:** $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ (real), $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$ (reactive), $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$ (apparent, in VA). - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Reactance is **not** dissipated as heat; it is **stored and returned**. Only resistance dissipates real power. - **Phasor Convention:** Current is usually taken as the **reference** (along 0° axis). Voltage phasor is then drawn relative to it. - **Always Show Units:** $\Omega$ for R, $X_L$, $X_C$, $|Z|$; H for L; F for C; Hz for f; rad/s for ω; W for real power; VAR for reactive power; VA for apparent power. - **KTU Favorite Question Pattern:** "Calculate X_L / X_C / |Z| / I / phase angle for given values of R, L, C, V, f." Memorize the four standard formulas: $X_L = 2\pi f L$, $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$, $|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$, $I = V/|Z|$. - **Power Factor Definition:** $\cos\phi$ — the ratio of real power to apparent power. Always state "lagging" or "leading." - **Energy Storage:** Inductors store energy in **magnetic fields** ($E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$). Capacitors store energy in **electric fields** ($E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$).

topics.title: RL, RC and RLC Series Circuits: Power Factor, Active, Reactive and Apparent Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** RL, RC and RLC Series Circuits: Power Factor, Active, Reactive and Apparent Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 | RL, RC and RLC Series Circuits — Power Factor & Power Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> In **Alternating Current (AC) circuits**, when a pure resistor (R), a pure inductor (L), and a pure capacitor (C) are connected in **series** with a sinusoidal source, the resulting circuit is called an **RL**, **RC**, or **RLC series circuit**. The combined opposition to current flow is termed **Impedance (Z)**, which is the phasor sum of Resistance (R) and Net Reactance (X). The cosine of the angle between voltage and current is defined as the **Power Factor (cos φ)**, while the energy consumed, exchanged, and supplied are called **Active Power (P)**, **Reactive Power (Q)**, and **Apparent Power (S)**, respectively.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Beer Mug" Intuition 🍺

Imagine you are drinking a freshly poured mug of beer:
- The **actual liquid (beer)** that satisfies your thirst = **Active Power (P)** in Watts.
- The **foam on top** that looks big but does not quench thirst = **Reactive Power (Q)** in VAR.
- The **total volume of the mug (beer + foam)** = **Apparent Power (S)** in VA.

In a purely resistive circuit, there is no foam — only beer (P = S, Power Factor = 1). In a reactive circuit, foam builds up — only a fraction of the supplied power is "consumed" usefully. The **Power Factor** is simply the ratio of beer to the total mug content.

### Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistance | R | **Ohm (Ω)** | Opposition that dissipates energy as heat |
| Inductive Reactance | $X_L$ | **Ohm (Ω)** | Opposition by inductor; energy stored in magnetic field |
| Capacitive Reactance | $X_C$ | **Ohm (Ω)** | Opposition by capacitor; energy stored in electric field |
| Impedance | Z | **Ohm (Ω)** | Total AC opposition (complex quantity) |
| Active Power | P | **Watt (W)** | Real, useful power dissipated |
| Reactive Power | Q | **Volt-Ampere Reactive (VAR)** | Power that oscillates between source and load |
| Apparent Power | S | **Volt-Ampere (VA)** | Product of RMS voltage and RMS current |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Ratio of real power to apparent power |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** In KTU exams, when a problem states "a coil has resistance R and inductance L," you must treat it as a **series RL combination** where the same current flows through R and L. The voltage across R and L are out of phase by **90°**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Pure R, L, and C Behavior in AC Circuits (Foundation)

Before combining R, L, and C, it is essential to recall how each element behaves individually under sinusoidal excitation $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

- **Resistor (R):** Voltage and current are **in-phase** ($\phi = 0°$). The element only dissipates energy.
- **Inductor (L):** Current **lags** voltage by **90°** ($\phi = +90°$). It only stores and returns energy to the source.
- **Capacitor (C):** Current **leads** voltage by **90°** ($\phi = -90°$). It only stores and returns energy to the source.

### 2.2 The Concept of Reactance

Reactance is the frequency-dependent opposition of energy-storing elements:

$$X_L = \omega L = 2 \pi f L \quad \text{(Inductive Reactance, Ω)}$$

$$X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C} \quad \text{(Capacitive Reactance, Ω)}$$

Where:
- $\omega$ = **angular frequency** in rad/s
- $f$ = **supply frequency** in Hz (in India, $f = 50$ Hz)
- $L$ = inductance in Henry (H)
- $C$ = capacitance in Farad (F)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** $X_L$ is directly proportional to frequency (increases with $f$), while $X_C$ is inversely proportional to frequency (decreases with $f$). This is why inductors are used as *chokes* for high frequencies and capacitors as *filters* for low frequencies.

### 2.3 RL Series Circuit Analysis

When R and L are connected in series, the same current I flows through both. Using **KVL (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)** in phasor form:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V_R} + \vec{V_L}$$

- $\vec{V_R}$ is in-phase with $\vec{I}$ (along real axis)
- $\vec{V_L}$ leads $\vec{I}$ by 90° (along positive imaginary axis)

The **impedance** in rectangular form:
$$Z = R + jX_L$$

Magnitude of impedance:
$$|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} \quad \text{(Ω)}$$

Phase angle (voltage leads current):
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right) \quad \text{(positive, LAGGING pf)}$$

**Behavior Summary (RL Circuit):**
- Circuit is **inductive** in nature
- Current **lags** voltage
- Power factor is **lagging** ($\cos\phi$)
- 0 < Power Factor < 1

### 2.4 RC Series Circuit Analysis

When R and C are connected in series, KVL gives:
$$\vec{V} = \vec{V_R} + \vec{V_C}$$

- $\vec{V_R}$ is in-phase with $\vec{I}$
- $\vec{V_C}$ **lags** $\vec{I}$ by 90° (along negative imaginary axis)

The **impedance** in rectangular form:
$$Z = R - jX_C$$

Magnitude of impedance:
$$|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2} \quad \text{(Ω)}$$

Phase angle (voltage lags current):
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-X_C}{R}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_C}{R}\right) \quad \text{(negative, LEADING pf)}$$

**Behavior Summary (RC Circuit):**
- Circuit is **capacitive** in nature
- Current **leads** voltage
- Power factor is **leading** ($\cos\phi$)
- 0 < Power Factor < 1

### 2.5 RLC Series Circuit Analysis (The General Case)

For all three elements in series:
$$\vec{V} = \vec{V_R} + \vec{V_L} + \vec{V_C}$$

The net reactance is $X = X_L - X_C$ (algebraic difference). The impedance is:
$$Z = R + j(X_L - X_C)$$

Magnitude of impedance:
$$|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2} \quad \text{(Ω)}$$

Phase angle:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$$

**Three Critical Operating Conditions:**

| Condition | Mathematical Test | Nature of Circuit | Power Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X_L > X_C$ | $\phi > 0°$ | **Inductive** (lagging) | Lagging ($\cos\phi$) |
| $X_L < X_C$ | $\phi < 0°$ | **Capacitive** (leading) | Leading ($\cos\phi$) |
| $X_L = X_C$ | $\phi = 0°$ | **Purely Resistive** (Resonance) | Unity ($\cos\phi = 1$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When a problem gives you $R = 10\ \Omega$, $L = 0.1$ H, $C = 100\ \mu F$, and $f = 50$ Hz, the FIRST step is to compute $X_L$ and $X_C$, then determine the dominant reactance. The sign of $(X_L - X_C)$ tells you the circuit's nature.

### 2.6 The Three Powers — Active, Reactive, and Apparent

For any AC circuit with RMS values $V_{rms}$, $I_{rms}$, and phase angle $\phi$:

**Active Power (Real Power) — P:**
$$P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi \quad \text{(Watts, W)}$$

This is the **actual power consumed** by the resistive element. It is converted to heat, light, mechanical work, etc. P can also be expressed as $I^2 R$.

**Reactive Power — Q:**
$$Q = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \sin\phi \quad \text{(VAR)}$$

This is the power that **oscillates** between the source and the reactive elements (L and C). It does no useful work but is necessary to sustain the magnetic and electric fields.

**Apparent Power — S:**
$$S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \quad \text{(VA)}$$

This is the product of the magnitude of voltage and current (without considering phase). It is the power the source must "appear" to supply.

### 2.7 Power Factor

The Power Factor is the cosine of the angle between voltage and current:

$$\text{Power Factor} = \cos\phi = \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{P}{S}$$

- **Unity PF (cos φ = 1):** Purely resistive load (only at resonance in RLC).
- **Lagging PF (0 < cos φ < 1):** Inductive load (current lags voltage).
- **Leading PF (0 < cos φ < 1):** Capacitive load (current leads voltage).
- **Zero PF (cos φ = 0):** Purely reactive load.

### 2.8 The Power Triangle (Geometric Visualization)

The three powers form a right-angled triangle, just like the impedance triangle:

$$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$$

$$P = S \cos\phi, \quad Q = S \sin\phi, \quad \tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P}$$

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of KTU 2024 numerical problems in this module.**

| # | Circuit | Impedance Z | $\vert Z \vert$ | Phase Angle $\phi$ | Power Factor | P (W) | Q (VAR) | S (VA) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pure R** | $R$ | $R$ | $0°$ | 1 (unity) | $I^2 R$ | $0$ | $I^2 R$ |
| 2 | **Pure L** | $jX_L$ | $X_L$ | $+90°$ | 0 (lagging) | $0$ | $I^2 X_L$ | $I^2 X_L$ |
| 3 | **Pure C** | $-jX_C$ | $X_C$ | $-90°$ | 0 (leading) | $0$ | $I^2 X_C$ | $I^2 X_C$ |
| 4 | **RL Series** | $R + jX_L$ | $\sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}$ | $\tan^{-1}(X_L/R)$ | $R \big/ \vert Z \vert$ lagging | $I^2 R$ | $I^2 X_L$ | $I^2 \vert Z \vert$ |
| 5 | **RC Series** | $R - jX_C$ | $\sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}$ | $-\tan^{-1}(X_C/R)$ | $R \big/ \vert Z \vert$ leading | $I^2 R$ | $I^2 X_C$ | $I^2 \vert Z \vert$ |
| 6 | **RLC Series** | $R + j(X_L - X_C)$ | $\sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$ | $\tan^{-1}\big((X_L - X_C)/R\big)$ | $R \big/ \vert Z \vert$ | $I^2 R$ | $I^2 (X_L - X_C)$ | $I^2 \vert Z \vert$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do NOT use $\vert \vert$ for absolute value inside tables — use $\big/ \vert Z \vert$ notation (the slash is just a horizontal line). In standard text, use $\vert Z \vert$ for the magnitude of impedance.

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Loads:** Induction motors, transformers, and solenoids are highly inductive → they draw **lagging** power factor. Utilities penalize consumers with low PF.
- **Power Factor Correction (PFC):** Capacitor banks are connected in parallel with inductive loads to bring PF close to **unity**, reducing line losses.
- **Resonance in RLC:** Used in tuning circuits (radios, TV), band-pass filters, and induction heating.
- **Smart Grids:** Modern grid operators mandate PF between 0.95 lagging to 1.0 for industrial consumers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Impedance of an RL Series Circuit from KVL

**Given:** A resistor R and inductor L are connected in series across a sinusoidal source $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the phasor voltage and phasor current relationship.**

For a pure resistor: $\vec{V_R} = R \cdot \vec{I}$ (in-phase with current)

For a pure inductor: $\vec{V_L} = j X_L \cdot \vec{I} = j \omega L \cdot \vec{I}$ (leads current by 90°)

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the loop.**

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V_R} + \vec{V_L} = R \cdot \vec{I} + j X_L \cdot \vec{I}$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the common current phasor $\vec{I}$.**

$$\vec{V} = \vec{I} \cdot (R + j X_L)$$

**Step 4 — Define Impedance as the ratio $Z = \vec{V} / \vec{I}$.**

$$Z = R + j X_L \quad \text{(rectangular form)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the magnitude of impedance.**

$$|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} \quad \text{(Ω)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the phase angle.**

The angle of Z is the angle by which voltage leads current:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right) \quad \text{(positive value, lagging pf)}$$

**Step 7 — Express Z in polar form.**

$$Z = |Z| \angle \phi = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} \;\angle\; \tan^{-1}(X_L/R)$$

**Conclusion:** The RL series circuit has a magnitude of impedance $\sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}$ and the current lags the voltage by angle $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X_L/R)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power in an AC Circuit (Active, Reactive, Apparent)

**Given:** A series circuit draws current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ from voltage $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $\phi$ is the phase angle by which current lags voltage.

**Step 1 — Write the instantaneous power $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$.**

$$p(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity** $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:

$$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \left[ \cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Separate the constant (DC) and oscillating (AC) terms.**

The first term $\frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos(\phi)$ is **constant** (time-independent).
The second term $\frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ is **oscillating** at double the supply frequency.

**Step 4 — Compute the average power over one full cycle.**

The average of the oscillating term over a complete cycle is **zero**.

$$P_{avg} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos(\phi)$$

**Step 5 — Convert peak values to RMS values** using $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ and $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$:

$$P = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \cos\phi = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

**Step 6 — Define apparent power** as the product of RMS values (magnitude only):

$$S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \quad \text{(VA)}$$

**Step 7 — Express power factor from the power relationship:**

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{P}{V_{rms} I_{rms}}$$

**Step 8 — Derive reactive power.** Since $P = S \cos\phi$ and $Q = S \sin\phi$:

$$Q = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \sin\phi \quad \text{(VAR)}$$

**Conclusion:** The three power equations are interconnected by the phase angle $\phi$:

$$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2 \quad \text{(from the power triangle)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resonant Frequency of an RLC Series Circuit

**Given:** An RLC series circuit is at **resonance** when the net reactance is zero.

**Step 1 — Set the imaginary part of impedance to zero.**

$$X_L - X_C = 0 \implies X_L = X_C$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the formulas for $X_L$ and $X_C$.**

$$2\pi f_r L = \frac{1}{2\pi f_r C}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $f_r$ (resonant frequency).**

$$(2\pi f_r)^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \implies f_r^2 = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2 LC}$$

$$\boxed{f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

**Step 4 — Note the key features at resonance:**

- Impedance $Z = R$ (purely resistive, minimum)
- Current $I = V/R$ (maximum for the given V and R)
- Phase angle $\phi = 0°$
- Power factor = 1 (unity)
- $X_L = X_C$, so they cancel each other

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A series RLC circuit has $R = 30\ \Omega$, $L = 0.2$ H, and $C = 50\ \mu F$. It is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply. Calculate:
(a) Inductive and Capacitive Reactance
(b) Impedance and Phase Angle
(c) RMS Current
(d) Active, Reactive, and Apparent Power
(e) Power Factor

**Solution:**

**(a) Reactances:**

$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 0.2 = 62.83\ \Omega$$

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}} = 63.66\ \Omega$$

**(b) Impedance and Phase Angle:**

$$Z = R + j(X_L - X_C) = 30 + j(62.83 - 63.66) = 30 - j0.83\ \Omega$$

$$|Z| = \sqrt{30^2 + (-0.83)^2} = \sqrt{900 + 0.69} = \sqrt{900.69} = 30.01\ \Omega$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-0.83}{30}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.0277) = -1.58°$$

Since $\phi$ is negative, the circuit is **capacitive** (leading pf).

**(c) RMS Current:**

$$I = \frac{V}{|Z|} = \frac{230}{30.01} = 7.66\ \text{A}$$

**(d) Powers:**

$$S = V \cdot I = 230 \cdot 7.66 = 1762.6\ \text{VA}$$

$$P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 1762.6 \cdot \cos(1.58°) = 1762.6 \cdot 0.9996 = 1761.7\ \text{W}$$

$$Q = V \cdot I \cdot \sin\phi = 1762.6 \cdot \sin(-1.58°) = 1762.6 \cdot (-0.0276) = -48.65\ \text{VAR}$$

(Negative Q confirms leading/capacitive nature.)

**(e) Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi = \cos(1.58°) = 0.9996 \quad \text{(leading)}$$

---

### 3.5 Summary Table of Key Derivations

| Derivation | Result | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Impedance of RL | $Z = R + jX_L$, $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}$ | Coils, chokes, motors |
| Impedance of RC | $Z = R - jX_C$, $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}$ | RC filters, integrators |
| Impedance of RLC | $Z = R + j(X_L - X_C)$ | Tuned circuits, filters |
| Active Power | $P = VI\cos\phi$ | Heat, work output |
| Reactive Power | $Q = VI\sin\phi$ | Magnetic/electric field energy |
| Apparent Power | $S = VI$ | Equipment rating (kVA) |
| Resonant Frequency | $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ | Radio tuning, filters |
| Power Triangle | $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ | PF correction analysis |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: AC Series Circuit Analysis Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **systematic procedure** to solve any RLC series circuit problem — a flow that is repeatedly tested in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given R, L, C, V, f] --> B[Compute XL = 2πfL]
    B --> C[Compute XC = 1 / 2πfC]
    C --> D{Net Reactance X = XL minus XC}
    D -->|X > 0| E[Circuit is Inductive: LAGGING pf]
    D -->|X < 0| F[Circuit is Capacitive: LEADING pf]
    D -->|X = 0| G[Circuit is at RESONANCE: UNITY pf]
    E --> H[Compute Z = R + jX]
    F --> I[Compute Z = R - jX]
    G --> J[Z = R purely real]
    H --> K[Compute magnitude Z and phase phi]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute I = V divided by Z magnitude]
    L --> M[Compute P = VI cos phi]
    L --> N[Compute Q = VI sin phi]
    L --> O[Compute S = VI]
    M --> P[Construct Power Triangle]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Report Power Factor = cos phi with nature]
```

### 4.2 Phasor Diagram Relationships (RL, RC, RLC)

The following diagram shows the **phasor relationships** for the three circuit types. In each case, the current phasor $\vec{I}$ is taken as the **reference** (along the positive real axis).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RL["RL Series Circuit: V Leads I"]
        RL1[I along Real axis] --> RL2[VR along Real axis in phase with I]
        RL1 --> RL3[VL along +Imag axis 90 degrees ahead of I]
        RL2 --> RL4[V = VR + VL: leads I by phi]
        RL3 --> RL4
    end
    subgraph RC["RC Series Circuit: V Lags I"]
        RC1[I along Real axis] --> RC2[VR along Real axis in phase with I]
        RC1 --> RC3[VC along -Imag axis 90 degrees behind I]
        RC2 --> RC4[V = VR + VC: lags I by phi]
        RC3 --> RC4
    end
    subgraph RLC["RLC Series Circuit: Depends on Net X"]
        RLC1[I along Real axis] --> RLC2[VR along Real axis]
        RLC1 --> RLC3[VL along +Imag axis]
        RLC1 --> RLC4[VC along -Imag axis]
        RLC3 --> RLC5[VX = VL minus VC net reactance]
        RLC4 --> RLC5
        RLC2 --> RLC6[V total: angle depends on sign of X]
        RLC5 --> RLC6
    end
```

### 4.3 Power Triangle — Geometric Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PT1[S = VI: Apparent Power in VA] --> PT2[P = VI cos phi: Active Power in W]
    PT1 --> PT3[Q = VI sin phi: Reactive Power in VAR]
    PT2 --> PT4[Power Triangle Right Angled at P]
    PT3 --> PT4
    PT4 --> PT5[S² = P² + Q²]
    PT4 --> PT6[cos phi = P divided by S: Power Factor]
    PT4 --> PT7[sin phi = Q divided by S]
    PT4 --> PT8[tan phi = Q divided by P]
```

### 4.4 Impedance Triangle vs Power Triangle — Correspondence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZT["Impedance Triangle Z"]
        Z1["|Z| = V/I"] --> Z2["R = resistance base"]
        Z2 --> Z3["X = net reactance height"]
        Z1 --> Z4["φ = tan⁻¹(X/R)"]
    end
    subgraph PT["Power Triangle S"]
        P1["S = V·I"] --> P2["P = active power base"]
        P2 --> P3["Q = reactive power height"]
        P1 --> P4["φ = tan⁻¹(Q/P)"]
    end
    ZT -.Same angle φ.-> PT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Visualization Insight:** The impedance triangle and the power triangle are **similar right-angled triangles** with the same angle $\phi$. The sides of the impedance triangle are multiplied by $I^2$ to obtain the sides of the power triangle:
> - $P = I^2 R$
> - $Q = I^2 X$
> - $S = I^2 \vert Z \vert$
