## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Power Factor. What is its significance in AC circuits?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Power factor is defined as the **cosine of the angle between the voltage and current** in an AC circuit. Mathematically, it is the **ratio of active power (P) to apparent power (S)**.

$$\text{Power Factor} = \cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{R}{|Z|}$$

**Significance:**
1. **Indicator of Power Utilization:** A low power factor means a large portion of the supplied current is reactive and does no useful work.
2. **Line Losses:** Low PF increases $I^2R$ losses in transmission and distribution lines.
3. **Penalty Clause:** Electricity boards charge penalties to industrial consumers with PF < 0.95.
4. **Equipment Sizing:** Generators and transformers must be rated in kVA (apparent power), not kW, because they must handle both real and reactive power.
5. **Voltage Drop:** Low PF causes excessive voltage drops in power systems, requiring voltage regulation equipment.
6. **Economic Operation:** Improving PF through capacitor banks reduces electricity bills and improves system efficiency.

A **unity power factor (cos φ = 1)** is the ideal condition (purely resistive), while **zero power factor** corresponds to a purely reactive load (no real power consumption).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Active Power, Reactive Power, and Apparent Power.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Active Power (P) | Reactive Power (Q) | Apparent Power (S) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Symbol/Unit** | P, measured in **Watts (W)** | Q, measured in **Volt-Ampere Reactive (VAR)** | S, measured in **Volt-Ampere (VA)** |
| **Definition** | The actual power consumed by the load and converted into useful work (heat, light, mechanical) | The power that oscillates between the source and the reactive elements (L and C) due to their energy storage | The product of RMS voltage and RMS current, representing the total power the source must supply |
| **Formula** | $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ | $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$ | $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$ |
| **Phase Relation** | Associated with resistive component R | Associated with reactive component X | Magnitude of the phasor sum of P and Q |
| **Physical Meaning** | Real, useful, billable power | "Wasted" power that sustains magnetic/electric fields | Total power handling capacity required |
| **Component** | Dissipative (non-recoverable) | Non-dissipative (recoverable) | Vector sum (geometric) |

**Key Relationship:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ (from the power triangle)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Both sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A series RLC circuit consists of $R = 25\ \Omega$, $L = 0.5$ H, and $C = 100\ \mu F$, connected across a 200 V, 50 Hz supply. Calculate:
(i) The inductive and capacitive reactance
(ii) The total impedance and phase angle
(iii) The RMS current drawn by the circuit
(iv) The power factor of the circuit **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the circuit in part (a), compute:
(i) Active Power (P)
(ii) Reactive Power (Q)
(iii) Apparent Power (S)
(iv) Draw the power triangle and verify $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — (i) Reactances:**

$$X_L = 2 \pi f L = 2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 0.5 = 157.08\ \Omega$$

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 31.83\ \Omega$$

**Part (a) — (ii) Impedance and Phase Angle:**

$$X = X_L - X_C = 157.08 - 31.83 = 125.25\ \Omega \quad \text{(positive, inductive)}$$

$$|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{25^2 + 125.25^2} = \sqrt{625 + 15687.6} = \sqrt{16312.6} = 127.72\ \Omega$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{125.25}{25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(5.01) = 78.71°$$

Since $\phi$ is positive, the circuit is **inductive** and the current **lags** the voltage.

**Part (a) — (iii) RMS Current:**

$$I = \frac{V}{|Z|} = \frac{200}{127.72} = 1.566\ \text{A}$$

**Part (a) — (iv) Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi = \cos(78.71°) = 0.195 \quad \text{(lagging)}$$

**Part (b) — (i) Active Power:**

$$P = V I \cos\phi = 200 \cdot 1.566 \cdot 0.195 = 61.07\ \text{W}$$

**Alternative check using $I^2 R$:**

$$P = I^2 R = (1.566)^2 \cdot 25 = 61.31\ \text{W} \quad \text{(minor rounding difference)}$$

**Part (b) — (ii) Reactive Power:**

$$Q = V I \sin\phi = 200 \cdot 1.566 \cdot \sin(78.71°) = 200 \cdot 1.566 \cdot 0.981 = 307.27\ \text{VAR}$$

**Part (b) — (iii) Apparent Power:**

$$S = V \cdot I = 200 \cdot 1.566 = 313.2\ \text{VA}$$

**Part (b) — (iv) Verification of Power Triangle:**

$$P^2 + Q^2 = (61.07)^2 + (307.27)^2 = 3729.5 + 94414.8 = 98144.3$$

$$S^2 = (313.2)^2 = 98094.2$$

The values match within rounding tolerance, confirming $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$. ✓

**Power Triangle Sketch (textual representation):**

```
              S = 313.2 VA
             /|
            / |
           /  |  Q = 307.27 VAR
          /   |
         /    |
        / φ=78.71°
       /_____|
       P = 61.07 W
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Define impedance. An electrical circuit consists of a resistance of 20 Ω in series with an inductance of 0.15 H, connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. Determine:
(i) The impedance of the circuit
(ii) The current drawn
(iii) The power consumed
(iv) The reactive power
(v) The apparent power **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the significance of power factor in AC circuits. A factory consumes 100 kW at a power factor of 0.7 lagging. Calculate:
(i) The apparent power and reactive power
(ii) The new power factor if a capacitor bank supplies 30 kVAR
(iii) The reduction in line current if the supply voltage is 400 V **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Definition:**
**Impedance (Z)** is the total opposition offered by an AC circuit to the flow of alternating current. It is the phasor sum of resistance (R) and reactance (X), measured in **ohms (Ω)**.

**Part (a) — (i) Impedance:**

$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 0.15 = 47.12\ \Omega$$

$$Z = R + jX_L = 20 + j47.12\ \Omega$$

$$|Z| = \sqrt{20^2 + 47.12^2} = \sqrt{400 + 2220.3} = \sqrt{2620.3} = 51.19\ \Omega$$

**Part (a) — (ii) Current:**

$$I = \frac{V}{|Z|} = \frac{230}{51.19} = 4.49\ \text{A}$$

**Part (a) — (iii) Active Power:**

$$P = I^2 R = (4.49)^2 \cdot 20 = 20.16 \cdot 20 = 403.2\ \text{W}$$

**Part (a) — (iv) Reactive Power:**

$$Q = I^2 X_L = (4.49)^2 \cdot 47.12 = 20.16 \cdot 47.12 = 949.94\ \text{VAR}$$

**Part (a) — (v) Apparent Power:**

$$S = V \cdot I = 230 \cdot 4.49 = 1032.7\ \text{VA}$$

**Verification:** $P^2 + Q^2 = 162574 + 902266 = 1064840 \approx S^2 = 1066473$ ✓

**Part (b) — Significance of Power Factor:**
The power factor of an AC circuit is the cosine of the angle between the applied voltage and the resulting current. Its significance includes:
1. Determines how effectively electrical power is being utilized.
2. A low PF means higher line current for the same real power, causing increased $I^2R$ losses.
3. Power systems equipment (transformers, alternators, cables) must be rated for apparent power (kVA), which is higher than real power (kW) when PF is low.
4. Utilities impose penalties on consumers with low PF.
5. PF correction using capacitor banks improves voltage regulation and reduces energy bills.

**Part (b) — (i) Apparent and Reactive Power:**

$$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1} = \frac{100}{0.7} = 142.86\ \text{kVA}$$

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.7) = 45.57°$$

$$Q_1 = S_1 \sin\phi_1 = 142.86 \cdot \sin(45.57°) = 142.86 \cdot 0.7141 = 102.02\ \text{kVAR}$$

**Part (b) — (ii) New Power Factor after Capacitor Bank:**

The capacitor supplies reactive power $Q_C = 30$ kVAR, which **subtracts** from the inductive Q.

$$Q_2 = Q_1 - Q_C = 102.02 - 30 = 72.02\ \text{kVAR}$$

$$S_2 = \sqrt{P^2 + Q_2^2} = \sqrt{100^2 + 72.02^2} = \sqrt{10000 + 5186.9} = \sqrt{15186.9} = 123.24\ \text{kVA}$$

$$\cos\phi_2 = \frac{P}{S_2} = \frac{100}{123.24} = 0.8114 \quad \text{(lagging)}$$

**Part (b) — (iii) Reduction in Line Current:**

$$I_{old} = \frac{S_1}{V} = \frac{142.86 \times 10^3}{400} = 357.14\ \text{A}$$

$$I_{new} = \frac{S_2}{V} = \frac{123.24 \times 10^3}{400} = 308.11\ \text{A}$$

$$\Delta I = 357.14 - 308.11 = 49.03\ \text{A} \quad \text{(reduction)}$$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I_{old}} \times 100 = \frac{49.03}{357.14} \times 100 = 13.73\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the nature of PF:** When $\cos\phi$ is computed, students write only "0.195" without writing "lagging" or "leading." KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for missing the nature.
> 2. **Sign of Q:** Reactive power for inductive circuits is taken as **positive**, while for capacitive circuits, it is **negative**. Wrong sign = full marks lost in Q calculation.
> 3. **Unit Confusion:** Active power in **W (Watts)**, Reactive in **VAR**, Apparent in **VA**. Mixing units loses easy marks.
> 4. **Inductor/Capacitor Reactance Formulas Reversed:** Some students write $X_C = 2\pi f C$ (wrong) instead of $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$. This is a critical 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not drawing the phasor or power diagram:** In 7-mark questions involving impedance/power, KTU's marking scheme typically allocates **1 to 2 marks** for the labeled diagram. Skipping it costs marks.
> 6. **Using peak values instead of RMS:** When the problem says "230 V" (which is the standard Indian RMS value), use 230 V directly in $P = VI\cos\phi$. Do not multiply by $\sqrt{2}$ unless the problem specifies "peak voltage."
> 7. **Arithmetic in PF Correction:** When capacitor bank reduces Q, students sometimes **add** Q_C instead of subtracting. Remember: capacitors **supply** reactive power to **cancel** the inductive demand.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

- **AC Behavior:** R is non-reactive, L causes current to **lag** V by 90°, C causes current to **lead** V by 90°.
- **Reactance Formulas:** $X_L = 2\pi f L$ (∝ f), $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ (∝ 1/f).
- **Impedance is complex:** $Z = R + jX$, with magnitude $|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ and phase $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$.
- **RL Series:** Inductive, lagging PF, X = +X_L.
- **RC Series:** Capacitive, leading PF, X = -X_C.
- **RLC Series:** $X = X_L - X_C$. Sign of X determines nature.
- **Resonance Condition:** $X_L = X_C$, $Z = R_{min}$, $I = I_{max}$, PF = 1, $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
- **Power Trio:**
  - $P = VI\cos\phi$ (W) — **real, useful**
  - $Q = VI\sin\phi$ (VAR) — **oscillating, field energy**
  - $S = VI$ (VA) — **apparent, equipment rating**
- **Power Triangle:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$, with $\phi$ between P and S.
- **Power Factor:** $\cos\phi = P/S = R/|Z|$; lagging for inductive, leading for capacitive.
- **PF Correction:** Capacitors in parallel **subtract** reactive power, bringing PF closer to unity.
- **KTU Golden Rule:** When asked for "nature of circuit," always state whether it is inductive (lagging) or capacitive (leading) — never just give a number for PF.
- **Quick check:** In any RLC series problem, $I = V/|Z|$ and $P = I^2 R$ should yield the same answer as $P = VI\cos\phi$. If not, you have a calculation error.
- **Always verify** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ as a sanity check in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase AC Systems: Generation of Three Phase Voltages, Advantages of Three Phase Systems, Star and Delta Connections (Balanced Only), Relation Between Line and Phase Voltages, Line and Phase Currents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Three Phase AC Systems: Generation of Three Phase Voltages, Advantages of Three Phase Systems, Star and Delta Connections (Balanced Only), Relation Between Line and Phase Voltages, Line and Phase Currents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Three-Phase AC System** is a polyphase system in which three independent sinusoidal alternating voltages or currents of the **same magnitude**, **same frequency**, but **displaced from each other by a phase angle of 120° (electrical)** are generated, transmitted, and utilized through a common network.

The three phases are conventionally designated as:
- **R-phase (Red)**
- **Y-phase (Yellow)**  
- **B-phase (Blue)**

Mathematically, the three instantaneous phase EMFs of a balanced three-phase system are expressed as:

$$e_R(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$e_Y(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$

$$e_B(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) = E_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition Box:** A *balanced* three-phase system is one in which the three phase voltages are equal in magnitude, mutually displaced by 120°, and have identical waveform and frequency. A *balanced* load draws equal currents from each phase with identical phase angles.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Phase Voltage | $V_{Ph}$ | Voltage across a single phase winding (between line and neutral in star; across winding in delta) |
| Line Voltage | $V_L$ | Voltage between any two line conductors (R-Y, Y-B, B-R) |
| Phase Current | $I_{Ph}$ | Current flowing through a single phase winding |
| Line Current | $I_L$ | Current flowing in a line conductor |
| Phase Sequence | — | Order in which the three phase voltages attain their peak value (R-Y-B is positive sequence) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Three-Worker Team

Imagine a heavy cart that must be pulled continuously. If **one worker (single-phase system)** pulls, he gets tired, the pull is jerky, and power delivery is pulsating. 

Now replace him with **three workers (three-phase system)** of equal strength, each pulling the rope but **staggered in time by 1/3 of a cycle**. At any instant, at least one worker is pulling at near-peak force, another is at moderate force, and the third is recovering. The result is:
- **Smooth, non-pulsating total power** (constant power delivery)
- **Higher efficiency** (no idle moments)
- **Self-starting rotating machines** (a rotating magnetic field is naturally created)
- **Economical transmission** (less copper needed for the same power)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Picture for KTU Exams:** A 3-phase system delivers constant instantaneous power, while a single-phase system delivers pulsating power. This is the single most important conceptual reason for the universal adoption of 3-phase systems in power generation, transmission, and industrial drives.

### 1.4 Generation of Three-Phase Voltages — Physical Setup

Three-phase voltages are generated in a **three-phase alternator (synchronous generator)**. The construction principle is:

1. **Rotor:** A field magnet (electromagnet fed by DC excitation) rotates at synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ rpm.
2. **Stator:** Three identical windings (coils) — R, Y, B — are placed on the stator, **physically displaced by 120° in space** around the air gap.
3. As the rotor's magnetic flux cuts each coil sequentially (because the coils are separated in space by 120°), the EMFs induced in the three coils are also **separated in time by 120° electrical**.

The coil that is **first cut by the flux** is the one whose EMF leads the others — in R-Y-B sequence, **R-phase leads, Y lags R by 120°, and B lags Y by 120° (or leads by 240°)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The 120° phase displacement in the output EMFs arises *naturally* from the 120° spatial displacement of the stator windings. There is no external electronic circuit creating the phase shift — it is purely a geometric/electromagnetic phenomenon.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The Rotating Magnetic Field Mechanism

When a balanced three-phase supply is given to three coils placed spatially 120° apart (as in a 3-phase induction motor or synchronous motor stator), a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** of constant magnitude is produced in the air gap. This is the operating principle behind almost all industrial AC motors.

The three stator phase currents $i_R$, $i_Y$, $i_B$ produce three pulsating magnetic fields. The vector sum of these three fields at any instant is a **single rotating vector of constant magnitude $\dfrac{3}{2} F_{max}$**, rotating at synchronous speed.

### 2.2 Phasor Representation of Balanced Three-Phase Voltages

In a balanced system, the three phase voltages (with respect to the neutral) can be represented as rotating phasors:

$$\bar{V}_{RN} = V_{Ph} \angle 0°$$

$$\bar{V}_{YN} = V_{Ph} \angle -120°$$

$$\bar{V}_{BN} = V_{Ph} \angle -240° = V_{Ph} \angle +120°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 scheme examiner expects students to remember the standard phase sequence. If R is taken as the reference ($0°$), then Y is always at $-120°$ and B at $-240°$ (or $+120°$) for the standard **R-Y-B positive sequence**. Reversing any two phases gives the *negative* (R-B-Y) sequence.

### 2.3 Star (Y) Connection — Concept

In a **star connection**:
- The **similar ends** (say, the "finish" ends) of all three phase windings are joined together at a common point called the **star point** or **neutral point (N)**.
- The **other three ends** (the "start" ends) are brought out as the three **line conductors**: R, Y, B.
- A neutral wire may or may not be brought out depending on the application (4-wire system if neutral is used, 3-wire system if not).

### 2.4 Delta (Δ) Connection — Concept

In a **delta connection**:
- The **finish end of one phase winding is connected to the start end of the next phase winding**, forming a closed loop.
- The three junction points A, B, C are brought out as the **line terminals**.
- There is **no neutral point** in a delta connection.
- The three windings are connected: A-B is Phase R, B-C is Phase Y, C-A is Phase B (standard convention).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** The *delta connection forms a closed loop*. If the three phase voltages are perfectly balanced, the sum of the three EMFs around the loop is zero (since $V_R + V_Y + V_B = 0$ for a balanced system), so **no circulating current flows in the loop even when no load is connected**. This is a direct consequence of the 120° phase symmetry.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any KTU 3-phase problem. Memorize it.

| Quantity | Star (Y) Connection | Delta (Δ) Connection |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Relation between Line and Phase **Voltage** | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$ | $V_L = V_{Ph}$ |
| Relation between Line and Phase **Current** | $I_L = I_{Ph}$ | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}$ |
| Total Active Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ |
| Total Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ |
| Total Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ |
| Neutral Available? | Yes (4-wire possible) | No (always 3-wire) |
| Phase Voltage Magnitude (if $V_L = 415$ V) | $V_{Ph} = \dfrac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6$ V | $V_{Ph} = 415$ V |
| Phase Current (if $I_L = 10$ A) | $I_{Ph} = 10$ A | $I_{Ph} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{3}} = 5.77$ A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In India, the standard 3-phase domestic and industrial supply is **415 V, 50 Hz** (line-to-line). For a star-connected load, the phase voltage is 239.6 V ≈ 240 V (used for single-phase loads taken between any line and neutral). For a delta-connected load, the phase voltage is 415 V itself (used for industrial 3-phase motors).

### 2.6 Why Three-Phase Systems? — Engineering Advantages

The KTU 2024 scheme syllabus explicitly tests the following advantages:

1. **Constant Instantaneous Power:** Total power delivered to a balanced 3-phase load is constant in time (no pulsation). For a single-phase load, power varies as $\cos(2\omega t)$.
2. **Higher Power Output from Same Machine Frame:** A 3-phase machine delivers approximately **1.5 times** the output of a single-phase machine of the same size and weight.
3. **Self-Starting Rotating Machines:** 3-phase induction and synchronous motors are **self-starting** due to the rotating magnetic field. Single-phase versions require auxiliary starting windings.
4. **Economical Transmission of Power:** To transmit a given amount of power over a given distance, a 3-phase system requires **less copper** in the conductors than an equivalent single-phase system. Specifically, the conductor material saving is about **25%** for the same power and losses.
5. **Smaller, Smoother, and More Efficient Machines:** Torque in a 3-phase motor is uniform (no torque pulsations), leading to longer machine life.
6. **Flexible Voltage Levels:** The same alternator can supply loads at two voltage levels — phase voltage (for single-phase loads via neutral) and line voltage (for 3-phase loads).
7. **Higher Power Factor Capability:** Industrial 3-phase systems can easily correct power factor using delta-connected or star-connected capacitor banks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Line Voltage = $\sqrt{3} \cdot$ Phase Voltage in Star Connection

**Given:** A balanced star-connected source with phase voltages (with respect to neutral N):
- $\bar{V}_{RN} = V_{Ph} \angle 0° = V_{Ph}(1 + j0)$
- $\bar{V}_{YN} = V_{Ph} \angle -120° = V_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$
- $\bar{V}_{BN} = V_{Ph} \angle +120° = V_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$

**Step 1: Express the line voltage $\bar{V}_{RY}$ (voltage from R-line to Y-line).**

By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied at the star point, the line voltage between any two lines is the **phasor difference of the two corresponding phase voltages** (both measured with respect to the neutral):

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = \bar{V}_{RN} - \bar{V}_{YN}$$

This is because $\bar{V}_{RY} = \bar{V}_{R} - \bar{V}_{Y}$ when both R and Y potentials are referenced to the same neutral N. The vector from Y to R is the difference.

**Step 2: Substitute the phasor expressions.**

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}(1 + j0) - V_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3: Simplify by distributing and combining like terms.**

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}\left[1 - \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\right] + j V_{Ph}\left[0 - \left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right]$$

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\right] + j V_{Ph}\left[\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]$$

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}\left(\dfrac{3}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude of $\bar{V}_{RY}$.**

The magnitude of a complex number $a + jb$ is $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$. Applying this:

$$\vert \bar{V}_{RY} \vert = V_{Ph} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}$$

$$\vert \bar{V}_{RY} \vert = V_{Ph} \sqrt{\dfrac{9}{4} + \dfrac{3}{4}}$$

$$\vert \bar{V}_{RY} \vert = V_{Ph} \sqrt{\dfrac{12}{4}} = V_{Ph} \sqrt{3}$$

**Step 5: Identify the result.**

Since $\bar{V}_{RY}$ is the line voltage between R and Y lines, and $\bar{V}_{RN}$ is the phase voltage:

$$\boxed{V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}}$$

**Step 6: Determine the phase angle of $\bar{V}_{RY}$.**

$$\angle \bar{V}_{RY} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}/2}{3/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = +30°$$

This means **each line voltage leads its corresponding phase voltage by 30°**. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

**Step 7: Verify that the other two line voltages yield the same magnitude.**

By symmetry:

$$\bar{V}_{YB} = \bar{V}_{YN} - \bar{V}_{BN} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph} \angle -90°$$

$$\bar{V}_{BR} = \bar{V}_{BN} - \bar{V}_{RN} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph} \angle +150°$$

All three line voltages have magnitude $\sqrt{3} V_{Ph}$ and are mutually displaced by 120° — confirming that a star-connected source produces a balanced set of line voltages from a balanced set of phase voltages.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Line Current = $\sqrt{3} \cdot$ Phase Current in Delta Connection

**Given:** A balanced delta-connected load with phase currents:
- $\bar{I}_{RY}$ (current in the phase between A and B)
- $\bar{I}_{YB}$ (current in the phase between B and C)  
- $\bar{I}_{BR}$ (current in the phase between C and A)

For a balanced load, the phase currents are mutually 120° apart. Taking $\bar{I}_{RY}$ as reference:

$$\bar{I}_{RY} = I_{Ph} \angle 0°$$

$$\bar{I}_{YB} = I_{Ph} \angle -120°$$

$$\bar{I}_{BR} = I_{Ph} \angle +120°$$

**Step 1: Apply KCL at terminal A.**

The line current entering terminal A from the external line, $\bar{I}_R$ (or $\bar{I}_A$), splits into the two phases connected to A. In a delta with phases A-B and C-A connected at terminal A:

$$\bar{I}_R = \bar{I}_{AB} - \bar{I}_{CA}$$

(Assuming the phase convention: $\bar{I}_{AB}$ flows from A to B, and $\bar{I}_{CA}$ flows from C to A. By KCL, the current entering A from the line must equal the current flowing from A into the A-B phase minus the current arriving at A from the C-A phase.)

Renaming: $\bar{I}_{AB} = \bar{I}_{RY}$ and $\bar{I}_{CA} = \bar{I}_{BR}$ (with proper sign):

$$\bar{I}_R = \bar{I}_{AB} - \bar{I}_{CA}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the phasors.**

$$\bar{I}_R = I_{Ph} \angle 0° - I_{Ph} \angle +120°$$

$$= I_{Ph}(1 + j0) - I_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$\bar{I}_R = I_{Ph}\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\right] + j I_{Ph}\left[0 - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]$$

$$\bar{I}_R = I_{Ph}\left(\dfrac{3}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude.**

$$\vert \bar{I}_R \vert = I_{Ph} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = I_{Ph} \sqrt{\dfrac{12}{4}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}$$

**Step 5: State the result.**

$$\boxed{I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}}$$

**Step 6: Phase angle check.**

$$\angle \bar{I}_R = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}/2}{3/2}\right) = -30°$$

This means **the line current lags the corresponding phase current by 30°** in a delta connection. Again, this is a tested KTU concept.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — Star vs Delta

| Parameter | Star (Y) | Delta (Δ) |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| Phase voltage / Line voltage | $V_{Ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ | $V_{Ph} = V_L$ |
| Phase current / Line current | $I_{Ph} = I_L$ | $I_{Ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$ |
| Total power (3-phase) | $P = 3 \cdot V_{Ph} \cdot I_{Ph} \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cos\phi$ | Same expression |
| Neutral wire current (balanced) | $I_N = 0$ | Not applicable |
| Power transmitted per phase | $V_{Ph} \cdot I_{Ph} \cos\phi$ | $V_{Ph} \cdot I_{Ph} \cos\phi$ |
| Phase voltage at 415 V line | 239.6 V | 415 V |
| Phase current at 10 A line | 10 A | 5.77 A |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Three-Phase Alternator — Generation Principle

This block-level schematic shows the physical arrangement of the three stator windings and the resulting phase-shifted EMFs.

```mermaid
graph LR
    rotor[Rotor Field Magnet DC Excited]
    coilR[Stator Coil R Position 0 deg]
    coilY[Stator Coil Y Position 120 deg]
    coilB[Stator Coil B Position 240 deg]
    slipR[Slip Ring 1]
    slipY[Slip Ring 2]
    slipB[Slip Ring 3]
    termR[Line R Terminal]
    termY[Line Y Terminal]
    termB[Line B Terminal]

    rotor --> coilR
    rotor --> coilY
    rotor --> coilB

    coilR --> slipR --> termR
    coilY --> slipY --> termY
    coilB --> slipB --> termB

    emfR[EMF eR leads by 0 deg]
    emfY[EMF eY lags by 120 deg]
    emfB[EMF eB lags by 240 deg]

    termR -.-> emfR
    termY -.-> emfY
    termB -.-> emfB
```

**Reading the diagram:** The rotor magnet rotates at synchronous speed. As it sweeps past the three stator coils (which are physically 120° apart in space), it induces an EMF in each. Because the coils are physically separated, the EMFs are **temporally separated by 120°**, giving the three-phase output.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Star (Y) Connection — Phasor and Circuit View

```mermaid
graph TD
    pR[Phase Winding R]
    pY[Phase Winding Y]
    pB[Phase Winding B]
    starPt[Star Point Neutral N]

    lineR[Line R]
    lineY[Line Y]
    lineB[Line B]

    pR --- starPt
    pY --- starPt
    pB --- starPt

    pR --- lineR
    pY --- lineY
    pB --- lineB

    vPhR[VRN at 0 deg]
    vPhY[VYN at minus 120 deg]
    vPhB[VBN at plus 120 deg]

    lineR -.-> vPhR
    lineY -.-> vPhY
    lineB -.-> vPhB

    vLRY[V_RY leads V_RN by 30 deg]
    lineR -.-> vLRY
    lineY -.-> vLRY
```

**Key insight from the diagram:**
- The **finish ends** of all three windings (R, Y, B) meet at the **star point N**.
- The **start ends** become the three line terminals.
- The phase voltages $V_{RN}$, $V_{YN}$, $V_{BN}$ are 120° apart, each measured with respect to the neutral N.
- The line voltage $V_{RY}$ is the phasor difference $V_{RN} - V_{YN}$, which equals $\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$ and **leads $V_{RN}$ by 30°**.

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Delta (Δ) Connection — Phasor and Circuit View

```mermaid
graph TD
    wAB[Phase Winding between A and B]
    wBC[Phase Winding between B and C]
    wCA[Phase Winding between C and A]

    termA[Line Terminal A]
    termB[Line Terminal B]
    termC[Line Terminal C]

    wAB --- wBC
    wBC --- wCA
    wCA --- wAB

    wAB --- termA
    wAB --- termB
    wBC --- termB
    wBC --- termC
    wCA --- termC
    wCA --- termA

    vPhAB[V_AB at 0 deg]
    vPhBC[V_BC at minus 120 deg]
    vPhCA[V_CA at plus 120 deg]

    termA -.-> vPhAB
    termB -.-> vPhBC
    termC -.-> vPhCA

    iLR[Line current IR lags I_AB by 30 deg]
    termA -.-> iLR
    termB -.-> iLR
```

**Key insight from the diagram:**
- The three windings form a **closed triangular loop** A-B-C-A.
- The line terminals A, B, C are at the three corners of the triangle.
- The **phase voltage equals the line voltage** since each winding is directly across two line terminals.
- The **line current is the phasor difference of two adjacent phase currents**, giving $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}$ and **lagging the phase current by 30°**.

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Power Flow Architecture in a 3-Phase System

```mermaid
graph LR
    gen[3-Phase Alternator]
    stepUp[Step Up Transformer]
    tline[3-Phase Transmission Line]
    stepDown[Step Down Transformer]
    indLoad[Industrial 3-Phase Motor Delta Connected]
    domLoad[Domestic Single Phase Loads Star Connected with Neutral]

    gen --> stepUp --> tline --> stepDown
    stepDown --> indLoad
    stepDown --> domLoad
```

**Engineering interpretation:** A single 3-phase generator feeds power through a 3-phase transmission network. At the consumer end, industrial loads (motors) are typically delta-connected (high-power, 415 V across each winding), while domestic loads (lights, fans, 230 V) are connected between one line and the neutral of a 4-wire star system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define a three-phase system. Why is the line voltage $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage in a balanced star connection?**

**Model Answer:**

A **three-phase system** is a polyphase system in which three sinusoidal voltages of equal magnitude, equal frequency, and displaced by 120° in time-phase are generated. The three phases are labeled R, Y, and B.

In a balanced star connection, the three phase windings share a common neutral point N. The phase voltages $V_{RN}$, $V_{YN}$, $V_{BN}$ are the voltages from each line to the neutral and are mutually 120° apart.

The line voltage $V_{RY}$ is the phasor difference of two phase voltages:

$$V_{RY} = V_{RN} - V_{YN}$$

By phasor subtraction, with $V_{RN} = V_{Ph} \angle 0°$ and $V_{YN} = V_{Ph} \angle -120°$:

$$V_{RY} = V_{Ph}\left(\dfrac{3}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

Taking the magnitude:

$$\vert V_{RY} \vert = V_{Ph}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$$

**Hence $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$ in star connection.**

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**List any four advantages of a three-phase system over a single-phase system.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Constant Power Delivery:** A balanced 3-phase system delivers constant instantaneous power, whereas a single-phase system has pulsating power ($p = v \cdot i$ varies as $\cos(2\omega t)$).

2. **Self-Starting Motors:** 3-phase induction motors are inherently self-starting due to the rotating magnetic field produced by the 3-phase supply. Single-phase motors require auxiliary starting windings and capacitors.

3. **Greater Power per Unit Weight:** A 3-phase machine produces approximately 1.5 times more output power than a single-phase machine of the same physical size and weight.

4. **Economical Transmission:** For transmitting the same amount of power at the same voltage and losses, a 3-phase system requires about **25% less copper** than an equivalent single-phase system.

5. **Smooth Torque:** 3-phase motors produce uniform torque without pulsations, leading to longer bearing life and quieter operation.

6. **Flexible Voltage Utilization:** The same 3-phase system can supply both 3-phase loads (at line voltage) and single-phase loads (at phase voltage via neutral).

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relation between **line voltage and phase voltage** in a three-phase star-connected system. Show all phasor steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A balanced star-connected load draws a line current of 10 A from a 415 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase supply. The load has a power factor of 0.8 lagging. Calculate: (i) Phase voltage, (ii) Phase current, (iii) Total active power, (iv) Total reactive power, (v) Total apparent power. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Given:** Balanced star-connected system with phase voltages $V_{RN}, V_{YN}, V_{BN}$ each of magnitude $V_{Ph}$, mutually 120° apart.

**Step 1: Write the phasor expressions (with $V_{RN}$ as reference).**

$$\bar{V}_{RN} = V_{Ph} \angle 0° = V_{Ph}(1 + j0)$$

$$\bar{V}_{YN} = V_{Ph} \angle -120° = V_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

$$\bar{V}_{BN} = V_{Ph} \angle +120° = V_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2: The line voltage $\bar{V}_{RY}$ equals the phasor difference.**

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = \bar{V}_{RN} - \bar{V}_{YN}$$

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}(1 + j0) - V_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3: Simplify by combining real and imaginary parts.**

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\right) + jV_{Ph}\left(0 + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

$$\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}\left(\dfrac{3}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude.**

$$\vert \bar{V}_{RY} \vert = V_{Ph}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = V_{Ph}\sqrt{\dfrac{9}{4} + \dfrac{3}{4}} = V_{Ph}\sqrt{\dfrac{12}{4}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph} \text{ (in star connection)}}$$

The line voltage leads the corresponding phase voltage by 30°.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Connection: Star (Y)
- Line voltage: $V_L = 415$ V
- Line current: $I_L = 10$ A
- Frequency: $f = 50$ Hz
- Power factor: $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging
- $\therefore \phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°$
- $\sin\phi = 0.6$

**(i) Phase Voltage:**

In star: $V_{Ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6$ V

**(ii) Phase Current:**

In star: $I_{Ph} = I_L = 10$ A

**(iii) Total Active Power:**

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 10 \times 0.8$$

$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 3320 = 5750.3 \text{ W} \approx 5.75 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Total Reactive Power:**

$$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$$

$$Q = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 10 \times 0.6$$

$$Q = \sqrt{3} \times 2490 = 4312.7 \text{ VAR} \approx 4.31 \text{ kVAR}$$

**(v) Total Apparent Power:**

$$S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 10 = 7188.5 \text{ VA} \approx 7.19 \text{ kVA}$$

**Verification:** $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{5750.3^2 + 4312.7^2} \approx 7188$ VA ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relation between **line current and phase current** in a three-phase delta-connected system. Show all phasor steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A balanced delta-connected load is supplied from a 400 V, 3-phase source. The impedance of each phase is $Z = (8 + j6) \, \Omega$. Calculate: (i) Phase voltage, (ii) Phase current, (iii) Line current, (iv) Total active power consumed, (v) Total reactive power consumed. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Given:** Balanced delta connection with phase currents $I_{AB}, I_{BC}, I_{CA}$ each of magnitude $I_{Ph}$, mutually 120° apart.

**Step 1: Write phase current phasors (with $I_{AB}$ as reference).**

$$\bar{I}_{AB} = I_{Ph} \angle 0° = I_{Ph}(1 + j0)$$

$$\bar{I}_{BC} = I_{Ph} \angle -120° = I_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

$$\bar{I}_{CA} = I_{Ph} \angle +120° = I_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2: Apply KCL at terminal A.**

The line current entering terminal A from the external line equals the current flowing into the A-B phase minus the current arriving from the C-A phase:

$$\bar{I}_A = \bar{I}_{AB} - \bar{I}_{CA}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify.**

$$\bar{I}_A = I_{Ph}(1 + j0) - I_{Ph}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

$$\bar{I}_A = I_{Ph}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\right) + jI_{Ph}\left(0 - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

$$\bar{I}_A = I_{Ph}\left(\dfrac{3}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude.**

$$\vert \bar{I}_A \vert = I_{Ph}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = I_{Ph}\sqrt{3}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph} \text{ (in delta connection)}}$$

The line current lags the corresponding phase current by 30°.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Connection: Delta (Δ)
- Line voltage: $V_L = 400$ V
- Phase impedance: $Z = 8 + j6 \, \Omega$
- $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{64 + 36} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \, \Omega$
- $\cos\phi = \dfrac{R}{\vert Z \vert} = \dfrac{8}{10} = 0.8$ lagging
- $\sin\phi = \dfrac{X}{\vert Z \vert} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6$
- $\phi = 36.87°$

**(i) Phase Voltage:**

In delta: $V_{Ph} = V_L = 400$ V

**(ii) Phase Current:**

$$I_{Ph} = \dfrac{V_{Ph}}{\vert Z \vert} = \dfrac{400}{10} = 40 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Line Current:**

In delta: $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph} = \sqrt{3} \times 40 = 69.28$ A

**(iv) Total Active Power:**

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 69.28 \times 0.8$$

$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 22169.6 = 38{,}400 \text{ W} = 38.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Alternative computation (per phase):**
$$P = 3 \cdot I_{Ph}^2 \cdot R = 3 \times 40^2 \times 8 = 3 \times 1600 \times 8 = 38{,}400 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

**(v) Total Reactive Power:**

$$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$$

$$Q = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 69.28 \times 0.6 = 28{,}800 \text{ VAR} = 28.8 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Alternative computation (per phase):**
$$Q = 3 \cdot I_{Ph}^2 \cdot X = 3 \times 40^2 \times 6 = 28{,}800 \text{ VAR} \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **The $\sqrt{3}$ swap trap (MOST COMMON ERROR):** Students frequently write $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{Ph}$ for **delta** and $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{Ph}$ for **star**. **Memory trick: "Star has a $\sqrt{3}$ in VOLTAGE; Delta has a $\sqrt{3}$ in CURRENT."** The voltage and current are inverted between the two connections.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $\sqrt{3}$ in power formula:** Students often write $P = V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ (single-phase formula) and lose 2-3 marks. Always use $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ for a balanced 3-phase system, **regardless** of whether the load is star or delta connected.
>
> 3. **Mixing up phase and line quantities in the power formula:** You can use $P = 3 \cdot V_{Ph} \cdot I_{Ph} \cos\phi$ **or** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cos\phi$ — both give the same result. But **do not mix** $V_L$ with $I_{Ph}$ or $V_{Ph}$ with $I_L$ in the same expression.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in phasor subtraction:** When computing $V_{RY} = V_{RN} - V_{YN}$, students often mistakenly write $V_{RY} = V_{RN} + V_{YN}$. Remember: the line voltage is the **difference** of two phase voltages (measured from the same neutral), not the sum.
>
> 5. **Not specifying R-Y-B phase sequence:** The KTU 2024 scheme examiner expects you to state the assumed phase sequence (usually R-Y-B positive sequence with R as reference) at the start of the problem. Omitting this loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Unit mistakes:** Express final answers with units (V, A, W, VAR, VA). A numerically correct answer without units is marked down.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

Use this as your **last-minute revision checklist** before the KTU exam.

- [ ] A **3-phase system** has three voltages, equal in magnitude, frequency, and **120°** apart in time-phase.
- [ ] The 120° phase shift originates from the **spatial 120°** separation of stator coils in the alternator.
- [ ] **Star (Y) connection** joins the similar ends of all three windings at a common neutral point. The other ends are line terminals.
- [ ] **Delta (Δ) connection** joins the end of one winding to the start of the next, forming a **closed loop** with no neutral.
- [ ] **In Star:** $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$ and $I_L = I_{Ph}$.
- [ ] **In Delta:** $V_L = V_{Ph}$ and $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}$.
- [ ] In a **balanced star system, the neutral current is zero** ($I_N = 0$).
- [ ] The line voltage **leads** the corresponding phase voltage by **30°** in star.
- [ ] The line current **lags** the corresponding phase current by **30°** in delta.
- [ ] **Power formulas (valid for BOTH star and delta in a balanced system):**
  - $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ (Total Active Power in Watts)
  - $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ (Total Reactive Power in VAR)
  - $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ (Total Apparent Power in VA)
  - $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ (Power factor)
- [ ] **Per-phase power formulas:**
  - $P_{per\,phase} = V_{Ph} \cdot I_{Ph} \cdot \cos\phi$ and $P_{total} = 3 \cdot P_{per\,phase}$
- [ ] The standard Indian 3-phase supply is **415 V, 50 Hz**.
- [ ] Phase voltage in star at 415 V line voltage = **239.6 V** (≈ 240 V, used for single-phase domestic supply).
- [ ] Phase voltage in delta at 415 V line voltage = **415 V** (used for industrial 3-phase motors).
- [ ] **Key advantages of 3-phase over 1-phase:** Constant power, self-starting motors, higher power-to-weight ratio, ~25% copper saving in transmission, smooth torque, flexible voltage utilization.
- [ ] **For KTU derivations:** Always state the assumed phase sequence (R-Y-B positive), reference phasor (R-phase at 0°), and write both real and imaginary components explicitly before taking the magnitude.
- [ ] **Memory hook for the $\sqrt{3}$:**
  - **"S + V"** → **Star has $\sqrt{3}$ in Voltage**
  - **"D + C"** → **Delta has $\sqrt{3}$ in Current**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passive and Active Components in Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Passive and Active Components in Electronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Passive and Active Components in Electronics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Electronic Component?
An **electronic component** is a fundamental discrete device or physical entity in an electronic system designed to influence electrons or their associated fields in a desired manner. They are the basic building blocks of every electronic circuit — from a simple mobile phone charger to a complex satellite communication system.

### 1.2 Classification of Electronic Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> Electronic components are broadly classified into two categories based on their ability to **generate, amplify, or control energy**:
> 1. **Passive Components** – Cannot generate energy or provide power gain.
> 2. **Active Components** – Can generate energy or provide power gain (amplification).

#### 📘 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Water Pipeline System:**
> - **Passive components** are like **pipes, valves, and storage tanks**. They can store water (energy), resist the flow, or let it pass — but they cannot *create* new water or *amplify* the existing flow.
> - **Active components** are like a **water pump or motor**. They take a small input and either *generate* flow on their own (using external power) or *amplify* the existing flow.

---

### 1.3 Passive Components — Detailed Overview

A **passive component** is a device that **cannot generate electrical energy** nor provide **power gain**. It can only **receive, store, dissipate, or filter** energy. It does not require an external power source to operate (except for biasing the circuit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property of Passive Components:**
> They obey the property of **reciprocity** and cannot introduce net energy into the circuit. The energy delivered to a passive load is always **less than or equal to** the energy supplied by the source.

#### The Three Fundamental Passive Elements:

| S.No | Component | Symbol | Primary Function | Energy Behavior |
|------|-----------|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Resistor (R)** | R | Opposes current flow | Dissipates energy as heat |
| 2 | **Capacitor (C)** | C | Stores electrical energy in an electric field | Stores & releases energy |
| 3 | **Inductor (L)** | L | Stores electrical energy in a magnetic field | Stores & releases energy |

---

### 1.4 Active Components — Detailed Overview

An **active component** is a device that **can generate, amplify, or control electrical signals** and **requires an external power source** (DC bias) to function. It can provide **power gain** — meaning the output power is **greater than** the input power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Active Components Need External Power:**
> Just like a loudspeaker amplifier needs a wall socket (230 V AC) to amplify a small microphone signal, an active electronic component needs a separate DC supply (like +5 V or +12 V) to boost the input signal.

#### Key Active Components Covered in Module 3:

| S.No | Component | Primary Function |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Diode (PN Junction / Zener / LED)** | Allows current in one direction only (rectification, regulation, indication) |
| 2 | **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** | Amplifies weak signals; acts as electronic switch |
| 3 | **Field Effect Transistor (FET / MOSFET)** | Voltage-controlled amplification and switching |
| 4 | **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** | High-gain DC amplifier for mathematical operations |

---

### 1.5 Why This Classification Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Focus Area:**
> Examiners frequently ask:
> - "Differentiate between active and passive components" (Direct 3-mark question)
> - "Identify whether a given component is active or passive" (Conceptual short answer)
> - "Explain the working of a PN junction diode" (5–14 mark descriptive)
> - "Sketch the V-I characteristics of a diode/transistor" (Graphical question)

---

### 1.6 Standard Physical Constants Used in This Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorize:
> - **Elementary charge (e):** $\mathbf{e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{C}}$
> - **Electron mobility in Silicon:** $\mathbf{\mu_n \approx 1350 \ \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}}$
> - **Hole mobility in Silicon:** $\mathbf{\mu_p \approx 480 \ \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}}$
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $\mathbf{k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}}$
> - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $\mathbf{V_T \approx 26 \ \text{mV}}$
> - **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si at 300 K:** $\mathbf{n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \ \text{cm}^{-3}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Resistor — The Fundamental Passive Element

A **resistor** is a two-terminal passive component that **opposes the flow of electric current** in a circuit, converting electrical energy into **heat** (Joule heating).

### 2.1.1 Ohm's Law (Foundational Principle)

Ohm's Law states that the voltage across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided physical conditions (like temperature) remain constant.

$$V = I \cdot R$$

Where:
- $V$ = Voltage across the resistor (in Volts)
- $I$ = Current through the resistor (in Amperes)
- $R$ = Resistance (in Ohms, $\Omega$)

### 2.1.2 Resistor Color Code (KTU Frequently Asked Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resistor Color Bands (4-Band System):**
> | Color | Digit | Multiplier | Tolerance |
> |-------|-------|------------|-----------|
> | Black | 0 | $10^0$ | — |
> | Brown | 1 | $10^1$ | ±1% |
> | Red | 2 | $10^2$ | ±2% |
> | Orange | 3 | $10^3$ | — |
> | Yellow | 4 | $10^4$ | — |
> | Green | 5 | $10^5$ | ±0.5% |
> | Blue | 6 | $10^6$ | ±0.25% |
> | Violet | 7 | $10^7$ | ±0.1% |
> | Grey | 8 | $10^8$ | — |
> | White | 9 | $10^9$ | — |
> | Gold | — | $10^{-1}$ | ±5% |
> | Silver | — | $10^{-2}$ | ±10% |

**Memory Trick:** "**B**ig **B**rown **R**abbits **O**ften **Y**ield **G**reat **B**ig **V**ocal **G**runts **W**hen **G**ently **S**lapped"

### 2.1.3 Types of Resistors
- **Fixed Resistors:** Carbon composition, metal film, wire-wound
- **Variable Resistors:** Potentiometer, rheostat, trimmer
- **Special Resistors:** Thermistor (NTC/PTC), LDR (Light Dependent Resistor), Varistor

---

## 2.2 Capacitor — Energy Storage in Electric Field

A **capacitor** is a two-terminal passive component that **stores electrical energy in an electric field** between two conductive plates separated by an insulator (dielectric).

### 2.2.1 Fundamental Capacitance Formula

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Capacitance (in Farads, F)
- $\varepsilon_0$ = Permittivity of free space $= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$
- $\varepsilon_r$ = Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the material
- $A$ = Area of overlap of the plates (in $\text{m}^2$)
- $d$ = Distance between the plates (in m)

### 2.2.2 Charging Equation of a Capacitor

When a DC voltage $V$ is applied through a resistor $R$:

$$V_c(t) = V \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

$$I_c(t) = \frac{V}{R} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

### 2.2.3 Discharging Equation of a Capacitor

$$V_c(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The product **$RC$** is called the **Time Constant (τ)** — the time required for the capacitor to charge to **63.2%** of the final voltage or discharge to **36.8%** of the initial voltage.

### 2.2.4 Types of Capacitors
- **Ceramic Capacitors** — small, non-polarized, used in high-frequency circuits
- **Electrolytic Capacitors** — polarized, high capacitance, used in power supplies
- **Tantalum Capacitors** — stable, used in precision timing circuits
- **Film Capacitors** — used in audio and filtering applications
- **Variable Capacitors** — used in radio tuning (ganged capacitors)

---

## 2.3 Inductor — Energy Storage in Magnetic Field

An **inductor** is a passive component consisting of a coil of wire that **stores energy in a magnetic field** when current flows through it.

### 2.3.1 Fundamental Inductance Formula

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Inductance (in Henrys, H)
- $\mu_0$ = Permeability of free space $= 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$
- $\mu_r$ = Relative permeability of the core
- $N$ = Number of turns
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the coil (in $\text{m}^2$)
- $l$ = Length of the coil (in m)

### 2.3.2 Voltage-Current Relationship

$$V_L = L \frac{dI}{dt}$$

### 2.3.3 Energy Stored in an Inductor

$$E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$

---

## 2.4 PN Junction Diode — The Fundamental Active Element

A **diode** is a two-terminal active semiconductor device formed by joining a **P-type** and **N-type** semiconductor material. It allows current to flow primarily in **one direction** (forward bias) and blocks it in the opposite direction (reverse bias).

### 2.4.1 Diode Current Equation (Shockley Equation)

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / (nV_T)} - 1 \right)$$

Where:
- $I_D$ = Diode current
- $I_S$ = Reverse saturation current (typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}$ A for Si)
- $V_D$ = Voltage across the diode
- $V_T$ = Thermal voltage $\approx 26 \ \text{mV}$ at 300 K
- $n$ = Ideality factor (1 to 2)

### 2.4.2 Threshold (Knee) Voltages
- **Silicon Diode:** $V_{Knee} \approx 0.7 \ \text{V}$
- **Germanium Diode:** $V_{Knee} \approx 0.3 \ \text{V}$

---

## 2.5 Transistor (BJT) — The Heart of Modern Electronics

A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** is a three-terminal active semiconductor device that can **amplify** weak signals or act as an **electronic switch**. It has two PN junctions.

### 2.5.1 Terminal Relationships

$$I_E = I_B + I_C$$

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} \quad \text{(Common-Emitter Current Gain)}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} \quad \text{(Common-Base Current Gain)}$$

$$\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1}$$

### 2.5.2 Three Configurations of BJT
| Configuration | Input | Output | Use |
|---------------|-------|--------|-----|
| **Common Base (CB)** | Emitter | Collector | High frequency |
| **Common Emitter (CE)** | Base | Collector | Most common — for amplification |
| **Common Collector (CC)** | Base | Emitter | Impedance matching (Emitter Follower) |

---

## 2.6 Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) — Versatile Active Device

An **op-amp** is a high-gain, DC-coupled electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output.

### 2.6.1 Key Ideal Characteristics
- **Open-loop voltage gain ($A_{OL}$):** Infinite
- **Input impedance ($Z_{in}$):** Infinite
- **Output impedance ($Z_{out}$):** Zero
- **Bandwidth:** Infinite
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** Infinite

### 2.6.2 Inverting Amplifier Gain Formula

$$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

### 2.6.3 Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain Formula

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

---

## 2.7 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for guaranteed marks:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $V = IR$ | Ohm's Law | $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $R$ in $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $P = I^2 R = V^2/R = VI$ | Power dissipated in resistor | $P$ in Watts |
| 3 | $R_T = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots$ (Series) | Series resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | $\frac{1}{R_T} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \cdots$ (Parallel) | Parallel resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel plate | F |
| 6 | $V_c(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ | Capacitor charging | Volts |
| 7 | $V_c(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$ | Capacitor discharging | Volts |
| 8 | $\tau = RC$ | RC Time Constant | Seconds |
| 9 | $L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{l}$ | Inductance of solenoid | Henrys |
| 10 | $V_L = L \frac{dI}{dt}$ | Inductor voltage | Volts |
| 11 | $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Energy in capacitor | Joules |
| 12 | $E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$ | Energy in inductor | Joules |
| 13 | $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$ | Shockley Diode Equation | Amperes |
| 14 | $V_T = kT/q$ | Thermal Voltage | $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K |
| 15 | $\beta = I_C / I_B$ | BJT Current Gain (CE) | Dimensionless |
| 16 | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | KCL at BJT terminals | Amperes |
| 17 | $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_{in}$ | Inverting Op-Amp Gain | Dimensionless |
| 18 | $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f / R_{in}$ | Non-Inverting Op-Amp Gain | Dimensionless |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these components are used in the real world:**
> - **Resistors:** Voltage dividers, current limiters (LED circuits), pull-up/pull-down in digital logic, heat generation in electric heaters.
> - **Capacitors:** Power supply filtering, AC coupling in audio, timing circuits (555 timer), energy storage in camera flashes.
> - **Inductors:** Power transformers, EMI filters, RF tuning circuits, energy storage in SMPS.
> - **Diodes:** Rectifiers in adapters, Zener diodes for voltage regulation, LEDs for indicators, photodiodes in solar panels.
> - **Transistors:** Amplifiers in audio systems, switching in digital logic (CPU), oscillators in radio transmitters.
> - **Op-Amps:** Active filters, comparators, integrators, differentiators, instrumentation amplifiers in biomedical devices.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Resistors in Series and Parallel

### 3.1.1 Series Combination

When resistors are connected end-to-end, the same current $I$ flows through each resistor. By applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop:

$$V_S = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \cdots + V_n$$

$$V_S = I R_1 + I R_2 + I R_3 + \cdots + I R_n$$

Factoring out $I$:

$$V_S = I (R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n)$$

Comparing with $V_S = I R_{eq}$:

$$\boxed{R_{eq, \text{series}} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Parallel Combination

When resistors are connected between the same two nodes, they have the **same voltage** $V$ across them. The total current is the sum of individual currents (KCL):

$$I_T = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \cdots + I_n$$

$$\frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{V}{R_1} + \frac{V}{R_2} + \frac{V}{R_3} + \cdots + \frac{V}{R_n}$$

Dividing both sides by $V$:

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{R_{eq, \text{parallel}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Charging of a Capacitor (RC Circuit)

Consider a series RC circuit with a DC source $V$, resistor $R$, and capacitor $C$. The switch is closed at $t = 0$.

### Applying KVL around the loop:

$$V = V_R + V_C$$

$$V = I R + \frac{Q}{C}$$

Since current is the rate of change of charge:

$$I = \frac{dQ}{dt}$$

Substituting:

$$V = R \frac{dQ}{dt} + \frac{Q}{C}$$

Rearranging:

$$R \frac{dQ}{dt} = V - \frac{Q}{C}$$

$$\frac{dQ}{V - Q/C} = \frac{dt}{R}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $C$:

$$\frac{C \cdot dQ}{CV - Q} = \frac{dt}{R}$$

Let $u = CV - Q$, so $du = -C \cdot dQ$:

$$\int \frac{du}{u} = -\int \frac{dt}{R}$$

$$\ln \vert u \vert = -\frac{t}{R} + k$$

Substituting back and applying initial condition $Q(0) = 0$:

$$\ln \left( \frac{CV - Q}{CV} \right) = -\frac{t}{RC}$$

$$\frac{CV - Q}{CV} = e^{-t/RC}$$

$$Q(t) = CV (1 - e^{-t/RC})$$

Since $V_C = Q/C$:

$$\boxed{V_C(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})}$$

At $t = \tau = RC$, we have $V_C = 0.632 V$ (i.e., 63.2% of full charge). After $5\tau$, the capacitor is considered **fully charged** (≈99.3%).

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: BJT Current Relationships (KCL Proof)

For a Bipolar Junction Transistor, by **Kirchhoff's Current Law**, the sum of currents entering the device must equal the sum of currents leaving it.

The emitter current $I_E$ **enters** the device (for NPN), while $I_B$ and $I_C$ **leave** the device:

$$I_E = I_B + I_C$$

Dividing both sides by $I_E$:

$$1 = \frac{I_B}{I_E} + \frac{I_C}{I_E}$$

Let $\alpha = I_C / I_E$ (common-base current gain):

$$1 = \frac{I_B}{I_E} + \alpha$$

$$\frac{I_B}{I_E} = 1 - \alpha$$

Since $\beta = I_C / I_B$ (common-emitter current gain):

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{\alpha \cdot I_E}{(1 - \alpha) \cdot I_E} = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\beta (1 - \alpha) = \alpha \implies \beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}}$$

And conversely:

$$\alpha = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1}$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Inverting Op-Amp Closed-Loop Gain

For an ideal op-amp in the inverting configuration:
- **Virtual Ground:** $V^- = 0$ (since $V^+ = 0$ and $A_{OL} = \infty$)
- **No current into input terminals:** $I^- = 0$

**Apply KCL at the inverting node:**

Current from $R_{in}$ = Current through $R_f$:

$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_{in}} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Substituting $V^- = 0$:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Identifying a Resistor Value Using Color Code

**Example:** A carbon resistor has color bands: **Yellow – Violet – Orange – Gold**

**Step 1:** Read the first two bands as digits:
- Yellow = **4**
- Violet = **7**

**Step 2:** Read the third band as the multiplier:
- Orange = **$\times 10^3$**

**Step 3:** Apply the formula: $R = (\text{Digit 1})(\text{Digit 2}) \times 10^{\text{Multiplier}}$

$$R = 47 \times 10^3 \ \Omega = 47,000 \ \Omega = 47 \ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Read the fourth band as tolerance:
- Gold = **±5%**

**Step 5:** Calculate tolerance range:

$$\text{Max} = 47,000 + 0.05 \times 47,000 = 49,350 \ \Omega = 49.35 \ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$\text{Min} = 47,000 - 0.05 \times 47,000 = 44,650 \ \Omega = 44.65 \ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R = 47 \ \text{k}\Omega \pm 5\% = (44.65 \ \text{k}\Omega \text{ to } 49.35 \ \text{k}\Omega)}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Capacitor Charging Time

**Problem:** A $100 \ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is charged through a $10 \ \text{k}\Omega$ resistor from a $12 \ \text{V}$ DC source. Find:
(a) The time constant
(b) The voltage across the capacitor after 2 seconds
(c) The time to reach 10 V

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Time constant
$$\tau = RC = 10,000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \ \text{second}$$

**Part (b):** Voltage at $t = 2$ s
$$V_C(2) = 12(1 - e^{-2/1}) = 12(1 - e^{-2}) = 12(1 - 0.1353) = 12 \times 0.8647$$
$$\boxed{V_C(2) \approx 10.38 \ \text{V}}$$

**Part (c):** Time to reach 10 V
$$10 = 12(1 - e^{-t})$$
$$\frac{10}{12} = 1 - e^{-t}$$
$$e^{-t} = 1 - 0.8333 = 0.1667$$
$$-t = \ln(0.1667) = -1.7918$$
$$\boxed{t \approx 1.79 \ \text{seconds}}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Passive vs. Active Components

| Parameter | Passive Component | Active Component |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Energy Generation** | Cannot generate energy | Can generate or amplify energy |
| **External Power** | Not required (for operation) | Required (DC bias supply) |
| **Power Gain** | Always $\leq 1$ (loss or unity) | Can be $> 1$ (amplification) |
| **Control over output** | Limited — only attenuates | Full control — amplifies, switches, modulates |
| **Examples** | R, L, C | Diodes, Transistors, Op-amps |
| **Direction of current** | Bidirectional (in most cases) | Unidirectional (semiconductor-based) |
| **Reciprocity** | Reciprocal | Non-reciprocal (in general) |
| **Linearity** | Mostly linear | Can be linear (BJT in active region) or non-linear (diode) |
| **Application** | Filtering, energy storage, biasing | Amplification, switching, regulation |
| **Failure Mode** | Usually open circuit | Short or open circuit |

## 📚 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between active and passive components. Give two examples of each.**

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | Active Components | Passive Components |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Can generate, amplify, or control energy | Cannot generate energy or provide power gain |
| **Power Gain** | Greater than 1 (provides amplification) | Less than or equal to 1 (loss or unity) |
| **External Power** | Requires external DC bias supply | Does not require external power for operation |
| **Direction of Current** | Unidirectional (in semiconductor devices) | Bidirectional |
| **Examples** | 1. Diode 2. Transistor (BJT/MOSFET) | 1. Resistor 2. Capacitor |

**Examples:**
- **Active:** Diode, Transistor, Op-Amp, MOSFET
- **Passive:** Resistor, Capacitor, Inductor

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for key difference, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain Ohm's Law. What are the limitations of Ohm's Law?**

#### Model Answer:

**Statement:** Ohm's Law states that the voltage across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided physical conditions (such as temperature) remain constant.

$$V = I \times R$$

**Explanation:** If $V$ is doubled, $I$ is also doubled, keeping $R$ constant. The constant of proportionality is the resistance $R$.

**Limitations of Ohm's Law:**
1. **Not valid for non-linear devices** like diodes, transistors, and thyristors.
2. **Not valid for non-ohmic conductors** such as vacuum tubes, semiconductors, and electrolytes.
3. **Fails at very high temperatures** as the resistance changes drastically.
4. **Not applicable for high-frequency AC signals** where skin effect becomes significant.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for limitations)*

---

## 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and waveform, explain the **charging and discharging of a capacitor through a resistor**. Derive the time constant expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $4.7 \ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor is connected in series with a $22 \ \text{k}\Omega$ resistor across a $15 \ \text{V}$ DC supply. Calculate:
   (i) The time constant of the circuit
   (ii) The initial charging current
   (iii) The voltage across the capacitor after 50 ms **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Circuit Description:**
A series RC circuit consists of:
- A DC voltage source $V$
- A switch $S$ (closed at $t = 0$)
- A resistor $R$
- A capacitor $C$ (initially uncharged)
- A voltmeter across $C$ to measure $V_C$

**Charging Phase (0 < t < ∞):**

Applying KVL: $V = IR + V_C$

$$V = R \frac{dQ}{dt} + \frac{Q}{C}$$

Rearranging and solving the differential equation:

$$\boxed{V_C(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})}$$

The charging current:

$$\boxed{I(t) = \frac{V}{R} \cdot e^{-t/RC}}$$

**Discharging Phase (after switch opens):**

The capacitor discharges through $R$:

$$\boxed{V_C(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC}}$$

$$\boxed{I(t) = \frac{V_0}{R} \cdot e^{-t/RC}}$$

**Time Constant:**
The time constant $\tau$ is defined as the time required for the capacitor to charge to **63.2%** of the final voltage or discharge to **36.8%** of the initial voltage.

$$\boxed{\tau = RC \quad \text{(in seconds)}}$$

**Waveform Characteristics:**
- At $t = 0$: $V_C = 0$, $I = I_{max} = V/R$
- At $t = \tau$: $V_C = 0.632V$, $I = 0.368 I_{max}$
- At $t = 5\tau$: $V_C \approx V$ (fully charged, ≈99.3%)

*(7 marks — 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for waveform/characteristics)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- $C = 4.7 \ \mu\text{F} = 4.7 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{F}$
- $R = 22 \ \text{k}\Omega = 22 \times 10^3 \ \Omega$
- $V = 15 \ \text{V}$
- $t = 50 \ \text{ms} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}$

**Step 1: Time Constant**
$$\tau = RC = 22 \times 10^3 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\tau = 22 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-3} = 103.4 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}$$
$$\boxed{\tau = 103.4 \ \text{ms}}$$

**Step 2: Initial Charging Current (at t = 0)**
At $t = 0$, the capacitor acts as a short circuit (since $V_C = 0$):
$$I_0 = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{15}{22 \times 10^3}$$
$$\boxed{I_0 = 0.6818 \ \text{mA}}$$

**Step 3: Voltage across Capacitor at t = 50 ms**
$$V_C(50 \ \text{ms}) = 15 \left(1 - e^{-50 \times 10^{-3} / 103.4 \times 10^{-3}}\right)$$
$$V_C(50 \ \text{ms}) = 15 \left(1 - e^{-0.4835}\right)$$
$$V_C(50 \ \text{ms}) = 15 \left(1 - 0.6167\right)$$
$$V_C(50 \ \text{ms}) = 15 \times 0.3833$$
$$\boxed{V_C(50 \ \text{ms}) \approx 5.75 \ \text{V}}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks for time constant, 2 marks for initial current, 3 marks for voltage at 50 ms)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a **V-I characteristic curve**, explain the working of a **PN junction diode** under forward and reverse bias conditions. Mention the significance of knee voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **construction and working of an NPN bipolar junction transistor (BJT)** in common-emitter configuration. Define $\alpha$ and $\beta$ and derive the relation $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**PN Junction Diode — Construction:**
A PN junction diode is formed by:
1. Joining P-type semiconductor (excess holes) with N-type semiconductor (excess electrons)
2. At the junction, a **depletion region** is formed due to recombination of charge carriers
3. A **barrier potential** is established across the junction (≈0.7 V for Si, 0.3 V for Ge)

**Forward Bias (V-I in Quadrant I):**
- P-side connected to positive terminal of battery
- N-side connected to negative terminal
- Depletion region **narrows**
- Once applied voltage **exceeds the knee voltage (0.7 V for Si)**, current increases rapidly
- Diode offers **low resistance** in forward bias

**Reverse Bias (V-I in Quadrant III):**
- P-side connected to negative terminal of battery
- N-side connected to positive terminal
- Depletion region **widens**
- Only a tiny **reverse saturation current ($I_S$)** flows (in $\mu$A or nA)
- At a critical **breakdown voltage**, the diode undergoes Zener/avalanche breakdown
- Diode offers **high resistance** in reverse bias

**Significance of Knee Voltage ($V_K$ or $V_{\gamma}$):**
- It is the minimum forward voltage at which the diode starts conducting significantly
- For Si: $V_K \approx 0.7 \ \text{V}$
- For Ge: $V_K \approx 0.3 \ \text{V}$
- Below $V_K$, the diode is practically **OFF**; above $V_K$, the diode is **ON**

**Shockley Diode Equation:**
$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / (nV_T)} - 1 \right)$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks construction, 2 marks forward bias, 2 marks reverse bias, 1 mark knee voltage significance)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**NPN BJT — Construction:**
- A BJT has **three terminals**: Emitter (E), Base (B), Collector (C)
- It consists of **two PN junctions**:
  - **Emitter-Base Junction (EBJ)**
  - **Collector-Base Junction (CBJ)**
- In NPN: a thin P-type base is sandwiched between two N-type regions (emitter and collector)
- The base is **thin and lightly doped**; emitter is **heavily doped**; collector is **moderately doped** and **largest in size**

**Common-Emitter (CE) Configuration:**
- Base is the **input terminal**
- Collector is the **output terminal**
- Emitter is **common to both** input and output (often grounded)
- This is the most widely used configuration for amplification

**Working Principle:**
1. The **EBJ is forward biased** (small $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V)
2. The **CBJ is reverse biased** (large $V_{CE}$)
3. Electrons injected from the emitter into the base
4. About 1-5% recombine with holes in the base (form $I_B$)
5. The remaining 95-99% are swept into the collector by the reverse-biased CBJ (form $I_C$)

**Current Definitions:**
$$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} \quad (\text{Common-base current gain, typically 0.95 to 0.99})$$

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} \quad (\text{Common-emitter current gain, typically 20 to 200})$$

**Derivation of $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$:**

Applying KCL at the transistor:
$$I_E = I_B + I_C \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Dividing equation (1) by $I_E$:
$$1 = \frac{I_B}{I_E} + \frac{I_C}{I_E} = \frac{I_B}{I_E} + \alpha$$

$$\frac{I_B}{I_E} = 1 - \alpha \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Taking the ratio $I_C / I_B$:
$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{I_C / I_E}{I_B / I_E} = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1}}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks construction, 2 marks working, 1.5 marks for α and β definitions, 1.5 marks for derivation)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing units:** Writing capacitance in $\Omega$ instead of **Farads**, or inductance in Farads instead of **Henrys**. Always check the SI unit of every quantity.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in inverting op-amp gain:** Many students write $A_v = R_f / R_{in}$ instead of $A_v = -R_f / R_{in}$. The minus sign is **mandatory** for an inverting amplifier.
>
> 3. **Mixing up α and β:** Remember — $\alpha < 1$ (close to 1) and $\beta > 1$ (typically 50–300). They are **not interchangeable**.
>
> 4. **Wrong color code reading:** Students often confuse the **multiplier band position** (3rd band) with the **tolerance band** (4th band). Memorize: "Digits – Multiplier – Tolerance".
>
> 5. **KCL sign convention error:** When applying KCL to a BJT, ensure that the algebraic sum of currents is zero. Emitter current enters (NPN) while base and collector currents leave.
>
> 6. **Drawing graphs without axes labels:** Always label X-axis as $V_D$ and Y-axis as $I_D$ for diode characteristics, with units (V and mA). Unlabelled graphs fetch **zero marks**.
>
> 7. **Writing "diode amplifies" or "resistor amplifies":** These are **active** operations. Resistors/diodes never amplify — they only attenuate or switch.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Night:**

### ✅ Passive Components

- **Resistor (R):** Opposition to current. $V = IR$. Power $P = I^2R$. Color code: 2 digits + multiplier + tolerance.
- **Capacitor (C):** Stores energy in electric field. $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. Blocks DC, passes AC.
- **Inductor (L):** Stores energy in magnetic field. $L = \mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A / l$. Passes DC, blocks AC.
- **Time constant $\tau = RC$** for RC circuits. Capacitor charges to 63.2% in one $\tau$.

### ✅ Active Components

- **Diode:** PN junction, conducts only in forward bias. Knee voltage = 0.7 V (Si), 0.3 V (Ge).
- **Zener Diode:** Special diode used for voltage regulation in reverse breakdown.
- **LED:** Light Emitting Diode — emits light when forward biased.
- **BJT:** Three-terminal device. $I_E = I_B + I_C$. $\beta = I_C/I_B$ (typically 20–200).
- **Op-Amp:** Ideal characteristics: infinite gain, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize

- **Ohm's Law:** $V = IR$
- **Capacitance:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$
- **Inductance:** $L = \mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A / l$
- **Charging:** $V_C(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$
- **Diode:** $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D / nV_T} - 1)$
- **BJT:** $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$
- **Op-Amp Inverting:** $A_v = -R_f / R_{in}$
- **Op-Amp Non-Inverting:** $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_{in}$

### ✅ Key Differences to Remember

| Property | Passive | Active |
|----------|---------|--------|
| Power gain | ≤ 1 | > 1 |
| External power | Not needed | Needed |
| Examples | R, L, C | Diode, BJT, Op-amp |
| Function | Store, dissipate, filter | Generate, amplify, switch |

### ✅ Important Constants

- $V_T = 26 \ \text{mV}$ at 300 K
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{C}$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> Always draw **neat, labelled circuit diagrams** with proper component symbols. Mention **units** in every numerical answer. Use **standard notations** ($V_{CE}$, $I_B$, etc.) and follow a **step-by-step approach** for derivations. This ensures **full marks** in the valuation!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working of PN Junction Diode, V-I Characteristics of PN Junction Diode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Working of PN Junction Diode, V-I Characteristics of PN Junction Diode ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **PN Junction Diode** is a two-terminal, unilateral semiconductor device formed by joining a **P-type** semiconductor material (rich in holes as majority carriers, doped with trivalent impurities like Boron or Gallium) with an **N-type** semiconductor material (rich in free electrons as majority carriers, doped with pentavalent impurities like Phosphorus or Arsenic) across a single crystalline lattice. The boundary surface where the P-region meets the N-region is called the **metallurgical junction**. At this junction, a natural **depletion region** (also called the space-charge region or transition region) is formed due to carrier diffusion and recombination. This depletion region establishes a **built-in potential barrier** $V_{bi}$ (typically **0.7 V** for Silicon and **0.3 V** for Germanium at 300 K) that opposes further carrier diffusion. The device permits conventional current to flow easily in only one direction (P → N) and blocks it in the reverse direction, hence functioning as an electronic "check valve." > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A diode is the most fundamental non-linear, active two-terminal device in electronics. Its entire behavior is governed by the **Shockley Diode Equation** and the underlying semiconductor physics of the depletion region. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Water Check Valve") Imagine a **water pipe with a one-way flap valve**: - When water pushes from the **P-side (left) to N-side (right)**, the flap opens easily, and water flows freely — this is **Forward Bias**. - When water tries to push from the **N-side to P-side**, the flap slams shut, and only a tiny trickle (leakage) seeps through — this is **Reverse Bias**, with a small **Reverse Saturation Current $I_S$**. - If you force water backward with extreme pressure, the flap may burst — this is **Breakdown** (Zener or Avalanche).
