The diode behaves **identically** for electric current: it conducts easily in one direction and blocks current in the other, with a controllable threshold voltage (the "flap's stiffness"). ### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning | |------|--------|------------------| | Built-in Potential | $V_{bi}$ | Internal barrier voltage that opposes carrier diffusion | | Depletion Width | $W$ | Total width of the charge-free region around the junction | | Forward Voltage | $V_F$ | External voltage applied to reduce the barrier (P→N) | | Reverse Saturation Current | $I_S$ | Tiny leakage current flowing in reverse bias | | Knee Voltage / Cut-in Voltage | $V_\gamma$ | Minimum forward voltage at which diode starts conducting significantly | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Temperature:** All diode characteristics in KTU problems are assumed at **T = 300 K** (Room Temperature), unless otherwise explicitly stated, where the thermal voltage $V_T \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Operating Regimes of a PN Junction Diode #### Regime 1: Zero Bias (Equilibrium / Unbiased Condition) - Diffusion of majority carriers across the junction creates the **depletion region**. - Electrons from N-side diffuse to P-side and recombine with holes. - Holes from P-side diffuse to N-side and recombine with electrons. - Result: A region near the junction is depleted of **mobile carriers**, leaving behind only **immobile ionized dopants** (positive donor ions on the N-side, negative acceptor ions on the P-side). - This sets up an **internal electric field** $\mathcal{E}$ pointing from N → P, which creates the **built-in potential $V_{bi}$**. - In equilibrium: **drift current = diffusion current**, so net current = 0. #### Regime 2: Forward Bias (P-side connected to +ve, N-side to −ve of battery) - The applied external voltage **opposes the built-in potential**, reducing the net barrier to $(V_{bi} - V_F)$. - The depletion region **narrows** (width decreases as $W \propto \sqrt{V_{bi} - V_F}$). - Majority carrier injection across the junction **increases exponentially**. - Once $V_F \geq V_\gamma$ (knee voltage), the diode conducts heavily with current rising rapidly. - The current follows: $I \approx I_S \, e^{V_F / nV_T}$ (since the exponential term dominates). #### Regime 3: Reverse Bias (P-side connected to −ve, N-side to +ve of battery) - The applied external voltage **adds to the built-in potential**, increasing the net barrier to $(V_{bi} + V_R)$. - The depletion region **widens** ($W \propto \sqrt{V_{bi} + V_R}$). - The diode conducts only a tiny **reverse saturation current $I_S$** due to thermally generated minority carriers. - For a wide range of reverse voltages, $I \approx -I_S$ (constant, voltage-independent). - At a sufficiently large reverse voltage $V_{BR}$, **breakdown** occurs (Avalanche or Zener mechanism). ### 2.2 Why the Diode Behaves This Way — The Core "Why" > The exponential current-voltage behavior is a direct mathematical consequence of the **Boltzmann distribution of carriers** over the potential barrier at the junction. When we apply forward bias, we are essentially **"tilting the energy hill"** so that more majority carriers can roll down from the P-side (or N-side) to the other side. The number of carriers that can surmount a lowered barrier of height $q(V_{bi} - V_F)$ scales as $e^{V_F/V_T}$, hence the exponential Shockley law. ### 2.3 The Three Regions of the V-I Characteristic Curve 1. **Forward Region** ($V > 0$): Exponential rise in current after the knee. 2. **Reverse Region** ($V < 0$, $\vert V \vert < V_{BR}$): Small constant reverse saturation current $I_S$ (in microamperes for Si, milliamperes for Ge). 3. **Breakdown Region** ($V \leq -V_{BR}$): Sharp, nearly vertical rise in reverse current at a nearly constant voltage — exploited in **Zener diodes** for voltage regulation. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master every equation in this table — these appear in 90% of KTU numerical problems.** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | $k$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; $T$ = absolute temperature (K); $q$ = $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C | | 2 | Thermal Voltage at 300 K | $V_T \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ | Often taken as **25 mV** for easier KTU calculations | | 3 | Built-in Potential | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$ | $N_A, N_D$ = doping concentrations (cm$^{-3}$); $n_i$ = intrinsic carrier concentration | | 4 | Depletion Width | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s}{q}\!\left(V_{bi} - V\right)\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$ | $\varepsilon_s$ = permittivity of semiconductor; $V$ = applied bias (+ve for forward, −ve for reverse) | | 5 | Maximum Electric Field | $\mathcal{E}_{max} = \dfrac{2(V_{bi} - V)}{W}$ | Field peaks at the metallurgical junction | | 6 | Depletion Widths on Each Side | $x_n = \dfrac{W}{1 + N_D/N_A}, \quad x_p = \dfrac{W}{1 + N_A/N_D}$ | Charge neutrality: $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$ | | 7 | **Shockley Diode Equation** | $I = I_S \!\left(e^{V / (nV_T)} - 1\right)$ | $n$ = ideality factor (1 ≤ n ≤ 2); $I_S$ = reverse saturation current (A) | | 8 | Forward Approximation | $I \approx I_S \, e^{V / nV_T}$ | Valid when $V \gg nV_T$ | | 9 | Reverse Approximation | $I \approx -I_S$ | Valid for $V < 0$ and $\vert V \vert \gg V_T$, before breakdown | | 10 | Dynamic / AC Resistance | $r_d = \dfrac{nV_T}{I_D} = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_D \text{ (mA)}}$ | Small-signal resistance at operating point $I_D$ | | 11 | Breakdown Voltage (empirical) | $V_{BR} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{doping}}$ | Higher doping ⇒ lower $V_{BR}$ | | 12 | Knee Voltage (typical) | $V_\gamma \approx 0.7$ V (Si), $0.3$ V (Ge) | Used in piecewise-linear diode model | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility PN junction diodes are the **fundamental building blocks** of nearly every electronic system: - **Rectifiers** (AC → DC conversion in power supplies, phone chargers, laptop adapters). - **Voltage Regulators** (Zener diodes hold output voltage constant). - **Signal Demodulators** in AM radio receivers. - **Logic Gates** (diode-resistor logic, an early form of digital logic). - **Photodiodes & Solar Cells** (when light strikes the depletion region, it generates electron-hole pairs, creating a photocurrent). - **LEDs** (Light Emitting Diodes — when electrons fall across the junction, they emit photons of energy $E = hf \approx E_g$). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Built-in Potential $V_{bi}$ **Starting Point:** In thermal equilibrium, the **Fermi level $E_F$** must be **constant** throughout the entire PN structure (a fundamental requirement of statistical mechanics for systems in equilibrium). On the P-side, $E_F$ lies close to the valence band edge $E_V$, while on the N-side, $E_F$ lies close to the conduction band edge $E_C$. The difference between the conduction band edges on the two sides equals $qV_{bi}$. **Step 1 — Write carrier concentrations in terms of $E_F$:** $$ p_p \approx N_A = n_i \, e^{(E_i - E_F)_p / V_T} \quad \text{(Boltzmann statistics on P-side)} $$ $$ n_n \approx N_D = n_i \, e^{(E_F - E_i)_n / V_T} \quad \text{(Boltzmann statistics on N-side)} $$ **Step 2 — Multiply the two equations:** $$ N_A \cdot N_D = n_i^{\,2} \, e^{\left[(E_i - E_F)_p + (E_F - E_i)_n\right] / V_T} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify the exponent using the band diagram:** The exponent simplifies (since $E_F$ is constant throughout): $$ (E_i - E_F)_p + (E_F - E_i)_n = (E_i)_p - (E_i)_n $$ Across the junction, the intrinsic Fermi level $E_i$ shifts by exactly the built-in potential energy $qV_{bi}$: $$ (E_i)_p - (E_i)_n = q V_{bi} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and solve for $V_{bi}$:** $$ N_A N_D = n_i^{\,2} \, e^{qV_{bi} / V_T} $$ Taking the natural logarithm of both sides: $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right) = \frac{qV_{bi}}{V_T} $$ $$ \boxed{\,V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)\,} $$ **Engineering Insight:** The built-in potential is **larger** for higher doping and **smaller** for larger $n_i$ (which is why Ge diodes have a smaller barrier than Si diodes at the same doping). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Depletion Width $W$ **Starting Point:** Poisson's equation in the depletion region (no free carriers, only ionized dopants): $$ \frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} $$ **Step 1 — Set up the charge density on each side of the junction:** - For $-x_p < x < 0$ (P-side depletion): $\rho = -q N_A$ (immobile ionized acceptors). - For $0 < x < x_n$ (N-side depletion): $\rho = +q N_D$ (immobile ionized donors). - For $x < -x_p$ or $x > x_n$ (neutral regions): $\rho = 0$. **Step 2 — Integrate Poisson's equation on the N-side ($0$ to $x_n$):** $$ \frac{dV}{dx} = -\int \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s} dx = -\frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n) $$ **Step 3 — Integrate a second time to get the potential:** $$ V(x) - V(x_n) = -\frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}(x - x_n)^2 $$ **Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition $V(x_n) = 0$ and $V(-x_p) = V_{bi} - V$ (the total barrier under applied bias $V$):** Similarly on the P-side, integrating gives: $$ V(-x_p) - V(0) = \frac{qN_A}{2\varepsilon_s}x_p^{\,2} $$ **Step 5 — Apply charge neutrality: $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$** From this: $$ x_p = \frac{N_D}{N_A} x_n $$ **Step 6 — Add the two voltage drops and substitute $x_p$:** $$ V_{bi} - V = \frac{qN_A}{2\varepsilon_s}x_p^{\,2} + \frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s}x_n^{\,2} $$ $$ V_{bi} - V = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s}\!\left[N_A \!\left(\frac{N_D}{N_A}\right)^{\!2} x_n^{\,2} + N_D x_n^{\,2}\right] $$ $$ V_{bi} - V = \frac{q N_D x_n^{\,2}}{2\varepsilon_s}\!\left[\frac{N_D}{N_A} + 1\right] = \frac{q N_D x_n^{\,2}}{2\varepsilon_s}\cdot\frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A} $$ **Step 7 — Solve for $x_n$ and $W$:** $$ x_n = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q N_D}\cdot\frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}} $$ $$ x_p = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q N_A}\cdot\frac{N_D}{N_A + N_D}} $$ Adding $x_n + x_p$: $$ \boxed{\,W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q}\!\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Forward Bias** ($V > 0$): $(V_{bi} - V)$ decreases ⇒ $W$ **decreases** (depletion shrinks). > **Reverse Bias** ($V < 0$): $(V_{bi} + \vert V \vert)$ increases ⇒ $W$ **increases** (depletion expands). --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Shockley Diode Equation (Qualitative-Analytical) **Starting Point:** Under forward bias, the barrier on the P-side for holes (and on the N-side for electrons) is reduced from $V_{bi}$ to $V_{bi} - V_F$. The minority carrier concentrations at the edges of the depletion region become: $$ p_n(x_n) = p_{n0} \, e^{V_F / V_T} \quad \text{(holes injected into N-side)} $$ $$ n_p(-x_p) = n_{p0} \, e^{V_F / V_T} \quad \text{(electrons injected into P-side)} $$ where $p_{n0} = n_i^{\,2}/N_D$ and $n_{p0} = n_i^{\,2}/N_A$ are the thermal equilibrium minority carrier concentrations. **Step 1 — Diffusion of minority carriers in the neutral regions:** The injected excess minority carriers diffuse away from the junction and recombine. The minority carrier diffusion currents are: $$ I_{p,\text{diff}} = -qA D_p \frac{dp}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x_n} \quad\text{and}\quad I_{n,\text{diff}} = -qA D_n \frac{dn}{dx}\bigg\vert_{-x_p} $$ **Step 2 — Solve the diffusion equation with the boundary conditions:** Using the standard solution for minority carrier diffusion in a semi-infinite region: $$ I_{p,\text{diff}} = \frac{qA D_p}{L_p} p_{n0} \!\left(e^{V_F/V_T} - 1\right) $$ $$ I_{n,\text{diff}} = \frac{qA D_n}{L_n} n_{p0} \!\left(e^{V_F/V_T} - 1\right) $$ where $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ and $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ are the minority carrier diffusion lengths. **Step 3 — Total current = sum of both diffusion components:** $$ I = I_{p,\text{diff}} + I_{n,\text{diff}} = \underbrace{qA\!\left(\frac{D_p}{L_p} p_{n0} + \frac{D_n}{L_n} n_{p0}\right)}_{I_S} \!\left(e^{V_F / V_T} - 1\right) $$ **Step 4 — Introduce the ideality factor $n$ to account for recombination in the depletion region:** $$ \boxed{\,I = I_S \!\left(e^{V / nV_T} - 1\right)\,} $$ This is the famous **Shockley Diode Equation** — the cornerstone of all diode circuit analysis in KTU examinations. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A silicon PN junction diode at 300 K has $I_S = 10^{-12}$ A, ideality factor $n = 1.5$, and is forward biased with $V = 0.7$ V. Calculate the diode current. **Solution:** $$ V_T = 26 \text{ mV} = 0.026 \text{ V} $$ $$ I = 10^{-12} \!\left(e^{0.7 / (1.5 \times 0.026)} - 1\right) = 10^{-12} \!\left(e^{17.95} - 1\right) $$ $$ e^{17.95} \approx 6.27 \times 10^{7} $$ $$ I \approx 10^{-12} \times 6.27 \times 10^{7} = 6.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 62.7 \text{ μA} $$ > [!NOTE] > A small increase in forward voltage (e.g., from 0.7 V to 0.75 V) causes a **massive increase** in current — this is why diode current-voltage characteristics are **exponentially steep** in the forward region. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Physical Structure of a PN Junction ```mermaid graph LR A["P TYPE REGION
Trivalent Doping
Majority Holes"] --> B["METALLURGICAL JUNCTION
Depletion Boundary"] B --> C["N TYPE REGION
Pentavalent Doping
Majority Electrons"] D["BUILT IN POTENTIAL Vbi"] -.-> B E["DEPLETION WIDTH W"] -.-> B style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Forward Bias Functional Flow ```mermaid graph TD F1["External Battery: + to P, - to N"] --> F2["Applied Voltage Vf Opposes Built in Barrier"] F2 --> F3["Net Barrier Reduces to Vbi minus Vf"] F3 --> F4["Depletion Width W Decreases"] F4 --> F5["Majority Carriers Cross Junction"] F5 --> F6["Exponential Rise in Forward Current I"] F6 --> F7["Diode Operates in Forward Conduction Mode"] style F1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Reverse Bias Functional Flow ```mermaid graph TD R1["External Battery: - to P, + to N"] --> R2["Applied Voltage Vr Adds to Built in Barrier"] R2 --> R3["Net Barrier Increases to Vbi plus Vr"] R3 --> R4["Depletion Width W Increases"] R4 --> R5["Majority Carrier Flow Almost Stops"] R5 --> R6["Only Tiny Reverse Saturation Current Is Flows"] R6 --> R7["Diode Blocks Current in Reverse Direction"] style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R6 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 V-I Characteristic Curve (Block-Level Schematic) ```mermaid graph LR V0["V equals 0V ZERO BIAS"] --> VF["V greater than 0V FORWARD BIAS"] V0 --> VR["V less than 0V REVERSE BIAS"] VR --> VBR["V reaches minus Vbr BREAKDOWN"] VF --> KF["Exponential Rise after Knee Voltage Vgamma"] VR --> RS["Constant small Is region"] VBR --> BD["Sharp vertical rise Avalanche or Zener"] style VF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style VR fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style VBR fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style KF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style BD fill:#FF4500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.5 Diode Symbol & Polarity Reference ```mermaid graph LR A["ANODE P SIDE"] --> B["TRIANGLE Arrow Points in Direction of Conventional Current"] B --> C["CATHODE N SIDE"] D["Bar Line Cathode Mark"] --> C style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#333,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a PN junction diode. Explain the formation of the depletion region with a neat energy band diagram. **Model Answer:** A **PN junction diode** is a semiconductor device formed by joining P-type and N-type materials, allowing current to flow predominantly in one direction (P → N). **Depletion Region Formation:** 1. At the instant of junction formation, free electrons from the N-side diffuse into the P-side and recombine with holes near the boundary. 2. Simultaneously, holes from the P-side diffuse into the N-side and recombine with electrons. 3. This recombination leaves behind **immobile ionized dopants** (negative acceptor ions on the P-side, positive donor ions on the N-side) in a narrow region around the junction. 4. This charge-free region of immobile ions is the **depletion region** (or space-charge region). 5. The separation of charges creates an **internal electric field** that opposes further diffusion, establishing a **built-in potential $V_{bi}$**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the **knee voltage** of a diode? State typical values for Silicon and Germanium diodes. **Model Answer:** The **knee voltage** (also called the **cut-in voltage** or threshold voltage $V_\gamma$) is the minimum forward voltage at which the diode begins to conduct a significant, noticeable current. Below this voltage, the forward current is negligibly small (in nA or μA range). It corresponds to the point on the V-I curve where the exponential rise becomes steep and observable on a standard milliammeter. | Material | Typical Knee Voltage | |----------|----------------------| | Silicon (Si) | **0.7 V** | | Germanium (Ge) | **0.3 V** | The lower value for Ge is because it has a smaller bandgap ($E_g = 0.67$ eV vs. $1.12$ eV for Si), making the built-in barrier smaller. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **V-I characteristics of a PN junction diode** in both forward and reverse bias. Clearly mark the **knee voltage** and **breakdown voltage** regions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A silicon PN junction diode has $I_S = 2 \times 10^{-12}$ A and ideality factor $n = 1.2$ at 300 K. Calculate the forward current when the applied voltage is **0.65 V**. Take $V_T = 25$ mV. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — V-I Characteristics Explanation The V-I characteristics of a PN junction diode are obtained by plotting the current $I$ flowing through the diode against the voltage $V$ applied across it. **Step 1 — Forward Characteristics (V > 0):** - When a small positive voltage is applied to the P-side, the diode initially does not conduct significantly because the external voltage is insufficient to overcome the built-in potential $V_{bi}$. - Once the applied voltage reaches the **knee voltage $V_\gamma$** (≈ 0.7 V for Si), the diode starts conducting heavily, and the current rises **exponentially**. - For $V > V_\gamma$: $I \approx I_S \, e^{V / nV_T}$, where $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K. - The slope of the curve in this region is very steep. **Step 2 — Reverse Characteristics (V < 0):** - When a reverse voltage is applied (P-side negative, N-side positive), the depletion region widens, blocking majority carrier flow. - Only a very small **reverse saturation current $I_S$** flows due to thermally generated minority carriers. This current is nearly constant (independent of reverse voltage) and is in the μA range for Si. - The diode is said to be in **cut-off** or **reverse-biased** state. **Step 3 — Breakdown Region (V ≤ −V_BR):** - If the reverse voltage is increased beyond a critical value called the **breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$**, the reverse current increases very sharply. - The voltage across the diode remains nearly constant at $V_{BR}$. - Breakdown mechanisms: **Zener effect** (in heavily doped diodes, due to quantum tunneling) and **Avalanche effect** (in lightly doped diodes, due to impact ionization). **Key Points to Mark on the Graph:** - Origin (0, 0) - Knee at $V_\gamma = 0.7$ V (Si) - Reverse saturation current $I_S$ in the μA range - Breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$ at large negative voltage (e.g., −50 V or −100 V) --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical **Given:** - $I_S = 2 \times 10^{-12}$ A - $n = 1.2$ - $V = 0.65$ V - $V_T = 25$ mV = 0.025 V - $T = 300$ K **Step 1 — Write Shockley equation:** $$ I = I_S \left(e^{V / nV_T} - 1\right) $$ **Step 2 — Compute the exponent:** $$ \frac{V}{nV_T} = \frac{0.65}{1.2 \times 0.025} = \frac{0.65}{0.030} = 21.67 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential:** $$ e^{21.67} \approx 2.60 \times 10^{9} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute back:** $$ I = 2 \times 10^{-12} \times \left(2.60 \times 10^{9} - 1\right) $$ $$ I \approx 2 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.60 \times 10^{9} = 5.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} $$ $$ \boxed{\,I \approx 5.20 \text{ mA}\,} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[Alternative choice for Q.A]* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **formation of the depletion region and built-in potential** in a PN junction diode under thermal equilibrium. Derive the expression for the **built-in potential $V_{bi}$**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a silicon PN junction diode with $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$, and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$ at 300 K, calculate: - (i) Built-in potential $V_{bi}$ **(3 marks)** - (ii) Depletion width $W$ at zero bias **(4 marks)** Take $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm and $V_T = 26$ mV. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Depletion Region and Built-in Potential **Step 1 — Carrier Diffusion at the Junction:** When P-type and N-type materials are joined, a large concentration gradient exists at the junction. Majority carriers (holes from P-side, electrons from N-side) diffuse across the junction and recombine with their opposite-type carriers. **Step 2 — Formation of Depletion Region:** Near the junction, the mobile carriers are depleted due to recombination, leaving behind a thin region containing only **immobile ionized dopants**: - On the P-side: **negatively charged acceptor ions** ($-qN_A$) - On the N-side: **positively charged donor ions** ($+qN_D$) This charge-imbalance region is the **depletion region** (or space-charge region) of width $W$. **Step 3 — Built-in Potential:** The separation of positive and negative charges creates an **internal electric field** $\mathcal{E}$ directed from N to P. This field produces a **built-in potential $V_{bi}$** across the junction that opposes further carrier diffusion. In thermal equilibrium: $$ \boxed{\,V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)\,} $$ (Detailed derivation is provided in Section 3.1 of these notes.) --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical **Given:** - $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$ - $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$ - $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$ - $V_T = 0.026$ V - $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} = 1.036 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm **Part (i) — Built-in Potential:** $$ V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right) $$ $$ V_{bi} = 0.026 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right) $$ $$ V_{bi} = 0.026 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{33}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.026 \times \ln(4.44 \times 10^{12}) $$ $$ \ln(4.44 \times 10^{12}) = \ln(4.44) + 12 \ln(10) = 1.491 + 27.631 = 29.122 $$ $$ \boxed{\,V_{bi} = 0.026 \times 29.122 \approx 0.757 \text{ V}\,} $$ **Part (ii) — Depletion Width at Zero Bias:** At zero bias, $V = 0$, so the formula simplifies to: $$ W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)} $$ **Step 1 — Compute $\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)$:** Since $N_D \ll N_A$, the term $1/N_D$ dominates: $$ \frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D} = \frac{1}{10^{18}} + \frac{1}{10^{15}} \approx \frac{1}{10^{15}} = 10^{-15} \text{ cm}^{3} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute all values:** $$ W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.757}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times 10^{-15}} $$ $$ W = \sqrt{\frac{1.569 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times 10^{-15}} $$ $$ W = \sqrt{9.806 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-15}} = \sqrt{9.806 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ \boxed{\,W \approx 9.90 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.99 \text{ μm} \approx 1 \text{ μm}\,} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the ideality factor $n$**: Many students write $I = I_S (e^{V/V_T} - 1)$ without including $n$ in the denominator. KTU examiners strictly check for $V / nV_T$ — losing 1 to 2 marks if omitted. > 2. **Sign confusion in Shockley's equation**: Under reverse bias, students often write $I = +I_S$. The correct reverse current is $I \approx -I_S$ (a negative value, indicating conventional current flowing out of the anode). > 3. **Wrong unit for $V_T$**: $V_T$ must be in **Volts** (0.026 V) when used in the exponent, not millivolts. A frequent error is plugging in 26 directly. > 4. **Confusing knee voltage with built-in potential**: Knee voltage $V_\gamma$ is the **applied** forward voltage at which conduction begins (0.7 V for Si). Built-in potential $V_{bi}$ is the **internal** barrier voltage at equilibrium (typically 0.6–0.9 V for Si depending on doping). > 5. **Forgetting the "-1" in Shockley's equation**: At very low forward voltages ($V \ll V_T$), the "-1" is significant. At $V > 0.1$ V, it is negligible and can be dropped. > 6. **Reverse Saturation Current $I_S$ varies with temperature**, doubling approximately every 10 °C rise. A common error is treating $I_S$ as a constant over wide temperature ranges. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master These Before Your Exam:** - A **PN junction diode** is a two-terminal, unidirectional device made by joining P-type and N-type semiconductors. - The **depletion region** contains immobile ionized dopants; it is depleted of mobile carriers. - The **built-in potential** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^{\,2})$ opposes carrier diffusion in equilibrium. - **Forward bias** (P→+, N→−) reduces the barrier, narrows the depletion region, and produces exponential current. - **Reverse bias** (P→−, N→+) increases the barrier, widens the depletion region, and produces a tiny saturation current $I_S$. - **Knee voltage** $V_\gamma \approx 0.7$ V (Si), $0.3$ V (Ge). - **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx 26$ mV at 300 K. - **Shockley equation:** $I = I_S (e^{V/nV_T} - 1)$ — the **master equation** of diode behavior. - **Forward region:** Exponential rise; **Reverse region:** Constant $-I_S$; **Breakdown region:** Sharp rise at $V_{BR}$. - **Depletion width:** $W = \sqrt{(2\varepsilon_s/q)(V_{bi} - V)(1/N_A + 1/N_D)}$. - **Dynamic resistance:** $r_d = nV_T / I_D = 26 \text{ mV} / I_D(\text{mA})$. - **Diodes conduct only in forward bias** (with a small threshold) and block current in reverse bias (until breakdown). - The **ideality factor $n$** lies between 1 (ideal) and 2 (with significant recombination). - **Real-world applications:** Rectifiers, regulators, demodulators, logic gates, photodiodes, LEDs, solar cells, clamping circuits.

topics.title: Zener Diode and Avalanche Breakdown, Basics of Zener Voltage Regulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Zener Diode and Avalanche Breakdown, Basics of Zener Voltage Regulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Zener Diode — Formal KTU Definition

A **Zener Diode** is a specially designed, heavily doped silicon PN-junction diode that is reverse-biased for its normal operation. Unlike a standard rectifier diode (which must avoid reverse breakdown), the Zener diode is engineered to operate **safely and repeatedly in the reverse breakdown region** without being destroyed. The specific reverse voltage at which it breaks down is called the **Zener Voltage ($V_Z$)**, and it remains nearly constant over a wide range of reverse currents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Zener diode is the foundational building block of all DC voltage regulator circuits. KTU expects you to know its symbol, characteristics, the two physical breakdown mechanisms, and its use as a shunt regulator.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Pressure Relief Valve

Imagine a water pipeline connected to a tap. The tap can supply any amount of pressure, but downstream appliances need a *fixed, safe* pressure (say, 2 bar). A **pressure relief valve** is installed that:

- **Stays shut** when the upstream pressure is below 2 bar.
- **Opens automatically** the moment pressure tries to exceed 2 bar, dumping the excess water.
- **Holds the downstream pressure exactly at 2 bar**, regardless of how much excess comes from upstream.

A Zener diode behaves exactly like this valve, but for **voltage** instead of pressure:

- Below $V_Z$, the diode is "closed" (very high resistance, negligible reverse current).
- At $V_Z$, the diode "opens" in reverse and clamps the voltage across itself to $V_Z$.
- The excess current is safely shunted to ground, protecting the load.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Zener Voltage ($V_Z$):** Typically from **2.4 V to 200 V**, depending on doping.
- **Breakdown Knee Current ($I_{ZK}$):** The minimum reverse current required to keep the diode in breakdown. Typical value ≈ **1 mA to 5 mA**.
- **Maximum Zener Current ($I_{ZM}$):** The largest reverse current the diode can safely conduct. Determined by power rating $P_Z = V_Z \times I_Z$.
- **Dynamic Impedance ($Z_Z$):** The small-signal AC resistance in the breakdown region, typically a few ohms. **Lower is better**.
- **Temperature Coefficient:** Negative for $V_Z < 5.6\text{ V}$ (Zener effect dominates), positive for $V_Z > 5.6\text{ V}$ (Avalanche effect dominates).
- **Standard Reference Voltage:** **5.6 V** is the crossover point where the temperature coefficient is approximately zero — useful as a precision reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Clarification:** The diode is called a "Zener diode" in honor of **Clarence Melvin Zener**, the American physicist who first mathematically described the quantum tunneling breakdown mechanism. However, in practice, *both* Zener breakdown and Avalanche breakdown can occur depending on the doping level.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Differences from a Normal Diode

| Parameter | Normal Rectifier Diode | Zener Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Doping Level | Light | **Heavy** (for low $V_Z$) to Moderate (for high $V_Z$) |
| Depletion Width | Wide | **Very Narrow** ($< 1\ \mu m$ for $V_Z < 5.6\text{ V}$) |
| Reverse Breakdown | Destructive (avoided) | **Operative Region** (designed for) |
| Power Rating | Low | Higher; must be sized for $P_Z$ |
| Typical Use | Rectification, switching | **Voltage regulation, clipping, clamping** |

### 2.2 The Two Reverse Breakdown Mechanisms — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Zener Breakdown (Dominant for $V_Z < 5.6\text{ V}$)

1. The depletion region is **extremely narrow** due to heavy doping on both P and N sides.
2. A very strong **electric field** ($E \approx 10^7$ V/m) is established across this narrow region even with a small applied reverse voltage.
3. This intense field is strong enough to **directly rip valence electrons** out of the covalent bonds of the semiconductor lattice — a process called **Quantum Mechanical Tunneling** (field emission).
4. The freed electrons are accelerated by the field, generating a sudden large reverse current.
5. The voltage across the junction remains essentially **pinned at $V_Z$**.

> **Key Insight:** Zener breakdown is a **quantum tunneling** phenomenon. It is *not* dependent on the carriers gaining kinetic energy — the field itself yanks electrons free.

#### B. Avalanche Breakdown (Dominant for $V_Z > 5.6\text{ V}$)

1. The depletion region is **wider** because the doping is moderate.
2. A thermally generated minority carrier (an electron) enters the depletion region and is accelerated by the reverse-bias field.
3. It gains enough **kinetic energy** to collide with a covalent bond of a silicon atom and break it — this collision creates a **new electron-hole pair**.
4. The newly created electron and hole are themselves accelerated and cause further collisions — a **chain reaction (avalanche multiplication)**.
5. A single initial carrier can produce thousands of carriers, causing a sharp rise in reverse current at a well-defined voltage.

> **Key Insight:** Avalanche breakdown is essentially **impact ionization** multiplied by a chain reaction. The voltage at which it occurs increases with temperature because lattice vibrations make collisions more frequent but reduce the mean free path.

### 2.3 Zener vs. Avalanche — The Definitive Comparison

| Feature | Zener Breakdown | Avalanche Breakdown |
|---|---|---|
| Governing Mechanism | Quantum tunneling of valence electrons | Impact ionization and carrier multiplication |
| Dominant Doping | **Very heavy** | Moderate to light |
| Operating $V_Z$ Range | **< 5.6 V** | **> 5.6 V** |
| Depletion Width | Very narrow | Wider |
| Temperature Coefficient | **Negative** ($V_Z$ decreases as $T$ rises) | **Positive** ($V_Z$ increases as $T$ rises) |
| Breakdown Voltage vs. Temp | Falls with temperature | Rises with temperature |
| Junction Behavior | Abrupt, sharp knee | Slightly softer knee |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The crossover at 5.6 V is a direct, frequently-asked question. Memorize the *signs* of the temperature coefficients: Zener → Negative, Avalanche → Positive.

### 2.4 V-I Characteristics — Region-by-Region Walkthrough

Reading the V-I graph from left (large negative $V$) to right (large positive $V$):

- **Forward Region (Quadrant I):** Behaves like an ordinary silicon diode. Conduction begins at approximately **0.7 V**.
- **Reverse Blocking Region (Quadrant III, before knee):** Tiny reverse saturation current ($I_S \approx \mu\text{A}$ or nA). The diode is "off."
- **Breakdown Knee Point:** At $V_R = -V_Z$, the curve **bends sharply downward**. This is the start of the breakdown region.
- **Breakdown / Regulation Region:** The curve is **nearly vertical** with a small slope. Voltage stays close to $V_Z$ as current changes from $I_{ZK}$ up to $I_{ZM}$.
- **Dynamic Impedance Definition:** $Z_Z = \dfrac{\Delta V_Z}{\Delta I_Z}$ (slope inverse in the breakdown region).
- **Beyond $I_{ZM}$:** If current exceeds the rated maximum, the diode overheats and suffers **thermal runaway** and permanent destruction.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_Z$ | Output voltage in regulator (ideal) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ | KCL: total series current splits | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $I_L = \dfrac{V_Z}{R_L}$ | Load current | A |
| 4 | $I_Z = I_S - I_L$ | Zener current | A |
| 5 | $V_S = V_R + V_Z$ | Input voltage splits across $R_S$ and Zener | V |
| 6 | $R_S = \dfrac{V_S - V_Z}{I_S}$ | Series resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 7 | $P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_Z$ | Zener power dissipation | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $P_{Z(\max)} = V_Z \cdot I_{ZM}$ | Maximum rated Zener power | W |
| 9 | $Z_Z = \dfrac{\Delta V_Z}{\Delta I_Z}$ | Dynamic (AC) resistance in breakdown | $\Omega$, typically a few $\Omega$ |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_L}{P_S} \times 100\%$ | Regulator efficiency (very low for shunt) | % |
| 11 | $I_{ZK}$ | Minimum current to keep Zener in breakdown | mA |
| 12 | $I_{ZM} = \dfrac{P_{Z(\max)}}{V_Z}$ | Max safe Zener current | A |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Zener-based shunt regulators are still used in low-power, low-cost reference circuits, **over-voltage protection** (crowbar-style clamps), waveform clippers, and as precision voltage references (when combined with op-amps and temperature-compensated pairs).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Zener Diode Shunt Regulator — Design Equation

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU question):**
A Zener diode with $V_Z = 12\text{ V}$ and $P_{Z(\max)} = 2\text{ W}$ is used as a shunt regulator. The unregulated DC input is $V_S = 24\text{ V}$, and the load is $R_L = 600\ \Omega$. Compute $R_S$, the load current $I_L$, the Zener current $I_Z$, and verify the diode is operating safely.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Output Voltage

Because the Zener is in reverse breakdown, the voltage across the load equals $V_Z$ (assuming ideal Zener).

$$V_{out} = V_L = V_Z = 12\text{ V}$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate the Load Current

The load current is the current through $R_L$ driven by $V_{out}$.

$$I_L = \frac{V_L}{R_L} = \frac{V_Z}{R_L}$$

$$I_L = \frac{12\text{ V}}{600\ \Omega} = 0.020\text{ A} = 20\text{ mA}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate the Maximum Safe Zener Current

Using the maximum power rating formula.

$$I_{ZM} = \frac{P_{Z(\max)}}{V_Z} = \frac{2\text{ W}}{12\text{ V}} = 0.1667\text{ A} = 166.7\text{ mA}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply KVL to Find the Voltage Across the Series Resistor

The series resistor $R_S$ must drop the difference between input and output.

$$V_{R_S} = V_S - V_Z = 24\text{ V} - 12\text{ V} = 12\text{ V}$$

#### Step 5 — Calculate $R_S$ (Two Methods)

**Method 1 — Using worst-case (no load, all current goes into Zener):**

$$R_S = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_{ZM}} = \frac{12\text{ V}}{0.1667\text{ A}} = 72\ \Omega$$

**Method 2 — Using the *actual* operating current (sum of load and Zener current):**

Assume the Zener is biased somewhere in the middle, say $I_Z = I_{ZM}/2 = 83.3\text{ mA}$.

$$I_S = I_L + I_Z = 20\text{ mA} + 83.3\text{ mA} = 103.3\text{ mA}$$

$$R_S = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_S} = \frac{12\text{ V}}{0.1033\text{ A}} \approx 116.1\ \Omega$$

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always use **Method 1** if the load may be disconnected, because *all* the current then flows through the Zener and it must not exceed $I_{ZM}$.

#### Step 6 — Verify Zener Safety

With $R_S = 72\ \Omega$, total current from source:

$$I_S = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{12\text{ V}}{72\ \Omega} = 166.7\text{ mA}$$

Zener current:

$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 166.7\text{ mA} - 20\text{ mA} = 146.7\text{ mA}$$

Zener power dissipation:

$$P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_Z = 12\text{ V} \times 0.1467\text{ A} = 1.76\text{ W}$$

Since $1.76\text{ W} < P_{Z(\max)} = 2\text{ W}$, the Zener is operating safely. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Line Regulation (Stabilization Factor)

**Line regulation** is the change in output voltage for a change in input voltage. It is one of the most important figures of merit for a regulator.

$$\text{Line Regulation } (S_V) = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_S} = \frac{Z_Z}{R_S + Z_Z} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + Z_Z}$$

When $R_L \gg Z_Z$ (typical for voltage reference circuits), this simplifies to:

$$S_V \approx \frac{Z_Z}{R_S + Z_Z}$$

The **smaller** $Z_Z$ is, the **better** the regulation. This is why Zener diodes with low dynamic impedance are preferred.

### 3.3 Derivation: Load Regulation

Load regulation is the change in output voltage when the load current changes.

$$\text{Load Regulation} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

Where $V_{NL}$ is the no-load output and $V_{FL}$ is the full-load output. Better regulators have a value **closer to 0%**.

For a Zener regulator with finite $Z_Z$:

$$V_{out} \approx V_Z + I_Z \cdot Z_Z$$

If $I_L$ increases, $I_Z$ decreases (since $I_S$ is roughly constant), so $V_{out}$ drops slightly.

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Condition for Proper Regulation

The Zener will *fail to regulate* if any of the following is violated:

| Condition | Inequality | Consequence of Violation |
|---|---|---|
| Diode is in breakdown | $I_Z \geq I_{ZK}$ | Output falls below $V_Z$ |
| Diode is not overloaded | $I_Z \leq I_{ZM}$ | Diode burns out |
| Input is high enough | $V_S \geq V_Z$ | No reverse bias, no regulation |
| Series resistor is bounded | $R_{S(\min)} \leq R_S \leq R_{S(\max)}$ | $R_S$ too small → excess $I_Z$; $R_S$ too large → no conduction |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Zener Shunt Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Unregulated DC Source VS] --> rsNode[Series Resistor RS]
    rsNode --> nodeJunction{Node A}
    nodeJunction --> zenerD[Zener Diode VZ to Ground]
    nodeJunction --> loadNode[Load Resistor RL]
    loadNode --> gndA[Ground]
    zenerD --> gndB[Ground]
    gndA --- gndB
    style src fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rsNode fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style zenerD fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style loadNode fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Current $I_S$ enters from the unregulated source VS, passes through RS, and reaches Node A. From Node A, KCL splits the current: $I_Z$ flows down through the Zener to ground, and $I_L$ flows down through the load. The voltage at Node A is **clamped at VZ**.

### 4.2 Mermaid V-I Characteristic (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph QI[Quadrant I - Forward]
        fwd[VF approx 0.7V for Si]
    end
    subgraph QIII[Quadrant III - Reverse]
        revBlock[Reverse Blocking - IS tiny]
        revKnee[Knee Point at VZ]
        revBreak[Breakdown Region - nearly vertical]
        revDestroy[Thermal Runaway at IZM]
    end
    revBlock --> revKnee
    revKnee --> revBreak
    revBreak --> revDestroy
    style revKnee fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style revBreak fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style revDestroy fill:#d90429,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style fwd fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Regulator Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains] --> B[Transformer Step Down]
    B --> C[Bridge Rectifier]
    C --> D[Smoothing Capacitor]
    D --> E[Unregulated DC Bus VS]
    E --> F[Series Resistor RS]
    F --> G[Zener Shunt Reference]
    G --> H[Filtered Regulated DC Output VZ]
    H --> I[Load Circuit]
    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style I fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the complete signal chain for a linear DC power supply. The Zener is the *reference element* that defines the output voltage; everything upstream (transformer, rectifier, capacitor) is just a means of providing a *higher-than-needed* voltage so that the Zener has something to drop across.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *Differentiate between Zener breakdown and Avalanche breakdown mechanisms. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Zener Breakdown | Avalanche Breakdown |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Quantum mechanical tunneling of valence electrons due to a very high electric field across a narrow depletion region | Impact ionization — a high-energy carrier collides with a lattice atom, freeing a new electron-hole pair in a chain reaction |
| Doping | Very heavily doped | Moderately doped |
| Operating Voltage | Below **5.6 V** | Above **5.6 V** |
| Temperature Coefficient | **Negative** ($V_Z$ falls as $T$ rises) | **Positive** ($V_Z$ rises as $T$ rises) |

The crossover occurs at approximately **5.6 V**, where the temperature coefficient is near zero. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *A Zener diode has $V_Z = 8.2\text{ V}$ and $P_{Z(\max)} = 1\text{ W}$. Find the maximum safe Zener current $I_{ZM}$. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Using the power formula for the Zener in breakdown:

$$I_{ZM} = \frac{P_{Z(\max)}}{V_Z}$$

$$I_{ZM} = \frac{1\text{ W}}{8.2\text{ V}}$$

$$I_{ZM} = 0.1220\text{ A} \approx 122\text{ mA}$$

**Answer:** $I_{ZM} \approx 122\text{ mA}$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Mandate (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Zener diode as a **shunt voltage regulator**. Derive the expression for the **series resistance $R_S$**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A Zener diode with $V_Z = 15\text{ V}$ and $P_{Z(\max)} = 3\text{ W}$ is used in a regulator circuit with input $V_S = 24\text{ V}$ and load $R_L = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate (i) the maximum safe Zener current, (ii) the value of $R_S$ for safe operation, and (iii) the load and Zener currents. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Zener as Shunt Voltage Regulator — Operation and $R_S$ Derivation:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):** A Zener diode is connected with its **cathode** at the positive terminal of the unregulated DC source and its **anode** at ground, i.e., it is **reverse-biased**. A series resistor $R_S$ is placed between the source $V_S$ and the load $R_L$. The Zener is in **parallel (shunt)** with $R_L$.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**

- When $V_S$ is large enough to push the reverse voltage across the Zener beyond $V_Z$, the Zener enters the **breakdown region**.
- Once in breakdown, the voltage across the Zener (and therefore across $R_L$) is **clamped at $V_Z$**, regardless of changes in $V_S$ or $R_L$.
- Any excess current $I_S - I_L$ is **diverted (shunted)** through the Zener to ground.
- If $V_S$ falls below $V_Z$, the Zener stops regulating and the output simply follows $V_S$ (minus the drop on $R_S$).

**Derivation of $R_S$ (2 marks):** Applying KVL around the loop:

$$V_S = I_S \cdot R_S + V_Z$$

By KCL at the output node:

$$I_S = I_Z + I_L$$

Substituting and solving for $R_S$:

$$R_S = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_Z + I_L}$$

For the Zener to operate safely, $I_{ZK} \leq I_Z \leq I_{ZM}$, giving bounds:

$$R_{S(\max)} = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L} \quad\quad R_{S(\min)} = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_{ZM}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Maximum safe Zener current:**

$$I_{ZM} = \frac{P_{Z(\max)}}{V_Z} = \frac{3\text{ W}}{15\text{ V}} = 0.200\text{ A} = 200\text{ mA}$$

**(ii) Series resistance $R_S$ (worst case: $I_Z = I_{ZM}$):**

$$R_S = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_{ZM}} = \frac{24 - 15}{0.200} = \frac{9}{0.200} = 45\ \Omega$$

**(iii) Currents:**

Load current:

$$I_L = \frac{V_Z}{R_L} = \frac{15\text{ V}}{1000\ \Omega} = 15\text{ mA}$$

Source current (with $R_S = 45\ \Omega$):

$$I_S = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{9}{45} = 0.200\text{ A} = 200\text{ mA}$$

Zener current:

$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 200 - 15 = 185\text{ mA}$$

**Verification:** $I_Z = 185\text{ mA} < I_{ZM} = 200\text{ mA}$ ✓ — Safe operation.

**Answer:** (i) 200 mA, (ii) $R_S = 45\ \Omega$, (iii) $I_L = 15\text{ mA}$, $I_Z = 185\text{ mA}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the **V-I characteristics** of a Zener diode. Mark clearly the **forward, reverse blocking, and breakdown regions**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With the help of neat sketches, explain **Zener breakdown** and **Avalanche breakdown** mechanisms. State **two differences** between them. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) V-I Characteristics of a Zener Diode (7 marks):**

**Sketch (3 marks):** The V-I curve is plotted with forward voltage on the right (Quadrant I) and reverse voltage on the left (Quadrant III). The forward region rises steeply after the cut-in voltage of **0.7 V**. In the reverse direction, a tiny current $I_S$ flows until the voltage reaches $-V_Z$, where the curve **bends sharply downward** (this is the **knee**), then runs almost vertically as the current increases rapidly with little change in voltage.

**Region-wise Description (4 marks):**

| Region | Voltage Range | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward** | $V > 0.7\text{ V}$ | Conducts like a normal Si diode; current rises exponentially |
| **Reverse Blocking** | $0 < V_R < V_Z$ | Only a small reverse saturation current $I_S$ flows (in $\mu\text{A}$/nA) |
| **Breakdown Knee** | $V_R = V_Z$ | The curve "breaks" — Zener/Avalanche mechanism activates |
| **Breakdown (Regulation)** | $V_R \approx V_Z$ | Voltage almost constant; current can vary widely between $I_{ZK}$ and $I_{ZM}$ |
| **Beyond $I_{ZM}$** | $V_R \approx V_Z$ | Diode overheats; thermal runaway → permanent damage |

**Key Points (1 mark each):**
- The slope in the breakdown region is small → very low **dynamic impedance $Z_Z$**.
- The "knee" must be sharp for good regulation.

**(b) Zener and Avalanche Breakdown — Sketches and Differences (7 marks):**

**Zener Breakdown (2 marks):** In a heavily doped diode, the depletion region is **extremely thin** (often $< 1\ \mu\text{m}$). A strong electric field ($\sim 10^7$ V/m) builds up across this thin layer. This field is sufficient to **tear valence electrons directly out of the covalent bonds** — a process called **field emission** or **quantum tunneling**. The freed electrons produce a sudden, large reverse current at a well-defined voltage $V_Z$. The process is essentially field-driven and does not require the carriers to gain kinetic energy.

**Avalanche Breakdown (2 marks):** In a moderately doped diode, the depletion region is wider. A thermally generated minority carrier entering this region is accelerated by the reverse-bias field and gains enough kinetic energy to **collide with a lattice atom**. The collision breaks a covalent bond, creating a new electron-hole pair. These new carriers are themselves accelerated and create more pairs, leading to a **chain reaction** (impact ionization or carrier multiplication). A single carrier can spawn thousands, leading to a sharp current rise at the avalanche voltage.

**Two Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Zener | Avalanche |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Field-induced tunneling | Impact ionization and chain reaction |
| Operating Voltage | Below 5.6 V | Above 5.6 V |
| Temperature Coefficient | Negative | Positive |
| Depletion Width | Very narrow | Wider |

**One additional difference (1 mark):** Zener breakdown is essentially independent of temperature at the crossover, while Avalanche breakdown voltage increases with temperature due to reduced mean free path of carriers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the **knee current $I_{ZK}$** with the maximum current $I_{ZM}$. The diode *stops regulating* if $I_Z$ falls below $I_{ZK}$, and *burns out* if it exceeds $I_{ZM}$.
> 2. **Do not** blindly use $I_L + I_{ZM}$ to compute $R_S$. Use $I_{ZM}$ alone (worst case, no load) to ensure safety if the load is ever disconnected.
> 3. **Do not** state that Zener breakdown is *caused* by heat. It is caused by the *electric field*; the temperature coefficient sign is a *secondary* effect.
> 4. **Do not** forget the units: report currents in mA and power in mW or W consistently.
> 5. **Do not** omit the diode symbol direction (cathode positive) in the regulator circuit diagram — many students draw it forward-biased.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Zener diode** is a heavily doped PN-junction diode designed to operate in **reverse breakdown**.
- ✅ The two breakdown mechanisms are **Zener (tunneling)** and **Avalanche (impact ionization)**.
- ✅ **Crossover voltage = 5.6 V.** Below → Zener dominant; Above → Avalanche dominant.
- ✅ **Temperature coefficient:** Zener → **Negative**; Avalanche → **Positive**; Crossover at 5.6 V → Zero.
- ✅ Key formulas (memorize cold):
  - $V_{out} = V_Z$
  - $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ (KCL)
  - $R_S = \dfrac{V_S - V_Z}{I_S}$ (KVL)
  - $P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_Z$
  - $I_{ZM} = \dfrac{P_{Z(\max)}}{V_Z}$
- ✅ Design constraint: keep $I_{ZK} \le I_Z \le I_{ZM}$.
- ✅ Line Regulation $\approx \dfrac{Z_Z}{R_S + Z_Z}$ — **smaller $Z_Z$** is better.
- ✅ Zener regulators are **shunt** regulators — they *waste* power in the series resistor; efficiency is low.
- ✅ Always draw the **cathode at the positive rail** and the **anode at ground** (reverse-biased configuration).
- ✅ The V-I characteristic has a **nearly vertical breakdown region** — that is the "regulation signature."
- ✅ Real-world uses: voltage reference, over-voltage protection, clipper, clamper, crowbar trigger, level shifter.
- ✅ Common exam trap: load disconnected → all source current goes through Zener → must not exceed $I_{ZM}$.

---

*End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Module 3, Zener Diode & Voltage Regulator.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of DC Power Supply, Circuit and Working of Half Wave, Full Wave and Bridge Rectifiers, Ripple Factor (With and Without Capacitor Filters)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Block Diagram of DC Power Supply, Circuit and Working of Half Wave, Full Wave and Bridge Rectifiers, Ripple Factor (With and Without Capacitor Filters) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The DC Power Supply — Formal Definition A **DC Power Supply** is an electronic circuit that converts the standard **Alternating Current (AC)** mains supply (typically **230 V, 50 Hz** in India) into a smooth, regulated **Direct Current (DC)** voltage suitable for powering electronic circuits, microcontrollers, op-amps, and communication systems. The complete conversion process involves a cascade of four functional stages: **Step-Down (or Step-Up) → Rectification → Smoothing/Filtering → Voltage Regulation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In GXEST104, students must memorize the exact sequence of blocks, the function of each block, and identify which block introduces pulsations, which removes them, and which finally stabilizes the output against input/load variations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Purifier" Viewpoint Think of a household RO water purifier: 1. **Municipal water (AC Mains)** arrives with strong pressure fluctuations (pulsating AC). 2. A **pressure-reducing valve (Transformer)** steps it down to a usable level. 3. A **sediment filter (Rectifier)** blocks the "backward" flow, allowing only one-way current (pulsating DC). 4. A **polishing carbon filter (Capacitor Filter)** smooths out the remaining ripples, producing a steady, drinkable flow (pure DC). 5. A **UV sterilizer + TDS controller (Voltage Regulator)** ensures the final output pressure and purity remain constant regardless of input variations (regulated DC). > [!NOTE] > **Why is regulated DC needed?** Most active electronic devices (transistors, ICs, microcontrollers) are polarity-sensitive. An unregulated DC contains AC components (ripples) that cause heating, noise, hum in audio amplifiers, and erratic logic behavior. ### 1.3 The Four Canonical Blocks of a DC Power Supply | Block # | Stage Name | Function | Standard Component | |:-------:|:-----------|:---------|:-------------------| | **1** | Transformer | Steps down/up AC voltage; provides isolation from mains | Iron-core step-down transformer | | **2** | Rectifier | Converts bidirectional AC into unidirectional pulsating DC | Diode (1 for HWR, 2 for FWR, 4 for Bridge) | | **3** | Filter | Smooths pulsating DC into nearly constant DC | Capacitor (C-filter), LC, or π-filter | | **4** | Regulator | Maintains constant DC output despite line/load changes | Zener diode, 78xx series IC, LM317 | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Cue:** A common KTU question reads — *"Which block removes ripples? Which block removes pulsations entirely?"* Answer: The **filter** reduces ripples; the **regulator** removes them almost completely. The rectifier only converts AC to pulsating DC; it does NOT filter. ### 1.4 Standardized Physical Constants (Use Bold in Answers) - Mains Frequency in India: **f = 50 Hz** - Angular Frequency: **ω = 2πf = 314.16 rad/s** - Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) is a **diode-rating parameter**, not a fixed number. - Ripple Factor (γ) is **dimensionless**. - Conversion factor: **Vm = Vrms × √2** for a pure sine wave. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Rectifier Topology #### 2.1.1 Half Wave Rectifier (HWR) - Uses **one diode** in series with the load. - Conducts **only during the positive half-cycle** of the AC input. - During the negative half-cycle, the diode is reverse-biased and **blocks** the input; no current flows through the load. - Output is a series of positive half-sine pulses separated by equal-duration zero intervals. - **Drawbacks:** High ripple content, poor transformer utilization factor (TUF ≈ 0.287), low efficiency (40.6 %). #### 2.1.2 Full Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier (FWR-CT) - Uses a **center-tapped transformer** with two identical secondary windings and **two diodes**. - During the **positive half-cycle**, D1 conducts and D2 is OFF. - During the **negative half-cycle**, D2 conducts and D1 is OFF. - The load current always flows in the **same direction**, producing two positive half-sine pulses per full cycle. - **Advantage over HWR:** Doubled DC output, ripple frequency = **2f = 100 Hz**, smaller ripple factor (0.482). - **Drawback:** Requires a center-tapped transformer, which is bulky and expensive; PIV rating of diodes = **2Vm**. #### 2.1.3 Bridge Rectifier (BWR) - Uses **four diodes** arranged in a bridge (Graetz) topology. - Does **NOT require a center-tapped transformer** — a simple secondary suffices. - During each half-cycle, **two diodes conduct in series**, guiding current through the load in one direction. - Output waveform is identical to a center-tapped FWR (two positive half-sines per cycle). - **PIV rating of each diode = Vm** (half that of the center-tapped FWR). - **Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) = 0.8106** (highest among the three topologies). - **Drawback:** Voltage drop across two series diodes = **2 × 0.7 V = 1.4 V**, reducing efficiency slightly in low-voltage applications. ### 2.2 The Ripple Factor (γ) — Conceptual Foundation The **Ripple Factor** quantifies the amount of unwanted AC (ripple) remaining in the DC output after rectification. It is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of the AC component to the **average (DC) value** of the output waveform. > [!IMPORTANT] > A smaller ripple factor means a purer DC output. An ideal DC supply has **γ = 0**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** Absolute value bars and divisibility in tables use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown syntax. | Parameter | Symbol | Half Wave Rectifier (HWR) | Full Wave / Bridge Rectifier (FWR / BWR) | Unit | |:----------|:------:|:------------------------:|:---------------------------------------:|:----:| | Peak Output Voltage | $V_m$ | $V_m$ | $V_m$ | V | | DC (Average) Output Voltage | $V_{DC}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ | V | | RMS Output Voltage | $V_{RMS}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | V | | DC Load Current | $I_{DC}$ | $\dfrac{I_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{2 I_m}{\pi}$ | A | | RMS Load Current | $I_{RMS}$ | $\dfrac{I_m}{2}$ | $\dfrac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | A | | Ripple Factor (No Filter) | $\gamma$ | $1.21$ | $0.482$ | dimensionless | | Rectification Efficiency | $\eta$ | $40.6 \,\%$ | $81.2 \,\%$ | dimensionless | | PIV of each Diode | $PIV$ | $V_m$ | $2 V_m$ (CT) $\vert$ $V_m$ (Bridge) | V | | Ripple Frequency | $f_r$ | $f$ (50 Hz) | $2f$ (100 Hz) | Hz | | Transformer Utilization Factor | $TUF$ | $0.287$ | $0.693$ (CT) $\vert$ $0.8106$ (Bridge) | dimensionless | ### 2.4 The Filter — Why a Capacitor Works A **filter capacitor** is placed in parallel with the load. It charges up to the peak voltage ($V_m$) during each conducting pulse and then **discharges slowly through the load** when the diode is OFF. This charging-and-discharging action "fills in the gaps" between the pulses, producing a much smoother DC. - **Charging time-constant:** $\tau_c = R_s C$ (where $R_s$ is diode forward resistance; very small). - **Discharging time-constant:** $\tau_d = R_L C$ (where $R_L$ is the load resistance; large). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Capacitor-input filters are universal in low-current power supplies (phone chargers, router adapters, 5V Arduino supplies). For high-current industrial supplies, **LC (inductor-capacitor) or π-filters** are preferred because pure capacitors cause high peak diode currents and transformer heating. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** All algebraic steps are shown explicitly. No truncation. ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — DC (Average) Output Voltage of a Half Wave Rectifier **Given:** Output voltage during positive half-cycle = $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $0 \le \omega t \le \pi$. During negative half-cycle, $v_o(t) = 0$ for $\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$. The average value over a full period $T$ is: $$ V_{DC} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\theta) \, d\theta $$ Substituting the piecewise definition: $$ V_{DC} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} 0 \, d\theta \right] $$ Evaluate the first integral: $$ \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta \;=\; V_m \left[ -\cos(\theta) \right]_{0}^{\pi} \;=\; V_m \left[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(0) \right] $$ $$ =\; V_m \left[ -(-1) + 1 \right] \;=\; V_m (2) \;=\; 2 V_m $$ Therefore: $$ V_{DC} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 V_m \;=\; \frac{V_m}{\pi} \;\approx\; 0.318 \, V_m $$ > **Logic Anchor:** Only the positive half contributes to the average; the negative half contributes zero. The factor $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ is the average operator over a full cycle. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Output Voltage of a Half Wave Rectifier The RMS definition (root of the mean of the square): $$ V_{RMS} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \left[ v_o(\theta) \right]^2 d\theta} $$ Substituting: $$ V_{RMS} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\theta) \, d\theta} $$ Use the identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$: $$ V_{RMS}^2 \;=\; \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2} \, d\theta \;=\; \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ \theta - \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{2} \right]_{0}^{\pi} $$ $$ =\; \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ \pi - 0 - 0 + 0 \right] \;=\; \frac{V_m^2}{4} $$ Take the square root: $$ V_{RMS} \;=\; \frac{V_m}{2} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ripple Factor of a Half Wave Rectifier (Without Filter) The AC (ripple) RMS component is: $$ V_{AC} \;=\; \sqrt{V_{RMS}^2 - V_{DC}^2} $$ Substitute known values: $$ V_{AC} \;=\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_m}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{V_m}{\pi}\right)^2} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4} - \frac{V_m^2}{\pi^2}} $$ Factor out $V_m^2$: $$ V_{AC} \;=\; V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{\pi^2}} \;=\; V_m \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 4}{4\pi^2}} $$ Apply ripple factor definition $\gamma = \dfrac{V_{AC}}{V_{DC}}$: $$ \gamma_{HWR} \;=\; \frac{V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 - 4}{4\pi^2}}}{\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{V_m} \cdot \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \sqrt{\pi^2 - 4} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{\pi^2 - 4}}{2} $$ Numerical evaluation: $$ \gamma_{HWR} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{9.8696 - 4}}{2} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{5.8696}}{2} \;=\; \frac{2.4226}{2} \;\approx\; 1.211 $$ > **Standard Tabulated Value:** $\gamma_{HWR} = 1.21$ ✓ ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — DC and Ripple Factor of a Full Wave / Bridge Rectifier For a full-wave rectified sine wave, $v_o(\theta) = V_m \sin(\theta)$ for $0 \le \theta \le \pi$ AND $v_o(\theta) = V_m \sin(\theta - \pi)$ for $\pi \le \theta \le 2\pi$. By symmetry, the negative half is "flipped" to be positive. **DC Component:** $$ V_{DC} \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot 2 V_m \;=\; \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} $$ > **Note:** The average is now computed over half-period (π) instead of full-period (2π), because every half-cycle contributes a positive lobe. **RMS Component:** $$ V_{RMS}^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\theta) \, d\theta \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \frac{V_m^2 \pi}{2} \;=\; \frac{V_m^2}{2} $$ $$ V_{RMS} \;=\; \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} $$ **Ripple Factor:** $$ V_{AC} \;=\; \sqrt{V_{RMS}^2 - V_{DC}^2} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2} - \frac{4 V_m^2}{\pi^2}} $$ $$ \gamma_{FWR} \;=\; \frac{V_{AC}}{V_{DC}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{\dfrac{V_m^2}{2} - \dfrac{4V_m^2}{\pi^2}}}{\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}} $$ Simplify the numerator: $$ V_{AC} \;=\; \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}} \;=\; \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 8}{\pi^2}} \;=\; \frac{V_m}{\pi\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\pi^2 - 8} $$ Therefore: $$ \gamma_{FWR} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{V_m}{\pi\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\pi^2 - 8}}{\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{\pi^2 - 8}}{2\sqrt{2}} $$ Numerical evaluation: $$ \gamma_{FWR} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{9.8696 - 8}}{2 \cdot 1.4142} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{1.8696}}{2.8284} \;=\; \frac{1.3674}{2.8284} \;\approx\; 0.483 $$ > **Standard Tabulated Value:** $\gamma_{FWR} = 0.482$ ✓ ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Ripple Factor with a Capacitor Filter (Approximate Formula) When a filter capacitor $C$ is connected across the load $R_L$, the discharge between pulses is approximately linear (exponential truncated to a short interval). Let $T$ be the time between successive peaks. For **full-wave**, $T = \dfrac{1}{2f} = 0.01 \text{ s}$ (at 50 Hz). The peak-to-peak ripple voltage is: $$ V_{r(pp)} \;=\; \frac{I_{DC}}{f_r \, C} \;=\; \frac{V_{DC}}{f_r \, R_L \, C} $$ where $f_r$ is the ripple frequency (50 Hz for HWR, 100 Hz for FWR). The RMS ripple voltage is approximately (for a sawtooth-like discharge waveform): $$ V_{r(RMS)} \;=\; \frac{V_{r(pp)}}{2\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \frac{I_{DC}}{2\sqrt{3} \, f_r \, C} $$ Therefore the ripple factor is: $$ \gamma_{filtered} \;=\; \frac{V_{r(RMS)}}{V_{DC}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \, f_r \, R_L \, C} $$ > **Physical Interpretation:** Larger $C$, larger $R_L$, or higher $f_r$ all **reduce** the ripple factor. This is why designers choose $C \ge 1000 \, \mu F$ in 5 V supplies and why a bridge rectifier (with $f_r = 100$ Hz) is preferred over a half-wave for the same filter capacitor. ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison | Property | HWR | FWR (CT) | Bridge | |:---------|:---:|:--------:|:------:| | Diodes required | 1 | 2 | 4 | | Transformer type | Normal | Center-tapped | Normal | | Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) | $V_m$ | $2 V_m$ | $V_m$ | | Output frequency of pulses | $f$ | $2f$ | $2f$ | | Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) | 0.287 | 0.693 | 0.8106 | | Total diode drop | 0.7 V | 0.7 V | 1.4 V | | Suitability for low-voltage | Excellent | Good | Poor (1.4 V loss) | | Cost | Lowest | Highest | Moderate | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes. No reserved keywords used. Labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting. ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete DC Power Supply ```mermaid flowchart LR mains1["AC MAINS
230V 50Hz"] --> transf1["BLOCK 1
TRANSFORMER
STEP DOWN AC"] transf1 --> rectfier1["BLOCK 2
RECTIFIER
AC TO PULSATING DC"] rectfier1 --> filter1["BLOCK 3
FILTER
SMOOTH PULSATIONS"] filter1 --> reg1["BLOCK 4
REGULATOR
CONSTANT DC OUTPUT"] reg1 --> load1["DC LOAD
ELECTRONIC CIRCUIT"] style mains1 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style transf1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38f00,color:#000 style rectfier1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 style filter1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style reg1 fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 style load1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Waveform Transformations ```mermaid flowchart TB waveA["STAGE 1: AC SINE WAVE
BIPOLAR SYMMETRY"] -->|TRANSFORMER| waveB["STAGE 2: STEPPED DOWN AC
BIPOLAR SMALLER AMPLITUDE"] waveB -->|RECTIFIER HWR| waveC["STAGE 3A: HWR OUTPUT
POSITIVE HALF PULSES WITH ZEROS"] waveB -->|RECTIFIER FWR| waveD["STAGE 3B: FWR OUTPUT
CONTINUOUS POSITIVE PULSES"] waveC -->|CAPACITOR FILTER| waveE["STAGE 4: HWR FILTERED
PEAKED DECAY RIPPLES"] waveD -->|CAPACITOR FILTER| waveF["STAGE 5: FWR FILTERED
LOW RIPPLE SMOOTH DC"] waveE -->|REGULATOR| waveG["STAGE 6: REGULATED DC
CONSTANT CLEAN DC"] waveF -->|REGULATOR| waveG style waveA fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style waveB fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style waveC fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style waveD fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style waveE fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style waveF fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style waveG fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Three Rectifier Topologies ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph halfWave["HALF WAVE RECTIFIER TOPOLOGY"] hwInput["AC SOURCE"] --> hwDiode["DIODE D1
ONEWAY VALVE"] hwDiode --> hwLoad["LOAD RESISTOR RL"] hwLoad --> hwReturn["RETURN TO SOURCE"] end subgraph fullWaveCT["FULL WAVE CENTER TAPPED TOPOLOGY"] ctInput["AC SOURCE WITH CENTER TAP"] --> ctUpper["UPPER WINDING"] ctInput --> ctLower["LOWER WINDING"] ctUpper --> ctD1["DIODE D1"] ctLower --> ctD2["DIODE D2"] ctD1 --> ctJoin["COMMON LOAD NODE"] ctD2 --> ctJoin ctJoin --> ctLoad["LOAD RESISTOR RL"] ctLoad --> ctCenter["CENTER TAP GROUND"] end subgraph bridgeRect["BRIDGE RECTIFIER TOPOLOGY"] brInput["AC SOURCE"] --> brD1["D1 TOP LEFT"] brInput --> brD3["D3 BOTTOM LEFT"] brD1 --> brD2["D2 TOP RIGHT"] brD3 --> brD4["D4 BOTTOM RIGHT"] brD2 --> brLoad["LOAD PLUS"] brD4 --> brLoadMinus["LOAD MINUS"] end style hwInput fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style hwDiode fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style hwLoad fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style ctInput fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style ctD1 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style ctD2 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style ctLoad fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style brInput fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style brD1 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style brD2 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style brD3 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style brD4 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style brLoad fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Functional Charge-Discharge Cycle of the Filter Capacitor ```mermaid flowchart LR cdStart["DIODE FORWARD BIASED"] --> cdCharge["CAPACITOR CHARGES
TO PEAK VM
TIME CONSTANT SMALL"] cdCharge --> cdPeak["VC REACHES VM
DIODE STOPS CONDUCTING"] cdPeak --> cdDischarge["CAPACITOR DISCHARGES
INTO LOAD RL
TIME CONSTANT LARGE"] cdDischarge --> cdDrop["VC DROPS BELOW VS"] cdDrop --> cdReconduct["DIODE RECONDUCTS
CYCLE REPEATS"] cdReconduct --> cdStart style cdStart fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 style cdCharge fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style cdPeak fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style cdDischarge fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style cdDrop fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style cdReconduct fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define ripple factor. Why is a lower ripple factor desirable in a DC power supply? Calculate the ripple factor of a full-wave rectifier. **Model Answer:** The ripple factor ($\gamma$) is the ratio of the RMS value of the AC (ripple) component to the average DC value of the rectifier output. It is given by: $$ \gamma \;=\; \frac{V_{r(RMS)}}{V_{DC}} \;=\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{RMS}}{V_{DC}}\right)^2 - 1} $$ A lower ripple factor indicates a smoother, purer DC output, which is essential for noise-sensitive electronics. For a full-wave rectifier, $\gamma \approx 0.482$. #### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Draw the block diagram of a DC power supply and state the function of each block. **Model Answer:** The four blocks in sequence are: 1. **Transformer:** Steps down the 230 V AC mains to the required low AC voltage; provides isolation. 2. **Rectifier:** Converts bidirectional AC into unidirectional pulsating DC using diodes. 3. **Filter:** Smooths the pulsating DC using a capacitor (or LC network), reducing the ripple. 4. **Regulator:** Maintains a constant DC output independent of input voltage or load variations. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### Question A **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **bridge rectifier**. Derive expressions for its **DC output voltage**, **RMS output voltage**, **ripple factor**, and **rectification efficiency**. Compare its performance with a half-wave rectifier. **(a) Circuit and Working — 7 Marks** A bridge rectifier consists of **four diodes** ($D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$) connected in a closed-loop bridge. The AC source is connected across one diagonal of the bridge, and the load resistor $R_L$ is connected across the other diagonal. **During the positive half-cycle** (upper AC terminal is positive): - $D_1$ and $D_2$ are forward-biased and conduct. - $D_3$ and $D_4$ are reverse-biased and OFF. - Current path: AC(+) → $D_1$ → $R_L$ → $D_2$ → AC(–). - The load voltage $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. **During the negative half-cycle** (upper AC terminal is negative): - $D_3$ and $D_4$ are forward-biased and conduct. - $D_1$ and $D_2$ are reverse-biased and OFF. - Current path: AC(–) → $D_4$ → $R_L$ → $D_3$ → AC(+). - The load voltage $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t + \pi)$ (but flipped, so still positive at the load). In both half-cycles, current flows through $R_L$ in the same direction, producing a **full-wave rectified output** with frequency $2f = 100$ Hz. **(b) Derivations and Comparison — 7 Marks** **DC Output Voltage:** $$ V_{DC} \;=\; \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \;\approx\; 0.636 \, V_m $$ **RMS Output Voltage:** $$ V_{RMS} \;=\; \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \;\approx\; 0.707 \, V_m $$ **Ripple Factor:** $$ \gamma_{BWR} \;=\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{RMS}}{V_{DC}}\right)^2 - 1} \;=\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 - 1} \;\approx\; 0.482 $$ **Rectification Efficiency:** $$ \eta \;=\; \frac{P_{DC}}{P_{AC}} \;=\; \frac{V_{DC}^2 / R_L}{V_{RMS}^2 / R_L} \;=\; \frac{V_{DC}^2}{V_{RMS}^2} \;=\; \left(\frac{2 V_m / \pi}{V_m / \sqrt{2}}\right)^2 $$ $$ \eta \;=\; \frac{8}{\pi^2} \;\approx\; 0.8106 \;=\; 81.06 \,\% $$ **Comparison with Half-Wave Rectifier:** | Parameter | Half Wave | Bridge | |:----------|:---------:|:------:| | $V_{DC}$ | $V_m / \pi$ | $2 V_m / \pi$ (Double) | | $\gamma$ | 1.21 | 0.482 (Better) | | $\eta$ | 40.6 % | 81.2 % (Better) | | PIV per diode | $V_m$ | $V_m$ (Same) | | Ripple frequency | 50 Hz | 100 Hz (Easier to filter) | --- #### Question B (Alternative Choice for Internal Option) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** With neat circuit diagrams, explain the working of **half-wave** and **full-wave (center-tapped) rectifiers**. Derive the expressions for the **ripple factor** of both with and without a capacitor filter. **(a) Half-Wave Rectifier — 7 Marks** A single diode $D$ is connected in series with the load $R_L$ across the secondary of the transformer. During the positive half-cycle, $D$ is forward-biased and the load voltage follows the input ($v_o = V_m \sin \omega t$). During the negative half-cycle, $D$ is reverse-biased, no current flows, and $v_o = 0$. The output consists of positive half-sine pulses separated by zero intervals. **Without capacitor filter:** $$ V_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}, \quad V_{RMS} = \frac{V_m}{2}, \quad \gamma_{HWR} = 1.21 $$ **With capacitor filter** (using $f_r = f = 50$ Hz): $$ \gamma_{filtered} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \, f \, R_L \, C} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \cdot 50 \cdot R_L C} $$ **PIV of diode** = $V_m$ (peak inverse voltage the diode must block). **(b) Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier — 7 Marks** A center-tapped transformer with two identical secondary windings feeds two diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$. The load is connected between the common cathode junction of the two diodes and the center tap. - **Positive half-cycle:** $D_1$ conducts, $D_2$ OFF. Current flows through the upper half of the secondary, $D_1$, $R_L$, and back to the center tap. - **Negative half-cycle:** $D_2$ conducts, $D_1$ OFF. Current flows through the lower half of the secondary, $D_2$, $R_L$, and back to the center tap. - The load current is unidirectional, producing two positive pulses per cycle. **Without capacitor filter:** $$ V_{DC} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}, \quad V_{RMS} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \gamma_{FWR} = 0.482 $$ **With capacitor filter** (using $f_r = 2f = 100$ Hz): $$ \gamma_{filtered} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \cdot 100 \cdot R_L C} \;=\; \frac{1}{200\sqrt{3} \, R_L C} $$ > **Key Insight:** For the same $R_L C$ product, the FWR ripple is **half** that of the HWR because the ripple frequency is doubled. **PIV of each diode = $2V_m$** (each diode must block the full secondary voltage during the OFF half-cycle). --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Each:** > 1. **Confusing $V_{RMS}$ and $V_{DC}$ for transformer secondary rating.** If a problem says the secondary is 12 V (RMS), students often plug 12 V into $V_m$. Correct step: $V_m = 12 \times \sqrt{2} \approx 16.97$ V first. > 2. **Forgetting the diode drop.** In a bridge rectifier, the peak output is $V_m - 1.4$ V (two diode drops), not $V_m$. This distinction is critical for low-voltage (e.g., 5 V) supplies. > 3. **Using $f$ instead of $2f$ in the filter formula for full-wave rectifiers.** The ripple frequency is $2f$ for FWR/BWR, not $f$. Mixing this up inflates the calculated ripple by 2x. > 4. **Mixing up PIV for center-tapped FWR vs Bridge.** Both produce the same output, but the PIV per diode is $2V_m$ for the center-tapped and $V_m$ for the bridge. KTU examiners **love** this trap. > 5. **Writing the unit of ripple factor as "V" or "A".** Ripple factor is **dimensionless**. Same applies to efficiency. --- ### Numerical Practice Problems (Self-Assessment) | # | Problem Statement | Key Values to Compute | |:-:|:------------------|:----------------------| | **1** | A half-wave rectifier feeds a 1 kΩ load from a 230 V (RMS), 50 Hz mains through a 10:1 step-down transformer. Silicon diodes used. | Find $V_{DC}$, $I_{DC}$, $\gamma$, PIV. | | **2** | A bridge rectifier supplies a 100 Ω load. Secondary RMS voltage = 24 V. | Find $V_{DC}$ (with diode drop), ripple factor, PIV per diode. | | **3** | A full-wave rectifier output is filtered with a 1000 μF capacitor. Load = 250 Ω, secondary RMS = 15 V, $f = 50$ Hz. | Find peak-to-peak ripple, RMS ripple, filtered ripple factor. | | **4** | A center-tapped FWR has a total secondary of 30 V-0-30 V (RMS).
