Load = 500 Ω. | Find $V_{DC}$, $V_{RMS}$, efficiency, PIV per diode. | > **Solutions Hint:** Always convert RMS to peak using $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times V_{RMS}$, then apply the formula table in Section 2.3. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Block Diagram Order (Mnemonic — "T-R-F-R" = "Try Running For Real"):** **T**ransformer → **R**ectifier → **F**ilter → **R**egulator. - **HWR DC voltage:** $V_{DC} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$. - **FWR / Bridge DC voltage:** $V_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ (twice the HWR for the same $V_m$). - **HWR RMS voltage:** $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}$. - **FWR / Bridge RMS voltage:** $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$. - **HWR ripple factor:** $\gamma = 1.21$ (high ripple — undesirable). - **FWR / Bridge ripple factor:** $\gamma = 0.482$ (much smoother). - **Maximum Rectification Efficiency:** HWR = **40.6 %**; FWR/Bridge = **81.2 %** (theoretical max). - **Ripple Frequency:** HWR → $f$ (50 Hz); FWR/Bridge → $2f$ (100 Hz). - **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV):** HWR → $V_m$; Center-Tapped FWR → $2V_m$; Bridge → $V_m$. - **Capacitor Filter Formula:** $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3} \, f_r \, R_L C}$, where $f_r = f$ (HWR) or $2f$ (FWR). - **Filter capacitor behavior:** Charges quickly through diode to $V_m$, discharges slowly through $R_L$ between pulses. - **Bridge Rectifier — Best TUF (0.8106)** of all three; no center-tap needed; trade-off is the 1.4 V total diode drop. - **Center-Tapped FWR** uses a bulky, expensive transformer but only one diode drop (0.7 V); better for low-voltage high-current applications. - **Diode drop (Silicon) = 0.7 V** (use 0.3 V if Germanium). Always subtract from $V_m$ for accurate low-voltage designs. - **Ideal DC supply** has $\gamma = 0$, $\eta = 100\%$. Real supplies get close after the regulator stage. - **The Regulator** is the *only* block that holds the output truly constant; the filter merely *reduces* ripple but does not eliminate it. - **Peak-to-Peak Ripple** with capacitor: $V_{r(pp)} = \dfrac{I_{DC}}{f_r C}$.

topics.title: Construction, Working and V-I Characteristics of BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Construction, Working and V-I Characteristics of BJT # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)**: A three-terminal, two-junction, current-controlled semiconductor device constructed by sandwiching a thin, lightly doped **base** region between two oppositely doped **emitter** and **collector** regions. It amplifies weak signals by using a small current at one terminal (base) to control a much larger current between the other two terminals (emitter and collector). The term *Bipolar* signifies that **both** majority and minority charge carriers (electrons and holes) participate in current conduction, unlike the unipolar FET. The BJT is the fundamental building block of discrete analog and digital electronics, and forms the active heart of nearly every operational amplifier, audio amplifier, and switching power supply in the 2024 KTU syllabus. ## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Variants A BJT is fabricated in only two complementary polarities: | Type | Emitter | Base | Collector | Symbol Arrow Direction | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **NPN** | N-type (heavily doped, $\approx 10^{25}\ \text{m}^{-3}$) | P-type (thin, lightly doped) | N-type (moderately doped, large area) | Arrow on Emitter points **OUT**ward (Not Pointing iN) | | **PNP** | P-type (heavily doped) | N-type (thin, lightly doped) | P-type (moderately doped) | Arrow on Emitter points **IN**ward (Pointing iN Permanently) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Focus:** NPN transistors are the most frequently analyzed devices in the syllabus because electrons (mobility $\mu_n \approx 1350\ \text{cm}^2/\text{Vs}$) move roughly **2.81 times faster** than holes ($\mu_p \approx 480\ \text{cm}^2/\text{Vs}$), giving NPN a superior switching speed and higher current-handling capability. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water-Pressure Tap" Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline carrying a massive flow of water. You cannot easily stop or regulate this main flow directly with your fingers, but you can install a **small control valve** on a side branch. A small turn of this valve can throttle or release the giant main flow. - The **high-pressure main pipe** = Collector–Emitter current ($I_C$). - The **small control valve handle** = Base current ($I_B$). - The **side-branch input pressure** = Base–Emitter voltage ($V_{BE}$). - The **amplified main flow** that results from a tiny valve adjustment = the amplified collector current $I_C = \beta I_B$. Just as a tiny valve movement governs a huge river, a small base current controls a far larger collector current — and **this is amplification**. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values > [!NOTE] > - Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ > - Thermal voltage at $T = 300\ \text{K}$: $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ (commonly approximated as **26 mV** for KTU problems). > - Intrinsic Silicon carrier concentration: $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ at 300 K. > - Base-emitter turn-on voltage (silicon BJT): $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx \mathbf{0.7\ V}$ (Germanium: $\approx 0.3\ \text{V}$). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a BJT A BJT consists of three doped semiconductor regions forming two back-to-back PN-junctions: - **Junction 1 — Emitter–Base (E–B) Junction:** Formed between the emitter and the base. It is normally **forward-biased** during active operation. - **Junction 2 — Collector–Base (C–B) Junction:** Formed between the collector and the base. It is normally **reverse-biased** during active operation. ### Doping & Geometry — The "Why" Behind Performance | Region | Doping Level | Physical Area | Engineering Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Emitter** | Very Heavy (Heaviest of all three) | Moderate | Must inject a large number of charge carriers into the base. | | **Base** | Very Light & physically **Thin** | Smallest | A thin, lightly doped base ensures most carriers cross it without recombining, giving high $\beta$. | | **Collector** | Moderate (Lighter than emitter) | Largest | Must dissipate heat generated by the large current passing through it. Larger area → better thermal sinking. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symmetry is NOT present.** A BJT is *asymmetric*: the emitter and collector are **not interchangeable**. Swapping them changes current gain drastically because the doping levels and geometries differ. This is a frequently tested KTU 2024 conceptual question. ## 2.2 Working Principle — Active Region Operation (NPN) The NPN transistor in the **active region** is biased as follows: 1. **Emitter–Base (E–B) Junction is Forward Biased:** The applied $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ lowers the depletion barrier, causing the heavily doped N-emitter to inject a massive stream of **electrons** into the thin P-base. A small hole current (from the P-base into the emitter) also flows in the opposite direction, but it is negligible because the base is very lightly doped. 2. **Collector–Base (C–B) Junction is Reverse Biased:** The applied $V_{CE}$ creates a strong electric field across the reverse-biased junction that sweeps across the entire thin base region. This field **pulls the injected electrons straight through the base** into the collector. 3. **The Base Current:** A small fraction (typically 1–2%) of the injected electrons recombine with holes in the thin base region. This lost charge must be continuously replenished by the external base current $I_B$. The remaining 98–99% of electrons form the collector current $I_C$. **The net result:** A small base current $I_B$ results in a huge collector current $I_C$, yielding amplification. ## 2.3 The Three Configurations of a BJT A BJT can be connected in three ways, depending on which terminal is used as the common reference: | Configuration | Common Terminal | Input Quantity | Output Quantity | Current Gain | Voltage Gain | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **CB** (Common Base) | Base | $I_E$ | $I_C$ | $\alpha \approx 1$ | High | High-frequency, RF amplifiers | | **CE** (Common Emitter) | Emitter | $I_B$ | $I_C$ | $\beta$ (50–400) | High | General-purpose amplifier, switching | | **CC** (Common Collector) | Collector | $I_B$ | $I_E$ | $\gamma = \beta+1$ | $\approx 1$ (unity) | Buffer, impedance matching (Emitter Follower) | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU module question on BJTs.** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning / KTU Note | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Kirchhoff's Current Law at the transistor** | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | The emitter current is the sum of base and collector currents. | | 2 | **Common-Base Current Gain** | $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ | Always $\alpha < 1$ (typically 0.95–0.99). Dimensionless. | | 3 | **Common-Emitter Current Gain** | $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | Typically 50–400 for small-signal BJTs. Dimensionless. | | 4 | **$\alpha$–$\beta$ Interconversion (1)** | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$ | Derived from $I_C = \alpha I_E$. | | 5 | **$\alpha$–$\beta$ Interconversion (2)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{1+\beta}$ | Used to convert $\beta$ to $\alpha$ in KTU problems. | | 6 | **Collector Current in terms of $I_B$** | $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ | $I_{CBO}$ is the leakage current (C open, E shorted). | | 7 | **Approximate Collector Current** | $I_C \approx \beta I_B$ | Valid when $I_{CBO}$ is negligible (most KTU problems). | | 8 | **Emitter Current in terms of $I_B$** | $I_E = (1+\beta) I_B$ | Direct consequence of $I_E = I_B + I_C$. | | 9 | **Thermal Runaway Condition** | $\dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial T} \neq 0$ | Power dissipation raises temperature → $I_C$ increases → more heat → destructive feedback loop. | | 10 | **Base-Emitter Diode Equation** | $I_B = I_{S} \left(e^{V_{BE}/V_T} - 1\right)$ | Where $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K. | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Amplifiers (CE mode):** Home-theater and headphone amplifiers use CE configuration to obtain high voltage and current gain simultaneously. - **RF & Microwave Preamplifiers (CB mode):** Telescopic antennas and satellite receivers exploit the excellent high-frequency response of the common-base stage because the Miller effect is minimal. - **Digital Logic (CE switch mode):** Every CMOS gate contains parasitic BJTs that must be suppressed; the early bipolar TTL and ECL logic families used CE-mode BJTs as fast switches. - **Voltage Regulation (CC mode):** The 7805/7812 linear voltage regulators use a Darlington pair in common-collector configuration to provide a stable, low-impedance output. - **Current Mirrors in ICs:** The fundamental analog building block in op-amps is a BJT current mirror (two matched BJTs in CE mode), used to bias every stage of an integrated circuit. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Current Relation $I_E = I_B + I_C$ This relation is a direct consequence of **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** applied at the transistor's central base node. We treat the BJT as a black box with three terminals and apply KCL: the algebraic sum of all currents entering a node must equal the sum of all currents leaving it. By the convention that currents flowing **into** the transistor are positive, the current entering the emitter is $I_E$, and the currents leaving through the base and collector are $I_B$ and $I_C$ respectively. $$ I_E = I_B + I_C $$ This is the **single most important equation** in BJT analysis. It is so fundamental that KTU examiners expect students to write it on the first line of any solution. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Common-Base Current Gain $\alpha = I_C / I_E$ The common-base current gain is defined as the ratio of the output current (collector) to the input current (emitter). Since a small fraction of the emitter current recombines in the base, the collector current is always slightly less than the emitter current. $$ \alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} $$ By definition, $\alpha < 1$ and is typically between 0.95 and 0.99 for a well-designed silicon BJT. The small difference $1 - \alpha$ represents the fraction of carriers that recombine in the base. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Common-Emitter Current Gain $\beta = I_C / I_B$ The common-emitter current gain is defined as the ratio of the output current (collector) to the input current (base). Because $I_B$ is the *small* lost current, the ratio $I_C / I_B$ is large — this is the origin of BJT amplification. $$ \beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} $$ A typical small-signal BJT has $\beta$ between **50 and 400**. This is the value quoted on a transistor's datasheet (e.g., "BC547B" has $\beta \approx 200\text{–}450$). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relationship Between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ Starting from the fundamental relation $I_E = I_B + I_C$, we divide both sides by $I_C$: $$ \frac{I_E}{I_C} = \frac{I_B}{I_C} + 1 $$ Recognising that $\alpha = I_C / I_E$ (so $I_E / I_C = 1/\alpha$) and that $1/\beta = I_B / I_C$, we obtain: $$ \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\beta} + 1 $$ Now combine the right-hand side into a single fraction: $$ \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1 + \beta}{\beta} $$ Inverting both sides of the equation gives the classical $\alpha$–$\beta$ interconversion formula: $$ \boxed{\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}} $$ To derive the reverse relation, we solve the same equation for $\alpha$. Starting from $1/\alpha = (1 + \beta)/\beta$, we invert both sides: $$ \alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Numerical Tip:** If a problem states $\beta = 100$, immediately compute $\alpha = 100/101 = 0.9901$. This single line can earn 1–2 marks even if the rest of the calculation is incomplete. ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Collector Current Equation Including Leakage In a real BJT, a small reverse saturation current $I_{CBO}$ flows across the reverse-biased collector–base junction even when the emitter is open-circuited. Applying KCL with this leakage current included: $$ I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO} $$ Substituting $I_E = I_B + I_C$ into the equation: $$ I_C = \alpha (I_B + I_C) + I_{CBO} $$ Expanding the right-hand side: $$ I_C = \alpha I_B + \alpha I_C + I_{CBO} $$ Group the $I_C$ terms on the left side: $$ I_C - \alpha I_C = \alpha I_B + I_{CBO} $$ Factor $I_C$ from the left side: $$ I_C (1 - \alpha) = \alpha I_B + I_{CBO} $$ Divide both sides by $(1 - \alpha)$: $$ I_C = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} I_B + \frac{1}{1-\alpha} I_{CBO} $$ Recognising that $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$ and $(1 + \beta) = 1 / (1 - \alpha)$, the collector current equation becomes: $$ \boxed{I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > At room temperature and for most KTU problems, $I_{CBO}$ is in the nanoampere range and can be neglected, giving the simpler approximation $I_C \approx \beta I_B$. However, **at elevated temperatures**, $I_{CBO}$ doubles every $10^\circ\text{C}$ and cannot be ignored. ## 3.6 Worked Example: KTU-Style Numerical Problem **Problem:** A silicon BJT operating in the active region has $\beta = 150$ and a base current of $I_B = 20\ \mu\text{A}$. The leakage current is $I_{CBO} = 5\ \text{nA}$. Calculate $\alpha$, $I_C$, and $I_E$. **Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$ from $\beta$:** $$ \alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} = \frac{150}{1 + 150} = \frac{150}{151} = 0.9934 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $I_C$ using the exact equation:** $$ I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO} $$ Substitute the numerical values: $$ I_C = (150)(20 \times 10^{-6}) + (151)(5 \times 10^{-9}) $$ Evaluate the first product: $$ (150)(20 \times 10^{-6}) = 3000 \times 10^{-6} = 3.000 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} $$ Evaluate the second product: $$ (151)(5 \times 10^{-9}) = 755 \times 10^{-9} = 7.55 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{A} $$ Sum the two terms: $$ I_C = 3.000 \times 10^{-3} + 7.55 \times 10^{-7} \approx 3.001 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} = 3.001\ \text{mA} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $I_E$ using KCL:** $$ I_E = I_B + I_C = 20 \times 10^{-6} + 3.001 \times 10^{-3} = 3.021 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} = 3.021\ \text{mA} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer Block:** $\alpha = 0.9934$, $I_C \approx 3.001\ \text{mA}$, $I_E \approx 3.021\ \text{mA}$. Note that $I_C < I_E$, confirming $\alpha < 1$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Construction (NPN) — Block-Level Architecture Because Mermaid cannot render true 3D semiconductor cross-sections, the following flow diagram conveys the **functional architecture** of an NPN BJT, mapping the doping regions, the biasing requirements, and the resulting charge-carrier flow. ```mermaid graph TD N1[Region 1: N-Type Emitter
Heavily Doped Donor Atoms] N2[Region 2: P-Type Base
Lightly Doped Thin Layer] N3[Region 3: N-Type Collector
Moderately Doped Large Area] J1[Function: Emitter-Base Junction JE-B
Operating State: Forward Biased VBE approx 0.7V] J2[Function: Collector-Base Junction JC-B
Operating State: Reverse Biased VCB is Negative] T1[Process Step 1: Forward Bias JE-B Lowers Barrier Electrons Injected From Emitter Into Base] T2[Process Step 2: Reverse Bias JC-B Creates Sweeping Electric Field Electrons Drifted From Base Into Collector] T3[Process Step 3: Tiny Electron Recombination In Base Hole Current Leaves as IB] T4[Output: IC approx 99 percent of IE IB is Only 1 to 2 percent of IE Amplification Achieved] N1 --- J1 J1 --- N2 N2 --- J2 J2 --- N3 J1 --> T1 J1 --> T2 T1 --> T3 T2 --> T4 T3 --> T4 ``` ## 4.2 Circuit Symbols and Terminal Identification ```mermaid graph LR SubA[Subgraph: NPN Transistor Symbol] NA[Terminal C: Collector] NB[Terminal B: Base] NC[Terminal E: Emitter With Outward Arrow] NA --- NB NB --- NC SubB[Subgraph: PNP Transistor Symbol] PA[Terminal C: Collector] PB[Terminal B: Base] PC[Terminal E: Emitter With Inward Arrow] PA --- PB PB --- PC ``` ## 4.3 Common-Emitter Configuration — Biasing & Signal Flow Architecture ```mermaid graph TD VIN[Input Signal Source Vin Applied Between B and E] VBB[DC Bias Supply VBB Forward Biases JE-B Through RB] VCC[DC Supply VCC Reverse Biases JC-B Through RC] Q1[Active NPN BJT In Common Emitter Configuration Emitter is Common Reference] OP[Output Voltage Vout Taken Across RC Between C and E] I1[Current Flow: IB From VBB Through RB Into Base] I2[Current Flow: IC From VCC Through RC Into Collector IC equals Beta times IB] I3[Current Flow: IE Leaves Emitter And Returns to Common Ground] VIN --> Q1 VBB --> Q1 VCC --> Q1 Q1 --> OP VBB --> I1 --> Q1 VCC --> I2 --> Q1 Q1 --> I3 ``` ## 4.4 Output Characteristics (CE) — Three Operating Regions ```mermaid graph TD R0[Common Emitter Output Characteristics Plot IC vs VCE at Fixed IB] R1[Region 1: Saturation Region
Both Junctions Forward Biased VCE Less Than 0.2V
IC Independent of IB IC Increases With VCE] R2[Region 2: Active Region
JE-B Forward JC-B Reverse
IC equals Beta IB Approximately Constant
Flat Horizontal Lines Used For Amplification] R3[Region 3: Cutoff Region
Both Junctions Reverse Biased IB equals 0
IC equals ICO Leakage Only Approximate Zero] R0 --> R1 R0 --> R2 R0 --> R3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Diagram Drawing Tip:** When asked to "sketch the output characteristics", always draw **three curves** for three different $I_B$ values (e.g., $I_B = 20\ \mu\text{A}$, $40\ \mu\text{A}$, $60\ \mu\text{A}$), label the saturation knee at $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$, the active region (flat), the cutoff region on the $V_{CE}$ axis, and indicate $\beta$ as the slope between curves. Missing any of these four markings costs 1–2 marks. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Q: With a neat diagram, explain the construction of an NPN transistor. Why is the base region made thin and lightly doped?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** **Construction:** An NPN transistor consists of a thin, lightly doped **P-type base** region sandwiched between a heavily doped **N-type emitter** and a moderately doped **N-type collector**. This forms two junctions: the forward-biased **emitter–base (E–B)** junction and the reverse-biased **collector–base (C–B)** junction. - **(1 mark)** **Thin Base:** A thin base minimises the transit time of charge carriers and reduces recombination losses. Most carriers cross the base before recombining, giving a high current gain $\beta$. - **(1 mark)** **Lightly Doped Base:** Light doping ensures the emitter injection efficiency remains very high (only a small number of holes from the base recombine in the emitter), and produces a large $\alpha$ close to unity. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]` **Q: Define the terms $\alpha$ and $\beta$ of a BJT. Establish the relationship $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** **Definitions:** $\alpha = I_C / I_E$ is the common-base current gain (typically 0.95–0.99). $\beta = I_C / I_B$ is the common-emitter current gain (typically 50–400). - **(2 marks)** **Derivation:** Starting from KCL, $I_E = I_B + I_C$, divide by $I_C$: $$ \frac{I_E}{I_C} = \frac{I_B}{I_C} + 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\beta} + 1 $$ Combining and inverting yields the required result: $$ \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1 + \beta}{\beta} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}} $$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Module 3 Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. You are expected to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Configuration, Biasing & V-I Characteristics **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]** #### (a) Draw the circuit diagram of an NPN transistor in common-emitter configuration. Explain the input and output V-I characteristics with a neat graph. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** - **(1 mark) Circuit Diagram:** In CE configuration, the emitter is the common terminal for both input (between base and emitter) and output (between collector and emitter) loops. The E–B junction is forward biased by $V_{BB}$ through $R_B$, and the C–B junction is reverse biased by $V_{CC}$ through $R_C$. - **(2 marks) Input Characteristics ($I_B$ vs $V_{BE}$ for fixed $V_{CE}$):** This curve is essentially the forward-biased diode curve of the E–B junction. It has a turn-on (knee) voltage of $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ for silicon. For $V_{BE} < 0.5\ \text{V}$, $I_B \approx 0$. Above 0.7 V, $I_B$ rises exponentially. Increasing $V_{CE}$ shifts the curve slightly to the right because a larger reverse bias on the C–B junction widens the depletion region, effectively reducing the base width (Early effect). - **(2 marks) Output Characteristics ($I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ for fixed $I_B$):** Three regions must be marked: - **Saturation region:** $V_{CE} < V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$. Both junctions are forward biased; $I_C$ rises rapidly with $V_{CE}$ and depends weakly on $I_B$. - **Active region:** $V_{CE} > 0.2\ \text{V}$. $I_C$ is almost constant and proportional to $I_B$ via $I_C = \beta I_B$. This is the amplification region. - **Cutoff region:** $I_B = 0$. Only the leakage $I_{CEO}$ flows; effectively $I_C \approx 0$. - **(1 mark) Slope Interpretation:** The vertical spacing between adjacent curves for $\Delta I_B$ gives $\beta = \Delta I_C / \Delta I_B$. - **(1 mark) Final labelled graph.** #### (b) A silicon NPN transistor has $\beta = 200$ and $I_{CBO} = 2\ \mu\text{A}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$. If the base current is $50\ \mu\text{A}$, calculate $\alpha$, $I_C$, and $I_E$. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** - **(1 mark)** $\beta = 200 \Rightarrow \alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta} = \dfrac{200}{201} = 0.995$. - **(1 mark)** Apply the exact collector current equation $I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}$: $$ I_C = (200)(50\ \mu\text{A}) + (201)(2\ \mu\text{A}) $$ - **(1 mark)** First product: $(200)(50\ \mu\text{A}) = 10000\ \mu\text{A} = 10\ \text{mA}$. - **(1 mark)** Second product: $(201)(2\ \mu\text{A}) = 402\ \mu\text{A} = 0.402\ \text{mA}$. - **(1 mark)** Sum: $I_C = 10\ \text{mA} + 0.402\ \text{mA} = 10.402\ \text{mA}$. - **(1 mark)** Emitter current: $I_E = I_B + I_C = 50\ \mu\text{A} + 10.402\ \text{mA} = 10.452\ \text{mA}$. - **(1 mark) Final boxed answer:** $\alpha = 0.995$, $I_C = 10.402\ \text{mA}$, $I_E = 10.452\ \text{mA}$. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Regions of Operation, Stability & Thermal Effects **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]** #### (a) Explain the three regions of operation of a BJT (cutoff, active, saturation) with the help of junction bias conditions and a V-I characteristic sketch. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** - **(1 mark) Cutoff Region:** Both the E–B and C–B junctions are **reverse biased**. $I_B = 0$ and $I_C \approx 0$ (only the leakage current $I_{CEO}$ flows). The transistor behaves like an **open switch**. Operating point lies on the $V_{CE}$ axis of the output characteristics. - **(2 marks) Active Region:** The E–B junction is **forward biased** ($V_{BE} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$) and the C–B junction is **reverse biased**. $I_C = \beta I_B$ holds true. The transistor behaves as a **linear amplifier** with high current gain. The output characteristic is a nearly horizontal line; this is where the transistor is used for amplification. - **(2 marks) Saturation Region:** Both the E–B and C–B junctions are **forward biased**. The collector current cannot increase further even if $I_B$ increases, because the collector voltage has collapsed to $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$. The transistor behaves as a **closed switch** with minimum voltage drop. - **(2 marks) V–I Sketch:** Labelled output characteristic with the three regions clearly marked, the knee at $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$, and the cutoff intercept on the horizontal axis. Three $I_B$ curves drawn to show the spacing that defines $\beta$. #### (b) What is thermal runaway? Explain the condition for thermal stability of a BJT. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** - **(2 marks) Definition of Thermal Runaway:** Thermal runaway is a self-destructive positive-feedback phenomenon in BJTs. An increase in collector current $I_C$ raises the junction temperature $T$, which in turn increases $I_C$ further (because $\beta$ rises and $I_{CBO}$ doubles every $10^\circ\text{C}$ with temperature). The increased $I_C$ produces more heat ($P = V_{CE} I_C$), which raises $T$ even more, and the cycle repeats. If not arrested, the device will exceed its maximum junction temperature ($T_{j,\max} \approx 150^\circ\text{C}$–$200^\circ\text{C}$ for silicon) and burn out. - **(2 marks) Condition for Thermal Stability:** The rate at which the transistor can dissipate heat to the surroundings must exceed the rate at which heat is generated internally. Mathematically: $$ \frac{\partial P_C}{\partial T_j} \leq \frac{1}{\theta_{JA}} $$ where $P_C = V_{CE} I_C$ is the collector power dissipation and $\theta_{JA}$ is the junction-to-ambient thermal resistance. Equivalently, the **stability factor** $S$ must be small. For self-bias (emitter resistor) configurations: $$ S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \left( \frac{R_E}{R_E + R_B} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}} $$ A smaller $S$ means greater thermal stability. - **(2 marks) Stabilisation Methods:** Use of an **emitter resistor** $R_E$ to provide DC negative feedback, a heat sink to lower $\theta_{JA}$, a thermistor to compensate $V_{BE}$ variation, and ensuring the operating point lies in the middle of the active region rather than near saturation. - **(1 mark) Conclusion:** Thermal runaway must always be prevented via proper bias design and heat-sinking. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on BJT Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting KCL first line** — Always write $I_E = I_B + I_C$ before any calculation. Examiners award 1 mark for this single line alone. > 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$ definitions** — A common mistake is writing $\beta = I_C / I_E$ (this is $\alpha$). Remember: $\beta$ is *Common-Emitter* (input is $I_B$), $\alpha$ is *Common-Base* (input is $I_E$). > 3. **Using $V_{BE} = 0.3\ \text{V}$ instead of $0.7\ \text{V}$** — This is a silicon BJT (the KTU 2024 syllabus uses silicon). Using 0.3 V is a Ge value and is wrong. > 4. **Ignoring $I_{CBO}$ in the wrong direction** — Some students write $I_C = \beta I_B - (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}$. The correct sign is **positive** because $I_{CBO}$ adds to $I_C$ via transistor action. > 5. **Unlabelled graphs** — A characteristic curve without axes labelled, units written, and regions marked loses 2–3 marks instantly. Always label $I_C$, $V_{CE}$, and the three regions. > 6. **Forgetting the "Why"** — Examiners reward *reasoning*, not just formulas. A complete answer always justifies *why* the base is thin, *why* $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$, and *why* $\alpha < 1$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **BJT = Bipolar Junction Transistor** — uses both electrons and holes for conduction. Two types: **NPN** and **PNP**. NPN is preferred due to higher electron mobility. - [x] **Three terminals:** **E**mitter (heavily doped, injects carriers), **B**ase (thin & lightly doped, controls flow), **C**ollector (moderately doped & large, collects carriers). - [x] **Two junctions:** **E–B** (forward biased in active region) and **C–B** (reverse biased in active region). - [x] **Three configurations:** **CB**, **CE**, **CC** — each with different input/output terminals and distinct gain characteristics. - [x] **Master Equation #1:** $I_E = I_B + I_C$ (KCL, always true, regardless of region). - [x] **Master Equation #2:** $I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}$ (active region, exact form). - [x] **Approximation (most KTU problems):** $I_C \approx \beta I_B$, $I_E \approx (1 + \beta) I_B$. - [x] **$\alpha$–$\beta$ Conversion:** $\beta = \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$ and $\alpha = \beta / (1 + \beta)$. Always between $0 < \alpha < 1$. - [x] **Three operating regions:** **Cutoff** (both junctions reverse biased, switch OFF), **Active** (E–B forward, C–B reverse, amplifier), **Saturation** (both forward biased, switch ON). - [x] **Silicon turn-on voltage:** $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$. **Saturation voltage:** $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$. - [x] **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K (used in diode equation derivations). - [x] **Thermal runaway:** Caused by positive feedback of $I_C \rightarrow \text{heat} \rightarrow T \rightarrow I_C$. Prevented by emitter resistor, heat sink, and proper bias design. - [x] **Leakage current:** $I_{CBO}$ doubles every $10^\circ\text{C}$. Often negligible in KTU problems but cannot be ignored at high temperature. - [x] **Asymmetry:** The emitter and collector are **NOT interchangeable** — doping levels and physical areas differ.

topics.title: Input Output Characteristics of CE Configuration, Comparison of CE, CB and CC Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Input Output Characteristics of CE Configuration, Comparison of CE, CB and CC Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — BJT CONFIGURATIONS: CE INPUT/OUTPUT CHARACTERISTICS & COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is the Common Emitter (CE) Configuration?

In a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT), there are three terminals — **Base (B)**, **Collector (C)**, and **Emitter (E)**. Out of these three terminals, **one terminal is common to both the input port and the output port**, and the other two terminals are used for signal input and signal output. The configuration is named after this common terminal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Emitter (CE) Configuration Definition (KTU 2024):**
> A transistor configuration in which the **emitter terminal is common to both the input loop (between base and emitter) and the output loop (between collector and emitter)**, the base–emitter junction is forward-biased and the collector–base junction is reverse-biased. It is the most widely used configuration for amplification because it provides both **current gain** and **voltage gain** simultaneously.

The CE configuration is also commonly called the **"grounded emitter"** configuration because in most practical circuits the emitter is tied to ground (either directly or through a bypass capacitor).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hydraulic Valve Model

Imagine a BJT as a **water-tap with a control handle**:
- The **base** is the **handle** (small effort here controls a big flow).
- The **collector** is the **main water inlet** (high-pressure, high-volume).
- The **emitter** is the **water outlet** (where the controlled, amplified flow finally emerges).
- A small movement of the handle (a tiny base current $I_B$) controls a huge, much larger flow of water from the inlet to the outlet (collector current $I_C$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuitive Takeaway:** In CE configuration, you are essentially using a small input signal at the base–emitter port to **control a much larger output current flowing between collector and emitter**. This is the very essence of amplification. The input side is essentially a forward-biased diode (low impedance, voltage-controlled), while the output side behaves like a current source whose value is set by the input current.

### 1.3 Why is CE Configuration Special?

Among the three BJT configurations (CB, CE, CC), the CE configuration is the **workhorse of analog electronics** because:
- It provides **high current gain** ($\beta$, typically 20 to 500).
- It provides **high voltage gain** simultaneously.
- It provides **moderate input impedance** and **moderate output impedance** — a reasonable balance.
- It introduces a **180° phase reversal** between input and output — useful in many amplifier topologies.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** For Module 3, you are expected to (a) sketch and interpret the input and output characteristics of CE, (b) identify the three operating regions (cutoff, active, saturation), and (c) tabulate a comparative study of CB, CE, CC across parameters like current gain, voltage gain, input/output resistance, phase shift, and typical applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Setup for CE Characteristics

To draw the input and output characteristics of a CE-configured NPN transistor, the following biasing is used:
- **Base–Emitter junction:** forward biased by a variable DC source $V_{BB}$ in series with a base resistor $R_B$.
- **Collector–Emitter loop:** reverse biased for the CB junction by a variable DC source $V_{CC}$ in series with a load resistor $R_C$.
- The **emitter** is the common reference (ground).

The input is applied between **Base and Emitter**, and the output is taken between **Collector and Emitter**.

## 2.2 Input Characteristics of CE Configuration

The input characteristics of a CE configuration is a curve plotted between:
- **Y-axis (dependent variable):** Base current, $I_B$ (in mA or $\mu$A)
- **X-axis (independent variable):** Base–Emitter voltage, $V_{BE}$ (in Volts)
- **Parameter held constant:** Collector–Emitter voltage, $V_{CE}$ (kept at one or more fixed values)

### Observations from the Input Characteristic Curve

1. The input characteristic looks **almost identical to the forward-biased characteristic of a PN-junction diode**, because the base–emitter junction *is* a forward-biased diode.
2. There is a **threshold (knee) voltage** below which $I_B$ is negligibly small:
   - $V_{BE(\text{knee})} \approx 0.5\,\text{V}$ for **Silicon**
   - $V_{BE(\text{knee})} \approx 0.1\,\text{V}$ for **Germanium**
3. Beyond the knee, $I_B$ rises **exponentially** with $V_{BE}$ — this is the well-known Shockley diode equation.
4. For a fixed $V_{BE}$, **as $V_{CE}$ is increased, the curve shifts slightly to the right**, meaning $I_B$ decreases. This is because increasing $V_{CE}$ widens the collector–base depletion region, which **reduces the effective base width** — known as the **Early Effect** (or base-width modulation).

### Mathematical Form of the Input Characteristic

The base–emitter junction current follows the standard diode equation:

$$I_B = I_{S(BE)} \left[ e^{\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}} - 1 \right] - I_{CO(BE)}$$

where $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\,\text{mV}$ at room temperature (300 K), and $I_{S(BE)}$ is the reverse saturation current of the B–E junction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the curve shifts with $V_{CE}$:** The Early Effect reduces the effective base width as $V_{CE}$ rises. A thinner base means **fewer carriers recombine in the base**, so for the same $V_{BE}$, fewer carriers must be supplied from the external base circuit — hence $I_B$ is smaller. The result is the curve shifts rightward (higher $V_{BE}$ needed for the same $I_B$) as $V_{CE}$ increases.

## 2.3 Output Characteristics of CE Configuration

The output characteristics of a CE configuration is a curve plotted between:
- **Y-axis (dependent variable):** Collector current, $I_C$ (in mA)
- **X-axis (independent variable):** Collector–Emitter voltage, $V_{CE}$ (in Volts)
- **Parameter held constant:** Base current, $I_B$ (one curve per constant $I_B$)

This produces a **family of curves**, one for each value of $I_B$ (e.g., $I_B = 0$, $20\,\mu\text{A}$, $40\,\mu\text{A}$, $60\,\mu\text{A}$, etc.).

### The Three Operating Regions

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 1 — Cutoff Region:**
> When $I_B = 0$, the transistor is essentially OFF. A small leakage current $I_{CEO}$ still flows from collector to emitter. Condition: emitter–base junction not forward-biased enough; $V_{BE} < V_{BE(\text{knee})}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 2 — Active (or Linear) Region:**
> The collector–base junction is **reverse-biased** and the base–emitter junction is **forward-biased**. The transistor behaves as a **current-controlled current source** with $I_C \approx \beta I_B$. The curves are nearly horizontal, with a slight upward slope due to the Early Effect. This is the **amplification region**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Region 3 — Saturation Region:**
> Both junctions are **forward-biased**. $V_{CE}$ drops to a small saturation voltage $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\,\text{V}$. $I_C$ no longer follows $\beta I_B$ but is limited by the external circuit. The transistor acts like a **closed switch** (ON state).

### Output Resistance in the Active Region

The output characteristic in the active region is not perfectly flat. It has a small positive slope. The output (drain) resistance of the transistor in CE mode is defined as:

$$r_{o} = \frac{\partial V_{CE}}{\partial I_C} \bigg\vert_{I_B = \text{const}} = \frac{V_A + V_{CE}}{I_C}$$

where $V_A$ is the **Early voltage** (intercept on the negative $V_{CE}$ axis when all curves are extrapolated).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | Kirchhoff's Current Law at the transistor | A |
| 2 | $I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO}$ | Collector current in terms of $\alpha$ | A |
| 3 | $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ | Collector current in terms of $\beta$ | A |
| 4 | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$ | Forward current gain conversion | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta}$ | Reverse current gain conversion | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\gamma = 1 + \beta$ | Current gain of CC configuration | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I_{CEO} = (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ | Leakage current CE ↔ CB | A |
| 8 | $I_B = I_{S(BE)} \left[ e^{V_{BE}/V_T} - 1 \right]$ | Input characteristic (Shockley) | A |
| 9 | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Transconductance (CE small-signal) | S (Siemens) |
| 10 | $r_{\pi} = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Base–emitter input resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | $r_o = \dfrac{V_A + V_{CE}}{I_C}$ | Output resistance (Early effect) | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K | Thermal voltage | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants to Memorize (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**
> - Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$
> - Electron charge $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$
> - Thermal voltage at room temperature (300 K): $V_T = 25.85\,\text{mV}$ (often approximated as **26 mV** in problems)
> - $V_{BE(\text{ON, Si})} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$
> - $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\,\text{V}$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CE configuration's characteristics and the small-signal parameters derived from them form the **backbone of every analog amplifier IC** ever designed — from the classic 2N2222 used in hobby circuits to the input stage of an op-amp, to the RF front-end of a mobile phone. The input resistance $r_\pi$, transconductance $g_m$, and output resistance $r_o$ are the three parameters that appear in **every SPICE model** of a BJT used in commercial EDA tools (Cadence, Synopsys, LTspice). Mastering these characteristics is therefore not just an academic exercise — it is the foundation of all integrated-circuit and discrete-amplifier design.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between $\alpha$ and $\beta$

Let us derive the most fundamental relationship between the two current gains used in BJT analysis.

**Step 1.** Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the transistor:

$$I_E = I_B + I_C$$

**Step 2.** By definition of the **common-base current gain** $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_C = \alpha I_E$$

**Step 3.** By definition of the **common-emitter current gain** $\beta$:

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $I_C = \alpha I_E$ and $I_B = I_C/\beta$ into the KCL equation:

$$I_E = \frac{I_C}{\beta} + I_C = I_C \left( \frac{1}{\beta} + 1 \right)$$

**Step 5.** Replace $I_C$ with $\alpha I_E$ on the right-hand side:

$$I_E = \alpha I_E \left( \frac{1}{\beta} + 1 \right)$$

**Step 6.** Cancel $I_E$ from both sides (since $I_E \neq 0$):

$$1 = \alpha \left( \frac{1}{\beta} + 1 \right) = \frac{\alpha}{\beta} + \alpha$$

**Step 7.** Rearrange to solve for $\beta$:

$$\frac{\alpha}{\beta} = 1 - \alpha \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

**Step 8.** Invert both sides to get the reverse relation:

$$\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}$$

$$\boxed{\;\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \quad;\quad \alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}\;}$$

**Step 9. Sanity check:** For a typical BJT, $\alpha = 0.99$. Then $\beta = 0.99 / (1 - 0.99) = 0.99 / 0.01 = 99$. This matches the typical range of $\beta$ (50 to 300) for small-signal BJTs. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exact $I_C$ Equation with Leakage Current

The ideal equation $I_C = \beta I_B$ assumes zero leakage. In reality, even with $I_B = 0$, a small leakage current $I_{CBO}$ flows from collector to base (CB junction reverse saturation current). We need the **exact** formula for $I_C$.

**Step 1.** Start from the KCL at the transistor:

$$I_E = I_B + I_C$$

**Step 2.** Start from the CB definition with leakage:

$$I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $I_E = I_B + I_C$ into the equation for $I_C$:

$$I_C = \alpha (I_B + I_C) + I_{CBO}$$

**Step 4.** Distribute $\alpha$:

$$I_C = \alpha I_B + \alpha I_C + I_{CBO}$$

**Step 5.** Move the $\alpha I_C$ term to the left side:

$$I_C - \alpha I_C = \alpha I_B + I_{CBO}$$

**Step 6.** Factor the left side:

$$I_C (1 - \alpha) = \alpha I_B + I_{CBO}$$

**Step 7.** Divide both sides by $(1 - \alpha)$:

$$I_C = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} I_B + \frac{I_{CBO}}{1 - \alpha}$$

**Step 8.** Use the identity $\dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} = \beta$ and $\dfrac{1}{1-\alpha} = 1 + \beta$:

$$\boxed{\;I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}\;}$$

**Step 9.** When $I_B = 0$, the collector current equals the **CE leakage current**:

$$I_{CEO} = (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}$$

This is a critically important result. Because $\beta$ is large (≈ 100), $I_{CEO}$ is **100 times larger** than $I_{CBO}$. This is why silicon transistors (with very low $I_{CBO}$) are strongly preferred over germanium for high-temperature applications.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transconductance from Output Characteristics

The **transconductance** $g_m$ is a key small-signal parameter that quantifies how effectively the input voltage $V_{BE}$ controls the output current $I_C$ in the active region.

**Step 1.** From the active-region large-signal relation (ignoring leakage):

$$I_C \approx I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

where $I_S$ is the B–E saturation current.

**Step 2.** Differentiate both sides with respect to $V_{BE}$:

$$\frac{d I_C}{d V_{BE}} = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T} \cdot \frac{1}{V_T} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$$

**Step 3.** By definition, transconductance is:

$$g_m = \frac{d I_C}{d V_{BE}} \bigg\vert_{V_{CE} = \text{const}}$$

**Step 4.** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{I_C}{26\,\text{mV}}\;}$$

**Step 5. Numerical example:** If $I_C = 10\,\text{mA}$, then $g_m = 10 \times 10^{-3} / 26 \times 10^{-3} = 0.3846\,\text{S} = 384.6\,\text{mS}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Base–Emitter Input Resistance $r_\pi$

**Step 1.** Start with $I_C = I_S e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$ and $I_B = I_C / \beta$.

**Step 2.** Express $I_B$ in terms of $V_{BE}$:

$$I_B = \frac{I_S}{\beta} \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

**Step 3.** Differentiate $I_B$ with respect to $V_{BE}$:

$$\frac{d I_B}{d V_{BE}} = \frac{I_S}{\beta} \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T} \cdot \frac{1}{V_T} = \frac{I_B}{V_T} = \frac{I_C}{\beta V_T}$$

**Step 4.** The dynamic input resistance $r_\pi$ is the inverse of this derivative:

$$r_\pi = \frac{d V_{BE}}{d I_B} = \frac{V_T}{I_B} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$$

**Step 5.** Using the previous result that $g_m = I_C / V_T$, we can also write:

$$\boxed{\;r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m}\;}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** A silicon NPN transistor in CE configuration has $\beta = 100$ and $I_{CBO} = 1\,\text{nA}$ at room temperature. The base current is set to $I_B = 50\,\mu\text{A}$. Find (a) $\alpha$, (b) $I_C$, (c) $I_E$, (d) $I_{CEO}$, and (e) $g_m$ if $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$.

**Step (a):** Compute $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} = \frac{100}{101} \approx 0.9901$$

**Step (b):** Compute $I_C$ using the exact formula:

$$I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CBO} = 100 \times 50\,\mu\text{A} + 101 \times 1\,\text{nA}$$

$$I_C = 5000\,\mu\text{A} + 0.101\,\mu\text{A} = 5000.101\,\mu\text{A} \approx 5.0001\,\text{mA}$$

(Notice how negligibly small the leakage term is for a silicon BJT. The simplification $I_C = \beta I_B$ is excellent here.)

**Step (c):** Compute $I_E$:

$$I_E = I_B + I_C = 50\,\mu\text{A} + 5000.101\,\mu\text{A} = 5050.101\,\mu\text{A} \approx 5.0501\,\text{mA}$$

**Step (d):** Compute $I_{CEO}$:

$$I_{CEO} = (1 + \beta) I_{CBO} = 101 \times 1\,\text{nA} = 101\,\text{nA} \approx 0.101\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step (e):** Compute $g_m$:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{5.0001 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.1923\,\text{S} = 192.3\,\text{mS}$$

**Step (f):** Compute $r_\pi$:

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C} = \frac{100 \times 26 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0001 \times 10^{-3}} \approx \frac{2.6}{5.0001 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 520\,\Omega$$

All these parameters form a coherent picture of the small-signal model of the BJT in CE configuration.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — CE Configuration Biasing & Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VBB["DC Source VBB"] -->|via RB| NodeB["Node B Base"]
    VCC["DC Source VCC"] -->|via RC| NodeC["Node C Collector"]
    NodeB -->|BE junction forward biased| NodeE["Node E Emitter COMMON GROUND"]
    NodeC -->|CB junction reverse biased| NodeE
    VBB -->|VBE measure| M1["Ammeter I_B Voltmeter V_BE"]
    VCC -->|VCE measure| M2["Ammeter I_C Voltmeter V_CE"]
    NodeE --> G["Ground Reference"]
    NodeB --> IN["Input Port B-E"]
    NodeC --> OUT["Output Port C-E"]
```

**Description:** The block diagram shows the CE test setup. A variable DC source $V_{BB}$ drives the base through $R_B$, while a separate variable DC source $V_{CC}$ drives the collector through $R_C$. The emitter is tied to the common ground. $I_B$ is measured by an ammeter in the base lead, $I_C$ by an ammeter in the collector lead. $V_{BE}$ is measured across the base–emitter port, and $V_{CE}$ across the collector–emitter port.

## 4.2 Output Characteristics Curve — Regions of Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OC["OUTPUT CHARACTERISTICS IC vs VCE  family of curves"]
        direction LR
        R1["CUTOFF REGION I_B = 0  IC equals I_CEO leakage only"]
        R2["ACTIVE REGION horizontal curves  IC equals beta times I_B  AMPLIFICATION ZONE"]
        R3["SATURATION REGION steep rise near origin  VCE less than VCE sat  TRANSISTOR ON"]
        R1 --> R2
        R2 --> R3
    end
    N1["Knee Voltage VCE sat approx 0.2 V"] -.-> R3
    N2["Early Voltage V_A  extrapolated intercept on negative VCE axis"] -.-> R2
    N3["Output resistance r_o equals VA plus VCE divided by I_C"] -.-> R2
```

**Description:** This flowchart represents the three regions of the output characteristics. The active region (middle) is where amplification occurs. To the left of $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ lies saturation, and at $I_B = 0$ lies the cutoff boundary.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]`
**Define input resistance and output resistance of a BJT in CE configuration. Why does the input characteristic of CE resemble a forward-biased diode curve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Input resistance** $R_{in} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta I_B} \bigg\vert_{V_{CE} = \text{const}}$ is the dynamic resistance offered by the base–emitter port when a small AC signal is applied. For a CE transistor it typically lies in the range of **1 kΩ to 5 kΩ** (around $r_\pi$).
- **Output resistance** $R_{out} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{CE}}{\Delta I_C} \bigg\vert_{I_B = \text{const}}$ is the dynamic resistance seen at the collector when looking back into the transistor. In the active region it is high, of the order of **50 kΩ to 500 kΩ** (equal to $r_o$).
- The input characteristic resembles a forward-biased diode curve because the **base–emitter junction in CE mode is a forward-biased PN junction**. The collector side has negligible influence for small $V_{CE}$, so the input port is essentially a diode.
- (1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the diode analogy)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Paper Q2]`
**Mention any three differences between CE, CB, and CC configurations of a BJT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Current gain:** CB has $\alpha < 1$ (≈ 0.95 to 0.99); CE has $\beta = 50$ to $500$; CC has $\gamma = 1 + \beta$ (slightly higher than CE).
2. **Phase shift between input and output:** CB produces 0° phase shift; CE produces 180° phase reversal; CC produces 0° phase shift.
3. **Input resistance:** CB has very low input resistance (20 to 100 Ω); CE has medium (1 to 5 kΩ); CC has very high (150 to 600 kΩ).
- (1 mark for each correct difference)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**A (a) [7 marks]** Draw and explain the **input and output characteristics of a Common Emitter (CE) configuration of an NPN transistor**. Mark the cutoff, active, and saturation regions on the output characteristics. Briefly explain the Early Effect.

**Model Answer:**

**Input Characteristics (3 marks):**
- The input characteristics is a plot of $I_B$ (Y-axis) vs $V_{BE}$ (X-axis) at constant $V_{CE}$.
- For a fixed $V_{CE}$, as $V_{BE}$ increases beyond the threshold (≈ 0.5 V for Si), $I_B$ increases exponentially.
- The curve closely resembles the forward characteristic of a PN-junction diode, since the B-E junction *is* a forward-biased diode.
- When $V_{CE}$ is increased (with $V_{BE}$ held constant), $I_B$ decreases slightly. This is because the **Early Effect** causes the effective base width to shrink, fewer carriers recombine in the base, and hence less external base current is required.

**Output Characteristics (3 marks):**
- The output characteristics is a family of curves of $I_C$ (Y-axis) vs $V_{CE}$ (X-axis), each for a different fixed value of $I_B$.
- **Cutoff region:** $I_B = 0$ gives $I_C = I_{CEO}$ (small leakage only).
- **Active region:** $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$. The curves are nearly horizontal, with a small positive slope (Early Effect).
- **Saturation region:** $V_{CE} < V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ ≈ 0.2 V. Both junctions are forward-biased; $I_C$ is limited by the external $R_C$ and $V_{CC}$.

**Early Effect (1 mark):**
- The Early Effect (or base-width modulation) is the variation of the effective base width with the collector–base reverse-bias voltage. As $V_{CB}$ (and hence $V_{CE}$) increases, the CB depletion region widens into the base, reducing the effective base width. This causes $\beta$ to increase slightly with $V_{CE}$ and gives the output curves their small positive slope. Extrapolating the active-region curves backwards, they all meet at a point on the negative $V_{CE}$ axis at $V_{CE} = -V_A$, the **Early voltage**.

**A (b) [7 marks]** For an NPN transistor in CE configuration, the emitter current is $I_E = 5.05\,\text{mA}$. If $\alpha = 0.99$, calculate (i) $I_C$, (ii) $I_B$, (iii) $\beta$, and (iv) $I_{CEO}$ if $I_{CBO} = 1\,\mu\text{A}$.

**Model Answer:**

**Step (i) — Collector Current $I_C$:**

$$I_C = \alpha \, I_E = 0.99 \times 5.05\,\text{mA} = 4.9995\,\text{mA} \approx 5.00\,\text{mA}$$

**Step (ii) — Base Current $I_B$:**

$$I_B = I_E - I_C = 5.05\,\text{mA} - 4.9995\,\text{mA} = 0.0505\,\text{mA} = 50.5\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step (iii) — Common-Emitter Current Gain $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{0.99}{1 - 0.99} = \frac{0.99}{0.01} = 99$$

**Step (iv) — Leakage Current $I_{CEO}$:**

$$I_{CEO} = (1 + \beta) I_{CBO} = (1 + 99) \times 1\,\mu\text{A} = 100 \times 1\,\mu\text{A} = 100\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Note:** Although $I_{CBO} = 1\,\mu\text{A}$ appears small, $I_{CEO} = 100\,\mu\text{A}$ because of the multiplication by $(1+\beta)$. This is why temperature stability matters so much in CE design.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023, Adapted]`

**B (a) [7 marks]** Compare the **Common Base (CB), Common Emitter (CE), and Common Collector (CC)** configurations of a BJT in tabular form with respect to: current gain, voltage gain, input resistance, output resistance, phase shift, and typical applications. State which configuration is used as a **buffer** and why.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CB (Common Base) | CE (Common Emitter) | CC (Common Collector) |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Current gain | $\alpha < 1$ (≈ 0.95 to 0.99) | $\beta = 50$ to $500$ | $\gamma = 1 + \beta$ |
| Voltage gain | High | High | ≈ 1 (slightly less than 1) |
| Input resistance | Very low (20 to 100 Ω) | Medium (1 to 5 kΩ) | Very high (150 to 600 kΩ) |
| Output resistance | Very high (1 to 10 MΩ) | Medium (50 to 500 kΩ) | Very low (25 to 50 Ω) |
| Phase shift (input to output) | 0° (in-phase) | 180° (inverted) | 0° (in-phase) |
| Power gain | Medium | Very high | Medium |
| Typical applications | RF/Microwave amplifiers, High-frequency oscillators | Audio amplifiers, General-purpose voltage amplifier, Switch | Impedance matching, Buffer stage, Cascaded amplifier input stage |

**The CC configuration is used as a buffer** (also called an **emitter follower** or **voltage follower**) because:
- It has **very high input resistance**, so it draws negligible current from the source, preventing loading of the previous stage.
- It has **very low output resistance**, so it can drive low-impedance loads without signal loss.
- Its **voltage gain is approximately unity**, so the output voltage faithfully follows the input voltage — perfect for impedance transformation without altering signal level.

(7 marks — 4 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for identifying CC, 2 marks for the buffer justification)

**B (b) [7 marks]** A silicon NPN transistor in CE configuration operates with $I_C = 10\,\text{mA}$ and $V_{CE} = 10\,\text{V}$. Given $\beta = 150$ and Early voltage $V_A = 100\,\text{V}$, calculate (i) $g_m$, (ii) $r_\pi$, and (iii) $r_o$ at $T = 300\,\text{K}$ where $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$.

**Model Answer:**

**Step (i) — Transconductance $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.3846\,\text{S} \approx 384.6\,\text{mS}$$

**Step (ii) — Base–Emitter Input Resistance $r_\pi$:**

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C} = \frac{150 \times 26 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3.9}{0.010} = 390\,\Omega$$

**Step (iii) — Output Resistance $r_o$:**

$$r_o = \frac{V_A + V_{CE}}{I_C} = \frac{100 + 10}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{110}{0.010} = 11{,}000\,\Omega = 11\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Summary of small-signal parameters:** $g_m = 384.6\,\text{mS}$, $r_\pi = 390\,\Omega$, $r_o = 11\,\text{k}\Omega$. These are the three values you would plug into the hybrid-$\pi$ small-signal model of the BJT for amplifier analysis.

(7 marks — 2 marks each for (i) and (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including the Early voltage concept)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the leakage term in $I_C$:** In the active region, the exact equation is $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$. Many students write $I_C = \beta I_B$ only. For silicon with very small $I_{CBO}$ this is acceptable as an approximation, but in a 14-mark derivation question, you may be asked to use the full formula.
> 2. **Confusing $I_{CBO}$ and $I_{CEO}$:** $I_{CBO}$ is the leakage current with **base open** in CB mode (≈ nA for Si). $I_{CEO}$ is the leakage with **base open** in CE mode and is $(1+\beta)$ times larger (≈ μA). Mixing these up costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong sign of $\beta$ in $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$:** Always confirm the polarity. For an NPN transistor, all currents are defined as positive when flowing **into** the device.
> 4. **Mixing up regions in the output characteristics:** When asked to identify the saturation region, students often confuse it with the cutoff region. Remember: **saturation = $V_{CE}$ small, transistor ON like a switch; cutoff = $I_B = 0$, transistor OFF.**
> 5. **Forgetting units in $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$ calculations:** Always write the units explicitly — KTU examiners look for this.
> 6. **Not mentioning the Early Effect:** Any question on the slope of the output characteristics in the active region MUST invoke the Early Effect for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **CE = Common Emitter** → Emitter is the common terminal between input and output. Input is at B-E, output is at C-E.
- [ ] **Input characteristics:** $I_B$ vs $V_{BE}$ at fixed $V_{CE}$. Resembles a forward-biased diode curve.
- [ ] **Output characteristics:** $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ at fixed $I_B$. Has three regions — **Cutoff, Active, Saturation**.
- [ ] **Cutoff:** $I_B = 0$, $I_C = I_{CEO}$.
- [ ] **Active region (amplification):** $I_C \approx \beta I_B$; CB junction reverse-biased; BE junction forward-biased.
- [ ] **Saturation:** $V_{CE} \leq V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\,\text{V}$; both junctions forward-biased.
- [ ] **Early Effect:** Increasing $V_{CE}$ widens the CB depletion region, reducing base width, slightly increasing $\beta$. Output curves have a small positive slope.
- [ ] **Key relationships:** $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$, $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{1+\beta}$, $\gamma = 1 + \beta$.
- [ ] **Exact $I_C$ equation:** $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$.
- [ ] **Leakage relationship:** $I_{CEO} = (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ — always much larger than $I_{CBO}$.
- [ ] **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$.
- [ ] **Transconductance:** $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$.
- [ ] **Input resistance:** $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$.
- [ ] **Output resistance:** $r_o = \dfrac{V_A + V_{CE}}{I_C}$.
- [ ] **CB (Common Base):** $\alpha < 1$, very low $R_{in}$, very high $R_{out}$, 0° phase shift, used in RF/microwave.
- [ ] **CE (Common Emitter):** $\beta = 50$ to $500$, medium $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$, 180° phase shift, used in audio/general amplifiers.
- [ ] **CC (Common Collector / Emitter Follower):** $\gamma = 1+\beta$, very high $R_{in}$, very low $R_{out}$, 0° phase shift, unity voltage gain, used as **buffer** for impedance matching.
- [ ] **Standard constants:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$, $V_{BE(\text{ON, Si})} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$, $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\,\text{V}$.
- [ ] **Phase relationship:** CE inverts the signal; CB and CC do not.
- [ ] **Master the $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$ triangle:** these three parameters fully describe the small-signal behaviour of a BJT and are the foundation of all amplifier analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For Part A questions, memorize the three regions of the output characteristics and the definitions of $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$. For Part B questions, **always show the full derivation steps** (KCL, substitution, algebraic manipulation) — KTU examiners award step marks generously. Use the exact $I_C$ equation when leakage is given. Mention the Early Effect whenever the output characteristics or output resistance is discussed.

topics.title: Concept of Biasing and Load Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Concept of Biasing and Load Line ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Biasing? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > **Biasing** is the process of applying external DC voltages to a transistor (BJT) to establish a predetermined **DC Operating Point**, also called the **Quiescent Point (Q-point)**, represented by the coordinates (V_{CEQ}, I_{CQ}) or (V_{CEQ}, I_{BQ}), which ensures the transistor operates in the desired region of its output characteristics for a given application. In the context of a BJT, the term *bias* refers to the DC voltages applied to the emitter-base junction (V_{BE}) and collector-base junction (V_{CB}) to fix the operating point. Without proper biasing, the transistor's response to AC input signals would be non-linear, distorted, and unpredictable. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology — Operating Point (Q-point):** > The Q-point is the *quiescent* (idle, no-signal) state of the transistor — the values of I_C and V_{CE} that exist when there is no AC input signal applied. This is the *starting point* on the output characteristic curves around which the AC signal causes the transistor to swing. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw on a Hill" Picture Imagine a **see-saw** with a person sitting on one end. The person wants the see-saw to oscillate up and down (this is the **AC signal** swing). However, the see-saw is placed on a slope (a hill). - If the see-saw is placed too close to the top of the hill (operating near **cutoff**), the downward swing goes off the platform — the person falls off (signal **cutoff distortion**). - If the see-saw is placed too close to the bottom of the hill (operating near **saturation**), the upward swing hits the hilltop — the person gets crushed (signal **saturation distortion**). - The **perfect spot** is the *center* of the hill, where equal swings in both directions can occur without hitting the boundaries. **This center spot is the Q-point**, and the process of *placing* the see-saw at this center is **biasing**. > [!NOTE] > **Why is biasing necessary?** > 1. To operate the BJT in the **active region** for linear amplification. > 2. To keep the Q-point **stable** despite variations in temperature (I_{CBO} doubles every 10 °C) and transistor parameters (β varies widely even within the same batch). > 3. To ensure the AC output signal is **faithful reproduction** of the input without distortion (clamping or cutoff). > 4. To prevent **thermal runaway** — a self-destructive condition where increasing temperature causes more collector current, which causes more heating, in a positive feedback loop. ### 1.3 The Three Operating Regions of a BJT (Recap) | Region | V_{BE} State | V_{CE} State | I_C Behaviour | Application | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Cutoff** | Reverse biased | High (~ V_{CC}) | I_C ≈ 0 | OFF switch | | **Active** | Forward biased | V_{CE} between V_{CE(sat)} and V_{CC} | I_C = β I_B | **Amplifier** | | **Saturation** | Forward biased | V_{CE} ≈ V_{CE(sat)} ≈ **0.2 V** | I_C ≈ I_{C(sat)} | ON switch | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Convention:** For an **NPN** BJT in the active region, V_{BE} ≈ **0.7 V** (silicon) and V_{CE(sat)} ≈ **0.2 V**. These are the assumed values in all KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems unless otherwise stated. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Need for Biasing — The Three Uncertainties A transistor's Q-point is unstable due to three primary variations: 1. **Temperature Variation (ΔT):** The reverse saturation current I_{CBO} approximately **doubles for every 10 °C** rise in junction temperature. Since I_C = β I_B + (β+1) I_{CBO}, even a small temperature rise can shift the Q-point dramatically. 2. **Beta (β) Variation:** β is **not constant** — it varies between units of the same transistor model (e.g., BC547 may have β from 110 to 800). This means a circuit designed for β = 100 will behave differently with a transistor having β = 300. 3. **Aging & Replacement:** As transistors age or get replaced, parameters like V_{BE} (typically −2 mV/°C) and I_{CBO} drift. > [!NOTE] > A **good biasing circuit** must: > - Establish the Q-point near the **middle of the active region** (V_{CEQ} ≈ V_{CC}/2 for maximum symmetrical swing). > - Have a **stability factor S** as small as possible (ideally S = 1). > - Be **independent of β** as far as possible (β-insensitive design). ### 2.2 Types of Biasing Circuits (Standard KTU Coverage) The four classical biasing configurations studied in GXEST104 are: #### A. Fixed Bias (Base Bias) The base is connected to V_{CC} through a single resistor R_B. The simplest, but **least stable** configuration. $$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$ $$I_C = \beta I_B \quad (\text{Active region assumption})$$ $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$$ **Stability Factor:** $S = 1 + \beta$ — worst stability. #### B. Collector-to-Base Bias R_B is connected between the collector and base, providing **negative feedback** that improves stability. $$I_B = \frac{V_{CE} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$ **Stability Factor:** $S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta (R_C / R_B)}$ — better than fixed bias. #### C. Voltage Divider Bias (Emitter Stabilized) The **most widely used** configuration. Two resistors R_1 and R_2 form a voltage divider that holds the base voltage nearly constant, while an emitter resistor R_E provides DC negative feedback. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thevenin Equivalent at the Base:** > $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad ; \quad R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$$ > $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (1+\beta) R_E}$$ > $$I_C = \beta I_B \approx I_E = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_E + (R_{TH}/\beta)}$$ **Stability Factor:** $S \approx 1 + \frac{R_{TH}}{R_E}$ — excellent stability (close to 1 if R_E >> R_{TH}). #### D. Emitter Bias (Two-Supply Bias) Uses a separate V_{EE} supply for the emitter. The base is held at a fixed potential (often ground), and V_{EE} biases the emitter resistor. $$I_C \approx I_E = \frac{\vert V_{EE}\vert - V_{BE}}{R_E}$$ **Stability Factor:** $S = 1$ — best possible stability, but requires two power supplies. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | Base current (Fixed Bias) | $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$ | A | | 2 | Collector current | $I_C = \beta I_B + (β+1)I_{CBO}$ | A | | 3 | KVL on Output Loop | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | V | | 4 | DC Load Line: y-intercept (I_C max) | $I_{C(sat)} = V_{CC}/R_C$ | A (when V_{CE}=0) | | 5 | DC Load Line: x-intercept (V_CE max) | $V_{CE(off)} = V_{CC}$ | V (when I_C=0) | | 6 | DC Load Line Slope | $m = -1/R_C$ | siemens | | 7 | Stability Factor S | $S = \frac{1 + β}{1 - β (\partial I_B / \partial I_C)}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | S' (V_{BE} sensitivity) | $S' = \partial I_C / \partial V_{BE}$ | A/V | | 9 | S'' (β sensitivity) | $S'' = \partial I_C / \partial β$ | A | | 10 | Voltage Divider Thevenin Voltage | $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ | V | | 11 | Voltage Divider Thevenin Resistance | $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$ | Ω | | 12 | I_C for Voltage Divider | $I_C ≈ (V_{TH} - V_{BE})/R_E$ | A (when β R_E >> R_{TH}) | | 13 | Thermal Runaway Condition | $S \cdot \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial T} < \frac{1}{V_{CE} \cdot \theta}$ | Stability criterion | | 14 | Q-point Coordinates | $(V_{CEQ},\ I_{CQ})$ or $(V_{CEQ},\ I_{BQ})$ | — | | 15 | AC Load Line (with R_C and R_E) | Slope $= -1/(R_C \parallel R_L)$ | siemens | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **DC load line** (slope = $-1/R_C$) with the **AC load line** (slope = $-1/(R_C \parallel R_L)$). The DC load line determines the Q-point; the AC load line determines the **swing** of the output signal. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Amplifiers:** Biasing places the transistor in the active region to amplify audio signals with minimum distortion. - **Switching Circuits (Digital Logic):** Biasing pushes the transistor between cutoff (logic 0) and saturation (logic 1) — the basis of TTL and RTL logic families. - **RF Amplifiers:** Stable biasing is critical to prevent drift in the gain and noise figure of the receiver front-end. - **Power Amplifiers:** Incorrect biasing leads to **thermal runaway**, the #1 cause of transistor failure in push-pull amplifiers. - **Integrated Circuits:** Voltage divider bias is hard to implement in ICs (large resistors), so ICs use **current mirror biasing** — an advanced version of emitter bias. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The DC Load Line Equation **Step 1: Write the KVL around the Output Loop (C–E side of the transistor)** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop containing V_{CC}, R_C, and the transistor (V_{CE}): $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$$ This is the **fundamental output loop equation** used in all BJT biasing analysis. **Step 2: Rearrange to the Standard Load Line Form** $$I_C R_C = V_{CC} - V_{CE}$$ $$I_C = -\frac{V_{CE}}{R_C} + \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$ This is the equation of a **straight line** in the I_C–V_{CE} plane, with slope $-1/R_C$ and y-intercept $V_{CC}/R_C$. **Step 3: Identify the Two Endpoints (Boundary Conditions)** **Case (a): Cutoff Point** — When I_C = 0 (transistor is OFF): $$0 = -\frac{V_{CE}}{R_C} + \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$ $$V_{CE} = V_{CC}$$ So **Point A (Cutoff) = (V_{CC}, 0)** on the V_{CE} axis. **Case (b): Saturation Point** — When V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} ≈ 0 (transistor is fully ON): $$I_C = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C} \quad (\text{idealized, ignoring } V_{CE(sat)})$$ $$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$ So **Point B (Saturation) = (0, V_{CC}/R_C)** on the I_C axis. **Step 4: Construct the Load Line** Join Point A (V_{CC}, 0) and Point B (0, V_{CC}/R_C) with a straight line on the output characteristics graph. This straight line is the **DC load line**. **Step 5: Locate the Q-point** The Q-point is the intersection of the load line with the **specific I_B curve** corresponding to the base current set by the biasing network. $$I_{BQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} \quad (\text{for fixed bias})$$ Reading vertically from the intersection down to the V_{CE} axis gives **V_{CEQ}**, and horizontally to the I_C axis gives **I_{CQ}**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Stability Factor S (for Fixed Bias) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **stability factor S** is defined as the rate of change of collector current with respect to the reverse saturation current I_{CBO}, with V_{BE} and β held constant: > $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} \bigg|_{V_{BE}, \beta = \text{const}}$$ **Step 1: Start with the collector current equation** $$I_C = \beta I_B + (β + 1) I_{CBO}$$ **Step 2: Differentiate both sides with respect to I_{CBO}** $$\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = \beta \cdot \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CBO}} + (β + 1)$$ **Step 3: For fixed bias, I_B is determined by V_{CC} and R_B** $$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$ Since V_{CC}, V_{BE}, and R_B are all independent of I_{CBO}: $$\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CBO}} = 0$$ **Step 4: Substitute and simplify** $$S = \beta \cdot 0 + (β + 1) = 1 + β$$ $$\boxed{S_{\text{fixed}} = 1 + \beta}$$ This is the **highest** stability factor, meaning the worst stability. For β = 100, S = 101 — a 1 µA change in I_{CBO} causes a 101 µA change in I_C. ### 3.3 Derivation: Q-point for Voltage Divider Bias (Exact Method) > [!NOTE] > The **exact** analysis uses Thevenin's theorem to simplify the base side. **Step 1: Find the Thevenin Equivalent looking into the base from the R_1–R_2 divider** $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ $$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ **Step 2: Apply KVL around the Base–Emitter loop (V_{TH}, R_{TH}, B-E junction, R_E)** $$V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E$$ **Step 3: Substitute I_E = (β+1) I_B** $$V_{TH} - V_{BE} = I_B R_{TH} + (β + 1) I_B R_E$$ $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (β + 1) R_E}$$ **Step 4: Compute I_C, I_E, and V_{CE}** $$I_C = \beta I_B$$ $$I_E = I_C + I_B \approx I_C$$ $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$$ ### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Standard) **Problem:** For a fixed bias circuit with V_{CC} = 12 V, R_B = 100 kΩ, R_C = 1 kΩ, β = 100, and V_{BE} = 0.7 V. Find: (a) I_B, (b) I_C, (c) V_{CE}, (d) coordinates of saturation and cutoff points, (e) Stability factor S. **Solution:** **Step (a): Base current** $$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{100 \times 10^3}$$ $$I_B = \frac{11.3}{100{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{I_B = 113\ \mu A}$$ **Step (b): Collector current** $$I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \times 113\ \mu A$$ $$\boxed{I_C = 11.3\ mA}$$ **Step (c): Collector-Emitter voltage** $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 12 - (11.3 \times 10^{-3})(1 \times 10^3)$$ $$V_{CE} = 12 - 11.3$$ $$\boxed{V_{CE} = 0.7\ V}$$ **Step (d): Load line endpoints** Cutoff: $V_{CE(\text{off})} = V_{CC} = 12\ V$, $I_C = 0$ Saturation: $I_{C(\text{sat})} = V_{CC}/R_C = 12/1\text{k} = 12\ mA$, $V_{CE} = 0$ **Step (e): Stability factor** $$S = 1 + \beta = 1 + 100 = 101$$ ### 3.5 Quick Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Bias | Application | Recommended Bias | Reason | |:---|:---:|:---| | Switching (ON/OFF) | Fixed bias | Simplicity, no need for stability | | Small-signal AC amplifier (single supply) | **Voltage Divider** | Best balance of stability & simplicity | | Precision DC amplifier | Emitter bias (two-supply) | S = 1, β-independent | | RF tuned amplifier | Collector-to-base | Compact, decent stability | | High-temperature environment | Voltage Divider + thermistor | Compensates for I_{CBO} drift | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Biasing Concept Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["VCC DC Power Supply"]:::power --> B["Biasing Network
R1, R2, RB, RE"]:::biasnet B --> C["Base Terminal
IB determined"]:::node C --> D["BJT Active Region
VBE = 0.7 V"]:::bjt D --> E["Collector Current
IC = beta times IB"]:::node E --> F["Load Resistor RC"]:::node F --> G["VCE across BJT"]:::node G --> H["Quiescent Point Q
VCEQ, ICQ"]:::qpoint H --> I["AC Input Signal"]:::signal I --> J["AC Output Signal
Swing around Q"]:::signal B -.Feedback.-> B classDef power fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef biasnet fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef bjt fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef node fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef qpoint fill:#ff99ff,stroke:#660066,color:#000 classDef signal fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Fixed Bias Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR VCC["+VCC"]:::power --- RCtop["RC"]:::resistor RCtop --- Cnode["Collector"]:::bjt Bnode["Base"]:::bjt --- RBtop["RB"]:::resistor RBtop --- VCC2["+VCC"]:::power Enode["Emitter"]:::bjt --- GND["Ground"]:::power Cnode --- Q1["NPN BJT
Active Region"]:::bjt Bnode --- Q1 Enode --- Q1 Cnode --> VCEout["VCE = VCC - IC x RC"]:::output Bnode --> IBRB["IB = VCC - VBE over RB"]:::output classDef power fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000066,color:#000 classDef bjt fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef output fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Schematic — Voltage Divider Bias (KTU Most Important) ```mermaid flowchart TB VCC["+VCC"]:::power --- R1top["R1"]:::resistor R1top --- Bnode["Base Node B"]:::bjt Bnode --- R2down["R2"]:::resistor R2down --- GND1["Ground"]:::power Bnode --- Q1["NPN BJT"]:::bjt RCtop["RC"]:::resistor --- Cnode["Collector C"]:::bjt RCtop --- VCC Enode["Emitter E"]:::bjt --- REdown["RE"]:::resistor REdown --- GND2["Ground"]:::power CEnode["CE bypass cap
optional"]:::capacitor -.shunts.-> REdown Bnode --> VTHcalc["VTH = VCC times R2 over R1+R2"]:::output Enode --> IEcalc["IE = VTH - VBE over RE approx"]:::output classDef power fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000066,color:#000 classDef bjt fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef capacitor fill:#ccffff,stroke:#006666,color:#000 classDef output fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Load Line Construction Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start with Output Characteristics
