S close to 1"]:::good classDef input fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef node fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000066,color:#000 classDef good fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define the term **Operating Point (Q-point)** of a BJT. Why is it necessary to stabilize the Q-point in transistor biasing? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Operating Point (Q-point)** of a BJT is the point on the output characteristics (I_C vs V_{CE}) that represents the DC collector current I_C and the DC collector-emitter voltage V_{CE} when no AC signal is applied to the transistor. It is specified by the coordinates (V_{CEQ}, I_{CQ}). (1 mark) Stabilization of the Q-point is necessary because transistor parameters such as β, V_{BE}, and I_{CBO} are **temperature-dependent** and **vary from unit to unit**. A change in temperature (or replacement of the transistor) can shift the Q-point away from the desired location, leading to: - Distortion of the amplified output signal (cutoff or saturation clipping), - Possible **thermal runaway**, causing permanent damage to the device. (1 mark) Hence, a properly designed biasing network — such as the **voltage divider bias** with an emitter resistor — is used to keep the Q-point stable near the middle of the active region regardless of temperature or β variations. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** Draw the DC load line of a BJT and write down the expressions for its two endpoints. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **DC load line** is a straight line drawn on the output characteristics (I_C vs V_{CE} plot) of a BJT, representing the locus of all possible (V_{CE}, I_C) combinations as I_B is varied, given a fixed supply V_{CC} and collector resistor R_C. (1 mark) It is derived from the KVL of the output loop: $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$$ **Endpoint 1 — Cutoff Point (on V_{CE} axis):** $$I_C = 0 \Rightarrow V_{CE} = V_{CC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Point A: } (V_{CC},\ 0)$$ **Endpoint 2 — Saturation Point (on I_C axis):** $$V_{CE} = 0 \Rightarrow I_C = V_{CC}/R_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Point B: } (0,\ V_{CC}/R_C)$$ The straight line joining A and B is the DC load line, with slope $-1/R_C$. (1 mark) > [!NOTE] > **Marking Tip:** A neat diagram (1 mark) + Endpoint equations (1 mark) + Slope expression (1 mark) = full marks. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Never assume V_{BE} = 0** — always use **V_{BE} = 0.7 V** for silicon BJTs unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. (Loss of up to 1 mark.) > 2. **Units must be consistent** — convert all resistances to Ω and voltages to V before computing currents in Amperes. Mixing kΩ with V gives mA correctly, but mixing MΩ with V requires extra care. > 3. **Show the KVL equations explicitly** — partial marks are awarded for writing the loop equations even if the final arithmetic is wrong. > 4. **Active region assumption** must be verified at the end: V_{CE} should be between 0 and V_{CC}. If V_{CE} ≤ 0, the BJT is in saturation; if V_{CE} ≥ V_{CC}, it's in cutoff. > 5. **The load line question almost always carries a graph** — practice drawing the load line neatly with labelled axes, endpoints, and Q-point. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** For a **fixed bias** circuit with V_{CC} = 15 V, R_B = 470 kΩ, R_C = 2.2 kΩ, β = 100, and V_{BE} = 0.7 V: - (i) Determine the Q-point (I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ}). (4 marks) - (ii) Find the stability factor S. (1 mark) - (iii) Calculate the new Q-point if β changes to 150 (R_B unchanged). (2 marks) **Model Solution:** **(i) Calculation of Q-point:** Step 1: Base current $$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{15 - 0.7}{470 \times 10^3}$$ $$I_B = \frac{14.3}{470{,}000} = 30.43\ \mu A$$ Step 2: Collector current $$I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \times 30.43\ \mu A = 3.043\ mA$$ Step 3: Collector-emitter voltage $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 15 - (3.043 \times 10^{-3})(2.2 \times 10^3)$$ $$V_{CE} = 15 - 6.695 = 8.305\ V$$ **Q-point = (V_{CEQ} = 8.3 V, I_{CQ} = 3.04 mA)** (4 marks — 1 for each correct sub-step + 1 for active region verification) Step 4: Active region check Since $0 < V_{CEQ} = 8.3\ V < V_{CC} = 15\ V$, the transistor is in the **active region**. ✓ **(ii) Stability factor:** $$S = 1 + \beta = 1 + 100 = \mathbf{101}$$ (1 mark) **(iii) New Q-point with β = 150:** In fixed bias, the base current I_B depends only on V_{CC}, V_{BE}, and R_B — **NOT on β**. So I_B remains 30.43 µA. $$I_C' = \beta' I_B = 150 \times 30.43\ \mu A = 4.565\ mA$$ $$V_{CE}' = 15 - (4.565 \times 10^{-3})(2.2 \times 10^3) = 15 - 10.04 = 4.96\ V$$ **New Q-point = (V_{CE}' = 4.96 V, I_{C}' = 4.57 mA)** (2 marks) > [!WARNING] > **Critical Insight:** The Q-point **shifted significantly** (from 8.3 V to 4.96 V) just because β changed from 100 to 150. This demonstrates the **poor stability of fixed bias** — a 50% increase in β caused a 40% shift in V_{CE}. This is why voltage divider bias is preferred. --- **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** For the **voltage divider bias** circuit, V_{CC} = 20 V, R_1 = 10 kΩ, R_2 = 5 kΩ, R_C = 2 kΩ, R_E = 1 kΩ, β = 100, V_{BE} = 0.7 V: - (i) Find the Thevenin equivalent voltage V_{TH} and resistance R_{TH} at the base. (2 marks) - (ii) Compute I_B, I_C, and V_{CE}. (4 marks) - (iii) Sketch the DC load line and mark the Q-point. (3 marks) **Model Solution:** **(i) Thevenin Equivalent:** $$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 20 \times \frac{5}{10 + 5} = 20 \times \frac{1}{3} = 6.667\ V$$ $$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{10 \times 5}{10 + 5} = \frac{50}{15} = 3.333\ k\Omega$$ (2 marks) **(ii) Currents and V_{CE}:** Step 1: Base current $$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (β+1) R_E} = \frac{6.667 - 0.7}{3.333\text{k} + (101)(1\text{k})}$$ $$I_B = \frac{5.967}{3.333\text{k} + 101\text{k}} = \frac{5.967}{104.333\text{k}}$$ $$I_B = 57.2\ \mu A$$ Step 2: Collector current $$I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \times 57.2\ \mu A = 5.72\ mA$$ Step 3: Collector-emitter voltage $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$$ $$V_{CE} = 20 - (5.72 \times 10^{-3})(2\text{k} + 1\text{k})$$ $$V_{CE} = 20 - (5.72 \times 3) = 20 - 17.16 = 2.84\ V$$ **Q-point = (V_{CEQ} = 2.84 V, I_{CQ} = 5.72 mA)** (4 marks) (3 marks for load line sketch — endpoints + Q-point) **DC Load Line:** - Saturation point: I_{C(sat)} = V_{CC}/(R_C + R_E) = 20/3k = 6.67 mA, V_{CE} = 0 - Cutoff point: V_{CE(off)} = V_{CC} = 20 V, I_C = 0 - Slope = $-1/(R_C + R_E) = -1/3000$ S (3 marks for the load line) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **fixed bias** method of biasing a BJT. Derive expressions for I_B, I_C, and V_{CE}. Mention one major drawback. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Circuit Diagram:** (2 marks) [Draw fixed bias circuit: V_{CC} — R_B — Base; V_{CC} — R_C — Collector; Emitter grounded; NPN BJT with arrow pointing out.] **Derivation:** Applying KVL around the **base–emitter loop** (V_{CC} → R_B → B–E junction → ground): $$V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE}$$ $$\boxed{I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}} \quad (1\ mark)$$ For an active region BJT: $$I_C = \beta I_B \quad (1\ mark)$$ Applying KVL around the **output loop** (V_{CC} → R_C → C–E junction → ground): $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$$ $$\boxed{V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C} \quad (1\ mark)$$ **Stability Factor:** $$S = 1 + \beta \quad (1\ mark)$$ **Major Drawback:** The stability factor is very high (S = 1 + β ≈ 100), meaning the Q-point is **highly sensitive** to variations in β and temperature. A small change in β causes a large change in I_C, leading to Q-point instability. (1 mark) --- **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]** Explain the concept of the **DC load line** with a suitable diagram. Discuss how the Q-point shifts when (i) R_C is increased, and (ii) I_B is increased. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Concept of DC Load Line:** (3 marks) The DC load line is the locus of all possible (V_{CE}, I_C) points the transistor can take for a given V_{CC} and R_C, when no AC signal is applied. It is derived from the KVL equation: $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE} \Rightarrow I_C = -\frac{V_{CE}}{R_C} + \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$ This is a straight line with: - Cutoff endpoint: (V_{CC}, 0) - Saturation endpoint: (0, V_{CC}/R_C) - Slope: $-1/R_C$ The Q-point is the **intersection** of this load line with the I_B curve. **(i) Effect of Increasing R_C:** (2 marks) If R_C is increased: - I_{C(sat)} = V_{CC}/R_C **decreases** → saturation point moves **down**. - V_{CE(off)} = V_{CC} **unchanged** → cutoff point stays the same. - The load line **rotates clockwise** about the cutoff point, becoming **steeper**. - For a given I_B, the Q-point moves **down and to the left** (lower I_C, lower V_{CE}), possibly entering saturation. **(ii) Effect of Increasing I_B:** (2 marks) If I_B is increased: - The load line itself does **not** change (it depends only on V_{CC} and R_C). - The transistor operates on a **higher I_B curve**. - The Q-point moves **up and to the left** along the fixed load line (higher I_C, lower V_{CE}), approaching saturation. > [!NOTE] > **Summary Diagram (Q-point shifts):** > - ↑R_C → steeper load line, Q moves toward saturation. > - ↑I_B → Q moves along the same load line toward saturation. > - ↓R_C → flatter load line, Q moves toward cutoff. > - ↓I_B → Q moves along the same load line toward cutoff. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Must Remember for KTU Exam:** - ✅ **Biasing** = setting the DC Q-point; **Load line** = graphical tool to find the Q-point. - ✅ Q-point coordinates: **(V_{CEQ}, I_{CQ})** for an NPN BJT. - ✅ V_{BE} = **0.7 V** (silicon) — the single most important assumed value in KTU problems. - ✅ V_{CE(sat)} ≈ **0.2 V**; I_{CBO} **doubles per 10 °C** rise in temperature. - ✅ Load line endpoints: **Cutoff (V_{CC}, 0)** and **Saturation (0, V_{CC}/R_C)**. - ✅ Load line slope: **−1/R_C** (DC), **−1/(R_C ∥ R_L)** (AC). - ✅ Stability factors: **Fixed bias: S = 1 + β**; **Voltage divider: S ≈ 1 + R_{TH}/R_E**; **Emitter bias: S = 1**. - ✅ **Voltage divider bias** is the most commonly used configuration (best trade-off). - ✅ Q-point should be near the **middle of the load line** (V_{CEQ} ≈ V_{CC}/2) for **maximum symmetrical AC swing**. - ✅ **Thermal runaway** condition: S × (∂I_C/∂T) must be < 1/(V_{CE} × θ) — failing this destroys the transistor. - ✅ For BJT to be in **active region**: V_{CE} must be **between 0 and V_{CC}** (strictly, V_{CE} > V_{CE(sat)}). - ✅ β (current gain) is **not constant** — it varies with I_C, temperature, and between units of the same model. - ✅ Thevenin equivalent at the base of voltage divider: V_{TH} = V_{CC} R_2/(R_1+R_2), R_{TH} = R_1 ∥ R_2. - ✅ In voltage divider bias with β R_E >> R_{TH}, I_C ≈ (V_{TH} − V_{BE})/R_E — **β-independent** design. - ✅ Always **verify the operating region** at the end of every numerical — partial marks are lost if this step is skipped. - ✅ The **load line is always drawn on the OUTPUT characteristics** (I_C vs V_{CE}), not on the input characteristics. - ✅ For switching applications, Q-point is set at **cutoff (OFF)** or **saturation (ON)** — never in the active region. - ✅ For linear amplifiers, Q-point must be in the **center of the active region** to avoid distortion on both halves of the AC cycle.

topics.title: Transistor as a Switch, Transistor as an Amplifier (Circuit Diagram and Working)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transistor as a Switch, Transistor as an Amplifier (Circuit Diagram and Working)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) — A Brief Recap

A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** is a three-terminal, current-controlled semiconductor device consisting of two PN junctions formed by sandwiching either a thin **P-type** layer between two **N-type** layers (**NPN**) or a thin **N-type** layer between two **P-type** layers (**PNP**). The three terminals are the **Emitter (E)**, **Base (B)**, and **Collector (C)**.

The BJT is the fundamental "workhorse" of analog and digital electronics. Depending on how we bias it, the same physical device can perform radically different functions — the two most important being acting as a **Switch** and acting as an **Amplifier**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The BJT has three operating regions defined by the biasing of its two junctions:
> 1. **Cutoff Region** — Both junctions reverse-biased → used for **Switch OFF** state.
> 2. **Active Region** — EB forward-biased, CB reverse-biased → used for **Amplification**.
> 3. **Saturation Region** — Both junctions forward-biased → used for **Switch ON** state.

---

### 1.2 Transistor as a Switch — The Intuitive Overview

#### Formal Definition
When a BJT is used as a switch, it operates between its two extreme states: **Cutoff (OFF)** and **Saturation (ON)**. In the OFF state, the transistor behaves like an **open circuit** (no current flows). In the ON state, it behaves like a **closed switch** (maximum current flows, limited only by the external circuit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** "A transistor switch is an electronic switching circuit in which a BJT is driven alternately into saturation and cutoff by a control signal applied to its base, thus controlling a much larger current through the collector-emitter path."

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Tap and Pipe Model
Imagine a **water tap** connected to a large overhead tank. The tap's small handle controls a huge water flow:

| Transistor Terminal | Water Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Base (B)** | Small handle of the tap | Tiny control signal |
| **Collector (C)** | Main water inlet (large pipe) | Large current to be controlled |
| **Emitter (E)** | Water outlet | Current returns to ground |
| **VCC (Supply)** | Overhead water tank | Energy source |

- A **small force on the handle (small base current)** opens the gate, allowing a **huge flow of water (large collector current)**.
- **No force = Tap closed (Cutoff, OFF).**
- **Enough force = Tap fully open (Saturation, ON).**

This is exactly how a transistor switch works — a **small base current controls a large collector current**, and the **ratio is set by β (current gain)**.

#### Practical Applications
- **Digital Logic Gates** (TTL — Transistor-Transistor Logic)
- **LED Drivers and Relay Drivers**
- **Inverter Circuits** in power electronics
- **Touch Switches and Alarm Circuits**
- **Microcontroller Output Buffers**

---

### 1.3 Transistor as an Amplifier — The Intuitive Overview

#### Formal Definition
When a BJT operates in its **active region**, a small AC signal applied between the **base and emitter** produces a much larger, faithful replica of the signal across the **collector and emitter** load. This process of **boosting signal power (voltage × current)** while preserving its waveform shape is called **amplification**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** "A transistor amplifier is a circuit that uses a BJT biased in the active region to convert a small input AC signal into a larger output signal, where the output signal is a scaled and faithful reproduction of the input, with the additional power supplied by the DC bias source (VCC)."

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Megaphone
Think of a person speaking softly into a **megaphone**:
- The person's soft voice (small AC input signal) enters the megaphone.
- The megaphone uses the **batteries inside it (DC supply VCC)** to power the speaker.
- The output is a **louder version of the same voice** (amplified AC signal).
- The transistors in the megaphone perform this exact function — they use **DC power to boost AC signals**.

**Key Insight:** The transistor itself does **NOT create energy**; it merely **controls the flow of DC energy from VCC** so that the output signal is a much larger copy of the input.

#### Practical Applications
- Audio amplifiers (radios, TVs, public address systems)
- RF amplifiers in wireless transmitters
- Operational amplifier (Op-Amp) input stages
- Sensor signal conditioning
- Communication receivers

---

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Silicon BJT) |
|---|---|---|
| Base-Emitter ON Voltage | $V_{BE(on)}$ | **0.7 V** |
| Base-Emitter Cut-in Voltage | $V_{BE(cut\text{-}in)}$ | **0.5 V** |
| Collector-Emitter Saturation Voltage | $V_{CE(sat)}$ | **0.2 V** |
| Base-Emitter Saturation Voltage | $V_{BE(sat)}$ | **0.8 V** |
| Thermal Voltage at 27°C | $V_T$ | **25.85 mV** |
| Common Current Gain (CE mode) | $\beta$ or $h_{FE}$ | **20 to 500** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The BJT — Three Operating Regions in Depth

The BJT's operation in any of the three regions is governed by the biasing voltages applied to its two PN junctions. The following table consolidates the critical conditions:

| Operating Region | EB Junction (B-E) | CB Junction (B-C) | Transistor Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff** | Reverse-biased | Reverse-biased | **Switch — OFF** (Open Circuit) |
| **Active** | Forward-biased | Reverse-biased | **Amplifier** (Linear region) |
| **Saturation** | Forward-biased | Forward-biased | **Switch — ON** (Closed Circuit) |

---

### 2.2 Transistor as a Switch — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Circuit Configuration (NPN Common-Emitter Switch)

The most common configuration for a transistor switch is the **NPN BJT in Common-Emitter (CE) configuration**. The circuit consists of:
- **$V_{CC}$**: DC supply voltage at the collector (typically +5V or +12V).
- **$R_C$**: Collector resistor that limits the collector current in saturation.
- **$R_B$**: Base resistor that limits the base current.
- **$V_{in}$**: Input control signal (low or high).
- **Load**: Could be an LED, relay coil, or motor connected between $V_{CC}$ and collector.

#### 2.2.2 Working — Step-by-Step Logic

**State 1: Switch OFF (Cutoff Region)**
1. Input voltage $V_{in} < 0.5\text{ V}$ (less than the cut-in voltage).
2. The base-emitter junction is **not forward-biased**; hence $I_B \approx 0$.
3. Since $I_C = \beta I_B$, we get $I_C \approx 0$.
4. The voltage drop across $R_C$ is $I_C \cdot R_C \approx 0$.
5. Therefore, the output voltage $V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C \approx V_{CC}$.
6. **The transistor acts as an OPEN SWITCH**. No current flows through the load.

**State 2: Switch ON (Saturation Region)**
1. Input voltage $V_{in} \geq 0.7\text{ V}$ (sufficient to forward-bias the EB junction).
2. A base current $I_B$ flows through $R_B$.
3. If $I_B$ is large enough, the transistor enters **saturation**.
4. The collector current reaches its **maximum possible value**, limited only by $R_C$ and $V_{CC}$:

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C}$$

5. The output voltage drops to $V_{out} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$.
6. **The transistor acts as a CLOSED SWITCH** with a small voltage drop (~$V_{CE(sat)}$).

#### 2.2.3 The Critical Saturated Base Current Calculation

For the transistor to enter saturation, the base current must be **greater than or equal to** the minimum value required:

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{\beta_{DC} \cdot R_C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule for Switching:** To **guarantee saturation**, the actual base current must be at least **2 to 5 times** $I_{B(sat)}$ (this is called the **overdrive factor**). This ensures reliable switching even with variations in $\beta$ and temperature.

**The minimum value of $R_B$ to ensure saturation:**

$$R_{B(min)} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B(sat)}}$$

---

### 2.3 Transistor as an Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.3.1 The Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier Circuit

The **Common-Emitter (CE) configuration** is the most widely used BJT amplifier topology because it provides:
- High **voltage gain**
- High **current gain**
- **Phase inversion** (180° phase shift) between input and output
- Moderate **power gain**

**Circuit Components:**
- **$V_{CC}$**: DC biasing supply at the collector.
- **$R_C$**: Collector load resistor (converts collector current variations into output voltage variations).
- **$R_B$**: Base bias resistor (sets the DC base current / operating Q-point).
- **$V_{in}$ (or $v_i$)**: Small AC input signal applied between base and emitter.
- **$V_{out}$ (or $v_o$)**: Amplified AC output signal taken between collector and emitter (with respect to ground).
- The **emitter is common** to both input (B-E) and output (C-E) loops — hence the name.

#### 2.3.2 Working — Why Amplification Occurs (The Core "Why")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Heart of Amplification:** A small change in $V_{BE}$ (input) causes a large change in $I_C$ (because $I_C = \beta I_B$ and $I_B$ depends exponentially on $V_{BE}$). This large change in $I_C$ produces a large change in the voltage drop across $R_C$, and hence a large change in $V_{CE}$ — which is the output voltage.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. A **DC bias** is applied to the base through $R_B$ so that the BJT operates in the **active region** at a quiescent (Q) operating point with $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$.
2. A **small AC input signal** $v_i$ is superimposed on the DC base bias.
3. This causes $V_{BE}$ to vary slightly: $V_{BE}(t) = 0.7 + v_i(t)$.
4. By the **Ebers-Moll equation**, a small change in $V_{BE}$ produces an **exponentially large change** in $I_B$, and hence in $I_C$.
5. The varying $I_C$ produces a varying voltage drop $I_C R_C$ across $R_C$.
6. Since $V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$, the output voltage varies **inversely** with $I_C$.
7. The result: $v_o$ is an **amplified, 180° phase-inverted** version of $v_i$.

#### 2.3.3 Important Amplifier Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current Gain** | $A_I$ | Ratio of output (collector) to input (base) AC current | $A_I = \dfrac{i_c}{i_b} = \beta_{ac} = h_{fe}$ |
| **Voltage Gain** | $A_V$ | Ratio of output AC voltage to input AC voltage | $A_V = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i}$ |
| **Power Gain** | $A_P$ | Product of current and voltage gain | $A_P = A_V \times A_I$ |
| **Input Resistance** | $R_{in}$ | Resistance seen looking into the base | $R_{in} \approx \beta \cdot r_e$ |
| **Output Resistance** | $R_{out}$ | Resistance seen looking into the collector | $R_{out} \approx R_C$ |
| **Transistor Input Resistance** | $r_e$ | Dynamic resistance of EB junction | $r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} = \dfrac{25\text{ mV}}{I_E}$ |
| **Voltage Gain (CE)** | $A_V$ | For CE amplifier | $A_V \approx \dfrac{-R_C}{r_e}$ |

The **negative sign** in the voltage gain formula indicates the **180° phase inversion** — when $v_i$ increases, $I_C$ increases, $I_C R_C$ increases, and $V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ **decreases**.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they form 90% of KTU 2024 numerical problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | Fundamental BJT current relation (active region) | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | $I_E = I_B + I_C = (1 + \beta) I_B$ | Emitter current relation | A |
| 3 | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C}$ | Max collector current in saturation (switch ON) | A |
| 4 | $I_{B(sat)} = \dfrac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{DC}}$ | Min base current to enter saturation | A |
| 5 | $R_{B(max)} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B(sat)}}$ | Max base resistor for reliable saturation | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | $V_{out(OFF)} = V_{CC}$ | Output voltage in cutoff | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $V_{out(ON)} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$ | Output voltage in saturation | V |
| 8 | $r_e = \dfrac{25\text{ mV}}{I_E}$ | Dynamic emitter resistance (amplifier) | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | $A_V = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$ | Voltage gain of CE amplifier | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $A_I = \beta_{ac}$ | Current gain of CE amplifier | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $A_P = A_V \times A_I$ | Power gain | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_{out} = A_P \times P_{in}$ | Output power in terms of input | Watts (W) |
| 13 | $V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$ | KVL across output loop | V |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Switching Applications:** The transistor switch is the basic building block of all **digital logic families** (TTL, DTL, RTL) and **microprocessor I/O pins**. Every logic gate in a CPU is essentially a network of transistor switches.
- **Amplifier Applications:** Every audio system, from a cheap earphone to a concert-grade amplifier, uses CE amplifier stages (often cascaded as multistage amplifiers) to boost weak audio signals to audible levels.
- **Mixed-Signal ICs:** Modern integrated circuits like op-amps, timers (555), and voltage regulators internally use both transistor switches (for biasing) and transistor amplifiers (for the gain stages).
- **Power Electronics:** Inverters and motor drives use power BJTs (or their modern equivalents — IGBTs, MOSFETs) as high-speed switches operating at kHz frequencies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Saturated Collector Current in a Transistor Switch

**Problem Setup:** An NPN transistor switch has $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$, $R_C = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, and is driven into saturation. The transistor's $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\text{ V}$. Find $I_{C(sat)}$.

**Step 1:** Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the collector-emitter loop (from $V_{CC}$ through $R_C$, through the transistor from C to E, to ground).

$$V_{CC} = I_C \cdot R_C + V_{CE}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values.

$$12 = I_C \cdot (1000) + 0.2$$

**Step 3:** Isolate $I_C$.

$$I_C \cdot 1000 = 12 - 0.2 = 11.8$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $I_C$.

$$I_C = \frac{11.8}{1000} = 0.0118\text{ A} = 11.8\text{ mA}$$

**Conclusion:** $I_{C(sat)} = 11.8\text{ mA}$. This is the **maximum possible collector current** when the switch is ON.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Base Current to Ensure Saturation

**Problem Setup:** Given $\beta_{DC} = 100$, find the minimum base current required to saturate the transistor from the previous example.

**Step 1:** Write the saturation condition. Saturation occurs when:

$$I_C \geq \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B$$

**Step 2:** For the **edge of saturation**, $I_C = I_{C(sat)}$ and $I_B = I_{B(sat)}$:

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{DC}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{11.8\text{ mA}}{100} = 0.118\text{ mA} = 118\text{ \mu A}$$

**Conclusion:** A base current of at least **118 μA** is required to just barely saturate the transistor. In practice, we use **2 to 5 times** this value (overdrive factor) for reliable switching.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Base Resistor for Reliable Switching

**Problem Setup:** With $V_{in} = 5\text{ V}$ (a digital HIGH from a logic gate), $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.8\text{ V}$, and $I_{B(sat)} = 118\text{ \mu A}$, find the maximum allowed value of $R_B$.

**Step 1:** Apply KVL around the base-emitter loop.

$$V_{in} = I_B \cdot R_B + V_{BE(sat)}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $R_B$.

$$R_B = \frac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_B}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$R_B = \frac{5 - 0.8}{118 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4.2}{118 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the result.

$$R_B = 35593\text{ }\Omega \approx 35.6\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Conclusion:** The base resistor $R_B$ must be **less than or equal to 35.6 kΩ** to ensure the transistor saturates. Designers typically choose a standard value like **10 kΩ** or **4.7 kΩ** to provide a comfortable overdrive factor.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Voltage Gain of a Common-Emitter Amplifier

**Problem Setup:** A CE amplifier has $I_E = 2\text{ mA}$ (DC emitter current) and $R_C = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$. Find the voltage gain $A_V$.

**Step 1:** Calculate the dynamic emitter resistance $r_e$.

$$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{25\text{ mV}}{2\text{ mA}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute.

$$r_e = 12.5\text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Apply the CE voltage gain formula.

$$A_V = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the values.

$$A_V = -\frac{4700}{12.5} = -376$$

**Conclusion:** The voltage gain is **−376** (dimensionless). The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output. The magnitude means the output signal amplitude is **376 times** the input signal amplitude.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Power Gain of the CE Amplifier

**Step 1:** Use the previously calculated values: $A_V = -376$ and assume $\beta_{ac} = 100$.

**Step 2:** Calculate the power gain.

$$A_P = \vert A_V \vert \times A_I = 376 \times 100 = 37600$$

**Step 3:** Express in decibels (dB) — a common engineering unit.

$$A_P(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(37600) = 10 \times 4.575 = 45.75\text{ dB}$$

**Conclusion:** The CE amplifier provides a power gain of **37,600** (or **45.75 dB**), meaning the output signal carries 37,600 times more power than the input signal — with the additional power drawn from the $V_{CC}$ supply.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Switch vs. Amplifier Comparison

| Property | Transistor as a Switch | Transistor as an Amplifier |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Region** | Cutoff or Saturation | Active (linear) region |
| **Input Signal** | Large (digital HIGH/LOW) | Small (AC waveform) |
| **Output Signal** | Switched DC level ($V_{CC}$ or $\approx 0$) | Amplified AC waveform |
| **Linearity** | Not required (binary operation) | **Essential** — output must be faithful reproduction |
| **Power Efficiency** | High (transistor is either fully OFF or fully ON) | Moderate (transistor dissipates power in active region) |
| **Switching Speed** | Limited by turn-on/turn-off times | Limited by transit frequency $f_T$ |
| **Power Dissipation** | Low (except during transition) | Continuous (DC quiescent power) |
| **Output Waveform** | Square wave (digital) | Sinusoidal or any waveform (analog) |
| **Typical Application** | Logic gates, relay drivers | Audio amps, RF amps |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Transistor as a Switch (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Control Signal V_in] --> B[Base Resistor R_B]
    B --> C[BJT Base Terminal]
    C --> D{NPN Transistor}
    D -- I_C --> E[Collector Resistor R_C]
    E --> F[DC Supply V_CC]
    D -- I_E --> G[Ground / Common Emitter]
    F --> H[Load: LED / Relay]
    H --> D

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style D fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#D32F2F,color:#000
    style H fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The small control voltage $V_{in}$ passes through $R_B$ to bias the BJT. When $V_{in}$ is HIGH, the transistor switches ON, allowing the large $V_{CC}$ current to flow through the load. When $V_{in}$ is LOW, the transistor switches OFF and the load is de-energized.

---

### 4.2 Circuit Schematic — NPN Transistor Switch (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_Loop [INPUT CONTROL LOOP]
        V_IN[Vin: 0V or 5V] --> R_B[RB: Base Resistor]
        R_B --> B_TERMINAL[Base B]
    end

    subgraph Output_Loop [OUTPUT POWER LOOP]
        VCC[VCC: +5V or +12V] --> R_C[RC: Collector Resistor]
        R_C --> C_TERMINAL[Collector C]
    end

    subgraph Transistor [NPN BJT Q1]
        B_TERMINAL --> Q1[Q1: NPN Transistor]
        C_TERMINAL --> Q1
        Q1 --> E_TERMINAL[Emitter E]
    end

    E_TERMINAL --> GND[Ground: 0V]
    VOUT[Vout: Taken at Collector] --> C_TERMINAL

    style V_IN fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    style VCC fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    style Q1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style R_B fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style R_C fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style VOUT fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    style GND fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#37474F,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Transistor as an Amplifier (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small AC Input Signal v_i] --> B[Coupling Capacitor C_in]
    B --> C[DC Bias Network R_B from V_CC]
    C --> D[Base of NPN BJT in Active Region]
    D --> E[BJT Q1: Amplifying Device]
    E -- i_c --> F[Collector Resistor R_C converts current to voltage]
    F --> G[DC Supply V_CC]
    E --> H[Emitter to Ground]
    F --> I[Coupling Capacitor C_out]
    I --> J[Amplified AC Output v_o with 180 degree phase shift]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style E fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style J fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

---

### 4.4 Operating Region Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Input Signal V_in to Base]) --> Q1{V_in greater than 0.7V?}
    Q1 -- No --> Cutoff[Cutoff Region: I_B approx 0, I_C approx 0]
    Cutoff --> Result1[Switch is OFF, V_out approx V_CC]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is I_B greater than I_B(sat)?}
    Q2 -- No --> Active[Active Region: Linear Amplification]
    Active --> Result2[Amplifier Mode: v_o is amplified version of v_i]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Sat[Saturation Region: I_C max limited by R_C]
    Sat --> Result3[Switch is ON, V_out approx V_CE_sat]

    style Start fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style Cutoff fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style Active fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style Sat fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style Result1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    style Result2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style Result3 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,color:#000
```

---

### 4.5 Signal Waveform Diagram (Amplifier Input vs. Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Waveform [INPUT SIGNAL v_i]
        I1[At t=0: v_i at minimum negative peak]
        I2[At t=T/4: v_i crosses zero going up]
        I3[At t=T/2: v_i at maximum positive peak]
        I4[At t=3T/4: v_i crosses zero going down]
        I5[At t=T: v_i completes one cycle]
    end

    subgraph Output_Waveform [OUTPUT SIGNAL v_o, AMPLIFIED and 180 PHASE INVERTED]
        O1[At t=0: v_o at MAXIMUM positive peak]
        O2[At t=T/4: v_o crosses zero going DOWN]
        O3[At t=T/2: v_o at minimum negative peak]
        O4[At t=3T/4: v_o crosses zero going UP]
        O5[At t=T: v_o completes one inverted cycle]
    end

    I1 -.-> O1
    I2 -.-> O2
    I3 -.-> O3
    I4 -.-> O4
    I5 -.-> O5

    style I1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style I3 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style O1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style O3 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** When the input signal $v_i$ is at its **positive peak**, the output $v_o$ is at its **negative peak** — and vice versa. The output amplitude is much larger (scaled by $\vert A_V \vert$). This is the signature of a **Common-Emitter amplifier**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between the cutoff and saturation regions of a BJT. Which of these is used when the transistor acts as a switch?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cutoff Region | Saturation Region |
|---|---|---|
| **Base-Emitter Junction** | Reverse-biased | Forward-biased |
| **Collector-Base Junction** | Reverse-biased | Forward-biased |
| **Base Current ($I_B$)** | Approximately 0 | Maximum (greater than $I_{B(sat)}$) |
| **Collector Current ($I_C$)** | Approximately 0 | Maximum, $I_{C(sat)} = (V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)})/R_C$ |
| **$V_{CE}$** | $\approx V_{CC}$ (high) | $\approx 0.2$ V (low) |
| **Transistor Behaviour** | **Open switch (OFF)** | **Closed switch (ON)** |
| **Application** | Switch-OFF state | Switch-ON state |

**Conclusion:** When used as a switch, the transistor operates in **cutoff (OFF state)** or **saturation (ON state)**. (3 marks)

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Why is the Common-Emitter (CE) configuration the most widely used BJT amplifier? List any two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Common-Emitter (CE) configuration is the most widely used BJT amplifier because:

1. **High Voltage Gain:** The CE amplifier provides a relatively high voltage gain ($A_V \approx -R_C / r_e$), typically in the range of 100 to 1000.

2. **High Current Gain:** It also provides current gain equal to $\beta$ (typically 50 to 300), leading to very high power gain.

3. **Moderate Input and Output Impedance:** It has a moderately high input impedance and moderately high output impedance, making it suitable as a **general-purpose intermediate stage** in multistage amplifiers.

4. **180° Phase Inversion:** The output is 180° out of phase with the input, which is useful in many feedback and oscillator applications.

5. **Ease of Biasing:** The biasing network is simple, requiring just a base resistor $R_B$ and collector resistor $R_C$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an NPN transistor as a switch in common-emitter configuration. Mention the conditions for cutoff and saturation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a transistor switch circuit, $V_{CC} = 10\text{ V}$, $R_C = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\text{ V}$, and $\beta_{DC} = 80$. The input voltage is $V_{in} = 5\text{ V}$ and $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.8\text{ V}$. Calculate: (i) the saturated collector current $I_{C(sat)}$, (ii) the minimum base current $I_{B(sat)}$ to ensure saturation, and (iii) the maximum value of base resistor $R_B$ to guarantee saturation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a):**

**Circuit Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
        V_CC (+10V)
          |
          |
         [R_C = 1.2 kΩ]
          |
          +-------- V_out (taken at collector)
          |
        Collector
          |
          |
        __|__   NPN BJT
       |     |  (Q1)
       |  B  |C
       |_____| ---------
          |E
          |
         GND (0V)

        V_in (+5V)
          |
         [R_B]
          |
          +------ Base (B)
```

**Working Explanation:**

**Condition 1 — Switch OFF (Cutoff):**
- When $V_{in} < V_{BE(cut\text{-}in)} \approx 0.5\text{ V}$, the base-emitter junction is **not forward-biased**.
- $I_B \approx 0$, hence $I_C = \beta I_B \approx 0$.
- The voltage across $R_C$ is $I_C R_C \approx 0$.
- Therefore, $V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C \approx V_{CC} = 10\text{ V}$.
- The transistor behaves as an **open switch** — no current flows through the load.

**Condition 2 — Switch ON (Saturation):**
- When $V_{in} \geq 0.7\text{ V}$, the base-emitter junction is forward-biased.
- A base current $I_B$ flows, given by $I_B = (V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)})/R_B$.
- If $I_B$ is sufficiently large, the transistor enters saturation.
- $I_C$ reaches its maximum value $I_{C(sat)}$, limited only by $R_C$.
- $V_{out} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$.
- The transistor behaves as a **closed switch** with a small voltage drop.

**Summary of Conditions:**

| State | Condition | $I_B$ | $I_C$ | $V_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OFF (Cutoff)** | $V_{in} < 0.5\text{ V}$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx V_{CC}$ |
| **ON (Saturation)** | $V_{in} \geq 0.7\text{ V}$ and $I_B \geq I_{B(sat)}$ | $I_{B(sat)}$ | $I_{C(sat)}$ | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$ |

**(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for OFF state, 2 for ON state)**

---

**Model Answer (b):**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 10\text{ V}$, $R_C = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\text{ V}$, $\beta_{DC} = 80$, $V_{in} = 5\text{ V}$, $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.8\text{ V}$.

**(i) Saturated Collector Current $I_{C(sat)}$:**

Using KVL around the output loop:

$$V_{CC} = I_{C(sat)} \cdot R_C + V_{CE(sat)}$$

Substituting values:

$$10 = I_{C(sat)} \cdot (1200) + 0.2$$

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{10 - 0.2}{1200} = \frac{9.8}{1200}$$

$$\boxed{I_{C(sat)} = 8.167\text{ mA}}$$

**(ii) Minimum Base Current $I_{B(sat)}$:**

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{DC}} = \frac{8.167\text{ mA}}{80}$$

$$\boxed{I_{B(sat)} = 0.1021\text{ mA} = 102.1\text{ μA}}$$

**(iii) Maximum Base Resistor $R_B$:**

Using KVL around the input loop:

$$V_{in} = I_{B(sat)} \cdot R_B + V_{BE(sat)}$$

$$5 = (0.1021 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot R_B + 0.8$$

$$R_B = \frac{5 - 0.8}{0.1021 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4.2}{1.021 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{R_B = 41,136\text{ }\Omega \approx 41.14\text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Design Note:** In practice, we choose $R_B$ **less than** this value (e.g., 10 kΩ) to provide a 4× overdrive factor and ensure **reliable saturation** even with $\beta$ variations and temperature changes. **(7 marks: 2 for part i, 2 for part ii, 3 for part iii)**

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an NPN transistor as a common-emitter (CE) amplifier. Derive the expression for voltage gain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CE amplifier has a DC emitter current of $I_E = 5\text{ mA}$ and a collector resistor $R_C = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$. The transistor's $\beta = 120$. Calculate: (i) the dynamic emitter resistance $r_e$, (ii) the voltage gain $A_V$, (iii) the current gain $A_I$, and (iv) the power gain $A_P$ (in dB). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a):**

**Circuit Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
           V_CC (+12V)
             |
             |
            [R_C = 2.2 kΩ]
             |
             +-------- v_out (taken at collector, with respect to ground)
             |
          Collector
             |
             |
           __|__   NPN BJT
          |     |  (Q1)
          |  B  |C
          |_____|
             |E
             |
            GND (0V)

    v_i --> [Base]
             |
            [R_B]  <-- bias from V_CC

    Note: Emitter is common (grounded) for both input and output loops.
```

**Working Explanation:**

**Step 1: DC Biasing:**
- A DC voltage is applied through $R_B$ to the base so that the transistor operates in the **active region** with a quiescent (Q-point) where $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ and $I_C$ is at a steady DC value.

**Step 2: AC Input Application:**
- A small AC input signal $v_i$ is applied between base and emitter. The total base-emitter voltage becomes: $V_{BE}(t) = 0.7 + v_i(t)$.

**Step 3: Amplification Mechanism:**
- The small change $\Delta V_{BE} = v_i$ produces a proportionally large change in $I_B$, and hence a much larger change in $I_C = \beta \cdot \Delta I_B$.
- This change in $I_C$ produces a change in the voltage drop across $R_C$: $\Delta V_{RC} = \Delta I_C \cdot R_C$.
- The output voltage changes as: $v_o = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$, so $\Delta v_o = -\Delta I_C \cdot R_C$.

**Step 4: Phase Inversion:**
- When $v_i$ increases positively, $I_C$ increases, $I_C R_C$ increases, so $V_{CE}$ **decreases** → the output is **180° out of phase** with the input.

**Derivation of Voltage Gain:**

The dynamic (small-signal) emitter resistance is:

$$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{25\text{ mV}}{I_E}$$

For a CE amplifier, applying small-signal analysis, the voltage gain is:

$$A_V = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

The **negative sign** confirms the **180° phase inversion**. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 2 for derivation)**

---

**Model Answer (b):**

**Given:** $I_E = 5\text{ mA}$, $R_C = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 120$.

**(i) Dynamic Emitter Resistance $r_e$:**

$$r_e = \frac{25\text{ mV}}{I_E} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 5\text{ }\Omega$$

$$\boxed{r_e = 5\text{ }\Omega}$$

**(ii) Voltage Gain $A_V$:**

$$A_V = -\frac{R_C}{r_e} = -\frac{2200}{5} = -440$$

$$\boxed{A_V = -440 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output.

**(iii) Current Gain $A_I$:**

$$A_I = \beta_{ac} = 120$$

$$\boxed{A_I = 120 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**(iv) Power Gain $A_P$ (in dB):**

$$A_P = \vert A_V \vert \times A_I = 440 \times 120 = 52{,}800$$

Converting to decibels:

$$A_P(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(52{,}800) = 10 \times 4.7226$$

$$\boxed{A_P \approx 47.23\text{ dB}}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for part i, 2 for part ii, 1 for part iii, 2 for part iv)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $A_V$:** Many students write $A_V = +R_C/r_e$. This loses 1 mark. The **negative sign is mandatory** because CE amplifiers invert the phase.
> 2. **Using $I_C$ instead of $I_E$ in $r_e$:** Strictly, $r_e = V_T / I_E$. Using $I_C$ gives a slight error. Use $I_E$ for full marks.
> 3. **Confusing $\beta_{DC}$ and $\beta_{AC}$:** For switching, use $\beta_{DC}$ (for $I_B(sat)$ calculation). For amplification, use $\beta_{AC}$ (≈ $h_{fe}$). They are slightly different.
> 4. **Forgetting to include $V_{CE(sat)}$ in $I_{C(sat)}$ calculation:** The full KVL is $V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE(sat)}$. Neglecting $V_{CE(sat)}$ gives a small error.
> 5. **Not mentioning the overdrive factor:** In switching design, mentioning the 2×–5× overdrive factor for $I_B$ shows deep understanding and earns extra credit.
> 6. **Drawing incorrect circuit diagrams:** The collector resistor $R_C$ must be between $V_{CC}$ and the collector, NOT between emitter and ground. Wrong placement = lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Master These 12 Points:**

1. ✅ A BJT has **three regions**: **Cutoff** (switch OFF), **Active** (amplifier), **Saturation** (switch ON). Know the biasing conditions for each.

2. ✅ In **Cutoff**: $I_B = 0$, $I_C = 0$, $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$ → transistor = **open switch**.

3. ✅ In **Saturation**: $V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$, $I_C$ is maximum and limited by $R_C$ → transistor = **closed switch**.

4. ✅ In **Active Region**: $I_C = \beta I_B$, both EB forward-biased and CB reverse-biased → **linear amplification**.

5. ✅ The **key switch formulas** are: $I_{C(sat)} = (V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)})/R_C$ and $I_{B(sat)} = I_{C(sat)}/\beta_{DC}$.

6. ✅ The **key amplifier formulas** are: $r_e = 25\text{ mV}/I_E$ and $A_V = -R_C/r_e$.

7. ✅ The **CE amplifier inverts phase (180°)** — input goes up, output goes down. This is a signature feature.

8. ✅ **Common-Emitter (CE)** is the most popular BJT amplifier because it provides **high voltage gain AND high current gain** (high power gain).

9. ✅ For **reliable switching**, always use an **overdrive factor of 2× to 5×** when calculating $I_B$.

10. ✅ Standard values to remember: $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7\text{ V}$, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\text{ V}$, $V_T = 25\text{ mV}$ at 27°C.

11. ✅ **Power gain in dB**: $A_P(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(A_P)$.

12. ✅ **Common mistakes** to avoid: (a) forgetting the negative sign in $A_V$, (b) using $I_C$ instead of $I_E$ in $r_e$, (c) drawing $R_C$ in the wrong position in the circuit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Coupled Amplifier: Circuit Diagram and Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RC Coupled Amplifier: Circuit Diagram and Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **RC Coupled Amplifier** is a multi-stage **Common Emitter (CE) transistor amplifier** in which the output of one stage is coupled to the input of the next stage through a **resistor–capacitor (RC) coupling network**. The coupling capacitor blocks the DC bias of the first stage from interfering with the DC bias of the second stage, while allowing the time-varying (AC) signal to pass forward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> "An RC coupled amplifier uses a capacitor to couple the output of one stage to the input of the next stage. It is the most widely used coupling method in audio-frequency (AF) amplifiers because it is inexpensive, compact, and provides excellent impedance matching between successive stages."

### Key Performance Parameters (As per KTU Exam Convention)

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Typical Value (Audio Amp) |
|---|---|---|
| **Midband Voltage Gain (A_mid)** | Dimensionless (or **dB**) | 40 dB to 60 dB per stage |
| **Lower Cutoff Frequency (f_L)** | **Hz** | 20 Hz – 100 Hz |
| **Upper Cutoff Frequency (f_H)** | **kHz – MHz** | 20 kHz – 1 MHz |
| **Bandwidth (BW)** | **Hz** | f_H − f_L |
| **Input Impedance (Z_in)** | **kΩ** | 1 kΩ – 5 kΩ |
| **Output Impedance (Z_out)** | **kΩ** | 2 kΩ – 50 kΩ |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of an **RC Coupled Amplifier** like a **relay team in athletics** 🏃‍♂️ → 🤝 → 🏃‍♀️ → 🤝 → 🏃:

- The **baton** = the weak AC signal (audio, music, voice)
- **Runner 1 (Stage 1)** receives the baton, runs fast, but his hands are sweaty — he can't pass the baton directly because the next runner is standing on a different starting block with a different stance. So they use a **handoff zone** (the coupling capacitor) that allows the baton to be transferred cleanly.
- The **sweaty hands** = different DC bias voltages of each stage. If connected directly, the DC of stage 1 would disturb the bias of stage 2 and both transistors would break down.
- The **coupling capacitor (C_C)** = a smart "filter hand-off" that **stops DC** (sweat/dirt) but **passes AC** (the baton/clean signal).
- **Each stage boosts the signal** — the next runner runs faster, the signal gets stronger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "RC" Coupled?**
> The signal is **R**esistively loaded by the collector resistor R_C and the load R_L, and **C**apacitively coupled to the next stage. The combination of R and C elements gives this amplifier its name.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** **R**esistor–**C**apacitor = **R**eceive & **C**ascade. The amplifier "receives" a small signal and "cascades" it through multiple stages for massive amplification — this is the soul of every public address (PA) system, radio, and TV audio front-end.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Design Constants Used

- **Boltzmann Voltage (V_T)** = **kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV at 27 °C** (used to calculate r'_e of transistor)
- **Base of Natural Logarithm (e)** = 2.71828
- **3 dB Cutoff** = the frequency at which voltage gain drops to **0.707 × A_mid** (or **70.7 %** of midband gain), equivalent to **half-power point**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Line:**
> "The 3 dB point is the **half-power frequency** because power is proportional to V², and (1/√2)² = 1/2."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why an RC Coupling Network? — The Engineering Logic

A single transistor stage gives a gain of only ~50–100. To obtain a gain of 1000 or more (e.g., for a microphone pre-amplifier), we must **cascade** multiple stages. But cascading directly (transformer or direct coupling) has drawbacks:

| Coupling Type | Drawback |
|---|---|
| **Transformer Coupling** | Heavy, expensive, distorts at low frequency, hum pick-up |
| **Direct Coupling** | DC drift problem — the operating point (Q-point) shifts between stages |
| **RC Coupling** ✅ | Compact, cheap, low DC drift, excellent frequency response for **audio** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Magic Roles of the Coupling Capacitor (C_C):**
> 1. Blocks DC between stages → preserves individual Q-points
> 2. Passes AC signal forward → maintains amplification
> 3. Provides AC isolation → stages don't load each other in DC
> 4. Rejects DC drift → thermal stability across the cascade

---

## 2.2 Circuit Components (One Stage of RC Coupled Amplifier)

```
     V_CC (+12 V)
        |
       [R_C]   ← Collector Resistor (≈ 4.7 kΩ)
        |
        ●──────┤├── C_C2 (Output Coupling Cap ≈ 4.7 µF)
        |              |
       (C) BJT         [R_L] ← Load Resistor (≈ 10 kΩ)
        |  (NPN)        |
   C_in┤├──            ─── V_out
        |              ─── GND
        B
        |
   [R_1]──┐
   [R_2]──┘ (Voltage Divider Bias)
        |
        E
        |
       [R_E] (Emitter Resistor ≈ 1 kΩ)
        |
       [C_E] (Emitter Bypass Capacitor ≈ 100 µF)
        |
       ─── GND
```

**Each component's job:**

- **R_1, R_2** — Voltage divider to set DC base bias (V_B)
- **R_E** — Provides thermal stability (DC negative feedback)
- **C_E** — Bypasses R_E for AC signals so AC gain is not reduced
- **R_C** — Converts collector current variations into voltage variations
- **C_in, C_C2** — Coupling capacitors (block DC, pass AC)
- **R_L** — Load where the amplified output is delivered

---

## 2.3 The Three Frequency Regions — How the Amplifier Behaves

The RC coupled amplifier exhibits **three distinct regions** of frequency response:

### 🔻 Region 1: Low-Frequency Region (f < f_L)
- The coupling capacitors (C_in, C_C2) and the bypass capacitor (C_E) act as **high impedances** at low frequencies.
- The reactance **X_C = 1/(2πfC)** is very large at low f → a large voltage drop occurs across the capacitor.
- Result: **Gain rolls off (decreases) as frequency decreases.**

### ➖ Region 2: Mid-Frequency Region (f_L < f < f_H)
- All capacitors behave as **short circuits** (X_C ≈ 0).
- All internal transistor capacitances behave as **open circuits** (very high reactance).
- The amplifier behaves **ideally**, giving a **flat maximum gain (A_mid)**.
- This is the **useful bandwidth** of the amplifier.

### 🔺 Region 3: High-Frequency Region (f > f_H)
- The coupling/bypass capacitors are still short circuits.
- But **internal transistor capacitances** (C_be, C_bc, C_ce — parasitic Miller capacitance) become significant.
- Their reactance **X_C decreases with frequency**, so they **shunt the signal** to ground.
- Result: **Gain rolls off as frequency increases.**

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLDEN TABLE** — Memorize this table fully. Every formula below has appeared (or will appear) in KTU previous year papers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reactance of Capacitor** | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Increases as f decreases → high impedance at LF |
| 2 | **Transistor Input Resistance (approx.)** | $r'_{\pi} = \dfrac{\beta \, V_T}{I_C}$ | Input impedance looking into base |
| 3 | **Transistor Emitter Resistance** | $r'_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \dfrac{25 \text{ mV}}{I_E \text{(mA)}}$ | Small-signal emitter resistance |
| 4 | **Midband Voltage Gain (no bypass)** | $A_{mid} = -\dfrac{\beta \,(R_C \Vert R_L)}{r'_{\pi}}$ | Negative sign = 180° phase inversion |
| 5 | **Midband Voltage Gain (with C_E bypass)** | $A_{mid} = -g_m \, (R_C \Vert R_L)$ | $g_m = I_C/V_T$ is transconductance |
| 6 | **Gain in Decibels (dB)** | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | Always express gain in dB in KTU paper |
| 7 | **Lower Cutoff (due to input coupling cap C_in)** | $f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, (R_S + r'_{\pi}) \, C_{in}}$ | Set by source R_S and base resistance |
| 8 | **Lower Cutoff (due to emitter bypass C_E)** | $f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, (r'_e \Vert R_E) \, C_E}$ | Most dominant LF cutoff in practice |
| 9 | **Lower Cutoff (due to output coupling C_C2)** | $f_{L3} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, (R_C + R_L) \, C_{C2}}$ | Generally smaller effect |
| 10 | **Effective Lower Cutoff** | $f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$ | Vector sum (since 3 dB points add in quadrature) |
| 11 | **Miller Capacitance (input side)** | $C_{M1} = C_{bc} \, (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Dominates high-frequency cutoff |
| 12 | **Upper Cutoff Frequency** | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, R_{eq} \, C_{M1}}$ | Set by Miller effect |
| 13 | **Bandwidth** | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Useful range of amplification |
| 14 | **Total Cascade Gain (n stages)** | $A_{total} = A_1 \times A_2 \times \cdots \times A_n$ | Multiplicative in linear scale |
| 15 | **Cascade Gain in dB** | $A_{total(dB)} = A_1(dB) + A_2(dB) + \cdots + A_n(dB)$ | **Additive in dB scale** ✅ |
| 16 | **Half-Power Point (3 dB)** | $P_{out} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2} \;\Leftrightarrow\; V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Definition of cutoff frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU SHORTCUT — Why dB scale is sacred:**
> Voltage gains of 50 × 50 × 50 = 125,000 are hard to write. In dB: 34 dB + 34 dB + 34 dB = **102 dB**. This is why KTU papers always quote gain in dB.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Why RC Coupled Amplifier is Used |
|---|---|
| **Public Address (PA) Systems** | Wide audio bandwidth (20 Hz – 20 kHz) matches human hearing |
| **Radio Receivers (AM/FM IF stages)** | Intermediate frequency stages use RC coupling |
| **TV Audio Front-Ends** | Low cost + flat response over voice range |
| **Hearing Aids** | Miniaturized, low-voltage RC cascade on a single IC chip |
| **Oscilloscope Pre-Amplifiers** | Wide bandwidth with high gain (e.g., 100 MHz scopes) |
| **Sensor Signal Conditioning** | Amplifies tiny signals from thermocouples, strain gauges |
| **Biomedical ECG/EEG Amplifiers** | Low-noise, high-gain first stage amplification |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate Active:** Every algebraic step is shown. No jumps. No shortcuts. You can reproduce this in your KTU answer script verbatim.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Midband Voltage Gain (A_mid) of a CE Stage

### Step 1 — Draw the AC equivalent circuit at midband frequencies
At midband, all coupling and bypass capacitors are **AC short circuits** (X_C ≈ 0). Replace C_in, C_E, C_C2 with wires.

### Step 2 — Identify the small-signal model of the BJT
Use the **hybrid-π model**:
- Input side: a resistance **r'_π** (also called **h_ie** or **β·r'_e**) between base and emitter
- Output side: a **current source g_m·v_be** from collector to emitter
- Here $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ = transconductance (in Siemens)

### Step 3 — Write the small-signal collector current
When an AC signal v_be appears between base and emitter, the collector AC current is:

$$i_c = g_m \cdot v_{be}$$

The text translation: the small variation in collector current is directly proportional to the small variation in base-emitter voltage, scaled by the transconductance.

### Step 4 — Compute the output voltage
The output voltage v_out appears across the **parallel combination of R_C and R_L**:

$$R_{ac} = R_C \Vert R_L = \dfrac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L}$$

Using Ohm's law (KVL on output loop):

$$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot R_{ac} = -g_m \cdot v_{be} \cdot R_{ac}$$

The negative sign appears because an **increase in v_be causes an increase in i_c, which causes a larger drop across R_C, which reduces v_C** — this is the famous **180° phase inversion** of the CE amplifier.

### Step 5 — Compute the voltage gain
Since v_in = v_be (input is applied at the base-emitter loop):

$$A_{mid} = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m \cdot R_{ac}$$

$$\boxed{\,A_{mid} = -g_m \, (R_C \Vert R_L)\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Expressing A_mid in dB (KTU-expected form):**
> $$A_{mid(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_{mid}\vert = 20 \log_{10}[g_m \cdot (R_C \Vert R_L)]$$

### Worked Numerical Example (for KTU practice)

**Given:** I_C = 1 mA, R_C = 4.7 kΩ, R_L = 10 kΩ, T = 27 °C.

**Step A** — Compute g_m:

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{1 \text{ mA}}{25 \text{ mV}} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04 \text{ S} = 40 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step B** — Compute R_C ∥ R_L:

$$R_C \Vert R_L = \dfrac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \dfrac{47}{14.7} = 3.197 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step C** — Compute A_mid:

$$A_{mid} = -0.04 \times 3197 = -127.9$$

**Step D** — Convert to dB:

$$A_{mid(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(127.9) = 20 \times 2.107 = 42.14 \text{ dB}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lower Cutoff Frequency f_L (Bypass Capacitor Dominant)

The **emitter bypass capacitor C_E** is usually the dominant low-frequency cutoff. Here's the exhaustive derivation.

### Step 1 — AC equivalent at low frequency
At low frequency, the bypass capacitor C_E is **not** a short circuit. It has impedance:

$$Z_E = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_E} \;\Vert\; R_E = \dfrac{R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}$$

### Step 2 — The effective emitter impedance seen by the AC signal
For small-signal analysis, the emitter sees R_E in parallel with C_E:

$$Z_E(\omega) = \dfrac{R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}$$

### Step 3 — Effective transconductance (with unbypassed emitter)
The gain with an unbypassed emitter resistor (at low f) becomes:

$$A_v(\omega) = \dfrac{-g_m R_{ac}}{1 + g_m Z_E(\omega)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Z_E

$$A_v(\omega) = \dfrac{-g_m R_{ac}}{1 + g_m \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j\omega R_E C_E)$:

$$A_v(\omega) = \dfrac{-g_m R_{ac} (1 + j\omega R_E C_E)}{(1 + j\omega R_E C_E) + g_m R_E}$$

Let $A_{mid} = -g_m R_{ac}$ and $R'_{E} = R_E \Vert r'_e \approx r'_e$ (since R_E ≫ r'_e):

$$A_v(\omega) = \dfrac{A_{mid}(1 + j\omega R_E C_E)}{1 + g_m R_E + j\omega R_E C_E}$$

### Step 5 — Define the corner frequency
The pole (denominator zero) is at:

$$\omega_{L2} = \dfrac{1 + g_m R_E}{R_E C_E} \approx \dfrac{g_m R_E}{R_E C_E} = \dfrac{g_m}{C_E}$$

For low frequencies, $1 + g_m R_E \approx g_m R_E$:

$$\omega_{L2} \approx \dfrac{1}{(r'_e) \, C_E}$$

### Step 6 — Final result

$$\boxed{\,f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, r'_e \, C_E}\,}$$

Or equivalently using the time-constant method:

$$f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, (R_E \Vert r'_e) \, C_E}$$

### Numerical Example
**Given:** r'_e = 25 Ω, C_E = 100 µF, R_E = 1 kΩ.

$$f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot (25 \Vert 1000) \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Since 25 Ω ≪ 1 kΩ, $R_E \Vert r'_e \approx 24.4 \;\Omega$:

$$f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 24.4 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1}{0.01533} = 65.2 \text{ Hz}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Upper Cutoff Frequency f_H (Miller Effect)

At high frequencies, the **internal base-collector capacitance C_bc** becomes the dominant factor because of the **Miller Effect** — it gets multiplied by the gain and appears as a much larger capacitance at the input.

### Step 1 — Miller's Theorem
For an impedance Z connected between input and output of an inverting amplifier with gain A_v, Miller's theorem states:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$$

For a capacitor $C_{bc}$, $Z = 1/(j\omega C_{bc})$:

$$C_{M1} = C_{bc} \, (1 - A_v) \approx C_{bc} \, (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

For large $\vert A_v \vert$:

$$C_{M1} \approx C_{bc} \cdot \vert A_v \vert$$

### Step 2 — Effective input capacitance
The total input capacitance becomes:

$$C_{in,total} = C_{be} + C_{M1} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

Since $C_{M1} \gg C_{be}$ in CE stages:

$$C_{in,total} \approx C_{bc} \cdot \vert A_v \vert$$

### Step 3 — Form the RC low-pass filter at the input
The input source resistance R_S and the transistor input resistance r'_π form a Thévenin source. The input capacitance C_in,total creates a low-pass RC filter:

$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, R_{Th,in} \, C_{in,total}}$$

where $R_{Th,in} = (R_S \Vert R_1 \Vert R_2) \Vert r'_{\pi}$

### Step 4 — Final result

$$\boxed{\,f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, (R_S \Vert R_1 \Vert R_2 \Vert r'_{\pi}) \, [C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)]}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bandwidth and Cascade Gain

### Bandwidth
The useful range of frequencies where gain is within 3 dB of A_mid:

$$BW = f_H - f_L$$

For $f_H \gg f_L$ (typical case):

$$BW \approx f_H$$

### Cascade (n-stage) Gain
When n identical RC stages are cascaded, the **overall midband gain multiplies** and the **overall bandwidth shrinks** (gain-bandwidth product is constant):

$$A_{total} = (A_{mid})^n$$

$$f_{L,total} = \dfrac{f_{L1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}} \;\;\text{(for n identical stages)}$$

For n = 2 identical stages:
$$f_{L,total} = \dfrac{f_L}{\sqrt{\sqrt{2} - 1}} = 1.55 \, f_L$$

For n = 3 identical stages:
$$f_{L,total} = \dfrac{f_L}{\sqrt{2^{1/3} - 1}} = 1.96 \, f_L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Cascading 3 stages of 40 dB each gives a total gain of 120 dB (one million times voltage), but the lower cutoff frequency **almost doubles** — illustrating the classic **Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix (Master Reference)

| Frequency Region | Dominant Capacitor | Behaviour of C | Behaviour of Gain | Slope |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **f < f_L** | Coupling (C_in, C_C2), Bypass (C_E) | High X_C → drops voltage | Decreases as f decreases | **+20 dB/decade** rise toward midband |
| **f_L < f < f_H** (Midband) | All external caps = short | X_C ≈ 0 | **Constant A_mid** | **0 dB/decade** (flat) |
| **f > f_H** | Internal Miller (C_M1) | Low X_C → shunts signal | Decreases as f increases | **−20 dB/decade** roll-off |

> [!TIP]
> **Visualize this with a sentence:** "A BJT amplifier **listens best in the middle** and **ignores both DC thunder and ultrasonic whispers**."
