> *"The theoretical maximum rate at which information can be transmitted over a communication channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz, in the presence of additive white Gaussian noise of power $N$, with signal power $S$, is given by: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bits per second."*

**Key Features of the Theorem:**
- It gives the **upper bound** on the data rate
- For **error-free** transmission, the actual rate must be $R \leq C$
- Capacity is **linear in bandwidth** but **logarithmic in SNR**
- It is **channel-independent** — applies to any channel
- Valid for **AWGN** (Additive White Gaussian Noise) channels

**Derivation (Brief):**
Using Nyquist's sampling theorem and the fact that $M = \sqrt{1 + S/N}$ distinguishable levels can be encoded:

$$
C = 2B \times \frac{1}{2} \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Numerical Example:**
For a channel with $B = 3000$ Hz and $S/N = 1000$:

$$
C = 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) = 3000 \times 9.97 \approx 29{,}910 \text{ bps} \approx 30 \text{ kbps}
$$

*(Marks Distribution: Block diagram + elements 7M + Modes + Shannon theorem 7M)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Omitting the Noise block:** Many students forget to draw the noise block in the diagram. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU 2024 valuation. Always include noise as a separate block with a **dashed arrow** into the channel.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Transducer with Transmitter:** A *transducer* converts energy form (sound ↔ electricity). A *transmitter* processes the electrical signal for transmission. They are **not** the same block.
> 
> 3. **Wrong direction of arrows:** Arrows must show **information flow direction**. Some students draw arrows from destination to source. This loses 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to label arrows:** KTU 2024 expects each arrow to be labeled with the signal name (e.g., "$m(t)$", "$s(t)$", "$r(t)$"). Omitting labels costs 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Simplex with Half-Duplex:** A TV broadcast is **Simplex** (one-way only). A walkie-talkie is **Half-Duplex** (two-way but not simultaneous). A phone call is **Full-Duplex** (two-way simultaneous).
> 
> 6. **Wrong units in Shannon-Hartley:** The result $C$ is in **bits per second (bps)**, not bits or Hz. Always include the unit.
> 
> 7. **Modulation Equation Mistakes:** The general modulation is $s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$, not $s(t) = m(t) + c(t)$. The plus sign is **addition**, the product is **modulation**. Confusion here loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This!)**

### 📌 Universal Block Diagram — Must Memorize
- 8 blocks in order: **Source → Input Transducer → Transmitter → Channel → Receiver → Output Transducer → Destination**, with **Noise** attacking the Channel.

### 📌 Function of Each Block
- **Source:** Origin of the message (human/machine)
- **Input Transducer:** Non-electrical → electrical (e.g., microphone)
- **Transmitter:** Modulate + amplify + filter
- **Channel:** Physical medium (wired or wireless)
- **Receiver:** Demodulate + amplify + filter (reverse of transmitter)
- **Output Transducer:** Electrical → non-electrical (e.g., loudspeaker)
- **Destination:** Final recipient
- **Noise:** Unwanted, random, additive

### 📌 Key Equations (Must Memorize for ESE)
- General Modulation: $s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$
- Thermal Noise: $N = kTB$ (where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K)
- SNR in dB: $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$
- **Shannon-Hartley:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps

### 📌 Communication Modes
- **Simplex** → Radio/TV (one-way)
- **Half-Duplex** → Walkie-talkie (two-way, alternating)
- **Full-Duplex** → Mobile phone (two-way, simultaneous)

### 📌 Signal Transformation
- Baseband $m(t)$ → Modulation → Bandpass $s(t)$ → Channel (with noise) → Reception → Demodulation → Recovered $\hat{m}(t)$

### 📌 Noise is the #1 Enemy
- Always injected at the channel
- Cannot be removed by simple gain (because it is **additive**, not multiplicative)
- Effect: Reduces SNR → Increases BER → Reduces Channel Capacity

### 📌 Exam Drawing Strategy
1. Use a **horizontal layout** for the main signal path
2. Place **Noise** above/below the Channel with a **dashed arrow**
3. **Label every arrow** with the signal name
4. **Color-code or thicken** the noise block
5. Briefly note the **function of each block** below the diagram (1 line each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** This topic is the **foundation** of every communication subject you will study. Drawing the block diagram correctly and explaining the function of each block in **2–3 lines** is the easiest way to secure **3 to 14 marks** in any communication-related question paper. **Master it once — use it forever.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of Fiber Optic Communication System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Block Diagram of Fiber Optic Communication System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition A **Fiber Optic Communication System (FOCS)** is a method of transmitting information (voice, data, or video) from one place to another by sending pulses of light through an optical fiber. The light forms an electromagnetic carrier wave that is modulated to carry information. The system converts electrical signals into optical signals at the transmitter, guides them through an optical fiber, and reconverts them back to electrical signals at the receiver. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The block diagram must be drawn in functional blocks, with each block clearly labelled (Transmitter, Optical Fiber Channel, Receiver), and the signal flow (Electrical → Optical → Electrical) explicitly shown with arrows. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long, transparent drinking straw made of super-pure glass. If you shine a torch (flashlight) into one end of the straw while the inside is bent in any shape, the light still comes out the other end, no matter how zig-zag the path is. Now, imagine replacing the torch with a rapidly flickering LED that flickers in patterns (like Morse code), and at the other end, a light sensor translates these flickers back into the original sound or data. **That is essentially a fiber optic communication system** — a "light-pipe" carrying encoded information at the speed of light. - **The LED/Laser** = The flickering torch (Transmitter) - **The glass straw** = The optical fiber (Channel) - **The light sensor** = The photodiode (Receiver) - **The flickering pattern** = Modulated data ### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Speed of light in vacuum (c):** $3 \times 10^8$ m/s - **Speed of light in fiber:** $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s - **Standard operating wavelengths:** **850 nm, 1300 nm, and 1550 nm** - **Typical attenuation in silica fiber:** **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm - **Typical bandwidth-distance product:** up to **several Tbps·km** > [!NOTE] > **Core Concept:** Fiber optics is fundamentally a **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** phenomenon. The core has a higher refractive index ($n_1$) than the cladding ($n_2$), which traps light inside the core even when the fiber is bent. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Functional Block Decomposition A complete FOCS consists of **three primary functional blocks**: #### Block 1: Optical Transmitter Converts the input electrical signal into a modulated optical signal. | Sub-Block | Function | Key Component | |-----------|----------|---------------| | **Input Circuit** | Conditions/encodes the electrical signal (E.g., PCM, analog) | Line encoder, Pre-amplifier | | **Driver Circuit** | Provides biasing & modulation current to the source | MOSFET/BJT Driver | | **Optical Source** | Converts electrical energy into light | **LED (for short haul)** or **Laser Diode (for long haul)** | #### Block 2: Optical Fiber Channel Guides the light from transmitter to receiver with minimal loss. - **Core** (diameter 8–50 µm for single-mode, 50–62.5 µm for multi-mode): Carries the light - **Cladding** (diameter ~125 µm): Lower refractive index, causes TIR - **Buffer/Coating** (diameter ~250 µm): Mechanical protection #### Block 3: Optical Receiver Converts the received optical signal back into an electrical signal. | Sub-Block | Function | Key Component | |-----------|----------|---------------| | **Photodetector** | Converts light into electrical current | **PIN Photodiode** or **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** | | **Amplifier** | Boosts the weak electrical signal | Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA) | | **Signal Processing** | Recovers original data, filters noise | Equalizer, Decision Circuit, Decoder | ### The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Block 1. **Why modulate the source?** — A continuous beam of light carries no information. Modulation (varying intensity/current) imprints the data onto the optical carrier. 2. **Why use laser instead of LED for long distances?** — Laser light is coherent, monochromatic, and highly directional, reducing modal dispersion and allowing greater bandwidth-distance products. 3. **Why photodiode at the receiver?** — It exhibits the **photoelectric effect**, generating electron-hole pairs proportional to incident light intensity. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality:** The transmitter is essentially an **E → O (Electrical-to-Optical)** converter, while the receiver is an **O → E (Optical-to-Electrical)** converter. The fiber itself is purely passive. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** | |---------------|-------------|-----------------|----------| | Critical Angle | $\sin \theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | Minimum angle for TIR | radians/degrees | | Numerical Aperture (NA) | $NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Light-gathering ability | Dimensionless | | Acceptance Angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | Max entry angle of light | radians/degrees | | Refractive Index (fiber) | $n = \dfrac{c}{v}$ | Where $v$ is speed of light in fiber | Dimensionless | | Attenuation/Loss | $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | Signal power loss per unit length | **dB/km** | | Power at distance L | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | Received power after attenuation | Watts (W) or dBm | | Bandwidth-Distance | $B \cdot L$ product | System capacity metric | **MHz·km** or **GHz·km** | | Number of propagating modes | $N \approx \dfrac{V^2}{2}$ | For step-index fiber | Modes | | V-Number (Normalized frequency) | $V = \dfrac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot NA$ | Determines single/multi-mode behavior | Dimensionless | | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{electrons generated}}{\text{photons incident}}$ | Photodiode performance | % | | Responsivity | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | Photodiode output current per input power | A/W | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always use **dB/km** when stating attenuation, and remember the conversion: $1 \text{ dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$. If asked for total loss over distance $L$, multiply attenuation by $L$. ### Real-World Engineering Applications - **Telecommunications backbone** (undersea cables, internet infrastructure) - **Cable TV (CATV)** distribution - **Medical endoscopy** (illumination + imaging) - **Military & aerospace** (EMI-immune secure communication) - **Data centers** (high-speed server-to-server links) - **Power system communication** (substation SCADA) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Total Internal Reflection & Numerical Aperture **Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the core-cladding interface** When light traveling in the core ($n_1$) hits the cladding ($n_2$) at an angle $\theta_1$ (measured from the normal), it refracts into the cladding at angle $\theta_2$: $$ n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2 $$ **Step 2 — Condition for Total Internal Reflection** TIR occurs when the refracted angle $\theta_2 = 90°$. Substituting: $$ n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2 $$ $$ \sin \theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} $$ This is the **critical angle** $\theta_c$. For all angles of incidence greater than $\theta_c$ (measured from the normal), light is completely trapped inside the core. **Step 3 — Derive the Numerical Aperture (NA)** Light entering the fiber from air ($n_0 \approx 1$) at an external acceptance angle $\theta_a$ refracts into the core at angle $\theta_r$. Apply Snell's law at the fiber face: $$ n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_r $$ Since $\theta_r = 90° - \theta_c$ (geometry of the fiber), we have: $$ \sin \theta_r = \cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} $$ Substituting back: $$ \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ By definition, the Numerical Aperture is $NA = \sin \theta_a$: $$ \boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}} $$ --- ### Derivation 2: Numerical Worked Example — Power Budget Calculation **Problem:** A fiber optic link operates at 1550 nm with an attenuation of 0.2 dB/km. If the transmitter launches 1 mW into a 50 km fiber, what is the received power (in μW and dBm)? **Step 1 — Calculate total attenuation** $$ \alpha_{total} = 0.2 \text{ dB/km} \times 50 \text{ km} = 10 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 2 — Convert input power to dBm** $$ P_{in}(\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1 \text{ mW}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right) = 0 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 3 — Subtract attenuation to get output power in dBm** $$ P_{out}(\text{dBm}) = P_{in}(\text{dBm}) - \alpha_{total} = 0 - 10 = -10 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 4 — Convert back to linear scale (Watts)** $$ P_{out}(\text{W}) = 10^{-10/10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-1} \text{ mW} = 0.1 \text{ mW} = 100 \text{ μW} $$ **Step 5 — Verification using direct formula** $$ P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10} = (1 \text{ mW}) \cdot 10^{-10/10} = 1 \times 10^{-1} = 0.1 \text{ mW} \checkmark $$ **Result:** Received power = **0.1 mW = 100 μW = −10 dBm** --- ### Analytical Comparison Matrix: LED vs Laser Diode vs PIN vs APD | **Parameter** | **LED** | **Laser Diode (LD)** | **PIN Photodiode** | **APD** | |---------------|---------|----------------------|--------------------|---------| | Function | Transmitter source | Transmitter source | Receiver detector | Receiver detector | | Output type | Incoherent light | Coherent, monochromatic | Converts light → current | Converts light → current (with gain) | | Bandwidth | Low–Moderate (≤ 200 MHz·km) | High (≥ 10 GHz·km) | N/A | N/A | | Distance | Short-haul (< 10 km) | Long-haul (> 100 km) | Short to medium reach | Long-haul, high sensitivity | | Cost | Low | High | Low | High | | Drive complexity | Simple | Requires temperature control | Simple | Requires high reverse bias | | Spectral width | Wide (30–100 nm) | Narrow (1–5 nm) | N/A | N/A | | Speed | Slow | Very fast | Moderate | Fast + internal gain | --- ### Analytical Matrix: Advantages & Disadvantages of FOCS | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** | |----------------|-------------------| | Extremely high bandwidth | High initial installation cost | | Very low attenuation (0.2 dB/km) | Fragile — requires careful handling | | Immune to EMI/RFI | Requires skilled splicing/connectorization | | Secure (no electromagnetic tapping) | Specialized test equipment (OTDR) needed | | Lightweight, small diameter | Electrical-to-optical conversion losses | | No ground loop problems | Limited bend radius (causes macrobending loss) | | Long repeater spacing (> 100 km) | | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Overall Block Diagram of Fiber Optic Communication System This is the **primary diagram** expected in KTU examinations. It shows the complete signal path from source to destination. ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA[Electrical Input Signal
Voice/Data/Video] --> encA[Encoder
PCM or Analog] encA --> drvA[Driver Circuit
Modulator] drvA --> srcA[Optical Source
LED or Laser Diode] srcA -- Light Pulse | E to O Conversion | fiberA[Optical Fiber Channel
Core + Cladding + Buffer] fiberA -- Propagating Light | detA[Photodetector
PIN or APD] detA -- Current | O to E Conversion | ampA[Amplifier
Transimpedance Amplifier] ampA --> decA[Decoder
Signal Processing] decA --> outA[Electrical Output Signal
Recovered Voice/Data/Video] classDef txBlock fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef chBlock fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rxBlock fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sigBlock fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class inputA,encA,drvA,srcA txBlock class fiberA chBlock class detA,ampA,decA,outA rxBlock ``` --- ### Diagram 2: Detailed Transmitter & Receiver Sub-Blocks This expanded view shows the internal sub-components that examiners often ask students to draw separately. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TRANSMITTER_BLOCK inS[Input Signal
Electrical] --> modS[Modulator
AM or Digital] modS --> drvS[LED or LD Driver
Current Source] drvS --> srcS[Optical Source
LED / ILD] end srcS -- Modulated Light Beam | Coupled via Lens | channelS[Optical Fiber
50 km typical] channelS -- Attenuated Signal | detS[Photodetector
PIN / APD] subgraph RECEIVER_BLOCK detS --> preS[Pre-Amplifier
TIA] preS --> mainS[Main Amplifier
Wideband] mainS --> filtS[Filter
Equalizer] filtS --> decS[Decision Circuit
Decoder] decS --> outS[Output Signal
Electrical Recovered] end classDef txGroup fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px classDef chGroup fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px classDef rxGroup fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px class inS,modS,drvS,srcS txGroup class channelS chGroup class detS,preS,mainS,filtS,decS,outS rxGroup ``` --- ### Diagram 3: Signal Waveform Evolution Through the System This flowchart visualizes the **signal transformation** at each block, which is a frequent high-mark question. ```mermaid flowchart LR sigA["INPUT: Analog Electrical
Sinusoidal Waveform"] -->|"Amplitude = V_max
Frequency = f"| sigB["ENCODER: PCM Coded
Binary Bit Stream"] sigB -->|"Bits = 0 and 1"| sigC["DRIVER: Current Pulses
I_on and I_off"] sigC -->|"I > I_threshold
Light ON"| sigD["OPTICAL SOURCE: Light Pulses
Optical Power vs Time"] sigD -->|"Power decays
alpha = 0.2 dB/km"| sigE["FIBER OUTPUT: Attenuated Light
Smaller amplitude"] sigE -->|"Photons to Electrons"| sigF["PHOTODETECTOR: Current Pulses
I_ph proportional to P_opt"] sigF -->|"Amplified current"| sigG["AMPLIFIER: Voltage Pulses
Larger amplitude"] sigG -->|"Regenerated bits"| sigH["OUTPUT: Recovered Electrical
Sinusoidal Approximation"] classDef waveStyle fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px class sigA,sigB,sigC,sigD,sigE,sigF,sigG,sigH waveStyle ``` --- ### Diagram 4: Functional Architecture Map (Examiner-Reference) A clean, board-friendly version that students can reproduce quickly in exams. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Electrical
Input"] --> B["Encoder"] B --> C["Driver
Modulator"] C --> D["Optical
Source"] D -->|"LIGHT IN"| E["Optical
Fiber Cable"] E -->|"LIGHT OUT"| F["Photo
Detector"] F --> G["Amplifier"] G --> H["Decoder"] H --> I["Electrical
Output"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Draw the block diagram of a fiber optic communication system and explain the function of each block.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A fiber optic communication system consists of three main blocks: 1. **Transmitter Block (E → O Converter):** It contains an encoder, driver circuit, and optical source (LED or Laser Diode). It converts the input electrical signal into a modulated light signal. 2. **Optical Fiber Channel:** It is a passive transmission medium consisting of a core, cladding, and protective buffer. Light propagates through the core by total internal reflection. 3. **Receiver Block (O → E Converter):** It contains a photodetector (PIN/APD), amplifier, and decoder. It converts the received optical signal back into the original electrical signal. (1 mark each block for the diagram, 3 marks total for functional explanation as a whole.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any six advantages of optical fiber communication over conventional copper cable communication.** **Model Answer (3 marks — any six):** 1. **Higher bandwidth** — supports Tbps data rates 2. **Lower attenuation** — 0.2 dB/km vs several dB/km for copper 3. **Immune to EMI/RFI** — uses light, not electrons 4. **Secure communication** — no electromagnetic radiation to tap 5. **Smaller size and lighter weight** — easy to install 6. **No cross-talk** between adjacent fibers 7. **Long repeater spacing** — reduces system cost 8. **Safe in hazardous/explosive environments** — no sparks (½ mark each, total 3 marks.) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional operation of a fiber optic communication system. Discuss the role of LED and Laser Diode as optical sources. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for Numerical Aperture of a step-index optical fiber and explain its physical significance. A fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$. Calculate the NA and acceptance angle. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram and Optical Sources (7 marks):** Draw the standard 3-block diagram (Transmitter → Fiber → Receiver) as shown in Section 4, Diagram 4. **(3 marks)** **Role of LED:** - Operates by **spontaneous emission** — produces incoherent, wide-spectrum light - Used for **short-distance, low-bandwidth** applications (< 10 km) - Lower cost, simpler drive circuit, longer lifetime - Spectral width ≈ 30–100 nm → higher chromatic dispersion **(1.5 marks)** **Role of Laser Diode (ILD — Injection Laser Diode):** - Operates by **stimulated emission** — produces coherent, narrow-spectrum light - Used for **long-distance, high-bandwidth** links (> 100 km) - Higher output power, faster modulation, narrow spectral width (1–5 nm) - Higher cost and requires temperature stabilization **(1.5 marks)** **(b) Derivation of NA (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Apply Snell's law at the core-cladding boundary: $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ **Step 2:** For TIR, $\theta_2 = 90°$, so $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ **(1 mark)** **Step 3:** Apply Snell's law at the fiber entrance: $n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_r$, with $\theta_r = 90° - \theta_c$ **(1 mark)** **Step 4:** Substitute $\sin\theta_r = \cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - (n_2/n_1)^2}$ and simplify: $$ \boxed{NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ **Physical Significance:** NA represents the light-gathering capability of the fiber. Higher NA = more light can enter the fiber, but also more modal dispersion. **(1 mark)** **Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** $$ NA = \sqrt{1.48^2 - 1.46^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} \approx 0.2425 $$ $$ \theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) \approx 14.03° \approx 14°\ 2' $$ **Result:** NA ≈ **0.2425**, Acceptance Angle ≈ **14.03°** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Draw the block diagram of an optical fiber communication system and explain the function of (i) Optical Source, (ii) Photodetector, and (iii) Optical Fiber. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 30 km fiber optic link operates at 1300 nm with attenuation of 0.5 dB/km. The transmitter launches +3 dBm of power. Calculate: (i) total attenuation in dB, (ii) received power in dBm, and (iii) received power in μW. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram and Functions (7 marks):** Draw the standard 3-block diagram. **(2 marks)** **(i) Optical Source (LED/LD):** Converts electrical signal to light via electroluminescence. Modulation is done by varying drive current. **(1.5 marks)** **(ii) Photodetector (PIN/APD):** Converts received optical signal back to electrical current using the **photoelectric effect**. APD provides internal gain via avalanche multiplication. **(1.5 marks)** **(iii) Optical Fiber:** Passive dielectric waveguide that guides light via **Total Internal Reflection** between higher-index core and lower-index cladding. Provides very low loss, high bandwidth, and EMI immunity. **(2 marks)** **(b) Power Budget Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** $L = 30$ km, $\alpha = 0.5$ dB/km, $P_{in} = +3$ dBm **(i) Total attenuation:** **(2 marks)** $$ \alpha_{total} = \alpha \times L = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ dB} $$ **(ii) Received power in dBm:** **(2 marks)** $$ P_{out}(\text{dBm}) = P_{in}(\text{dBm}) - \alpha_{total} = 3 - 15 = -12 \text{ dBm} $$ **(iii) Received power in μW:** **(3 marks)** Convert −12 dBm to milliwatts: $$ P_{out}(\text{mW}) = 10^{P_{out}(\text{dBm})/10} = 10^{-12/10} = 10^{-1.2} $$ $$ 10^{-1.2} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{0.8} = 0.01 \times 6.3096 \approx 0.0631 \text{ mW} $$ $$ P_{out} = 0.0631 \text{ mW} = 63.1 \text{ μW} $$ **Final Result:** Total attenuation = **15 dB**, Received power = **−12 dBm** = **63.1 μW** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Block diagram arrows must be directional** — students often draw boxes without arrows, which loses 1 mark. > 2. **Signal conversion labels are mandatory** — write **E → O** at transmitter and **O → E** at receiver. Many students skip this. > 3. **NA formula derivation steps** — don't jump directly to $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$. Show Snell's law application at both boundaries; partial derivation = partial marks. > 4. **Power budget units** — mixing up dBm (decibels relative to 1 mW) and dB (pure ratio) is a common error. State units clearly. > 5. **Numerical precision** — KTU typically expects 3 significant figures. Avoid giving answers like "0.24" for NA without context. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ FOCS = **Transmitter + Optical Fiber + Receiver** - ✅ **Transmitter = E → O converter** (uses LED or Laser Diode) - ✅ **Receiver = O → E converter** (uses PIN or APD photodiode) - ✅ **Optical fiber** = passive waveguide using **Total Internal Reflection** - ✅ Operating wavelengths: **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm** - ✅ Critical angle: $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ - ✅ Numerical Aperture: $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ - ✅ Acceptance angle: $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ - ✅ Attenuation formula: $\alpha = (10/L) \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$ dB/km - ✅ Power after distance: $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ - ✅ **LED** → incoherent, short haul, low cost - ✅ **Laser Diode** → coherent, long haul, high bandwidth - ✅ **PIN** → simple photodiode, no internal gain - ✅ **APD** → photodiode with internal avalanche gain, high sensitivity - ✅ Key advantages: high bandwidth, low loss, EMI immunity, security - ✅ Key disadvantages: high cost, fragile, requires skilled installation

topics.title: Concept of AM and FM (No Derivation Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of AM and FM (No Derivation Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Modulation?

**Modulation** is the process of varying one or more properties of a high-frequency **carrier signal** (a periodic waveform, typically a sine wave) with respect to a low-frequency **message signal** (which contains the information to be transmitted, e.g., voice, music, or data).

The three fundamental parameters of a carrier signal that can be modulated are:

- **Amplitude** → leads to **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**
- **Frequency** → leads to **Frequency Modulation (FM)**
- **Phase** → leads to **Phase Modulation (PM)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the module "Communication Systems" (Module 4 of GXEST104), the focus is limited to the **conceptual understanding** of AM and FM. **Mathematical derivations are explicitly excluded** by the syllabus directive *"No Derivation Required."* However, key equations, formulae, and waveform sketches are still examinable.

## 1.2 Definition of Amplitude Modulation (AM)

**Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is a modulation technique in which the **amplitude of the high-frequency carrier signal is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message (modulating) signal**, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant.

In the time domain, an AM signal is given by the expression:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\left[1 + m(t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where:
- $A_c$ = amplitude of the carrier signal
- $f_c$ = frequency of the carrier signal
- $m(t)$ = normalized message signal

## 1.3 Definition of Frequency Modulation (FM)

**Frequency Modulation (FM)** is a modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency of the high-frequency carrier signal is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message (modulating) signal**, while the amplitude of the carrier remains constant.

In the time domain, an FM signal is expressed as:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

where $k_f$ is the **frequency sensitivity** of the modulator (in Hz/volt).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
>
> - **Think of AM like adjusting the brightness of a torch (flashlight)** using a dimmer knob. The brightness (amplitude) keeps changing in step with the information you want to send, but the rate of switching ON–OFF (frequency) remains fixed.
> - **Think of FM like a person whistling**. The loudness of their voice (amplitude) stays the same, but the **pitch of the whistle (frequency) rises and falls** according to the emotional content of the message. The whistle’s volume doesn't change—only its pitch.

## 1.4 Why is Modulation Needed? (Justification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following four engineering reasons **justify** why modulation is indispensable in any communication system:
>
> 1. **Reduction of antenna height:** A practical antenna must have a length of at least $\lambda/4$. For a 1 kHz audio signal, $\lambda = 300,000$ m, requiring an antenna of length 75 km. Modulation shifts the signal to a higher frequency band, drastically reducing the required antenna size.
> 2. **Avoiding signal mixing (multiplexing):** Without modulation, all baseband signals from different sources would occupy the same frequency range and interfere with each other. Modulation translates them to separate frequency bands.
> 3. **Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** FM is particularly resistant to amplitude noise (e.g., thunderstorms, electrical interference), giving better fidelity.
> 4. **Long-range communication:** Higher-frequency signals can be propagated over long distances via skywave propagation or via satellite links.

## 1.5 The Modulating Signal, Carrier, and Modulated Signal

| Signal | Description | Typical Frequency | Typical Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Modulating Signal** $m(t)$ | Contains the actual information (voice, music, data) | Low frequency (audio: 20 Hz – 20 kHz) | Also called **baseband signal** |
| **Carrier Signal** $c(t)$ | High-frequency sinusoidal waveform used to "carry" the information | High frequency (e.g., 540 kHz – 1600 kHz for AM broadcast, 88 MHz – 108 MHz for FM broadcast) | Used for transmission |
| **Modulated Signal** $s(t)$ | Output of the modulation process | Centered around $f_c$ | Transmitted through the channel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** The **frequency band** allocated for AM radio broadcast in India is **540 kHz to 1600 kHz (medium wave)** and for FM broadcast is **88 MHz to 108 MHz (VHF Band II)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Working of Amplitude Modulation

In AM, the carrier amplitude is made to vary in accordance with the modulating signal. If we let the carrier be $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and the modulating signal be $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the AM signal is:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\left[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

The term $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ is called the **modulation index** (or depth of modulation). It must satisfy $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$ to avoid **over-modulation** (which causes distortion).

### Frequency Spectrum of AM

When the AM signal is expanded, three frequency components appear:
- **Carrier component** at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$
- **Upper Sideband (USB)** at $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $\dfrac{\mu A_c}{2}$
- **Lower Sideband (LSB)** at $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $\dfrac{\mu A_c}{2}$

Hence the **total bandwidth of an AM signal** is:

$$BW_{AM} = 2 f_m$$

where $f_m$ is the maximum frequency present in the message signal.

### Types of AM

| Type | Full Form | Carrier Transmission? | Bandwidth | Power Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DSB-FC** | Double Sideband – Full Carrier (standard AM) | Yes | $2 f_m$ | Low (carrier wastes power) |
| **DSB-SC** | Double Sideband – Suppressed Carrier | No | $2 f_m$ | Higher than DSB-FC |
| **SSB-SC** | Single Sideband – Suppressed Carrier | No | $f_m$ | Highest |
| **VSB** | Vestigial Sideband | Partial | $\approx 1.25 f_m$ | Used in TV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard AM broadcast (the "AM radio" in cars) uses DSB-FC (Double Sideband – Full Carrier)**, which is the simplest and cheapest, but is power-inefficient because more than two-thirds of the transmitted power is consumed by the carrier alone, which carries no information.

## 2.2 Detailed Working of Frequency Modulation

In FM, the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier varies linearly with the modulating signal:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \cdot m(t)$$

The frequency deviation $\Delta f$ is the maximum departure of the instantaneous frequency from the carrier frequency:

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = \max \vert f_i(t) - f_c \vert$$

The **modulation index for FM** is:

$$\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$$

### Classification of FM

- **Narrowband FM (NBFM):** When $\beta < 1$ (or sometimes $\beta \ll 1$). Bandwidth $\approx 2 f_m$ (similar to AM).
- **Wideband FM (WBFM):** When $\beta \gg 1$ (typical commercial FM uses $\beta \approx 5$). Bandwidth is given by **Carson's Rule**:

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carrier signal | $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | Standard sinusoidal |
| 2 | AM signal (general) | $s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | $m(t)$ normalized |
| 3 | AM modulation index | $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$ |
| 4 | AM bandwidth | $BW_{AM} = 2 f_{m,\max}$ | Hz | DSB-FC and DSB-SC |
| 5 | Total AM power | $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | Watts | DSB-FC |
| 6 | Carrier power | $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ | Watts | $R$ is the antenna resistance |
| 7 | FM modulation index | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | Dimensionless | Can exceed 1 |
| 8 | Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m$ | Hz | Max departure from $f_c$ |
| 9 | FM bandwidth (Carson) | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$ | Hz | Universal for FM |
| 10 | FM deviation ratio | $DR = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_{m,\max}}$ | Dimensionless | Same as $\beta$ at max $f_m$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Note:** In the above table, all absolute-value operations use the standard math typesetting symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ rendered cleanly by the markdown engine. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) when typing mathematical vertical bars inside a markdown table cell to avoid breaking the table layout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AM is used in:** AM radio broadcasting (MW/SW bands), Citizen Band (CB) radio, aircraft VHF communications, and old analog TV video transmission.
- **FM is used in:** FM radio broadcasting (88–108 MHz), two-way radio communications, analog TV audio, public safety radios, and the FM subcarrier in analog TV (for stereo audio).
- **Why FM dominates high-fidelity audio:** FM is highly **resistant to amplitude noise** because the information is encoded in frequency variations. Any amplitude disturbance (lightning, electrical noise) can be removed by a **limiter circuit** at the receiver, leaving the message intact.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Mathematical Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Directive:** The syllabus explicitly states **"No Derivation Required."** Therefore, instead of long derivations, this section presents the **key mathematical expressions, algebraic expansions, and power-analysis formulas** step-by-step, with each line explained. The intent is to build the **analytical matrix** required to solve numerical problems and interpret waveforms.

## 3.1 AM Signal — Mathematical Expansion (Step-by-Step)

Let the carrier be:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Let the single-tone modulating signal be:

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

The modulation index is defined as:

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

Substituting the modulating signal into the general AM expression, we get:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\left[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 1:** Distribute the carrier over the bracket:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \mu A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric identity for product of two cosines:

$$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A - B) + \cos(A + B)\right]$$

Applying this to the second term:

$$\mu A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\left[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\right] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\left[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right]$$

**Step 3:** Combine everything to obtain the expanded AM signal:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\left[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\right] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\left[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right]$$

**Step-by-Step Explanation of the Three Resulting Terms:**

- The **first term** is the **carrier** at frequency $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$ (carries no information).
- The **second term** is the **Lower Sideband (LSB)** at frequency $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $\dfrac{\mu A_c}{2}$ (carries a copy of the message).
- The **third term** is the **Upper Sideband (USB)** at frequency $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $\dfrac{\mu A_c}{2}$ (carries another copy of the message).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **total bandwidth** is the difference between the highest and lowest frequency components:
>
> $$BW = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m$$

## 3.2 AM Power Analysis (Step-by-Step)

The total average power of the AM signal (in DSB-FC) delivered to a load resistance $R$ is:

$$P_t = P_{USB} + P_{LSB} + P_c$$

**Step 1:** Carrier power (using RMS value of a sinusoid):

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}$$

**Step 2:** Power in each sideband (each sideband has amplitude $\dfrac{\mu A_c}{2}$):

$$P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2R} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8R} = \frac{\mu^2}{4} P_c$$

**Step 3:** Sum the three components:

$$P_t = P_c + 2 \times \frac{\mu^2}{4} P_c = P_c + \frac{\mu^2}{2} P_c = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

**Key Observation:**

$$P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power-Efficiency Insight:** When $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation, the maximum useful case), only **one-third of the total transmitted power** ($P_t / 3 = P_c/3$) is in the sidebands (the information). The remaining **two-thirds is wasted in the carrier**. This is why DSB-FC is **power-inefficient**, and the carrier is suppressed in DSB-SC and SSB systems.

## 3.3 FM Signal — Mathematical Analysis (Step-by-Step)

Let the single-tone modulating signal be $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous frequency of the FM signal is:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) = f_c + \Delta f \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

where the **frequency deviation** is $\Delta f = k_f A_m$.

**Step 2:** The instantaneous phase $\theta_i(t)$ is the time-integral of $2\pi f_i(t)$:

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi \int_0^t f_i(\tau)\, d\tau = 2\pi f_c t + \frac{\Delta f}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the phase into the carrier expression gives the FM signal:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

where the **FM modulation index** is:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

## 3.4 FM Bandwidth via Carson's Rule (Step-by-Step)

Carson’s empirical rule provides a practical estimate of the FM transmission bandwidth:

**Step 1:** Define the frequency deviation $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ and the modulating frequency $f_m$.

**Step 2:** Apply Carson’s rule:

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$$

**Step 3:** The 98% of total signal power is contained within this bandwidth, which is the standard for engineering design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU-style numerical):** If a commercial FM broadcast station has $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $f_{m,\max} = 15$ kHz, then:
>
> - Modulation index $\beta = 75/15 = 5$
> - Bandwidth $BW = 2 \times 15 \times (5+1) = 180$ kHz
>
> Compare this with the corresponding AM bandwidth of only $2 \times 15 = 30$ kHz. FM uses **6× more bandwidth** but delivers a vastly superior signal-to-noise ratio.

## 3.5 Comparative Numerical Matrix (AM vs FM)

| Parameter | AM (DSB-FC) | FM (WBFM, $\beta = 5$) |
|---|---|---|
| Modulated parameter | Amplitude | Frequency |
| Carrier amplitude | Varies with $m(t)$ | Constant ($A_c$) |
| Bandwidth (single tone) | $2 f_m$ | $2 f_m (\beta + 1) = 12 f_m$ |
| SNR performance | Lower | Higher (better) |
| Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent |
| Circuit complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Power efficiency | Low (carrier wastes power) | High (constant envelope) |
| Typical applications | AM radio, CB radio | FM radio, two-way comms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Rendering Note:** All diagrams in this section are **Mermaid block diagrams** (or labeled block-architecture flow diagrams) which render directly in markdown previewers. **Physical hand-drawn waveform sketches are not possible in this format**, so the Mermaid blocks below provide a **functional block-level mapping** of how AM and FM signals are generated, transmitted, and received.

## 4.1 AM Transmitter Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input1[Message Source]
        A1[Microphone or Audio Source]
    end

    subgraph input2[Modulator Section]
        B1[Audio Amplifier]
        B2[Modulator Multiplier]
    end

    subgraph input3[Carrier Generation]
        C1[Crystal Oscillator fc]
    end

    subgraph input4[Transmission Chain]
        D1[Power Amplifier]
        D2[Band Pass Filter]
        D3[Transmitting Antenna]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2
    C1 --> B2
    B2 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3

    classDef source fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef carrier fill:#caffbf,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef tx fill:#ffc6c6,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000

    class A1 source
    class B1,B2 process
    class C1 carrier
    class D1,D2,D3 tx
```

**Functional Description of the AM Transmitter Blocks:**

1. **Microphone / Audio Source:** Converts the human voice (or music) into a low-frequency electrical signal (typically 20 Hz – 20 kHz).
2. **Audio Amplifier:** Boosts the weak microphone signal to a level suitable for modulation.
3. **Crystal Oscillator:** Generates a stable, high-frequency carrier sinusoid at the allocated carrier frequency $f_c$.
4. **Modulator (Multiplier):** Multiplies the amplified audio signal with the carrier, producing the AM waveform.
5. **Power Amplifier:** Increases the power level of the AM signal for long-range transmission.
6. **Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Restricts the transmitted spectrum to the allocated channel bandwidth.
7. **Antenna:** Radiates the AM signal into free space as an electromagnetic wave.

## 4.2 FM Transmitter Functional Block Diagram (Armstrong Indirect Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph node1[Input Stage]
        A1[Microphone or Audio Input]
        A2[Audio Pre Amplifier]
    end

    subgraph node2[NBFM Generation]
        B1[Crystal Oscillator fc]
        B2[Phase Modulator PM]
    end

    subgraph node3[Frequency Multiplier Chain]
        C1[Frequency Multiplier 1]
        C2[Frequency Multiplier 2]
    end

    subgraph node4[Mixer and Up Conversion]
        D1[Local Oscillator fLO]
        D2[Mixer]
        D3[Band Pass Filter]
    end

    subgraph node5[Power Output]
        E1[Power Amplifier]
        E2[FM Transmitting Antenna]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1 --> C2 --> D2
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3 --> E1 --> E2

    classDef inp fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef nbfm fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef mult fill:#e5c1ff,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#000
    classDef mix fill:#caffbf,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef pwr fill:#ffc6c6,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000

    class A1,A2 inp
    class B1,B2 nbfm
    class C1,C2 mult
    class D1,D2,D3 mix
    class E1,E2 pwr
```

**Block-Level Explanation of the FM Transmitter:**

1. **Input & Audio Preamplifier:** The message signal is amplified to a usable voltage.
2. **Crystal Oscillator (at $f_c$):** Generates the highly stable carrier sinusoid.
3. **Phase Modulator (PM):** Produces a Narrowband FM (NBFM) signal. The audio is first integrated and then used to phase-modulate the carrier.
4. **Frequency Multipliers:** Multi-stage frequency multipliers raise both the carrier frequency and the frequency deviation to their final (much higher) broadcast values, simultaneously converting NBFM to WBFM.
5. **Mixer + Local Oscillator:** Translates the FM signal to the final allocated broadcast frequency (e.g., 100 MHz).
6. **Band-Pass Filter:** Selects the desired mixer output and rejects spurious sidebands.
7. **Power Amplifier & Antenna:** Boosts signal power and radiates the FM wave.

## 4.3 AM and FM — Conceptual Waveform Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph wave1[Stage A - Baseband Message]
        M1[Modulating Signal m of t - Slow Sine Wave]
    end

    subgraph wave2[Stage B - Carrier]
        M2[Carrier c of t - High Frequency Constant Amplitude Sine]
    end

    subgraph wave3[Stage C - AM Output]
        M3[AM Signal - Envelope Follows m of t, Frequency stays at fc]
    end

    subgraph wave4[Stage D - FM Output]
        M4[FM Signal - Constant Amplitude A sub c, Frequency changes with m of t]
    end

    M1 --> M3
    M2 --> M3

    M1 --> M4
    M2 --> M4

    classDef msg fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef car fill:#caffbf,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef amout fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef fmout fill:#e5c1ff,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#000

    class M1 msg
    class M2 car
    class M3 amout
    class M4 fmout
```

**Reading the Waveform Flowchart:**

- The **modulating signal** is a slow-varying sine wave (e.g., voice).
- The **carrier** is a high-frequency constant-amplitude sine wave.
- In the **AM output**, the carrier's frequency is unchanged, but its amplitude rises and falls in step with the modulating signal—the **envelope** of the AM waveform is a faithful replica of $m(t)$.
- In the **FM output**, the carrier's amplitude is unchanged, but the spacing between zero-crossings (instantaneous frequency) becomes tighter when $m(t)$ is high and looser when $m(t)$ is low.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question is designed for 3 marks and expects a **concise, definition-based or comparison-based answer** of 4–6 lines. These are typical KTU 2-mark-to-3-mark rapid-fire questions that appear in every End-Semester Examination (ESE).

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Modeled)]**
**Define Amplitude Modulation. What is the role of the carrier in an AM system?**

**Model Answer:**

Amplitude Modulation (AM) is the process of varying the **amplitude of a high-frequency carrier signal** in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency message (modulating) signal, while keeping the carrier frequency and phase constant.

The carrier performs three critical roles: (1) it **translates the low-frequency message to a high-frequency band**, which enables the use of small, practical antennas; (2) it allows **multiplexing** of multiple signals by allocating each a different carrier frequency; and (3) it provides the **medium of propagation** through free space, since electromagnetic waves at audio frequencies cannot be radiated efficiently.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three roles. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Modeled)]**
**What is modulation index in AM? What happens when modulation index exceeds unity?**

**Model Answer:**

The **modulation index** in AM is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the modulating signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier signal:

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

It is a **dimensionless quantity** that indicates the depth of modulation. For distortion-free transmission, the condition $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$ must be satisfied. When $\mu > 1$ (**over-modulation**), the envelope of the AM waveform becomes distorted (the negative half of the envelope flips over), causing **severe signal distortion** and the generation of **out-of-band harmonics** that interfere with adjacent channels. This is unacceptable in broadcasting and is avoided by careful transmitter design.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for condition, 1 mark for explanation of over-modulation. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 14-mark Module questions follow the **internal choice pattern**. Below are **two completely independent question choices** (Question A and Question B). Each is structured with two 7-mark sub-parts: part (a) typically a descriptive/conceptual 7-mark question, and part (b) a numerical/problem-solving 7-mark question. **Students answer EITHER Q-A or Q-B, not both.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, May 2024 — Adapted]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Conceptual**
**Explain the concept of Frequency Modulation (FM) with a neat sketch of the modulating signal, carrier, and FM signal waveforms. Compare FM with AM in terms of bandwidth, noise immunity, and power efficiency.**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Definition of FM** (1 mark): FM is the process of varying the instantaneous frequency of the carrier in proportion to the amplitude of the modulating signal, while keeping the carrier amplitude constant.
2. **Waveform Sketch** (2 marks): Show three waves stacked vertically — (i) the low-frequency message $m(t)$ sine, (ii) the high-frequency carrier $c(t)$, and (iii) the FM signal where the zero-crossings of the carrier are compressed (higher frequency) when $m(t) > 0$ and stretched (lower frequency) when $m(t) < 0$. The envelope of FM is a constant rectangle.
3. **Comparison Table** (4 marks):

| Parameter | AM | FM |
|---|---|---|
| Modulated parameter | Amplitude | Frequency |
| Bandwidth | $2 f_m$ | $2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$ |
| Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent (limiter removes AM noise) |
| Power efficiency | Low (carrier consumes 2/3 power) | High (constant envelope) |
| Circuit complexity | Simple | More complex |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical**
**An AM transmitter radiates a carrier power of 10 kW and operates at a modulation index of $\mu = 0.7$. Calculate: (i) total radiated power, (ii) power in each sideband, and (iii) the percentage of total power carried by the sidebands (modulation efficiency).**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**

- $P_c = 10$ kW = 10,000 W
- $\mu = 0.7$

**(i) Total radiated power:**

$$P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = 10{,}000 \left(1 + \frac{0.7^2}{2}\right)$$

$$P_t = 10{,}000 \left(1 + \frac{0.49}{2}\right) = 10{,}000 \times 1.245 = 12{,}450 \text{ W} = 12.45 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Power in each sideband:**

$$P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{\mu^2}{4} P_c = \frac{0.49}{4} \times 10{,}000 = 0.1225 \times 10{,}000 = 1{,}225 \text{ W} = 1.225 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Modulation efficiency (percentage of total power in sidebands):**

$$\eta_{AM} = \frac{P_{USB} + P_{LSB}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2/2}{1 + \mu^2/2} = \frac{0.245}{1.245} = 0.1968$$

$$\eta_{AM} \approx 19.68\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Even at $\mu = 0.7$, only about **19.7% of the total power** is in the information-carrying sidebands; the rest is wasted in the carrier. At $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation), efficiency is $1/3 \approx 33.3\%$. To eliminate this waste, DSB-SC and SSB systems suppress the carrier.

**Mark Split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks = 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, May 2024 — Adapted]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Conceptual**
**With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an FM transmitter using the Armstrong indirect method. What is the role of the frequency multiplier stages?**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Block diagram** (2 marks): The Armstrong indirect FM transmitter consists of: Audio input → Audio pre-amplifier → Integrator → Phase Modulator (using a stable crystal oscillator at $f_c$) → Multi-stage frequency multipliers → Mixer with local oscillator → Band-pass filter → Power amplifier → Antenna.
2. **Working** (3 marks): The modulating signal is first integrated and then used to phase-modulate a stable carrier. Because the phase deviation of a phase modulator is kept small, the output is a Narrowband FM (NBFM). The frequency multiplier chain raises both the carrier frequency and the frequency deviation. The mixer translates the final frequency to the broadcast band.
3. **Role of frequency multipliers** (2 marks): They simultaneously increase the carrier frequency and the frequency deviation $\Delta f$ by an integer factor $N$ (e.g., $N = 24$ for commercial FM). The ratio $\Delta f / f_m$ (i.e., $\beta$) is preserved across each multiplier stage. Without them, a phase modulator cannot directly generate the wide frequency deviation required for WBFM broadcast.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical**
**A commercial FM station broadcasts at a carrier frequency of 100 MHz with a maximum frequency deviation of 75 kHz. The maximum modulating frequency is 15 kHz. Calculate: (i) the FM modulation index $\beta$, (ii) the bandwidth using Carson's rule, and (iii) the percentage of bandwidth saved if the station used AM instead.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**

- $f_c = 100$ MHz (not used in bandwidth calculation)
- $\Delta f = 75$ kHz
- $f_m = 15$ kHz

**(i) FM modulation index $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{75}{15} = 5$$

**(ii) Bandwidth using Carson's rule:**

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(75 + 15) = 2 \times 90 = 180 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Bandwidth used by AM (DSB-FC) for the same audio:**

$$BW_{AM} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 15 = 30 \text{ kHz}$$

**Bandwidth saved if AM were used:**

$$\text{Saved BW} = 180 - 30 = 150 \text{ kHz}$$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{150}{180} \times 100 = 83.33\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The FM system occupies **6× the bandwidth** of an equivalent AM system. This is the **engineering trade-off**: FM gives superior noise immunity and audio fidelity, but at the cost of a much larger bandwidth allocation. The international standard 200 kHz channel spacing for FM broadcast (88–108 MHz) accommodates this 180 kHz requirement with a small guard band.

**Mark Split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks = 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks in AM/FM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "modulation index" for AM and FM.** AM modulation index $\mu$ is bounded by 1; FM modulation index $\beta$ can exceed 1. Writing $\mu = 5$ in an FM problem is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting Carson's rule's 2× factor.** The bandwidth is $2(\Delta f + f_m)$, NOT $(\Delta f + f_m)$. Students often drop the factor of 2 in exam pressure.
> 3. **Mixing up $f_m$ and $\Delta f$.** $\Delta f$ is the *frequency deviation* (in Hz), $f_m$ is the *modulating signal frequency* (in Hz). Using one in place of the other is a frequent error.
> 4. **Not drawing envelope carefully in AM.** The envelope of an AM waveform should *exactly* trace the message signal $m(t)$ (in its positive excursions). Any inaccuracy in the sketch loses the diagram mark.
> 5. **Forgetting units.** Always state the final answer in kHz or MHz (whichever is consistent with the question).
> 6. **In power calculations, mixing $P_c$ (carrier) with $P_t$ (total).** Many students substitute $P_t$ where $P_c$ is needed, or vice versa, leading to wrong totals.
> 7. **Not stating the $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$ condition.** The syllabus specifically expects this condition. A bare formula without the condition loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Modulation** = process of varying one parameter (amplitude/frequency/phase) of a high-frequency **carrier** by a low-frequency **message**.
- **AM** → amplitude of carrier is varied; **frequency stays constant**.
- **FM** → frequency of carrier is varied; **amplitude stays constant** (envelope is a clean rectangle).
- The **modulation index of AM** is $\mu = A_m / A_c$, constrained to $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$.
- The **modulation index of FM** is $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$, with no upper bound.
- AM bandwidth (DSB-FC and DSB-SC) = $\mathbf{2 f_{m,\max}}$.
- FM bandwidth (Carson's rule) = $\mathbf{2(\Delta f + f_{m,\max}) = 2 f_{m,\max}(\beta + 1)}$.
- AM total power = $P_c (1 + \mu^2 / 2)$. At $\mu = 1$, carrier wastes $2/3$ of the power.
- Standard AM broadcast: **540 kHz – 1600 kHz** (India, MW).
- Standard FM broadcast: **88 MHz – 108 MHz** (VHF Band II).
- AM is **simple** but **noise-prone**; FM is **complex** but **noise-resistant**.
- **Phase Modulation (PM)** is closely related to FM (PM of $m(t)$ is FM of $m'(t)$).
- AM is generated using a **multiplier/modulator**; FM is commonly generated using the **Armstrong indirect method** (NBFM + frequency multipliers + mixer).
- **Limiter** in an FM receiver removes amplitude noise → better SNR.
- AM has three spectral components: carrier, USB, LSB. **SSB** removes both carrier and one sideband for better efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of AM and FM Super-Heterodyne Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Block Diagram of AM and FM Super-Heterodyne Receiver # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Super-Heterodyne Receiver** is a radio receiver design that employs the principle of **frequency mixing (heterodyning)** to convert the incoming Radio Frequency (RF) signal — whose carrier frequency may vary across a wide broadcast band — into a **fixed, lower Intermediate Frequency (IF)** signal, where the bulk of the signal amplification and selectivity operations are performed. This architecture, patented by **Edwin Howard Armstrong in 1918**, is the universally adopted standard for AM, FM, TV, and practically all modern communication receivers. The term *heterodyne* is derived from the Greek *heteros* (different) + *dynamis* (force/power), meaning "different force," referring to the mixing of two signals of different frequencies to produce new frequency components at their sum and difference. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A student must be able to draw the **complete block diagram**, identify **each functional block**, and state the **standard IF values** (455 kHz for AM broadcast, **10.7 MHz** for FM broadcast) along with the **image frequency relations**. ## 1.2 The Core Insight — Why Super-Heterodyne? Before Armstrong's invention, receivers used **Tuned Radio Frequency (TRF)** architecture where each stage had to be tuned to the incoming RF frequency directly. This required: - Multiple, simultaneously tuned, ganged LC stages - Difficult-to-achieve uniform gain and selectivity across the band - Severe instability (oscillation) due to high gain at RF > [!NOTE] > **The Super-Heterodyne Solution:** By converting ALL incoming RF signals (regardless of carrier frequency) to a **single fixed IF**, the receiver can be designed so that the IF amplifier stages provide: > 1. **Maximum gain** at one fixed frequency (easy to optimize) > 2. **Maximum selectivity** using high-Q fixed-frequency bandpass filters > 3. **Stability** because IF is lower than RF (less stray feedback) ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "The Universal Translator" Imagine you are an international diplomat who must understand speeches given in 50 different languages: - **TRF approach (old method):** You must personally become fluent in all 50 languages, with varying quality in each. Difficult and inconsistent. - **Super-Heterodyne approach (clever method):** Hire 50 simultaneous translators who **all** translate their respective speech into **one common language (English)**. You only need to master one language (the IF stage) to understand all inputs. Translation = the **Mixer + Local Oscillator** process. Similarly, the **Local Oscillator + Mixer** translates every RF signal to a common IF, and the IF amplifier chain handles it uniformly with maximum efficiency. ## 1.4 Key Numerical Standards (Bold for Emphasis) - **AM Broadcast IF = 455 kHz** - **FM Broadcast IF = 10.7 MHz** - **AM Broadcast Band = 540 kHz to 1640 kHz** - **FM Broadcast Band = 88 MHz to 108 MHz** - **Local Oscillator (LO) frequency:** Always tuned to be **455 kHz above** (or below) the incoming RF for AM - **Image Frequency:** Located at **2 × IF away** from the desired signal frequency --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Frequency Relation The **Mixer** is a non-linear device (or a linear multiplier fed with one signal and the LO output) that performs the multiplication: $$v_{mixer}(t) = A_{RF} \cos(2\pi f_{RF} t) \times A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$ Applying the trigonometric product-to-sum identity yields two new frequency components: $$v_{mixer}(t) = \frac{A_{RF} A_{LO}}{2} \Big[ \cos\big(2\pi(f_{LO} - f_{RF})t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(f_{LO} + f_{RF})t\big) \Big]$$ A bandpass filter following the mixer selects only the **difference frequency** component, which becomes the Intermediate Frequency. > [!NOTE] > **Why the difference frequency?** The sum frequency is much higher and easily filtered out. The difference is lower, easier to amplify with high gain, and represents the original modulating information now carried on a new fixed carrier (the IF). ## 2.2 Local Oscillator Tuning (The "Tracking" Concept) For the receiver to convert **any** incoming RF signal to the **same** IF, the Local Oscillator must be mechanically/electronically **ganged** with the RF amplifier tuning. Two possible tracking relationships exist: | Parameter | High-Side Injection (LO > RF) | Low-Side Injection (LO < RF) | |---|---|---| | LO frequency formula | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} - f_{IF}$ | | Image frequency formula | $f_{img} = f_{RF} - 2 f_{IF}$ | $f_{img} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ | | Practical usage | **Standard for AM broadcast** | Used in some specialized designs | | Reason for preference | Easier to design stable LO at higher freq | Avoids LO radiation problems | The receiver is designed such that the RF amplifier's preselector and the LO are **ganged** (mechanically coupled) so that as the user tunes $f_{RF}$, the LO automatically tracks it to maintain $f_{LO} - f_{RF} = f_{IF}$ at all times. ## 2.3 Image Frequency — The Critical Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** The **Image Frequency** is the unwanted RF signal frequency that, after mixing with the LO, **also** produces a difference frequency equal to the IF. If not rejected, it appears as a co-channel interference at the speaker output. **Why it happens:** Squaring (multiplying) the signal with the LO produces both sum AND difference. The mixer is non-directional — it cannot tell if the incoming RF is **above** or **below** the LO by exactly $f_{IF}$. So **two** RF frequencies produce the same IF: 1. **Desired signal:** $f_{RF} = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ 2. **Image signal:** $f_{img} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$ (i.e., $2 f_{IF}$ above the desired) > [!WARNING] > The image frequency is **always 2·IF away** from the desired signal. This is why the RF amplifier (preselector) must have enough selectivity to attenuate the image BEFORE it reaches the mixer. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Intermediate Frequency (AM) | $f_{IF,AM} = 455$ | 455 | kHz | | 2 | Intermediate Frequency (FM) | $f_{IF,FM} = 10.7$ | 10.7 | MHz | | 3 | LO frequency (high-side) | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ | varies | Hz | | 4 | LO frequency (low-side) | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} - f_{IF}$ | varies | Hz | | 5 | Image frequency (high-side LO) | $f_{img} = f_{RF} - 2 f_{IF}$ | varies | Hz | | 6 | Image frequency (low-side LO) | $f_{img} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ | varies | Hz | | 7 | Image Rejection Ratio | $IRR = \dfrac{\alpha_{img}}{\alpha_{sig}}$ | 60–80 | dB | | 8 | Mixer output difference | $f_{diff} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ | = $f_{IF}$ | Hz | | 9 | Mixer output sum | $f_{sum} = f_{LO} + f_{RF}$ | filtered out | Hz | | 10 | Tracking accuracy | $\Delta f = 0$ (ideal) | $\pm 2$ | kHz | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note on Pipes:** In exam scripts and tables, $\vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ is the absolute difference, **not** the bracket symbol used for table separators. Always use the proper mathematical absolute value notation. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The super-heterodyne architecture is the backbone of: - **AM/FM broadcast radios** (home and automotive) - **Television receivers** (with 38.9 MHz video IF, 41.25 MHz sound IF) - **Radar systems** (with IF typically 30–60 MHz) - **Software Defined Radios (SDR)** — digitize the IF stage - **Cellular base stations** - **Spectrum analyzers** and laboratory instrumentation - **Satellite communication transponders** (downlink IF stages) The shift to a fixed IF solved the historical impossibility of building uniformly high-Q variable filters across megahertz of tuning range, and is therefore one of the most consequential inventions in the history of electronics. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Image Frequency Formula **Given conditions:** - Receiver is designed for **high-side LO injection** (standard AM/FM) - Desired signal at $f_{RF}$ - IF = $f_{IF}$ **Step 1:** The LO is set so that the desired signal produces the IF. $$f_{LO} - f_{RF} = f_{IF}$$ Solving for the LO frequency: $$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$$ **Step 2:** The mixer is bilateral in frequency. Any input at $f_{LO} + f_{IF}$ will also produce the same IF at its output: $$f_{LO} - f_{img} = -f_{IF} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_{img} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the value of $f_{LO}$ from Step 1 into Step 2: $$f_{img} = (f_{RF} + f_{IF}) + f_{IF}$$ **Step 4:** Simplify: $$\boxed{f_{img} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This shows clearly that the image frequency lies **2·IF above** the desired RF for high-side injection. Analogously, for low-side injection one obtains $f_{img} = f_{RF} - 2 f_{IF}$. ## 3.2 Numerical Example — AM Receiver **Problem:** An AM super-heterodyne receiver is tuned to receive a station at **f_RF = 1000 kHz**. The IF is standard **455 kHz** with high-side LO injection. Find the LO frequency, image frequency, and verify the mixer outputs. **Step 1: Compute LO frequency.** $$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 1000 + 455 = 1455 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 2: Compute image frequency.** $$f_{img} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} = 1000 + 2(455) = 1000 + 910 = 1910 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 3: Verify mixer output for the desired signal.** $$f_{diff} = f_{LO} - f_{RF} = 1455 - 1000 = 455 \text{ kHz} = f_{IF} \;\;\checkmark$$ **Step 4: Verify mixer output for the image signal (unwanted).** $$f_{diff} = f_{LO} - f_{img} = 1455 - 1910 = -455 \text{ kHz}$$ The **magnitude** $\vert -455 \text{ kHz} \vert = 455$ kHz = $f_{IF}$. So the image signal masquerades as the desired signal. The RF preselector's job is to **reject** $f_{img} = 1910$ kHz before it reaches the mixer. **Step 5: Mixer output spectrum for the desired signal.** The mixer output contains: - $f_{LO} - f_{RF} = 455$ kHz (wanted IF — passed by IF filter) - $f_{LO} + f_{RF} = 1455 + 1000 = 2455$ kHz (sum — rejected by IF filter) ## 3.3 Numerical Example — FM Receiver **Problem:** An FM super-heterodyne receiver is tuned to **f_RF = 98.5 MHz** (a popular FM station in Kerala, e.g., AIR Kozhikode). The IF is standard **10.7 MHz** with high-side LO injection. Find the LO frequency and image frequency. **Step 1: LO frequency.** $$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 98.5 + 10.7 = 109.2 \text{ MHz}$$ **Step 2: Image frequency.** $$f_{img} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} = 98.5 + 2(10.7) = 98.5 + 21.4 = 119.9 \text{ MHz}$$ **Step 3: Practical implication.** The image is 21.4 MHz away from the desired signal. The RF amplifier must be a bandpass filter centered on 98.5 MHz with a Q-factor high enough to attenuate the 119.9 MHz image. Modern FM receivers use **double-tuned or ceramic filters** for this purpose. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AM vs FM Super-Heterodyne | Block / Function | AM Super-Heterodyne | FM Super-Heterodyne | |---|---|---| | RF Amplifier | Tuned RF stage, bandpass | Tuned RF stage, bandpass (88–108 MHz) | | Local Oscillator | Tunable, ganged with RF | Tunable, ganged with RF | | Mixer | Non-linear / multiplier | Non-linear / multiplier | | **IF value** | **455 kHz** | **10.7 MHz** | | IF Amplifier | High-gain fixed-frequency amp | High-gain fixed-frequency amp | | **Limiter** | **Not required** | **Required** (removes AM noise) | | **Detector / Demodulator** | **Envelope detector** (diode + RC) | **FM discriminator** (Foster-Seeley / Ratio / PLL) | | AFC (Auto Frequency Control) | Usually absent | Often present (drives LO via varactor) | | De-emphasis network | Absent | **Present** (75 µs in most countries) | ## 3.5 The Limiter (FM-Specific Stage) — Functional Derivation **Input:** FM IF signal with amplitude variations due to noise and fading. **Output:** FM IF signal with **constant amplitude** (hard-limited), preserving frequency variations which contain the audio information. **Mathematical model:** If $v_{in}(t) = A(t) \cos(2\pi f_{IF} t + \phi(t))$, the limiter produces: $$v_{out}(t) = V_{sat} \cdot \text{sgn}\big(v_{in}(t)\big)$$ The amplitude information $A(t)$ is destroyed, but the instantaneous frequency (which carries the audio modulation) is preserved. This is the **key advantage** of FM over AM: AM receivers cannot distinguish amplitude noise from the signal because the audio IS in the amplitude. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AM Super-Heterodyne Receiver — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[ANTENNA] --> A2[RF AMPLIFIER
Tuned Preselector] A2 --> A3[MIXER
Non-linear Device] A4[LOCAL OSCILLATOR
Ganged with RF tuning] --> A3 A3 --> A5[IF AMPLIFIER
Fixed at 455 kHz
Multi-stage High Gain] A5 --> A6[AM DETECTOR
Envelope Detector
Diode plus RC] A6 --> A7[AUDIO AMPLIFIER
Voltage and Power stages] A7 --> A8[LOUDSPEAKER
Transducer] A5 -.AGC feedback.-> A2 A6 -.AGC feedback.-> A5 style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A4 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A5 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A6 fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A8 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Color code key:** - 🟡 Gold = Input/Output transducers (Antenna, Speaker) - 🔴 Red = Frequency translation block (Mixer + LO) - 🟢 Teal = Fixed-frequency IF chain and detection ## 4.2 FM Super-Heterodyne Receiver — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[ANTENNA] --> F2[RF AMPLIFIER
Tuned to 88 to 108 MHz] F2 --> F3[MIXER] F4[LOCAL OSCILLATOR
Ganged plus AFC] --> F3 F3 --> F5[IF AMPLIFIER
Fixed at 10.7 MHz
Multi-stage High Gain] F5 --> F6[LIMITER
Removes AM noise
Hard clipper] F6 --> F7[FM DEMODULATOR
Foster Seeley
or Ratio Detector
or PLL Discriminator] F7 --> F8[DE EMPHASIS
75 microsecond RC] F8 --> F9[AUDIO AMPLIFIER] F9 --> F10[LOUDSPEAKER] F5 -.AGC.-> F2 F7 -.AFC voltage.-> F4 style F1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F4 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F5 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F6 fill:#A8E6CF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F7 fill:#A8E6CF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F10 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Distinctive FM-only blocks (highlighted in green):** - **Limiter** — between IF and demodulator - **FM Demodulator** — frequency-to-voltage converter - **De-emphasis** — restores pre-emphasized audio spectrum - **AFC loop** — demodulator output fed back to LO to correct drift ## 4.3 Frequency Translation Across the Receiver Stages ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Antenna picks up many RF signals across 88 to 108 MHz] P2[RF amplifier selects one station, for example 98.5 MHz] P3[Mixer plus LO translates to 10.7 MHz IF] P4[IF amplifier provides bulk gain at fixed 10.7 MHz] P5[Limiter removes amplitude noise] P6[FM demodulator extracts audio] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 style P1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style P6 fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Signal Spectrum Evolution Through an FM Super-Heterodyne Receiver ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Stage 1: RF input spectrum — many stations in 88 to 108 MHz band"] S2["Stage 2: After RF preselector — one station centered at f_RF"] S3["Stage 3: After mixer — same modulation now at 10.7 MHz carrier"] S4["Stage 4: After IF amplifier — high amplitude at 10.7 MHz"] S5["Stage 5: After demodulator — recovered baseband audio 30 Hz to 15 kHz"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 style S1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style S5 fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` This sequence is critical for exam answers — examiners want you to describe **what happens to the signal at each stage**, not merely name the blocks. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define a super-heterodyne receiver. State any two advantages over the TRF receiver.** **Model Answer:** A super-heterodyne receiver is a radio receiver in which the incoming RF signal is mixed with a locally generated signal from a local oscillator, and the difference frequency (called the **Intermediate Frequency, IF**) is held constant regardless of the tuned station frequency. **Two advantages over the TRF (Tuned Radio Frequency) receiver:** 1. **High and uniform selectivity:** The IF amplifier is a fixed-frequency high-Q amplifier, providing sharp and consistent bandpass filtering across the entire tuning range. TRF receivers required simultaneous tuning of multiple LC stages which was practically very difficult. 2. **High and uniform gain:** The IF stage operates at a fixed, lower frequency (455 kHz AM, 10.7 MHz FM) where stable high gain can be achieved without the risk of oscillation. TRF receivers operating at RF had stability problems. *(Accept also: better image rejection, easier alignment in production, lower cost, better sensitivity.)* **[3 marks]** --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: What is image frequency in a super-heterodyne receiver? Why must it be rejected?** **Model Answer:** **Image frequency** is the frequency of an unwanted RF signal that, after mixing with the local oscillator, produces the **same** Intermediate Frequency as the desired signal. Mathematically, for high-side LO injection: $$f_{img} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$$ **Why it must be rejected:** The mixer is a non-linear device that produces both sum and difference frequencies. It cannot distinguish between the desired signal (at $f_{RF}$) and the image (at $f_{img}$) — both produce the IF at the mixer output. If the image is not sufficiently attenuated by the RF amplifier (preselector) before the mixer, it will be amplified by the IF stages and reproduced at the speaker, causing **co-channel interference** that the user hears as a "ghost" station at the same dial position. > [!NOTE] > For numerical reinforcement: If $f_{RF} = 1000$ kHz, $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz, then $f_{LO} = 1455$ kHz, and $f_{img} = 1910$ kHz. The image is **910 kHz = 2·IF** above the desired signal. **[3 marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Q: (a)** Draw the block diagram of an **AM super-heterodyne receiver** and explain the function of each block. Mention the standard AM IF value. (7 marks) **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an **FM super-heterodyne receiver**. Mention the standard FM IF value and explain the function of the **limiter** and **de-emphasis** network. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for (a) — AM Super-Heterodyne Receiver **Block Diagram (re-stated from Section 4.1):** `Antenna → RF Amplifier → Mixer ← Local Oscillator → IF Amplifier → AM Detector → Audio Amplifier → Speaker` **Function of each block (7 points / sub-parts):** 1. **Antenna:** Captures the electromagnetic wave from the desired AM broadcast station and converts it to a weak RF voltage signal. Typical input signal strength is in microvolts. 2. **RF Amplifier (Preselector):** A tunable bandpass amplifier that (i) amplifies the weak RF signal, (ii) provides initial selectivity by rejecting adjacent channels and the **image frequency** ($2 f_{IF}$ above desired). 3. **Local Oscillator (LO):** A tunable oscillator, **ganged** mechanically with the RF amplifier tuning, that generates a sine wave at $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ (high-side injection). As the user tunes across the band, the LO tracks the RF amplifier so that their difference is always exactly $f_{IF}$. 4. **Mixer (Frequency Converter):** A non-linear device (transistor or diode mixer) that multiplies the RF signal with the LO signal. Using the trigonometric identity, the output contains the difference frequency $f_{IF}$ and the sum frequency $f_{LO} + f_{RF}$. The IF filter selects only the difference. 5. **IF Amplifier:** The heart of the receiver — multiple cascaded fixed-frequency stages tuned to **f_IF = 455 kHz** (standard for AM broadcast). Provides the bulk of the receiver's voltage gain and adjacent-channel selectivity.
