Modern receivers use **ceramic or mechanical IF filters** for sharp bandpass response. 6. **AM Detector (Envelope Detector):** A simple diode detector followed by an RC low-pass filter. The IF AM signal's envelope (which varies at audio rate) is recovered as the audio signal. The carrier (455 kHz) is filtered out by the RC network. 7. **Audio Amplifier and Speaker:** The recovered audio is amplified to a level sufficient to drive a loudspeaker. Includes volume and tone control stages. 8. **AGC (Automatic Gain Control):** Although not always drawn as a separate block, AGC takes a DC voltage derived from the detected AM signal and feeds it back to control the gain of the RF and IF stages, preventing overload on strong signals. **Standard AM IF value: 455 kHz** (must be stated explicitly to score full marks). **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution for (b) — FM Super-Heterodyne Receiver **Block Diagram (re-stated from Section 4.2):** `Antenna → RF Amplifier → Mixer ← Local Oscillator (with AFC) → IF Amplifier (10.7 MHz) → Limiter → FM Demodulator → De-emphasis → Audio Amplifier → Speaker` **Functions of blocks and key concepts (7 marks):** 1. **RF Amplifier:** Operates in the 88–108 MHz band; selects and amplifies the desired FM station; provides image rejection (image is at $2 \times 10.7 = 21.4$ MHz above desired). 2. **Local Oscillator with AFC:** Generates $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + 10.7$ MHz. The Automatic Frequency Control (AFC) loop uses a DC error voltage from the FM demodulator to slightly retune the LO via a **varactor diode**, compensating for any thermal or mechanical drift. 3. **IF Amplifier:** Operates at the standard **f_IF = 10.7 MHz**. Multi-stage amplifier with ceramic or crystal bandpass filters. Provides high gain and sharp selectivity (typically 200 kHz bandwidth for 150 kHz FM deviation). 4. **Limiter:** A crucial FM-specific block. It is essentially a **high-gain amplifier driven into saturation**, which clips the amplitude of the IF signal to a constant value. This **removes all amplitude variations** (caused by noise, fading, ignition interference) while preserving the frequency variations that contain the audio modulation. This is the fundamental reason for FM's superior noise immunity compared to AM. 5. **FM Demodulator (Discriminator):** Converts the frequency variations of the IF signal back to an amplitude (voltage) variation — the recovered audio. Common types: **Foster-Seeley discriminator, Ratio detector, Quadrature detector, Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) demodulator.** Each type uses the slope of a tuned circuit (or PLL tracking) to convert $\Delta f$ to $\Delta V$. 6. **De-emphasis Network:** A simple **75 µs RC low-pass filter** placed after the demodulator. It reverses the **pre-emphasis** applied at the transmitter (which boosted high audio frequencies to improve SNR). Without de-emphasis, the recovered audio would sound overly bright/hissy. 7. **Audio Amplifier and Speaker:** Same function as in AM — amplify the recovered baseband audio to speaker-driving level. **Standard FM IF value: 10.7 MHz** (must be stated). **[7 marks]** --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper] **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **intermediate frequency (IF)** and **local oscillator (LO)** in a super-heterodyne receiver. Derive the expression for image frequency. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare the AM and FM super-heterodyne receivers. Why is the **limiter** used only in the FM receiver and not in the AM receiver? Explain with a neat block diagram. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for (a) — IF, LO, and Image Frequency Derivation **Concept of Intermediate Frequency (IF):** (3 marks) The Intermediate Frequency is a **fixed, lower frequency** (compared to the incoming RF) at which all the received signals — regardless of their original carrier frequencies in the broadcast band — are processed for amplification and selectivity. The IF value is **standardized** to 455 kHz for AM broadcast and 10.7 MHz for FM broadcast. **Why is IF lower than RF?** - Lower frequency = **higher stable gain** achievable without oscillation - **High-Q fixed-frequency bandpass filters** can be built economically (LC, ceramic, crystal, mechanical) - Adjacent channel selectivity becomes **sharper and more uniform** across the tuning range **Concept of Local Oscillator (LO):** (2 marks) The Local Oscillator is a **tunable** sine-wave generator whose output frequency is **ganged** (mechanically/electronically coupled) with the RF amplifier's tuning capacitor. As the user tunes to a new station, the LO is automatically readjusted to maintain the relation: $$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} \quad \text{(high-side injection, standard)}$$ The mixer subtracts (or adds) the LO and RF frequencies, and the difference $f_{LO} - f_{RF} = f_{IF}$ is selected as the IF output. **Derivation of Image Frequency:** (2 marks — full derivation as in Section 3.1) Given: $f_{LO} - f_{RF} = f_{IF}$ and $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$. The image is the RF signal $f_{img}$ that also produces the same IF: $$f_{LO} - f_{img} = -f_{IF}$$ $$\Rightarrow f_{img} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = (f_{RF} + f_{IF}) + f_{IF}$$ $$\boxed{f_{img} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}}$$ **Conclusion:** The image lies $2 f_{IF}$ above the desired signal and must be rejected by the RF preselector. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution for (b) — Comparison, Limiter Function, Block Diagram **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | AM Super-Heterodyne | FM Super-Heterodyne | |---|---|---| | IF value | 455 kHz | 10.7 MHz | | Detector/Demodulator | Envelope detector (diode + RC) | FM discriminator (Foster-Seeley, Ratio, PLL) | | Limiter stage | Not required | **Required** | | AFC | Optional / not used | **Commonly used** | | De-emphasis | Not used | **75 µs RC network** | | AGC | Yes (essential) | Yes (used) | | Bandwidth | ~10 kHz | 200 kHz | | Sensitivity | Moderate | Higher | **Why the Limiter is Used Only in FM (3 marks):** In an **AM signal**, the audio information is carried in the **amplitude variations** of the carrier. If a limiter were used, it would destroy the very information we are trying to recover — so **limiters cannot be used in AM receivers.** In an **FM signal**, the audio information is carried in the **frequency variations** of the carrier, while the **amplitude remains theoretically constant.** A limiter saturates the signal to remove **amplitude noise** (caused by atmospheric disturbances, ignition, fading) without affecting the frequency content. This is the fundamental reason for FM's superior noise immunity. The limiter hard-clips the IF signal to a constant amplitude, and the FM demodulator (which responds only to frequency changes) recovers the audio faithfully. **Block Diagram of Limiter Action:** `IF Amplifier → Limiter (hard clipper / saturated amp) → Constant amplitude FM IF → FM Demodulator` **[7 marks]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common reasons students LOSE marks on super-heterodyne receiver questions (KTU 2024):** > > 1. **Forgetting the standard IF values.** Writing "high frequency" instead of **455 kHz (AM)** or **10.7 MHz (FM)** costs 1 mark per occurrence. > 2. **Drawing blocks without arrows** showing signal flow direction. Examiners want a clear flow: Antenna → RF → Mixer → IF → Detector → Audio → Speaker. Missing arrows = −1 to −2 marks. > 3. **Not showing the ganged coupling** between the Local Oscillator and the RF amplifier tuning. This is the heart of the "super-heterodyne" concept and must be visible in the diagram. > 4. **Confusing "image" with "intermediate" frequency.** Image is at the RF input side; IF is at the mixer output. Many students mix these up. > 5. **Forgetting the AGC loop** in the AM receiver diagram. Examiners expect a feedback arrow from detector to RF/IF stages. > 6. **Drawing the limiter in an AM receiver** — this is conceptually wrong and penalized. > 7. **Not writing the image frequency formula** explicitly with a derivation when the question asks for it. > 8. **Missing the de-emphasis block** in the FM receiver — examiner will deduct 1 mark. > 9. **In numerical problems, forgetting to verify** that the mixer output difference equals $f_{IF}$ for both the desired and image frequencies. > 10. **Not stating the high-side vs. low-side LO assumption** before writing the image frequency formula. Always declare the assumption first. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Block Diagram of AM & FM Super-Heterodyne Receiver** ### Conceptual Foundation - ⭐ **Super-heterodyne** = "above the heterodyne" → translates all RF inputs to a common, fixed IF - ⭐ **Inventor:** Edwin H. Armstrong, 1918 - ⭐ **Replaced the older TRF (Tuned Radio Frequency) receiver** which was hard to align and unstable ### Key Frequency Relations - ⭐ **AM IF = 455 kHz** ; **FM IF = 10.7 MHz** (memorize!) - ⭐ $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ (standard high-side injection) - ⭐ $f_{img} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ (image is 2·IF above desired) - ⭐ **Mixer outputs:** difference (= IF, passed) and sum (filtered out) ### AM Receiver Block Chain - ⭐ Antenna → RF Amplifier → Mixer + LO → IF Amplifier (455 kHz) → **Envelope Detector** → Audio Amp → Speaker - ⭐ **AGC feedback** is essential ### FM Receiver Block Chain - ⭐ Antenna → RF Amplifier → Mixer + LO (with AFC) → IF Amplifier (10.7 MHz) → **Limiter** → **FM Discriminator** → **De-emphasis (75 µs)** → Audio Amp → Speaker - ⭐ **Three FM-specific blocks:** Limiter, FM Demodulator, De-emphasis ### Image Frequency Facts - ⭐ Image is at the **antenna input side**, not at the IF - ⭐ Image is **2·IF away** from desired signal - ⭐ Rejection is the job of the **RF preselector (front-end bandpass filter)** - ⭐ **Higher IF → better image rejection** (this is why FM uses 10.7 MHz vs AM's 455 kHz) ### Why FM is Noise-Immune - ⭐ **Limiter** removes amplitude noise - ⭐ FM audio is in the **frequency**, not amplitude - ⭐ **Pre-emphasis + De-emphasis** reduces high-frequency hiss ### Examiner-Favored Keywords (use in answers) - "Ganged tuning" - "Fixed intermediate frequency" - "Image rejection ratio" - "Non-linear mixing" - "Adjacent channel selectivity" - "Automatic Gain Control (AGC)" - "Automatic Frequency Control (AFC)" - "Limiter action / hard clipping" - "Pre-emphasis and de-emphasis" ### Numerical Quick-Reference Template For any super-heterodyne problem, write down in order: 1. Given: $f_{RF}$ and $f_{IF}$ (and state high/low side) 2. Compute: $f_{LO} = f_{RF} \pm f_{IF}$ 3. Compute: $f_{img} = f_{RF} \pm 2 f_{IF}$ 4. Verify: $f_{LO} - f_{RF} = \pm f_{IF}$ 5. State the role of each block in one line. This 5-step template, written clearly, will secure **full marks** on any super-heterodyne numerical under the KTU 2024 scheme.

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Wired and Wireless Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Wired and Wireless Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Communication System?

A **communication system** is an engineering arrangement that transfers information (voice, data, video, or control signals) from a source (transmitter) to a destination (receiver) over a physical medium (channel) with acceptable fidelity, speed, and reliability.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A communication system is a collection of subsystems designed to generate, transmit, and receive analog or digital information signals. The goal is to reproduce the source message at the receiver with minimum distortion and noise.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Letter

Think of communication exactly like sending a **letter through the post office**:

| Real-World Post | Communication System |
|---|---|
| The person writing the letter | Information **Source** |
| The thoughts/words on paper | The **Message Signal** |
| Writing the letter in a specific language | **Encoding / Modulation** |
| The envelope and address | **Transmitter** + Channel coding |
| The postman / roads / postal network | The **Channel** (wired/wireless medium) |
| Rain, traffic, lost parcels | **Noise** and Interference |
| The recipient reading the letter | **Receiver** + Decoder |
| Understanding the meaning | **Destination** / Output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** In **any** communication system — whether a 1950s AM radio, a fiber-optic internet link, or a 5G smartphone — the same three blocks always exist: **Transmitter → Channel → Receiver**. The *medium* and *modulation technique* simply change.

---

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Branches

The entire subject of communication is divided into two grand families based on the **physical transmission medium**:

### A) Wired (Guided) Communication
Information travels through a **solid physical conductor** that "guides" the signal from one point to another. The electromagnetic wave is **confined** within the cable.

> [!NOTE]
> Examples: **Twisted pair cable** (telephone landline, Ethernet LAN), **Coaxial cable** (Cable TV, early Ethernet), **Optical fiber cable** (modern broadband internet, undersea telecom cables).

### B) Wireless (Unguided) Communication
Information travels through **free space (air/vacuum)** as **Electromagnetic (EM) waves** radiated from an antenna. There is no physical conductor between the transmitter and the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> Examples: **AM/FM Radio broadcasting**, **Television broadcast**, **Mobile cellular (2G/3G/4G/5G)**, **Satellite communication (DTH TV, GPS)**, **Wi-Fi**, **Bluetooth**, **Radar**.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Communication Theory?

Without a structured theory, we cannot:
1. Choose the **right medium** (copper vs. fiber vs. radio).
2. **Maximize data rate** within a given bandwidth.
3. **Minimize errors** caused by noise.
4. Allow **multiple users** to share one channel (multiplexing).
5. Ensure **security** of transmitted information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** Every KTU question on this module is based on the central theme — *"How to send more information, faster, with fewer errors, over any distance, using any medium."*

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Must Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> These **bolded** metrics appear in almost every numerical and 3-mark question.

- **Bandwidth (B)** — The range of frequencies that a channel/transmission system can carry, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Data Rate / Bit Rate (R)** — Number of bits transmitted per second (**bits per second, bps**).
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (S/N or SNR)** — Ratio of signal power to noise power; **dimensionless** (often in dB).
- **Attenuation** — Loss of signal strength as it travels, measured in **dB/km**.
- **Propagation Delay** — Time taken by the signal to travel from sender to receiver.
- **Channel Capacity (C)** — Maximum achievable data rate over a channel, measured in **bps**.

---

## 1.5 Modes of Communication

| Mode | Direction of Flow | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | One direction only | TV broadcast, Radio |
| **Half-Duplex** | Both directions, but **one at a time** | Walkie-Talkie (Push-to-Talk) |
| **Full-Duplex** | Both directions **simultaneously** | Mobile phone call, Video call |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Block Diagram of a Communication System

Every wired or wireless system contains the following fundamental blocks:

$$
\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Input Transducer} \rightarrow \text{Transmitter} \rightarrow \text{Channel} \rightarrow \text{Receiver} \rightarrow \text{Output Transducer} \rightarrow \text{Destination}
$$

### Step-by-Step Functional Description

- **Information Source:** Originates the message — human voice, computer file, video frames.
- **Input Transducer:** Converts the message into a usable electrical form (e.g., microphone converts sound → audio voltage).
- **Transmitter:** Performs **modulation, encoding, and power amplification** to make the signal suitable for the channel.
- **Channel:** The physical medium — copper wire, optical fiber, or free-space air. **Noise** is added here.
- **Receiver:** Performs the **inverse operations** of the transmitter — amplification, demodulation, decoding.
- **Output Transducer:** Converts the recovered electrical signal back to the original form (e.g., loudspeaker → sound).
- **Destination:** The end user who consumes the information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Modulation is Non-Negotiable:** Baseband signals (e.g., 20 Hz – 20 kHz audio) cannot travel long distances because their wavelength is huge. By **modulating** them onto a high-frequency **carrier wave**, we get smaller antennas, better propagation, and the ability to multiplex multiple users using different carrier frequencies.

---

## 2.2 Wired Communication — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Twisted Pair Cable

- Two insulated copper conductors **twisted helically** to cancel out electromagnetic interference (crosstalk).
- **Why twist?** A uniform twist ensures both wires are equally exposed to external noise, so the **common-mode noise** is cancelled by a differential receiver.
- Two varieties: **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair)** and **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair)**.
- Used in **telephone lines, Ethernet LAN (Cat5, Cat6)**, DSL internet.
- Bandwidth: typically up to a few hundred MHz; Distance limit: ~100 m for Ethernet.

### 2.2.2 Coaxial Cable

- A central copper conductor surrounded by a **dielectric insulator**, a **metallic shield (braid)**, and an **outer plastic jacket**.
- The shield provides excellent immunity to external noise.
- Used in **cable TV (CATV), older Ethernet (10BASE-2/5), CCTV, and as a feeder to TV antennas**.
- Bandwidth: hundreds of MHz; Lower attenuation than twisted pair.

### 2.2.3 Optical Fiber Cable

- Hair-thin strands of **glass (SiO₂)** or plastic that carry information as **pulses of light** (typically at 850 nm, 1310 nm, or 1550 nm wavelengths).
- Works on the principle of **Total Internal Reflection** (TIR). Light entering at an angle greater than the *critical angle* reflects totally within the core.
- Two types: **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** — long-distance, telecom-grade, and **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)** — short-distance, LAN-grade.
- Bandwidth: **THz range**; Attenuation: as low as **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm.
- Immune to **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** and extremely secure (hard to tap).

---

## 2.3 Wireless Communication — Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 Electromagnetic Wave Fundamentals

Wireless communication is the transmission of information using **Electromagnetic (EM) waves** that propagate through free space at the speed of light, **c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**.

The relationship between wavelength, frequency, and wave speed is:

$$
c = f \cdot \lambda
$$

where:
- $c$ = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $f$ = frequency in Hz
- $\lambda$ = wavelength in meters

### 2.3.2 The EM Frequency Spectrum for Communication

| Band | Frequency Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **VLF (Very Low Frequency)** | 3 – 30 kHz | Naval submarine communication |
| **MF (Medium Frequency)** | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | **AM radio broadcasting** |
| **HF (High Frequency)** | 3 – 30 MHz | Shortwave radio, amateur radio |
| **VHF (Very High Frequency)** | 30 – 300 MHz | **FM radio, TV, aviation** |
| **UHF (Ultra High Frequency)** | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | **TV, mobile phones, Wi-Fi, GPS** |
| **SHF (Super High Frequency)** | 3 – 30 GHz | **Satellite, Radar, 5G mmWave** |
| **EHF (Extremely High Frequency)** | 30 – 300 GHz | 5G mmWave, advanced radar |

### 2.3.3 Antennas — The Heart of Wireless

An **antenna** is a transducer that converts electrical signals into EM waves (transmitting) and EM waves into electrical signals (receiving). A basic half-wave dipole antenna has a physical length of:

$$
L = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{c}{2f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why higher frequency means smaller antenna:** Mobile phones use ~900 MHz / 1800 MHz / 2.4 GHz / 5 GHz precisely because this allows small, embedded antennas. A 1 MHz AM radio station would require a 150-meter-long antenna!

### 2.3.4 Propagation Modes of EM Waves

Three principal ways EM waves travel from transmitter to receiver:

1. **Ground Wave / Surface Wave Propagation** — Follows the curvature of the earth. Used for AM radio, low-frequency bands.
2. **Sky Wave / Ionospheric Propagation** — Bounces off the **ionosphere** (a layer of charged particles ~60–400 km above the earth). Used for shortwave international radio.
3. **Space Wave / Line-of-Sight (LOS) Propagation** — Travels in straight lines. Used for **FM radio, TV, mobile, satellite, microwave links**. Range is limited by the curvature of the earth and antenna height.

> [!NOTE]
> The maximum LOS distance between two antennas of heights $h_1$ and $h_2$ (in meters) is:
> $$ d_{max} \approx 4.12 \left( \sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2} \right) \text{ km} $$

### 2.3.5 Cellular Concept (Foundation of Mobile Phones)

The earth is divided into small hexagonal regions called **cells**, each served by a **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)**. The same set of radio frequencies can be **reused in non-adjacent cells**, multiplying system capacity dramatically. This is called the **Frequency Reuse** concept.

---

## 2.4 Modulation — The Engine of Communication

**Modulation** is the process of varying one or more properties of a high-frequency **carrier wave** (amplitude, frequency, or phase) in accordance with the low-frequency **message signal**.

$$
c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)
$$

where $A_c$ is amplitude, $f_c$ is carrier frequency, and $\phi$ is phase.

### 2.4.1 Types of Analog Modulation

| Modulation Type | What is Varied | Parameter Changed in Carrier |
|---|---|---|
| **AM (Amplitude Modulation)** | Amplitude | $A_c \rightarrow A_c(1 + k \cdot m(t))$ |
| **FM (Frequency Modulation)** | Frequency | Instantaneous frequency follows $m(t)$ |
| **PM (Phase Modulation)** | Phase | $\phi \rightarrow \phi_0 + k \cdot m(t)$ |

### 2.4.2 Digital Modulation (Key Techniques)

| Technique | Description |
|---|---|
| **ASK** (Amplitude Shift Keying) | Amplitude switches between two levels for bits 0 and 1 |
| **FSK** (Frequency Shift Keying) | Two distinct frequencies for 0 and 1 |
| **PSK** (Phase Shift Keying) | Carrier phase flips for 0 and 1 |
| **QAM** (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation) | Combined ASK + PSK; used in 4G/5G/Wi-Fi |

---

## 2.5 Multiplexing — Sharing the Channel

**Multiplexing** is the technique of transmitting multiple independent signals over a single channel.

| Type | Domain | How Signals are Separated |
|---|---|---|
| **FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing)** | Frequency | Each signal gets a unique carrier frequency band |
| **TDM (Time Division Multiplexing)** | Time | Each signal gets a unique time slot in a repeating frame |
| **WDM (Wavelength Division Multiplexing)** | Wavelength (optical) | Each signal uses a unique light wavelength — used in optical fiber |
| **CDMA (Code Division Multiplexing)** | Code | Each signal uses a unique spread-spectrum code |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every formula below has appeared in past KTU examinations.** Memorize units and conditions carefully.

| # | Formula / Law | Description | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f \lambda$ | Wave relation | c in m/s, f in Hz, $\lambda$ in m |
| 2 | $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2f}$ | Half-wave dipole antenna length | m |
| 3 | $d_{max} \approx 4.12 \left( \sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2} \right)$ | Line-of-sight distance | km (heights in m) |
| 4 | $C_{N} = 2B \log_2 M$ | **Nyquist Bit Rate** (noiseless channel) | bps, M = signal levels |
| 5 | $C_{S} = B \log_2 \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon Channel Capacity** (noisy channel) | bps |
| 6 | $\text{SNR (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{S}{N} \right)$ | Signal-to-Noise ratio in dB | dB |
| 7 | $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}} \right)$ | Attenuation / Gain in dB | dB |
| 8 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | $f_s$ = sampling rate, $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency |
| 9 | $n_c(t) = c(t) + n(t)$ | Received signal = clean signal + noise | Combined waveform |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students write $\log_2$ as $\log$ in the Shannon formula. **Always use base 2** for the log. Using natural log or base-10 log gives numerically wrong answers.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical fiber networks** carry **>99% of global internet traffic** because of THz bandwidth and near-zero attenuation.
- **5G cellular systems** use mmWave bands (24–52 GHz), massive MIMO antennas, and OFDM (Orthogonal FDM).
- **Satellite communication** enables **DTH TV, GPS navigation, weather forecasting, and rural broadband (e.g., Starlink)**.
- **In radar systems**, the same EM wave fundamentals (reflection, Doppler shift) are used to detect aircraft and precipitation.
- **EMI/EMC engineering** uses twisted-pair and shielding principles to make medical and aerospace equipment safe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wavelength of an EM Wave

**Statement:** Find the wavelength of a 900 MHz cellular signal (GSM 900 band).

**Given:** $f = 900 \text{ MHz} = 900 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1 —** Start from the fundamental wave relation.

$$
c = f \cdot \lambda
$$

**Step 2 —** Rearrange to isolate wavelength.

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f}
$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{900 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 4 —** Simplify by cancelling the powers of 10.

$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{9 \times 10^{8}} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 —** Compute the final numerical value.

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{3} \text{ m} \approx 0.333 \text{ m} = 33.3 \text{ cm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A quarter-wave antenna for GSM 900 would be **≈ 8.3 cm long**, which is why old Motorola phones had visible external antennas of this length.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Line-of-Sight Distance Between Two Antennas

**Statement:** A TV transmitting antenna is 100 m tall, and a rooftop Yagi antenna on a house is 10 m tall. Calculate the maximum LOS range.

**Given:** $h_1 = 100$ m, $h_2 = 10$ m.

**Step 1 —** Use the LOS formula (derived from the geometry of the curved earth and radio horizon):

$$
d_{max} \approx 4.12 \left( \sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2} \right) \text{ km}
$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the square roots.

$$
\sqrt{h_1} = \sqrt{100} = 10
$$

$$
\sqrt{h_2} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162
$$

**Step 3 —** Add the values.

$$
\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2} = 10 + 3.162 = 13.162
$$

**Step 4 —** Multiply by 4.12.

$$
d_{max} \approx 4.12 \times 13.162 \approx 54.23 \text{ km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why **TV transmitter towers are built extremely tall (e.g., 300 m)** — each extra meter of height increases coverage radius significantly. The world's tallest structures (Burj Khalifa, Tokyo Skytree) host communication masts precisely for this reason.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Data Rate Using Nyquist's Theorem

**Statement:** A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. How many bits per second can be sent using 16 discrete signal levels?

**Given:** $B = 4000$ Hz, $M = 16$ levels.

**Step 1 —** State Nyquist's bit-rate formula for a noiseless channel.

$$
C_N = 2B \log_2 M
$$

**Step 2 —** Compute $\log_2 M$.

$$
\log_2 16 = \log_2 2^4 = 4 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 3 —** Multiply the constants.

$$
C_N = 2 \times 4000 \times 4
$$

**Step 4 —** Final numerical result.

$$
C_N = 32000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why factor 2?** Nyquist's theorem states that a noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ can support a maximum of $2B$ independent signal changes per second. Each change can carry $\log_2 M$ bits if $M$ discrete levels are used.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Shannon's Channel Capacity (Noisy Channel)

**Statement:** A channel of bandwidth 3 kHz and SNR of 30 dB is used. Compute the maximum error-free data rate.

**Given:** $B = 3000$ Hz, SNR (dB) = 30.

**Step 1 —** Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.

$$
\text{SNR (linear)} = 10^{\frac{\text{SNR(dB)}}{10}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{S}{N} = 10^{\frac{30}{10}} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

**Step 2 —** Apply Shannon's theorem.

$$
C_S = B \log_2 \left( 1 + \dfrac{S}{N} \right)
$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute the values.

$$
C_S = 3000 \times \log_2 (1 + 1000)
$$

$$
C_S = 3000 \times \log_2 (1001)
$$

**Step 4 —** Evaluate $\log_2(1001)$.

$$
\log_2(1001) \approx 9.967
$$

**Step 5 —** Multiply by B.

$$
C_S \approx 3000 \times 9.967 \approx 29900 \text{ bps} \approx 29.9 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's theorem sets an absolute upper bound.** No coding or modulation scheme, no matter how clever, can exceed $C_S$ error-free bits per second on that channel.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Attenuation in dB Along an Optical Fiber

**Statement:** An optical signal of 1 mW is launched into a fiber. After 50 km, the received power is 10 µW. Compute the attenuation in dB/km.

**Given:** $P_{\text{in}} = 1 \text{ mW} = 10^{-3}$ W, $P_{\text{out}} = 10 \text{ µW} = 10^{-5}$ W, $L = 50$ km.

**Step 1 —** Total attenuation in dB.

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}} \right)
$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the power ratio.

$$
\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-5}} = 100
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate the log.

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 —** Per-km attenuation.

$$
A_{\text{dB/km}} = \frac{20 \text{ dB}}{50 \text{ km}} = 0.4 \text{ dB/km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern single-mode fibers at 1550 nm achieve **~0.2 dB/km** — among the lowest attenuation of any transmission medium ever engineered.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Medium

> [!NOTE]
> **How to think about this matrix in an exam:** Always argue using **bandwidth, distance, cost, EMI immunity, and security**.

| Property | Twisted Pair | Coaxial Cable | Optical Fiber | Free-Space Radio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Very High (THz) | Medium (depends on band) |
| **Attenuation** | High | Moderate | Very Low | Depends on path |
| **EMI Immunity** | Poor (better when twisted) | Good (shielded) | Excellent (immune) | Susceptible to noise/interference |
| **Security** | Low (easy to tap) | Moderate | Very High (hard to tap) | Low (anyone in range can intercept) |
| **Cost per meter** | Very Low | Low | Moderate–High | Lowest (no cable) |
| **Typical Use** | LAN, Telephone | Cable TV, CCTV | Internet backbone, undersea cables | Mobile, Wi-Fi, satellite |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src[Information Source] --> InTr[Input Transducer]
    InTr --> Mod[Modulator and Encoder]
    Mod --> Tx[Transmitter with Power Amplifier]
    Tx --> Ant1[Transmitting Antenna]
    Ant1 --> Ch[Channel with Noise and Interference]
    Ch --> Ant2[Receiving Antenna]
    Ant2 --> Rx[Receiver with Low Noise Amplifier]
    Rx --> Dem[Demodulator and Decoder]
    Dem --> OutTr[Output Transducer]
    OutTr --> Dest[Destination]
    Noise[Noise Source] -.-> Ch
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Information flows from left → right. The dotted arrow indicates that **noise** intrudes into the channel from outside and corrupts the signal — a central challenge addressed by all of communication theory.

---

## 4.2 Classification of Communication Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Com[Communication Systems] --> Wired[Wired Guided Media]
    Com --> Wireless[Wireless Unguided Media]
    Wired --> TP[Twisted Pair]
    Wired --> Cox[Coaxial Cable]
    Wired --> OFC[Optical Fiber Cable]
    Wireless --> Radio[Radio Frequency Bands]
    Wireless --> MW[Microwave]
    Wireless --> Sat[Satellite]
    Wireless --> OptComm[Optical Wireless Infrared]
    Radio --> AM[AM Broadcast]
    Radio --> FM[FM Broadcast]
    Radio --> Cell[Cellular 2G 3G 4G 5G]
    MW --> WiFi[WiFi Bluetooth]
    MW --> Radar[Radar Systems]
    Sat --> DTH[DTH Television]
    Sat --> GPS[GPS Navigation]
    Sat --> Starlink[Broadband Internet]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow of Modulation and Demodulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS[Low Frequency Message Signal] --> ModBlk[Modulator Block]
    CW[High Frequency Carrier Wave] --> ModBlk
    ModBlk --> ModOut[Modulated RF Signal]
    ModOut --> Channel[Transmission Channel]
    Channel --> DemBlk[Demodulator Block]
    DemBlk --> RecOut[Recovered Message Signal]
    DemBlk --> Dest[Destination Loudspeaker or Display]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The carrier is essential because baseband signals have frequencies that would require **multi-kilometer long antennas** and are easily swamped by atmospheric noise. Modulation shifts information to a band where propagation is efficient.

---

## 4.4 Cellular Frequency Reuse Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cluster["Cluster of 7 Cells with Frequency Groups A B C D E F G"]
        C1[Cell 1 - Frequency A] --- C2[Cell 2 - Frequency B]
        C2 --- C3[Cell 3 - Frequency C]
        C3 --- C4[Cell 4 - Frequency D]
        C4 --- C5[Cell 5 - Frequency E]
        C5 --- C6[Cell 6 - Frequency F]
        C6 --- C7[Cell 7 - Frequency G]
    end
    Cluster --> Reuse[Same Frequencies Reused in Adjacent Cluster]
    Reuse --> BTS[Each Cell has a Base Transceiver Station BTS]
    BTS --> MSC[Mobile Switching Center MSC routes calls]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hexagonal Cell Model** is an *idealization* used by engineers for capacity planning. Real-world cell shapes are irregular due to terrain, buildings, and antenna patterns.
