# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice) = 14 marks
> - Total for this module-level question set: 20 marks

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Communication System. List its basic blocks. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **communication system** is a collection of subsystems designed to transmit information (voice, data, video) from a source to a destination over a channel with acceptable fidelity.

**Basic Blocks:**
1. Information Source
2. Input Transducer
3. Transmitter (with modulator, encoder, amplifier)
4. Channel (transmission medium with noise)
5. Receiver (with amplifier, demodulator, decoder)
6. Output Transducer
7. Destination

**Block diagram:**

$$
\text{Source} \to \text{Transducer} \to \text{Transmitter} \to \text{Channel(+Noise)} \to \text{Receiver} \to \text{Transducer} \to \text{Destination}
$$

---

### Question 2: Compare Wired and Wireless Communication. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wired Communication | Wireless Communication |
|---|---|---|
| **Medium** | Physical cable (copper/fiber) | Free space (air/vacuum) |
| **Signal** | Electrical or optical | Electromagnetic waves |
| **Mobility** | Limited by cable length | High mobility |
| **EMI Susceptibility** | Low (fiber is immune) | High |
| **Security** | High (hard to tap, esp. fiber) | Lower (anyone in range can intercept) |
| **Installation Cost** | High (cabling, ducts) | Low (no cabling) |
| **Bandwidth** | Very high (fiber) | Limited by spectrum |
| **Example** | Optical fiber backbone | 5G mobile, Wi-Fi |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE rules, every 14-mark question has an **internal choice** between two sub-options. Both options are provided below.

---

### Question A: Communication Mediums and Antennas (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** With neat diagrams, explain the construction and working of **Optical Fiber Communication**. State two advantages and one disadvantage.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
An optical fiber consists of three concentric cylindrical layers:
1. **Core** — central glass strand (refractive index $n_1$ ≈ 1.48) that carries the light.
2. **Cladding** — outer glass layer (refractive index $n_2$ ≈ 1.45) surrounding the core.
3. **Buffer / Jacket** — protective plastic coating.

**Working Principle — Total Internal Reflection (TIR):**
When light enters the core at an angle greater than the **critical angle** $\theta_c$, it reflects entirely back into the core instead of refracting into the cladding. The condition is:

$$
n_1 > n_2
$$

$$
\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

As long as the angle of incidence inside the core exceeds $\theta_c$, the light zig-zags down the fiber with **negligible loss**.

**Diagram (functional flow):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LED[LED or Laser Source] --> Core[Glass Core n1]
    Core --> TIR[Total Internal Reflection]
    TIR --> Photo[Photodetector PIN or APD]
    Photo --> Amp[Amplifier and Decoder]
    Amp --> Out[Recovered Digital or Analog Signal]
```

**Advantages:**
1. **Extremely high bandwidth** — supports THz range, suitable for high-speed internet.
2. **Immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI)** — can be used near high-voltage power lines.
3. **Low attenuation** — ~0.2 dB/km, allowing transcontinental undersea links without repeaters.
4. **High security** — extremely difficult to tap without detection.

**Disadvantage:**
- **Fragile and expensive to install.** Bending beyond the minimum bend radius causes signal loss or fiber breakage. Splicing and connectorization require specialized tools and trained technicians.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A cellular base-station antenna operates at 900 MHz. Calculate (i) the wavelength of the signal, and (ii) the length of a half-wave dipole antenna. State the line-of-sight range if the antenna is 100 m high and the receiving antenna is 10 m high.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $f = 900$ MHz $= 900 \times 10^6$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $h_1 = 100$ m, $h_2 = 10$ m.

**Part (i) — Wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{900 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9 \times 10^8} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ m} \approx 0.333 \text{ m} = 33.3 \text{ cm}
$$

**Part (ii) — Half-wave dipole length:**

$$
L = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{0.333}{2} \approx 0.1667 \text{ m} \approx 16.7 \text{ cm}
$$

**Part (iii) — Line-of-sight range:**

$$
d_{max} = 4.12 \left( \sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2} \right) \text{ km}
$$

$$
\sqrt{h_1} = \sqrt{100} = 10
$$

$$
\sqrt{h_2} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162
$$

$$
d_{max} = 4.12 \times (10 + 3.162) = 4.12 \times 13.162 \approx 54.23 \text{ km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Many students use the formula $d = \sqrt{2Rh}$ (in km) where $R \approx 6371$ km and forget to multiply by 2 for **two antennas**. KTU key expects the **standard $4.12(\sqrt{h_1}+\sqrt{h_2})$** form. **Lose 2 marks** if you write the wrong formula.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice): Modulation, Capacity & Wireless Concepts (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Define **modulation**. Explain **AM and FM** with waveforms. State **two advantages of FM over AM**.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
**Modulation** is the process of varying one or more parameters (amplitude, frequency, or phase) of a high-frequency **carrier wave** in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency **message signal**.

**Carrier wave:**

$$
c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)
$$

**1) Amplitude Modulation (AM):**
The amplitude $A_c$ of the carrier is varied in proportion to the message signal $m(t)$, while frequency and phase are kept constant.

$$
s_{AM}(t) = A_c [1 + k_a \cdot m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Waveforms["AM Waveform Shape"]
        M[Message Signal m of t: low frequency sine] --> AMOut[AM Output: high frequency carrier with envelope shaped like m of t]
    end
```

**2) Frequency Modulation (FM):**
The instantaneous frequency of the carrier is varied in proportion to $m(t)$, while amplitude remains constant.

$$
s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos \left( 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(t)\, dt \right)
```

**Advantages of FM over AM:**
1. **Better noise immunity** — Most noise is amplitude-based. Since FM has constant amplitude, noise can be clipped off by a limiter at the receiver.
2. **Higher fidelity** — FM has a better signal-to-noise ratio and supports stereo broadcast, making it ideal for high-fidelity music.
3. **Smaller capture effect** — FM receivers can reject weaker interfering signals of the same frequency (the *capture effect*).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A communication channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz and an SNR of 1023. Calculate (i) the **Shannon channel capacity**, and (ii) the **Nyquist bit rate** assuming 8-level signaling.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000$ Hz, $\dfrac{S}{N} = 1023$, $M = 8$.

**Part (i) — Shannon Capacity:**

$$
C_S = B \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{S}{N} \right)
$$

$$
C_S = 4000 \times \log_2 (1 + 1023) = 4000 \times \log_2 (1024)
$$

Since $1024 = 2^{10}$:

$$
\log_2 (1024) = 10
$$

$$
C_S = 4000 \times 10 = 40000 \text{ bps} = 40 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Part (ii) — Nyquist Bit Rate:**

$$
C_N = 2B \log_2 M
$$

$$
\log_2 8 = \log_2 2^3 = 3
$$

$$
C_N = 2 \times 4000 \times 3 = 24000 \text{ bps} = 24 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Comparison and Engineering Insight:**

$$
C_S (40 \text{ kbps}) > C_N (24 \text{ kbps})
$$

The **Shannon limit** is the absolute upper bound. The **Nyquist rate** is the practical limit when we restrict ourselves to a fixed number of signal levels $M$. By using **advanced coding + QAM with high $M$** (e.g., 64-QAM, 256-QAM), real systems can approach Shannon's bound.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Writing $\log$ instead of $\log_2$.** Always specify the base in Shannon's formula — *lose 1 mark* if you don't.
> 2. **Mixing up the two formulas.** Nyquist is for *noiseless* channels; Shannon is for *noisy* channels. If the question says "noisy" or "with SNR", **always use Shannon**.
> 3. **Forgetting to add 1 inside the log of Shannon's formula.** The correct term is $\log_2(1 + S/N)$, NOT $\log_2(S/N)$.
> 4. **Wrong units:** $B$ must be in Hz (not kHz) when using the formula directly. Convert **before** substituting.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — General Pitfalls for This Module

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks in Module 4 questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to draw block diagrams** — A 7-mark question without a block diagram loses 2 marks automatically.
> 2. **Confusing Hz with kHz / MHz** — Leads to answers off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. **Not labelling axes** in waveform questions (AM/FM). Always mark *Amplitude* and *Time* axes.
> 4. **Writing only definitions** for 7-mark questions** — KTU expects derivations, examples, advantages, disadvantages, and applications.
> 5. **Confusing $C_N$ with $C_S$** — Memorize the conditions: Nyquist = noiseless, Shannon = noisy.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and read 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] A communication system always has: **Source → Transmitter → Channel (+Noise) → Receiver → Destination**.
- [ ] **Wired media:** Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Optical Fiber. **Wireless:** Radio, Microwave, Satellite, Optical.
- [ ] **Optical fiber** works on **Total Internal Reflection**; condition is $n_1 > n_2$.
- [ ] **EM wave equation:** $c = f\lambda$, with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- [ ] **Half-wave dipole length:** $L = \lambda/2 = c / (2f)$.
- [ ] **Line-of-sight distance:** $d_{max} \approx 4.12(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})$ km.
- [ ] **Modulation types:** AM (Amplitude), FM (Frequency), PM (Phase). Digital: ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM.
- [ ] **Why modulate?** Antenna size reduction, propagation efficiency, multiplexing, noise reduction.
- [ ] **Multiplexing:** FDM (frequency), TDM (time), WDM (wavelength in fiber), CDMA (code).
- [ ] **Nyquist Bit Rate:** $C_N = 2B \log_2 M$ — for *noiseless* channels.
- [ ] **Shannon Capacity:** $C_S = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — for *noisy* channels. Sets the **theoretical maximum**.
- [ ] **SNR in dB:** $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$. **Attenuation in dB:** $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$.
- [ ] **Modes:** Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex.
- [ ] **Cellular concept:** Frequency reuse across non-adjacent hexagonal cells.
- [ ] **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — must sample at least twice the highest signal frequency.
- [ ] **Fiber attenuation:** ~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm — extremely low.
- [ ] **FM is better than AM** for noise immunity and fidelity; **AM is simpler** and used for long-range broadcasting.
- [ ] **5G mmWave band:** 24–52 GHz; **Wi-Fi bands:** 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz; **GSM:** 900 / 1800 MHz.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of GSM, Comparison of 3G, 5G and 6G Communication Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of GSM, Comparison of 3G, 5G and 6G Communication Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) — Formal Definition

**GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications)** is a **2G digital cellular network standard** developed by the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (**ETSI**) to replace the incompatible first-generation (1G) analog systems. It uses a combination of **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)** and **FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)** over a set of frequency bands (commonly **900 MHz** and **1800 MHz** in India/Europe; **850 MHz** and **1900 MHz** in the Americas), providing circuit-switched voice, SMS, and low-rate data services (GPRS/EDGE extensions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** GSM is a *second-generation (2G)* system. The relevant KTU 2024 module focuses on the **functional block diagram of the GSM architecture** and how its sub-systems (MS, BTS, BSC, MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR) interact to deliver a mobile call.

### 1.2 3G, 5G, and 6G — Formal Definitions

| Generation | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **3G (UMTS)** | Universal Mobile Telecommunications System — packet-switched network based on **WCDMA/CDMA2000**, supporting data rates up to **2 Mbps** and enabling mobile multimedia, video calling, and internet access. |
| **5G (IMT-2020)** | Fifth-generation mobile network — **OFDM-based** system using **sub-6 GHz** and **mmWave (24–52 GHz)** bands, **Massive MIMO**, and **network slicing**, targeting peak data rates of **10–20 Gbps** and **< 1 ms** latency. |
| **6G (IMT-2030)** | Sixth-generation mobile network — envisioned to use **sub-THz frequencies (100 GHz – 10 THz)**, **AI-native** air interface, and **holographic communications**, targeting **1 Tbps** peak data rate and **< 0.1 ms** latency. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Telephone" Model

Imagine a giant international hotel chain (the GSM/3G/5G/6G network) where every guest (subscriber) is given a unique room key (SIM card). The chain has a **front desk** (HLR — knows your home address and subscription), **local reception desks** in every city (VLR — knows which guest is currently in which city), and **doormen** at every entrance (BTS — the cell tower catching your signal). The **lift operator** (BSC) connects multiple doormen to the front desk, and the **hotel manager** (MSC) actually routes your call to another hotel (another network).

> When a friend calls you, the network does not know your location — it asks the **front desk (HLR)** where you are registered, then the **local reception (VLR)** in that city, and finally hands the call to the nearest **doorman (BTS)**, who rings the bell on your room's phone (MS). This *location-tracking, call-routing* handshake is the heart of GSM.

### 1.4 The Generational Analogy — From Letters to Teleportation

- **1G** was like shouting across a field (analog voice, no privacy).
- **2G (GSM)** was like writing a *coded letter* (digital SMS, encrypted).
- **3G** was like sending a *small parcel with a picture* (mobile internet, video call).
- **4G** was like *streaming a movie over a fiber line* (high-speed broadband).
- **5G** is like *being in the same room, virtually* (real-time AR/VR, IoT).
- **6G** will be like *telekinesis* — holographic, tactile, and instantaneous.

### 1.5 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light (in free space):** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **GSM carrier spacing:** **200 kHz** per channel
- **GSM TDMA slots per carrier:** **8**
- **UMTS WCDMA chip rate:** **3.84 Mcps**
- **5G NR sub-carrier spacing (numerology $\mu$):** $15 \times 2^\mu$ kHz
- **mmWave 5G band:** **24–52 GHz**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU question on GSM expects you to **draw and label the block diagram** and **explain the role of at least four functional entities** (MS, BTS, BSC, MSC). Memorize the acronyms — they are the most-missed part in the valuation key.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture of GSM — The "Why & How"

The GSM network is logically divided into **three major subsystems**, each with a specific operational role. Understanding *why* each subsystem exists is the key to scoring full marks in 14-mark questions.

**A) Mobile Station (MS)**
- **Why?** It is the only part the user physically sees.
- **How?** It contains the **Mobile Equipment (ME)** — the physical handset — and the **SIM (Subscriber Identity Module)** — a smart card storing the IMSI (International Mobile Subscriber Identity) and the secret key $K_i$.
- Together they uniquely identify the subscriber worldwide.

**B) Base Station Subsystem (BSS)**
- **Why?** The radio interface must be managed locally, not from a single central office thousands of kilometers away.
- **How?** It contains:
  - **BTS (Base Transceiver Station)** — the cell tower with the antennas; performs radio transmission/reception on the air interface (**Um**).
  - **BSC (Base Station Controller)** — manages several BTSs; handles **radio-channel allocation, handover decisions, and power control**.

**C) Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS)**
- **Why?** Calls must be switched and subscribers tracked across the entire network.
- **How?** It contains:
  - **MSC (Mobile Switching Center)** — the heart of the network; performs call routing, switching, and signaling (**SS7**).
  - **HLR (Home Location Register)** — permanent database of all subscribers of the home network (IMSI, services, current VLR address).
  - **VLR (Visitor Location Register)** — temporary database of subscribers *currently roaming* in a particular MSC area.
  - **AUC (Authentication Center)** — stores the secret key $K_i$ and generates **triplets (RAND, SRES, $K_c$)** for authentication and ciphering.
  - **EIR (Equipment Identity Register)** — blacklists stolen handsets using the **IMEI** (International Mobile Equipment Identity).
  - **GMSC (Gateway MSC)** — interface point to the **PSTN** (Public Switched Telephone Network) and other PLMNs.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step GSM Call Setup (How a Call is Placed)

1. **Power-on:** MS scans the Broadcast Control Channel (**BCCH**) of nearby BTSs and registers with the strongest one.
2. **Location update:** MS sends its **IMSI**; VLR stores it and notifies the HLR.
3. **Authentication:** AUC generates a **RAND**, MS computes **SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)**; network verifies.
4. **Ciphering:** Communication is encrypted with key $K_c$ derived via $K_c = \text{A8}(K_i, \text{RAND})$.
5. **Call origination:** MS requests a channel on **RACH**; BSC allocates a **SDCCH** for signaling, then a **TCH** (Traffic Channel).
6. **Routing:** MSC queries HLR → forwards to destination MSC → connects to called party.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Metrics Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important revision artifact** for this topic. Memorize it for the 14-mark comparison question.

\begin{aligned}
\text{Spectral Efficiency} \;\eta &= \frac{\text{Data Rate } R}{\text{Bandwidth } B} \quad \text{(bits/s/Hz)}
\end{aligned}

| Parameter | 3G (UMTS) | 5G (NR) | 6G (IMT-2030) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year of commercial launch | **2001** | **2019** | **~2030 (expected)** |
| Peak data rate | 2 Mbps | 20 Gbps | 1 Tbps |
| User-experienced data rate | ~1 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 1 Gbps |
| Latency (one-way) | 100 ms | 1 ms | $\lt 0.1$ ms |
| Mobility support | 120 km/h | 500 km/h | 1000 km/h |
| Frequency band | 1.8 – 2.1 GHz | sub-6 GHz; 24–52 GHz | sub-THz (100 GHz – 10 THz) |
| Multiple access | WCDMA / CDMA2000 | OFDMA (DL) / SC-FDMA (UL) | AI-driven NOMA / OAM-MDM |
| Channel bandwidth | 5 MHz | $\le 400$ MHz | $\ge 1$ GHz |
| Spectral efficiency | 2.5 bit/s/Hz | 30 bit/s/Hz | 100 bit/s/Hz |
| Connection density | $10^3$ /km$^2$ | $10^6$ /km$^2$ | $10^8$ /km$^2$ |
| Core network | Packet-switched (SGSN/GGSN) | Service-Based (5GC) | AI-Native (6GC) |
| Key technology drivers | CDMA, packet core | Massive MIMO, mmWave, slicing | Terahertz, holographic, AI/ML |
| Typical applications | Video call, mobile internet | IoT, autonomous cars, AR/VR | Digital twin, tactile internet, BCI |
| ITU specification | IMT-2000 | IMT-2020 | IMT-2030 |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Telecom industry:** These metrics directly drive the **cell-site density**, **antenna design**, and **backhaul capacity** planning by operators.
- **Smart cities:** 5G and 6G are foundational for **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)**, **Industry 4.0**, and **remote surgery**.
- **Spectrum policy:** The shift from sub-6 GHz to **mmWave and sub-THz** demands new propagation models (Friis path-loss with $f^2$ dependence at higher frequencies).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Spectral Efficiency Comparison

Spectral efficiency $\eta$ is the cornerstone parameter for comparing generations. It is defined as:

\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{R_b}{B} \quad \text{[bits/s/Hz]}
\end{aligned}

where $R_b$ is the achievable bit rate and $B$ is the occupied bandwidth.

**Step 1 — For 3G (WCDMA):**
The chip rate is fixed at $R_c = 3.84$ Mcps. With a spreading factor $SF$ (range 4 to 512), the user data rate is:

\begin{aligned}
R_b^{(3G)} &= \frac{R_c}{SF} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{SF} \text{ bits/s}
\end{aligned}

For a typical voice call with $SF = 128$:
\begin{aligned}
R_b^{(3G)} &= \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{128} = 30{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 30 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}

For a data user with $SF = 4$ (lowest spreading), in a 5 MHz channel:
\begin{aligned}
R_b^{(3G,\max)} &\approx \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{4} = 960 \text{ kbps} \;\to\; 2 \text{ Mbps (with multi-code)}
\end{aligned}

Spectral efficiency:
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{3G} &= \frac{2 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.4 \text{ bit/s/Hz} \;\text{(practical)} \;\to\; 2.5 \text{ bit/s/Hz} \;\text{(peak)}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — For 5G NR (OFDM with 64-QAM):**
Using Shannon's capacity bound for a baseline SNR $\rho$:

\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_2(1 + \rho) \quad \text{[bits/s]}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow \eta_{\max} &= \log_2(1 + \rho) \quad \text{[bits/s/Hz]}
\end{aligned}

For $\rho = 1023$ (high-SNR mmWave link, ~30 dB):
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{5G,\max} &= \log_2(1024) = 10 \text{ bit/s/Hz per layer}
\end{aligned}

With **Massive MIMO** providing 16–64 spatial layers, and **64-QAM/256-QAM** modulation, 5G achieves:

\begin{aligned}
\eta_{5G,\text{peak}} &\approx 30 \text{ bit/s/Hz}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — For 6G (sub-THz, OAM, AI-coding):**
6G is expected to use **terahertz bands** with very wide bandwidths and AI-optimized adaptive coding. The theoretical Shannon bound for a 1 GHz channel at $\rho = 30$ dB is:

\begin{aligned}
C_{6G} &= 10^9 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) \approx 10^9 \cdot 9.97 \approx 9.97 \text{ Gbps per layer}
\end{aligned}

With 100 spatial layers and orbital angular momentum (OAM) multiplexing:
\begin{aligned}
R_{b,\text{peak}}^{(6G)} &\approx 1 \text{ Tbps}
\end{aligned}
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{6G,\text{target}} &\approx 100 \text{ bit/s/Hz}
\end{aligned}

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Quick-Revision Table)

| Metric | Improvement 3G → 5G | Improvement 5G → 6G |
|---|---|---|
| Peak data rate | ×10 (2 Mbps → 20 Gbps) | ×50 (20 Gbps → 1 Tbps) |
| Latency | ÷100 (100 ms → 1 ms) | ÷10 (1 ms → 0.1 ms) |
| Spectral efficiency | ×12 (2.5 → 30 bit/s/Hz) | ×3.3 (30 → 100 bit/s/Hz) |
| Connection density | ×1000 ($10^3$ → $10^6$/km$^2$) | ×100 ($10^6$ → $10^8$/km$^2$) |
| Mobility | ×4 (120 → 500 km/h) | ×2 (500 → 1000 km/h) |

### 3.3 GSM Channel Frequency Reuse Calculation (Optional Exam Hook)

GSM uses a **frequency reuse cluster of size N**, typically $N = 4$ or $7$ for omni-directional cells. The **co-channel reuse ratio (CCRR)** is:

\begin{aligned}
Q &= \sqrt{3N} \quad \text{(for hexagonal cells)}
\end{aligned}

For $N = 7$:
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58
\end{aligned}

The co-channel interference ratio $C/I$ is given by:
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{I} &= \frac{Q^n}{6} \quad \text{where } n \text{ is the path-loss exponent (typically 3.5 to 4)}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{I} &= \frac{(4.58)^4}{6} = \frac{440}{6} \approx 73.3 \;\text{(linear)} \approx 18.6 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}

The GSM standard requires $C/I \ge 9$ dB — the calculated value of 18.6 dB comfortably satisfies the requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> The 3.5 above shows that a tighter reuse cluster increases capacity *but* lowers $C/I$. KTU often tests the trade-off using the above $Q$ formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram of GSM Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUB1["Mobile Station MS"]
        node1["Mobile Equipment ME"]
        node2["SIM Card with IMSI and Ki"]
    end

    subgraph SUB2["Base Station Subsystem BSS"]
        node3["BTS 1 with Cell Tower"]
        node4["BTS 2 with Cell Tower"]
        node5["BSC Base Station Controller"]
    end

    subgraph SUB3["Network and Switching Subsystem NSS"]
        node6["MSC Mobile Switching Center"]
        node7["HLR Home Location Register"]
        node8["VLR Visitor Location Register"]
        node9["AUC Authentication Center"]
        node10["EIR Equipment Identity Register"]
        node11["GMSC Gateway MSC"]
    end

    subgraph SUB4["External Networks"]
        node12["PSTN Public Telephone Network"]
        node13["ISDN and Data Networks"]
    end

    node1 -->|"Um radio interface"| node3
    node1 -->|"Um radio interface"| node4
    node3 -->|"Abis interface"| node5
    node4 -->|"Abis interface"| node5
    node5 -->|"A interface signaling"| node6
    node6 <-->|"D and MAP"| node7
    node6 <-->|"B and C interfaces"| node8
    node7 <-->|"MAP H interface"| node9
    node6 <-->|"F interface IMEI check"| node10
    node6 -->|"call to other PLMN or fixed line"| node11
    node11 --> node12
    node11 --> node13
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** The KTU key mandates that you label the **interfaces** (Um, Abis, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H) connecting the blocks. The **A interface** (BSS to MSC) and **Um interface** (MS to BTS) are the most frequently tested.

### 4.2 Mermaid Comparison Flow — 3G vs 5G vs 6G Functional Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN3["3G UMTS IMT-2000"]
        g3a["WCDMA air interface"]
        g3b["Packet core SGSN and GGSN"]
        g3c["Video calling and mobile internet"]
    end

    subgraph GEN5["5G NR IMT-2020"]
        g5a["OFDM with mmWave 24 to 52 GHz"]
        g5b["Massive MIMO 64 to 256 antennas"]
        g5c["Network slicing and URLLC"]
        g5d["5GC service based architecture"]
    end

    subgraph GEN6["6G IMT-2030"]
        g6a["Sub THz 100 GHz to 10 THz"]
        g6b["AI native air interface"]
        g6c["OAM and orbital multiplexing"]
        g6d["Holographic and tactile internet"]
    end

    GEN3 -->|"Evolution 2010"| GEN5
    GEN5 -->|"Evolution 2030"| GEN6

    g3c -.->|"enables"| g5c
    g5c -.->|"extends to"| g6d
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Block Diagram — GSM Logical Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["L1 User Layer"]
        L1a["Subscriber with Mobile Equipment"]
        L1b["SIM card authenticates user"]
    end

    subgraph L2["L2 Access Layer BSS"]
        L2a["BTS radio transmission reception"]
        L2b["BSC channel allocation and handover"]
    end

    subgraph L3["L3 Core Network Layer NSS"]
        L3a["MSC call switching"]
        L3b["HLR permanent subscriber DB"]
        L3c["VLR temporary subscriber DB"]
        L3d["AUC secret keys and triplets"]
        L3e["EIR blacklists stolen handsets"]
    end

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L3a
    L3 --> L3b
    L3 --> L3c
    L3 --> L3d
    L3 --> L3e
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Define GSM. List any four major functional entities of a GSM network and state the function of each in one line.

**Model Answer:**
GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) is a **2G digital cellular standard** using **TDMA/FDMA** in the **900/1800 MHz** band to provide voice, SMS, and data services.

Four major functional entities:
1. **MS (Mobile Station):** The user handset containing the SIM; identified by IMSI.
2. **BTS (Base Transceiver Station):** The cell tower; transmits and receives on the **Um** radio interface.
3. **BSC (Base Station Controller):** Controls a group of BTSs; handles handover and radio-channel allocation.
4. **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** Central switching node; routes calls between subscribers and to external networks.

*(Any four entities with correct one-line functions — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Compare 3G and 5G on the basis of (i) peak data rate, (ii) latency, and (iii) access technology.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 3G (UMTS) | 5G (NR) |
|---|---|---|
| Peak data rate | 2 Mbps | 20 Gbps |
| Latency | 100 ms | $\lt 1$ ms |
| Access technology | WCDMA / CDMA2000 | OFDMA / SC-FDMA, Massive MIMO, mmWave |

*(Tabular comparison with correct numbers and terminology — 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

**Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **Question A OR Question B**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]*

**(a)** Draw the **functional block diagram of a GSM system** and explain the functions of **MS, BTS, BSC, MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, and EIR**. (10 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **GSM call-setup procedure** between two mobile subscribers. (4 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid block in SECTION 4.)* The student must draw **three vertical layers** (MS, BSS, NSS) and label **all eight** entities. Each entity's role:

- **MS:** User device = ME + SIM. SIM stores IMSI and $K_i$.
- **BTS:** Cell-site radio; communicates with MS on the **Um** interface.
- **BSC:** Manages many BTSs; performs **handover, channel allocation, power control**.
- **MSC:** Switches calls, performs **SS7 signaling**, links to PSTN via GMSC.
- **HLR:** Permanent DB of all home subscribers (IMSI, services, current VLR pointer).
- **VLR:** Temporary DB of subscribers currently in the area; copies HLR data on entry.
- **AUC:** Stores $K_i$; generates **RAND–SRES–$K_c$ triplets** for authentication and ciphering.
- **EIR:** Maintains **IMEI black-list/white-list** to block stolen handsets.

*(For each of the 8 entities — 1 mark each = 8 marks. Block diagram 2 marks.)*

**(b) Call Setup Steps (4 marks):**
1. Caller dials the B-number and presses "send". MS requests a channel on **RACH**.
2. BSC allocates a **SDCCH**; MS transmits the called number and its own IMSI.
3. MSC receives the call request and queries the **HLR** to find the called subscriber.
4. HLR returns the address of the VLR currently serving the called MS; MSC routes the call to that VLR/MSC.
5. Paging is broadcast on the **PCH** of all BTSs in the called party's location area.
6. The called MS responds on **RACH**; a **TCH** is allocated; ringing starts; speech path is established.

*(Each step ~0.7 mark; full credit if flow is correct.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]*

**(a)** Compare **3G, 5G, and 6G** communication technologies on any **seven technical parameters** in a tabular form. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat **functional flow diagram**, describe how **5G enables URLLC, eMBB, and mMTC** services through **network slicing**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table:** *(Refer to the comparison table in SECTION 2.3 above.)* The student should reproduce a clean, well-labeled table covering: peak data rate, latency, frequency band, multiple access, spectral efficiency, connection density, applications, and ITU specification.

*(7 marks — half mark per row × 7 rows + 0.5 for the header = 4 marks. A brief explanation of *why* the values differ gets the remaining 3 marks.)*

**(b) Network Slicing in 5G (7 marks):**
- **eMBB (enhanced Mobile BroadBand):** Sliced for **high throughput**; uses **mmWave (28 GHz)** and **sub-6 GHz** bands; **Massive MIMO** with 64T64R antennas.
- **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Comm.):** Sliced for **sub-1 ms latency** and **99.999% reliability**; uses **mini-slots** in 5G NR (slot duration = 0.125 ms); numerology $\mu = 3$ at 120 kHz SCS.
- **mMTC (massive Machine-Type Comm.):** Sliced for **$10^6$ devices/km²**; uses **narrowband IoT (NB-IoT)** and **LTE-M**; designed for low-power, low-data sensors.

The **5G Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** allows each slice to have its own **AMF, SMF, UPF** instances logically separated over a shared physical infrastructure.

*(Slice diagram 3 marks + service descriptions 4 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse HLR and VLR.** HLR is *permanent and at the home network*; VLR is *temporary and at the visited area*. This single mistake can cost 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Do NOT write "5G uses CDMA."** It is a common fallacy. 5G uses **OFDM/OFDMA**; 3G was the generation that used CDMA/WCDMA.
> 3. **Block diagrams must be drawn with boxes and arrows**, not just bullet points. The KTU key explicitly awards **2 marks** for the diagram's structure.
> 4. **Acronyms without expansion** are penalized: write **"BSC (Base Station Controller)"** at least once.
> 5. **6G data is "expected/projected"** — never write "6G uses …" in absolute present tense. Use *"6G is expected to use sub-THz bands."*
> 6. **Numerology $\mu$:** for 5G URLLC, remember the SCS = $15 \times 2^\mu$ kHz formula. A sub-slot at $\mu = 3$ gives 120 kHz SCS, which is the URLLC workhorse.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **GSM = 2G, TDMA/FDMA, 900/1800 MHz** (India/Europe).
- ✅ **GSM architecture has 3 layers**: MS, BSS (BTS + BSC), NSS (MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR, GMSC).
- ✅ **Interface Um** = MS to BTS (radio); **Abis** = BTS to BSC; **A** = BSS to MSC.
- ✅ **3G** = WCDMA, 2 Mbps, 100 ms latency, packet core, video calling.
- ✅ **5G** = OFDM + Massive MIMO + mmWave, 20 Gbps, 1 ms latency, three slices (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC).
- ✅ **6G** = sub-THz, AI-native, 1 Tbps, 0.1 ms, holographic + tactile + digital-twin applications.
- ✅ **Spectral efficiency** $\eta = R_b / B$ is the universal metric for generational comparison.
- ✅ **Connection density** jumps by **1000× from 3G to 5G** — this is the IoT enabler.
- ✅ **Always draw the block diagram in 3 vertical boxes** (MS, BSS, NSS) for clean valuation.
- ✅ **Frequency-reuse Q-factor**: $Q = \sqrt{3N}$; for $N=7$, $Q \approx 4.58$.
- ✅ **Authentication in GSM**: AUC generates RAND → MS returns SRES = A3($K_i$, RAND).
- ✅ **6G is projected, not deployed** — use future tense; do not over-commit on numbers.

> **One-line memory hook:**
> *"GSM tracks, 3G connects, 5G slices, 6G thinks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagrams of Electronic Instrumentation System, Digital Multimeter, Function Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Block Diagrams of Electronic Instrumentation System, Digital Multimeter, Function Generator # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Electronic Instrumentation System (EIS) **Formal Definition:** > An **Electronic Instrumentation System (EIS)** is a precisely engineered arrangement of physical and electronic components designed to measure, monitor, record, and control physical quantities (such as temperature, pressure, flow, strain, or voltage) by converting these quantities into manageable electrical signals for accurate analysis, display, and decision-making. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every instrumentation system, regardless of complexity, follows a standardized functional block architecture. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to draw, label, and explain this canonical block diagram. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Think of an Electronic Instrumentation System exactly like a **human nervous system**: - Your **eyes/skin** are the *primary sensors* (detect light, heat, pressure). - The **nerve fibers** act as the *transmission lines* (data transmission element). - Your **spinal cord/brain** performs *signal processing and decision-making* (variable manipulation element). - Your **voice/actions** are the *output* (data presentation/control element). The "primary sensed variable" is simply whatever the sensor is built to "feel" in the physical world. --- ## 1.2 The Six Canonical Functional Blocks Any complete measurement chain consists of these **three mandatory stages**, often subdivided into **six functional blocks** as mandated by KTU's standard syllabus. ### Stage A — Input / Sensing Stage | Block | Function | Key Word | |---|---|---| | **Primary Sensing Element** | Directly interacts with the physical quantity. | "Sense" | | **Variable Conversion Element** | Converts the sensed physical signal into a more usable form (e.g., RTD changes resistance with temperature). | "Convert" | ### Stage B — Intermediate / Processing Stage | Block | Function | Key Word | |---|---|---| | **Variable Manipulation Element** | Amplifies, filters, integrates, or otherwise conditions the signal. | "Condition" | | **Data Transmission Element** | Carries the conditioned signal from the field to the processing/display unit. | "Transmit" | ### Stage C — Output / Termination Stage | Block | Function | Key Word | |---|---|---| | **Data Presentation Element** | Displays, records, or controls — the final read-out. | "Display" | | **Data Storage Element** | Archives data for later analysis. | "Store" | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** *"S → C → M → T → P → S"* — **S**ense, **C**onvert, **M**anipulate, **T**ransmit, **P**resent, **S**tore. --- ## 1.3 Digital Multimeter (DMM) **Formal Definition:** > A **Digital Multimeter (DMM)** is a versatile, multi-function electronic measuring instrument that quantifies electrical quantities — typically **Voltage (V)**, **Current (A)**, and **Resistance (Ω)** — and displays the result as a discrete numerical value on a digital LCD/LED readout, replacing the needle movement of legacy analog meters. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Swiss Army Knife Just as a Swiss Army Knife combines a blade, scissors, screwdriver, and corkscrew into one compact tool, a **DMM** combines a **voltmeter**, **ammeter**, **ohmmeter**, and often a **frequency meter / continuity tester** into a single hand-held device. It is the electrician's and engineer's "multi-tool." --- ## 1.4 Function Generator **Formal Definition:** > A **Function Generator** is a precision signal-generating instrument that produces standardized, repetitive electronic waveforms — most commonly **sine, square, and triangular waves** — with user-adjustable **frequency**, **amplitude**, and **DC offset**, used to test, troubleshoot, and characterize electronic circuits. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Audio Tuning Fork Imagine a musician's tuning fork that can produce pure notes of *any* frequency from a low rumble to a high whistle. A **Function Generator** is the electronic equivalent — it is the "voice box" of a lab, creating clean, well-defined electrical tones that drive circuits under test. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):** A **Function Generator** is a simple analog/digital device producing standard waveshapes. A more sophisticated version, the **Arbitrary Waveform Generator (AWG)**, can synthesize *any* user-defined waveform. KTU Module-4 focuses on the standard Function Generator. --- ## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants / Metrics Used in This Module | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Standard Mains Frequency | $f_{mains}$ | $\mathbf{50\ \text{Hz}}$ (India / KTU standard) | | Logic Voltage Levels (TTL) | $V_{CC}$ | $\mathbf{+5\ \text{V}}$ | | CMOS Voltage Levels | $V_{DD}$ | $\mathbf{+3.3\ \text{V}}$ to $\mathbf{+15\ \text{V}}$ | | Standard DMM Input Impedance | $R_{in}$ | $\mathbf{10\ \text{M}\Omega}$ | | Standard Reference | $V_{ref}$ | $\mathbf{2.5\ \text{V}}$ / $\mathbf{5.0\ \text{V}}$ | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Block-Wise Operation of an Electronic Instrumentation System ### Block 1 — Primary Sensing Element (Sensor) - **Why needed:** The physical world speaks in non-electrical "languages" — heat, force, light, pressure. We must first *listen* to it. - **How it works:** A transducer directly couples with the measurand and produces a *first-stage* response. - **Examples:** Thermocouple (heat → mV), Strain Gauge (force → resistance), LDR (light → resistance), Piezoelectric crystal (pressure → charge). ### Block 2 — Variable Conversion Element - **Why needed:** Sensor output is often too weak or in a non-standard form (e.g., resistance change). - **How it works:** Converts one form of variable into another, more practical, form — usually into an electrical signal (voltage or current). - **Examples:** - RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) → resistance change → converted by a **Wheatstone bridge** into a voltage. - Bourdon tube (pressure → mechanical displacement) → converted by an **LVDT** into a voltage. ### Block 3 — Variable Manipulation Element (Signal Conditioning) - **Why needed:** Raw signals are noisy, weak, and contaminated. - **How it works:** Performs **amplification, filtering, integration, differentiation, linearization, and isolation**. - **Examples:** Operational amplifier circuits, Active filters (low-pass, high-pass), Instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD620, INA128). ### Block 4 — Data Transmission Element - **Why needed:** In large plants (refineries, power stations), the sensor may be 1 km away from the control room. - **How it works:** Carries the conditioned signal using **wired (4–20 mA current loop, twisted pair)** or **wireless (RF, fiber optic)** media. - **Why current loops (4–20 mA)?** Because current is *immune to voltage drop* over long distances. ### Block 5 — Data Presentation Element - **Why needed:** Humans need to *read* the data or automated controllers need to *act* on it. - **How it works:** Displays on **meters, LCDs, LEDs**, or sends control signals to **valves, relays, motors**. - **Examples:** Digital indicator, CRO, Recorder, Alarm, PLC actuator. ### Block 6 — Data Storage Element - **Why needed:** For later analysis, audit, regulatory compliance, and trending. - **How it works:** Records on **magnetic tapes, hard disks, SSDs, cloud servers**, or chart recorders. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** This entire architecture is the backbone of **SCADA**, **DCS (Distributed Control Systems)**, **IoT sensor nodes**, and **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** platforms. Every smart factory, every patient monitor in an ICU, and every modern car's Engine Control Unit (ECU) is a working embodiment of this block diagram. --- ## 2.2 Digital Multimeter — Deep Dive ### 2.2.1 Block Diagram of a DMM A modern DMM is essentially a **voltmeter at heart** that cleverly converts current and resistance into measurable voltages using internal shunt resistors and a current source. The internal signal flow is: $$\text{Input} \rightarrow \text{Attenuator / Range Selector} \rightarrow \text{Function Selector (V/I/}\Omega\text{)} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioner} \rightarrow \text{A/D Converter} \rightarrow \text{Decoding \& Display Driver} \rightarrow \text{LCD/LED Display}$$ ### 2.2.2 Key Specifications of a DMM | Specification | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | **Resolution** | $N$ | Smallest detectable change at full scale. | $\mathbf{3.5,\ 4.5,\ 5.5}$ digits | | **Range** | $R$ | Maximum measurable magnitude. | $\mathbf{mV, V, kV}$ | | **Accuracy** | $\pm\%$ | Closeness to true value. | $\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 0.025\%$ | | **Input Impedance** | $R_{in}$ | Resistance presented to circuit under test. | $\mathbf{10\ M\Omega}$ (V mode) | | **Display Count** | $C$ | Maximum integer value the display can show. | e.g., $\mathbf{1999}$ ($3\frac{1}{2}$ digit) | | **True RMS** | — | Measures correct heating value of non-sinusoidal AC. | Yes / No (spec dependent) | ### 2.2.3 Resolution Formula (High-Yield) $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Full Scale Voltage}}{\text{Display Count}}$$ For a $3\frac{1}{2}$ digit DMM on the $\mathbf{2\ V}$ range: $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{2\ \text{V}}{1999} \approx \mathbf{1\ \text{mV}}$$ ### 2.2.4 AC Measurement — True RMS vs Average Responding A typical DMM internally rectifies AC and assumes a **sine wave** to convert the average value to RMS via the **form factor (1.11)**. For non-sinusoidal waves (square, triangular, distorted), this is **wrong**, and a **True-RMS DMM** must be used. $$V_{rms,\text{sine assumed}} = 1.11 \times V_{avg,\text{rectified}}$$ $$V_{rms,\text{true}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** When asked the *conversion factor* between average and RMS for a sine wave, write $\mathbf{1.11}$ (form factor), and for peak-to-RMS, write $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ (crest factor's inverse). Mixing them up costs marks. --- ## 2.3 Function Generator — Deep Dive ### 2.3.1 Block Diagram of a Function Generator (Hartley-Based Architecture) Modern function generators use **Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)** or the classical **astable-multivibrator + shaper** approach. The classical KTU-prescribed block flow is: $$\text{DC Power Supply} \rightarrow \text{Variable-Frequency Oscillator} \rightarrow \text{Waveform Shaper} \rightarrow \text{Amplitude Control} \rightarrow \text{DC Offset Adder} \rightarrow \text{Output Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Output Terminals}$$ ### 2.3.2 Key Specifications | Specification | Symbol | Typical Range | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Frequency Range** | $f$ | $\mathbf{0.1\ \text{Hz}}$ to $\mathbf{20\ \text{MHz}}$ | Determines coverage of audio / RF tests. | | **Amplitude Range** | $V_{p-p}$ | $\mathbf{10\ mV_{p-p}}$ to $\mathbf{20\ V_{p-p}}$ | Strength of output. | | **DC Offset** | $V_{off}$ | $\mathbf{\pm 5\ V}$ to $\mathbf{\pm 10\ V}$ | Shifts waveform above/below ground. | | **Output Impedance** | $Z_{out}$ | $\mathbf{50\ \Omega}$ | Standard RF match. | | **Waveform Choices** | — | Sine, Square, Triangle, Ramp, Pulse | Use-case flexibility. | | **Total Harmonic Distortion** | $THD$ | $\mathbf{<1\%}$ for sine | Waveform purity. | ### 2.3.3 Critical Waveform Parameters (Formulas) | Parameter | Formula | Where Used | |---|---|---| | **RMS value of sine wave** | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | KTU 3-markers | | **Average value of full sine** | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | Rectifier outputs | | **RMS of square wave** | $V_{rms} = V_m$ | Digital clock simulations | | **RMS of triangle wave** | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Ramp testing | | **Frequency–Period relation** | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Universal | | **Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100\%$ | Pulse generation | ### 2.3.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > **Save this table — it is the KTU Module-4 Formula Bible.** | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Sine wave RMS | | 2 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ | Sine wave average (full wave) | | 3 | $V_{rms} = V_m$ | Square wave RMS | | 4 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Triangle wave RMS | | 5 | $V_{pp} = 2 V_m$ | Peak-to-peak | | 6 | $\text{Form Factor} = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = 1.11$ | Sine wave specific | | 7 | $\text{Crest Factor} = \dfrac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \sqrt{2}$ | Sine wave specific | | 8 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency-period | | 9 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | | 10 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{FSV}}{C}$ | DMM resolution | > **Engineering Utility:** Function generators are the "heartbeat" of every electronics lab — they drive audio amplifiers, sweep filters, test ADC/DAC chips, and modulate RF transmitters. Every smartphone chipset is tested using a function generator before being shipped. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Average Value of a Half-Wave Rectified Sine Wave The half-wave rectified sine is described by: $$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for } 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi$$ $$v(t) = 0 \quad \text{for } \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$$ **Step 1 — Definition of Average Value** The average over one full period $T$ is: $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\,dt$$ **Step 2 — Split the Integral Across the Two Sub-Intervals** Since the function is zero in the second half: $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2} V_m \sin(\omega t)\,dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} 0\,dt\right]$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the First Integral** Using $\int \sin(\omega t)\,dt = -\dfrac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega}$: $$\int_0^{T/2} V_m \sin(\omega t)\,dt = V_m \left[-\frac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega}\right]_0^{T/2}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Limits** At $t = T/2$, $\omega t = \pi$, so $\cos(\pi) = -1$. At $t = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$. $$= V_m \left[-\frac{\cos(\pi)}{\omega} + \frac{\cos(0)}{\omega}\right] = \frac{V_m}{\omega}\left[-(-1) + 1\right] = \frac{2V_m}{\omega}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi / T$** $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{2V_m}{2\pi/T} = \frac{2V_m}{2\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}$$ $$\boxed{V_{avg,\text{half-wave}} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318\, V_m}$$ > This is the standard "DC equivalent" of a half-wave rectified sine — a foundational result for rectifier and DMM-AC questions. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — RMS Value of a Sine Wave from First Principles **Step 1 — Definition of RMS** $$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt$$ For $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: $$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\,dt$$ **Step 2 — Use the Identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$** $$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2T}\int_0^{T} \left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right]dt$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate Integral** The cosine term integrates to zero over a full period because it is symmetric: $$\int_0^{T} \cos(2\omega t)\,dt = \left[\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega}\right]_0^{T} = 0$$ So the integral reduces to: $$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2T}\int_0^{T} 1\,dt = \frac{V_m^2}{2T} \cdot T = \frac{V_m^2}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Take the Square Root** $$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\, V_m}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation — DMM Resolution for a $4\tfrac{1}{2}$ Digit Meter on $20\ \text{V}$ Range **Step 1 — Determine the Display Count** A $4\tfrac{1}{2}$ digit meter can display up to: $$C = 19999$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Resolution Formula** $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Full Scale Voltage}}{C} = \frac{20\ \text{V}}{19999}$$ $$\text{Resolution} = 0.00100005\ \text{V} = \mathbf{1\ \text{mV}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Percentage Resolution** $$\%\text{Resolution} = \frac{1\ \text{mV}}{20\ \text{V}} \times 100\% = \mathbf{0.005\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Higher digit count = higher resolution.** A $5\tfrac{1}{2}$ digit meter would give $1\ \mu\text{V}$ resolution on the $20\ \text{V}$ range — used in lab-grade instruments. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Function Generator Output in Sine Mode **Problem:** A function generator is set to produce a sine wave with amplitude $V_m = 10\ \text{V}$ and frequency $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$, with a DC offset of $+2\ \text{V}$. **Step 1 — Express the Waveform Mathematically** $$v(t) = V_{off} + V_m \sin(2\pi f t) = 2 + 10 \sin(2000\pi t)\ \text{V}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Peak-to-Peak Voltage** $$V_{p-p} = 2 V_m = 2 \times 10 = \mathbf{20\ V}$$ **Step 3 — Compute RMS of AC Component Only** $$V_{rms,\text{ac}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10}{1.414} = \mathbf{7.07\ V}$$ **Step 4 — Total RMS (Including DC Offset)** The true total RMS is: $$V_{rms,\text{total}} = \sqrt{V_{dc}^2 + V_{rms,\text{ac}}^2} = \sqrt{2^2 + 7.07^2} = \sqrt{4 + 50} = \sqrt{54} \approx \mathbf{7.35\ V}$$ **Step 5 — Period** $$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = \mathbf{1\ \text{ms}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU frequently asks comparison tables in 7–14 mark questions. Memorize this matrix.** | Feature | Analog Meter | Digital Multimeter (DMM) | |---|---|---| | **Display** | Needle on scale | Numeric LCD/LED | | **Reading Speed** | Slow (mechanical) | Fast (instant) | | **Accuracy** | Low ($\pm 2$–$5\%$) | High ($\pm 0.025$–$0.5\%$) | | **Input Impedance** | Low ($20\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{V}$) | High ($10\ \text{M}\Omega$) | | **Auto-ranging** | No | Yes (in modern DMMs) | | **Power Required** | None (passive) | Battery / mains | | **Parallax Error** | Yes | No | | **Microprocessor Interface** | No | Yes (RS-232, USB, Bluetooth) | | **Cost** | Very low | Moderate to high | | Feature | Function Generator | Signal Generator | Arbitrary Waveform Generator (AWG) | |---|---|---|---| | **Waveforms** | Std (sine, square, triangle) | Mostly sine (RF) | Any user-defined | | **Frequency Range** | $0.1\ \text{Hz}$ – $20\ \text{MHz}$ | $100\ \text{kHz}$ – $40\ \text{GHz}$ | DC – $1\ \text{GHz}$ | | **Modulation** | None / AM/FM (basic) | AM, FM, PM, Pulse | All + IQ modulation | | **Cost** | Low | High | Very high | | **Application** | Lab testing | RF / Communication | Radar, medical, research | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Canonical Electronic Instrumentation System — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Physical Quantity

RTD, LVDT, Bridge] B2 --> C1[Variable Manipulation Element
Amplifier, Filter, Linearizer] C1 --> D1[Data Transmission Element
4 to 20 mA Loop, Fiber, Wireless] D1 --> E1[Data Presentation Element
Display, Recorder, Controller] E1 --> F1[Data Storage Element
Disk, Cloud, Chart, Database] F1 --> G1[Final Output Readout
LCD, Alarm, Actuator] A1 -.measured by.-> B1 classDef sensor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef signal fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 class A1,B1,B2 sensor class C1,D1 signal class E1,F1,G1 output ``` > **Reading Guide:** The signal flows *left to right* — from the physical world to the final human/machine-readable output. Each block transforms the signal in a defined way. --- ## 4.2 Digital Multimeter (DMM) — Internal Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB IN1[Input Terminals

Dual Slope or Sigma Delta] ADC1 --> DEC1[Decoder and Display Driver] DEC1 --> DSP1[LCD or LED Display

Auto Ranging Logic] CTL1 -.controls.-> SW1 CTL1 -.controls.-> FN1 CTL1 -.controls.-> SC1 PWR1[Battery or DC Supply] --> REG1[Voltage Regulator] REG1 --> CTL1 REG1 --> ADC1 REG1 --> DEC1 classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef procNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000 classDef outNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef pwrNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 class IN1,SW1,FN1 inputNode class SC1,ADC1,DEC1,CTL1 procNode class DSP1 outNode class PWR1,REG1 pwrNode ``` > **Reading Guide:** Notice that the *microcontroller* (gray block) closes the loop, controlling ranging and function selection. This is why modern DMMs can "auto-range" — a feature the analog meter could never have. --- ## 4.3 Function Generator — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR PS1[DC Power Supply

0 dB to 60 dB in 10 dB Steps] ATT1 --> OUT1[Output Terminals
50 Ohm BNC Connector] OFF1 --> OUT1 KF1[Frequency Control Knob] -.tunes.-> OSC1 KA1[Amplitude Control Knob] -.controls.-> AMP1 KO1[DC Offset Knob] -.controls.-> OFF1 KW1[Waveform Selector Switch] -.selects.-> WFS1 classDef pwrNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef sigNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef outNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef ctrlNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000 class PS1,AMP1,OFF1,ATT1 pwrNode class OSC1,WFS1 sigNode class OUT1 outNode class KF1,KA1,KO1,KW1 ctrlNode ``` > **Reading Guide:** The four colored knob nodes at the bottom represent the **user controls** that an examiner may ask you to identify. The waveform shaper is the "personality block" — it transforms the oscillator's basic output into any chosen waveshape. --- ## 4.4 End-to-End Application — Temperature Monitoring System This diagram shows how **all three topics** integrate in a real industrial scenario. ```mermaid flowchart LR TP[Boiler Surface] --> TS[Thermocouple
